experiments.title: Can Highlighting the Monthly Impact Boost Recurring Giving?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a monthly giving callout above the donation form on the main donation page will increase monthly donor conversions, as framing monthly giving as impactful and rewarding will motivate recurring support without displacing larger one-time donations. Framing monthly giving as impactful and rewarding will motivate recurring support without displacing larger one-time donations.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/26/2025 - 10/26/2025
The organization’s main donation page serves both committed donors and new visitors, playing a central role in overall giving. Unlike acquisition-focused pages meant to convert new audiences, this page often attracts high-value donors and returning supporters. As such, it presents a high-opportunity but high-risk space for testing.
In previous tests on acquisition donation pages, the organization found that adding a monthly gift callout that highlights the recurring impact and donor benefits increased the number of donors who chose to give monthly. Those tests indicated that, with the right framing, some page visitors can be persuaded to commit to becoming long-term donors.
This experiment asked whether that insight would transfer to the main donation page (without cannibalizing revenue from larger one-time gifts). Would prompting donors to “consider a monthly gift” increase monthly conversions? Or would it cause high-capacity donors to downshift from large one-time gifts to smaller recurring ones?
The Control used the standard donation page layout, which defaults to a one-time gift and includes no additional prompts about monthly giving.
The Treatment introduced a soft callout above the form that read: “Would you consider a monthly donation? Your monthly gift of $15 ensures families in spiritual crisis can find trusted help the moment they need it—and receive biblical support year-round. Plus, you’ll get to choose a new free resource every month for your family.”
No other changes were made to the page layout or the form’s functionality. We randomly split traffic 50/50 between both versions and ran this test for 1 month.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This test demonstrated that a soft monthly giving prompt above the donation form can significantly increase recurring donor conversions—without suppressing large gifts and with potential upside for smaller one-time donations.
The Treatment produced a 125% increase in monthly donor conversions (2.1% vs. 0.9%), with a statistically significant 99.3% level of confidence.
 	Recurring donor volume more than doubled (33 vs. 15), while the average monthly gift remained steady ($30 on Treatment vs. $31 on Control, after removing an outlier)
 	While the Treatment had fewer gifts over $1,000 (35 vs. 41), this difference was not statistically significant (44% level of confidence), indicating no reliable evidence that large one-time gifts were cannibalized.
smaller one-time gifts (under $1,000)
The Treatment saw a 9.5% lift in volume, with an 86.4% level of confidence -- this is a strong directional signal, though not statistically conclusive
 	The average gift amount also increased, from $132 on the Control to $140 on the Treatment
Next Steps for Testing:
Explore alternate monthly ask amounts (e.g., $20, $25, or $30) to assess the revenue impact of shifting the default suggested amount.
 	Refine the callout language or visual design to test whether urgency, impact framing, or reward emphasis further improves performance.
 	Test this approach on other key donation pathways (e.g., campaign pages, seasonal asks) to evaluate if the effect carries over beyond the main donation page.

experiments.title: How adding a monthly callout impacts conversion rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using a visual call out for monthly giving for potential donors will achieve a change in conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/17/2026 - 09/26/2026
With monthly giving a big focus for Save the Children, we want to test whether a graphic call-out highlighting the impact of monthly giving would increase conversion rates. We ran 3 different variants for this test, only for desktop traffic (since the buttons stacked on mobile):
Control
Variant 1- monthly call out text below the monthly/one-time array button
Variant 2- monthly call out text above the monthly/one-time array button
experiments.keyLearnings:
This test aimed to assess the impact of adding a visual monthly callout on the donation form. We hypothesized that this would strengthen the proposition for monthly giving.
Key Results:
This test showed that the visual monthly callout made the recurring option more salient and easier to process. By reducing cognitive friction and highlighting it as a “default-worthy” choice, users were nudged to see monthly as the standard, or even preferred, behavior.
Rather than deterring one-time donors, the callout acted as a commitment anchor. Donors compared themselves against the recurring option and felt reassured in choosing either. The result is a “lift all boats” effect: greater clarity increases confidence, which increases giving.
This suggests that visual emphasis on giving types does more than shift behavior between options. It can grow the whole pie by lowering decision friction and reinforcing giving as the normative action.

experiments.title: How a Dear Reader on Blog Pages Impacts Email and Donor Acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a dear reader presenting a content offer for users on the Time of Grace blog and written devotion pages will increase email and donor acquisition, as it offers Time of Grace content to users who are already interested in and enjoy Time of Grace resources.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/14/2024 - 08/26/2024
Time of Grace receives significant traffic to its blogs and written devotion pages. Our hypothesis was that this traffic left a huge opportunity to capture emails and donors by presenting traffic that we already know is interested in Time of Grace content, with an opportunity to receive a free content offer. At the end of this funnel, they would also then be presented with an ask.
experiments.keyLearnings: Key Learnings from this Experiment: The experiment showed that presenting a content offer to users on Time of Grace blog and written devotion pages resulted in a significant increase in email acquisition. This indicates that users who are already engaged with Time of Grace content are more likely to provide their email in exchange for additional resources. The experiment also demonstrated a substantial increase in transactions, showcasing the potential to convert these engaged users into donors, resulting in $802 in revenue from this additional passive stream of audience cultivation. This indicates that providing valuable content offerings can not only increase email acquisition but also boost conversion rates. Further, both the increase in email acquisition and transactions were statistically significant, underscoring the validity and reliability of the findings. Overall, the key learnings from this experiment highlight the importance of providing valuable content offerings to an already engaged audience to boost email acquisition and drive transactions. By leveraging these insights and implementing them in future experimentation, Time of Grace Ministry can further enhance its outreach and impact on its audience.

experiments.title: Can a homepage overlay outperform a full redirect for year-end fundraising?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believed that using an on-page overlay takeover with a donation form for all homepage visitors would achieve higher conversion rates than a full-page redirect because preserving user context and choice would reduce bounce and resistance associated with forced interruption.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/28/2025 - 01/01/2026
WUNC is a public media organization serving central North Carolina, relying on community support to fund independent journalism, local storytelling, and regional news coverage. As part of its 2025 growth goals, WUNC is focused on increasing digital fundraising performance during high-urgency moments while protecting audience trust and brand experience.
This experiment ran on the WUNC homepage during the final four days of the year—its highest-impact fundraising window—to test two different homepage takeover approaches. The team compared a full-page redirect to a donation form against an on-page overlay featuring a donation form or gift array with supporting year-end messaging. The test was prioritized to inform long-term homepage fundraising strategy, specifically whether urgency-driven interruption or contextual choice better aligns with WUNC’s audience expectations during major drives.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Big takeaway:

The homepage redirect significantly outperformed the overlay on primary conversion rate, winning with 99% statistical confidence. While the overlay experience generated more total downstream donations overall, it underperformed on direct donation transactions during the experiment window.
📊 Performance Metrics (Primary KPI)
Conversion rate (donations / samples):
Redirect: 78 / 1,323 = 5.9%
 	Overlay: 44 / 1,491 = 3.0%
 	Result: Redirect wins at 99% confidence (two-proportion z-test)
✂️ Donor Type Breakdowns (Directional, not statistically significant)
Sustainer conversion rate:
Redirect: 6 / 1,323 = 0.45%
 	Overlay: 4 / 1,491 = 0.27%
 	Confidence: Not statistically significant (low sample size)
One-time donor conversion rate:
Redirect: 72 / 1,323 = 5.4%
 	Overlay: 40 / 1,491 = 2.7%
 	Confidence: ~99% (driven by overall transaction volume)
First-time donor conversion rate:
Redirect: 24 / 1,323 = 1.8%
 	Overlay: 9 / 1,491 = 0.6%
 	Confidence: ~98–99% (directionally strong, but smaller n)
📎 Additional Context (Not Primary KPI)
Revenue:
 	Redirect: $25,285
 	Overlay: $10,375
 	Downstream donations from segment (not direct experiment click):
 	Overlay: 85
 	Redirect: 78
Next Steps

experiments.title: How a homepage takeover (redirect) impacts conversion rates on Giving Tuesday
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using a homepage takever for potential donors on Giving Tuesday will achieve an increase in conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/01/2025 - 12/02/2025
For Save the Children US, Giving Tuesday has historically been one of the most important fundraising days of the year. This year, we wanted to test whether using a homepage takeover–where a user visiting the main homepage would instantly be redirected to a donation page featuring Giving Tuesday language–could increase conversion rates. The control had no takeover, and the treatment was set to show once per user per day, and featured a ‘continue to savethechildren.org’ link at the top of the page.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This test aimed to assess the impact of redirecting homepage traffic to a donation form on a high-volume giving day. We hypothesized that this would increase conversion by reducing friction for those who were looking to donate already, as well as help nudge those users on the fence of donating.
Key Results:
-       +14% statistically significant increase (96%) in overall conversion rate.
-       +12% non-statistically significant increase (84%) in overall RPV.
The results confirm that during peak giving times, homepage visitors include a high share of donation-intent users. Directing them straight to the donation form reduced friction and choice overload, elevating focus on the single task of giving and better aligning with user motivation.
Specific Results:
Overall performance when looking at transaction conversion rates had a 14% increase with a 96% level of confidence. In terms of revenue per visitor, the homepage takeover saw a 12% increase with an 83% level of confidence.
Desktop vs. Mobile:
Desktop:
Control: 19% conversion rate
Treatment: 22% conversion rate, which is a 15% increase with a 95% level of confidence. Revenue per visitor also increased by 16% with an 87% level of confidence.
Mobile:
Control: 13% conversion rate
Treatment: 14.2% conversion rate, which is an 8% increase in conversion rate with a 55% level of confidence. Revenue per visitor only increased by 2.3%, with a 54% level of confidence.
Our hypothesis for this big difference between desktop and mobile is that on mobile most potential donors are presented with a donation module at the top of the page, and therefore did not see a radically different experience like desktop users did.
Since Save is focusing on increasing recurring gifts, the emphasis on both control and treatment was pre-selecting monthly gifts. Therefore, it is interesting to look at whether the takeover had an impact on those:
One-time gifts vs. recurring
Recurring:
Control had a 1.2% conversion rate
Treatment: 1.1% conversion rate, which is a 9% decrease with a 29% level of confidence. Revenue per visitor also decreased by 28% with an 82% level of confidence
One-time:
Control had a 15% conversion rate.
Treatment: 17.6% conversion rate, which is a 16% increase with a 97% level of confidence. Revenue per visitor also increased by 14% with an 86% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How a more modernized donation form design impacted conversion rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that providing a more modern design for potential donors will achieve an increase in conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/13/2025 - 11/21/2025
Save the Children US has been using the same donation form design for a number of years, so we wanted to test whether a more modern look and improved visibility of key features would increase conversion rates by making the experience more appealing and accessible to potential donors. We ran this experiment on their evergreen donation form so as to not impact any campaign-specific results.
Instead of testing every single element to get to a ‘best practices’ form, we decided to test a major redesign first, and then in subsequent tests look at how changes to each element of the form impacted conversion rates.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This test aimed to assess the impact of redesigning the main donation form. We hypothesized that a modernized design, combined with improved visibility of the form, would increase conversion by making the experience more appealing and accessible to donors.
Key Results:
-       47% statistically significant increase (99%) in overall conversion rate.
-       200% statistically significant increase (100%) in overall RPV.
The results show a directional increase in overall conversion and RPV, suggesting that the improved design and visibility of the donation form encouraged more users to complete a donation.
Overall results:
Control: 25% conversion rate
Treatment: 37% conversion rate, which is a 47% increase with a 99% level of confidence. Revenue per visitor also increased dramatically, with a 200% increase and a 99% level of confidence.
Desktop versus mobile:
Since the biggest design changes were on desktop (the mobile version of the new form stacked similarly to the control) we expected the biggest changes on desktop:
Desktop:
Control: 27% conversion rate
Treatment: 41% conversion rate, which is a 48% increase with a 98% level of confidence. Revenue per visitor also increased by 225% with a 99% level of confidence.
Mobile:
Control: 21% conversion rate
Treatment: 26% conversion rate, which was a 22% increase with only a 43% level of confidence. Revenue per visitor on mobile also increased slightly, with an 8% increase, but only a 56% level of confidence.
One-time versus recurring:
Recurring:
Control: 5.9% conversion rate
Treatment: 4.2% conversion rate, which is a 28% decrease with a 56% level of confidence. Revenue per visitor increased, however, by 91% with a 76% level of confidence (the donation sample size, however, was low, with a larger gift skewing this metric).
One-time:
Control: 19.5% conversion rate.
Treatment: 33% conversion rate, which is a 70% increase with a 99% level of confidence. Revenue per visitor also increased by 211% with a 99% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How including an image of the premium in a web asset donation ask impacts engagement
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that the addition of the premium image for Today in the Word website visitors will achieve an increase in engagement and downstream donor metrics because it visually reinforces the offer and increasing its perceived value to the user.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/03/2025 - 12/14/2025
During Today in the Word’s CYE campaign, we tested the impact of adding a premium image to the website Dear Reader.
Both the control and treatment included identical premium language; however, only the treatment featured the premium image.
The test ran for one and a half weeks, with traffic evenly split between both variants.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Key Learning
The primary takeaway from this experiment is that adding a premium image to the Today in the Word website did not increase engagement or downstream donor metrics. Instead, the treatment featuring the premium image experienced a statistically valid 47% decrease in clicks compared to the control.
This decline extended to giving behavior. The control generated six gifts through the Dear Reader, while the treatment generated none—resulting in a 100% decrease in gifts at a 98% level of confidence.
Importantly, this outcome was consistent across devices. Clicks declined on mobile by 43% (valid), on desktop by 81% (valid), and on tablet by 21% (27% LOC). While desktop users responded most negatively to the image, the pattern indicates that users on all devices were less likely to click the CTA when the image was present.
Implications
These results suggest that the premium image may have distracted from the core message or failed to reinforce the value of the offer for this audience. Rather than increasing motivation, the visual element appears to have introduced friction at a critical decision point.
Next Steps
Future experimentation should approach visual elements with caution and intentionality. Continued testing of alternative imagery—or the removal of images altogether—will help identify what best supports clarity, engagement, and conversion. Ongoing analysis of engagement and donation data will remain essential to guiding optimizations that drive meaningful donor response.

experiments.title: Redesigning the Recurring Ask Module: Can a More Distinct and Motivating Post-Gift Offer Increase Monthly Donor Revenue?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that making the recurring ask module more visually distinct, emotionally motivating, and easier to act on for recent one-time donors will increase revenue per visitor by prompting more monthly gifts or higher-value commitments, because reducing friction and reinforcing urgency and donor identity leverages post-gift momentum and encourages deeper investment.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/15/2025 - 11/12/2025
CitizenGO is a political advocacy organization that mobilizes nearly 20 million citizens around the world to take action in defense of life, family, and freedom. Their digital engagement model invites users to sign petitions aligned with their values, then make a one-time donation to support the movement. After donating, supporters land on a receipt page that includes an additional opportunity: to become a monthly donor and sustain the fight long-term.
In an effort to increase the rate of monthly donor conversion, we wanted to test into how we could acquire more monthly gifts on these receipt pages. And in our review of the current recurring gift ask on the receipt page, we identified some key areas of opportunity. The existing module blended into the page layout and offered three preset gift amounts plus a custom field, which may have created friction and choice overload. It also lacked strong motivational framing, focusing mainly on organizational independence.
Because recurring conversions on this page are typically low, the team recognized that minor copy or layout tweaks would likely be insufficient to produce a measurable change. Instead, we pursued a holistic redesign to increase both the visibility and motivational clarity of the recurring ask.
The Treatment featured a complete visual and copy refresh: a bright blue design to visually separate it from the receipt content, simplified choice architecture (one default gift amount with optional customization), and more emotionally compelling language. The new copy emphasized urgency, opposition, and donor identity—positioning monthly supporters as critical to winning ongoing battles. This design was based off of a well-performing recurring gift popup from a donation platform that CitizenGO is not using.
One important thing to note is that the Treatment did not use any higher gift amount than the Control. Instead, the default amount shown in the Treatment’s recurring ask matched the middle ask option from the Control experience. So if a user would have been asked on the Control for $7, $10, or $20 a month, the Treatment would just ask for $10/month.
This test ran in five languages—English, Spanish, French, German, and Italian—and, while expected to have low absolute conversion volume, aimed to determine whether a more distinct, motivating, and streamlined module could better convert new donors into committed monthly givers.
experiments.keyLearnings:
1. Change in Recurring Gifts
Across all languages, the Treatment increased the number of recurring gifts by 17%, but with a confidence level of 82%. While this does not meet the 95% threshold for statistical significance, it represents a strong directional lift, especially given the relatively low sample size.
No individual language segment reached statistical confidence, suggesting that more traffic is needed to validate conversion differences at the segment level.
2. Average Gift Amount
Donors who gave monthly in the Treatment variant contributed higher average gift amounts, rising from €13.93 in the Control to €16.83 in the Treatment. This lift contributed meaningfully to the overall revenue gains and suggests that the redesigned module not only encouraged giving, but also positioned the monthly commitment in a way that inspired greater generosity.
3. Revenue Per Visitor
This was the most compelling outcome of the test.
•	Among the EN (UK) audience, the Treatment drove a 67% lift in revenue per visitor (€0.78 → €1.30) with 98% statistical confidence.
•	The Italian segment saw an 83% lift in revenue per visitor (€0.73 → €1.35), with 93% confidence—just shy of statistical significance, but strongly directional.
•	Most importantly, in aggregate across all languages, the Treatment drove a 39.5% increase in revenue per visitor (from €0.66 to €0.91), with 98.3% statistical confidence.
🎉 This is a major win for CitizenGO, showing that the new module significantly increased the value of post-donation traffic. The combination of higher conversion volume (directionally) and higher gift size resulted in meaningful revenue growth.
⸻
This test shows us that a thoughtfully designed, psychologically grounded upsell experience can convert new donors into higher-value monthly supporters—especially when the ask is timely, low-friction, and tied to the donor’s identity and urgency.
For future testing, we can exlore more angles of personalization, value proposition, or increased ask amounts to drive more engagement and revenue.

experiments.title: How adding an impact callout to a pre-selected monthly tab affects recurring donations
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a callout on the monthly tab, highlighting the impact of monthly giving for donation page visitors, will increase recurring donations because the callout will help users understand the potential impact they can have on the FUS mission and encourage them to make a recurring donation.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/26/2025 - 10/08/2025
Franciscan University has content offers of their video series’ running on Facebook, which perform really well. They get a lot of donations from these offers, so we wanted to look into ways we could get more recurring donors from audiences coming to the donation pages.
For this test, we had the monthly tab pre-selected and we added a callout to hover above the tab that had language about how monthly giving was the most impactful way they could help FUS continue doing their mission.
This was tested on the donation page of the offers currently running for them in Facebook.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Key learnings from this experiment include:
1. Adding a callout on the monthly tab highlighting the impact of monthly giving led to a 1.5% increase in Recurring Donations for All Traffic at a 100.0% level of statistical confidence.
2. However, this increase in recurring donations was accompanied by a 211% decrease in Transactions with a 96% level of confidence and a 411% decrease in One-Time Donations with a 100% level of confidence.
In future experimentation, it is crucial to carefully weigh the benefits of increasing recurring donations against the significant decline observed in one-time donations and transactions. Further analysis of why one-time donations and transactions dropped could reveal ways to promote recurring giving without causing large declines in other donation types.
Test different callout messages or positions to optimize recurring donations without hurting other metrics. Segment the audience by behavior and preferences to tailor the approach and maximize total donations.
Continue experimenting with new strategies and closely monitor all donation metrics. Aim to increase recurring donations while maintaining overall donation levels.

experiments.title: How reinforcing security on a campaign donation page donor conversion and overall revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a statement of security to the year end donation page will increase donor conversion and overall revenue, as it enhances clarity regarding how a potential donor’s data is being used.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/23/2025 - 04/30/2025
America Needs Fatima ran a calendar year-end campaign during December. With increasing concerns about privacy and user data, the team wanted to test whether adding a statement about the organization’s privacy commitment on the donation page would affect transactions and overall campaign revenue. The next step is to analyze the test results to determine whether the privacy statement affected donor behavior or revenue. The test revealed that including a privacy statement had a measurable effect on donation page conversions and total year-end campaign revenue.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding a statement of security on the donation page significantly increased donor conversion and overall revenue. The treatment group, which included the statement of security, saw a 50.5% increase in revenue compared to the control group. This result was statistically significant at a 98.5% confidence level.
This suggests that potential donors highly value transparency and clarity regarding the use of their data. By addressing their concerns about privacy and security, we were able to gain their trust and increase their willingness to make a donation.
In future experiments, consider adding a statement regarding security or privacy on donation pages. Use this on any page where user data is collected. This can help alleviate concerns and increase conversion rates. Further explore other ways to enhance transparency and build trust with donors. Regularly test and iterate on these strategies to boost revenue.

experiments.title: How a popup prompting homepage visitors who abandon the donation page during a matching challenge to complete their gift impacts donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a popup reminding visitors who abandon the donation page to complete their gift to double their impact will increase donor conversion.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/26/2022 - 01/23/2023
The Christian Broadcasting Network is a Christian news and media ministry that reaches millions of people around the world and provides humanitarian aid and practical resources to those in need. During their Giving Tuesday campaign, they tested a popup that would display for visitors to their homepage who started the process of making a gift but then abandoned the donation page before it was completed.
That experiment found that it increased donor revenue. They wanted to also see the impact it had on donations and, because of how the experiment had been set up, we weren’t able to determine the impact it had on donor conversion. So, we retested this popup during the last days of their December campaign to see the impact on donor conversion of this popup.
Because this popup only fires for those who abandon the donation page without making a gift, the control audience in this experiment shows only one sample in the results. Because the control audience, those who abandon the page but don’t get a popup, aren’t recorded in these results. Rather, we are looking to see the conversion of presenting that popup to abandons, so how many donors can we draw back to the donation page.
experiments.keyLearnings:
In all, 53 people who abandoned the donation page and saw the popup clicked to return to the page. Of these, 26% completed their gift. So, the popup successfully brought back 14 donors who otherwise would have been lost and with them approximately $5K in additional revenue.

This tactic is one that we will continue to test with other value propositions. So, for example, would we reactivate more abandoned donors by presenting them with a lower appeal than what they were considering making? Or would we reactivate more by offering them an eBook or pledge that then pointed to a donation page? These are questions we will seek to answer in future testing.

experiments.title: How including a button in an email appeal affects click-through rate for donors
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a button to an email for donors will achieve an increase in click-through rate because it provides a clear, visually distinct call-to-action that reduces friction, draws the reader’s eye, and makes it easier for people to take the intended next step.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 08/26/2025
The Heritage Foundation is running its annual Leader’s Club campaign. The second email of the campaign offered a free ebook called “Flags of Our Fathers.” Within this email, they wanted to test how changing the raw URL to a button would affect the click-through rate to the landing page. This experiment ran on one email, and this segment included donors to The Heritage Foundation.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding a button to an email for donors can significantly increase the click-through rate. In this case, the treatment with the button resulted in a 59.2% increase in clicks compared to the control group with a 100.0% statistical level of confidence.

This result confirms the hypothesis that a clear, visually distinct call-to-action, such as a button, can reduce friction, draw the reader’s eye, and make it easier for people to take the intended next step.

In future experimentation, we should continue to test the effectiveness of different call-to-action elements, such as buttons, in emails targeted to donors. By consistently analyzing and optimizing our email designs, we can maximize engagement and ultimately drive more conversions for The Heritage Foundation’s campaigns.

experiments.summary:
Ended On: 08/26/2025
The Heritage Foundation is running its annual Leader’s Club campaign. The second email of the campaign offered a free ebook called “Flags of Our Fathers.” Within this email, they wanted to test how changing the raw URL to a button would affect the click-through rate to the landing page. This experiment ran on one email, and this segment included donors to The Heritage Foundation.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding a button to an email for subscribers resulted in a significant increase in the click-through rate. The treatment group, which received the email with a button linking to the ebook "Flags of Our Fathers," saw a 25.6% increase in clicks compared to the control group, which had a raw URL link.

This outcome supports the hypothesis that visually distinct call-to-action buttons reduce friction, draw the reader's eye, and make it easier for people to take the intended next step. Moving forward, this learning should be applied to future experimentation by emphasizing the use of clear and prominent buttons in emails to encourage higher engagement and click-through rates. Additionally, A/B testing different variations of buttons, such as color, text, size, and placement, can further optimize email performance and drive desired actions from subscribers.

experiments.title: How will donations be impacted if we add a step in the giving process?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that leading users to the landing page instead of the donation page for the 48 Hours of Impact hero image link will achieve less transactions because we will be adding more friction for the user to get to the donation page.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 08/01/2025
Moody Radio is running a 48 Hours of Impact high urgency campaign. Leading up to the event, we wanted to test and see how leading users from the homepage to the landing page instead of the giving page would impact overall transactions.
To test this on the homepage we updated the 48 Hours of Impact Hero Image to go to the landing page instead of the donation page. Below is what we found.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that leading users from the homepage to the landing page instead of the donation page for the 48 Hours of Impact hero image link resulted in a significant decrease in donations. The treatment group saw a 90.8% decrease in donations compared to the control group, with a high level of statistical confidence of 99.6%.

This suggests that adding an extra step or friction for users to get to the donation page can deter them from completing the donation process. In future experimentation, it is important to prioritize a seamless and direct path for users to make donations. It is also valuable to consider the user experience and minimize any unnecessary steps that could potentially hinder donation conversions.

Overall, this experiment highlights the importance of streamlining the donation process and making it as easy and straightforward as possible for users to support the cause.

experiments.title: How an adding a relevant image on a petition landing page impacts email acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: Adding the same relevant image to a petition landing page that people saw on the social media ad will increase the email acquisition rate because the image provides continuity for people.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/21/2025 - 05/29/2025
Mountain States Legal Foundation protects property rights and economic liberty, defends the right to keep and bear arms, champions free speech and association, and upholds the principle of equal protection.
We ran this test on an instant donation page from a petition. Audience: People from social media ads.
We wanted to know if more people will donate because people see an image on the ad and then the same image on the landing page. We thought this would provide continuity between the channels.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding the same relevant image to a petition landing page that people saw on the social media ad did not lead to an increase in email acquisition rate. In fact, the treatment group saw a significant decrease in transactions and recurring donations compared to the control group.

In future experimentation, it is important to carefully consider the impact of image continuity between different channels. While the hypothesis was based on the idea that continuity could enhance the user experience and engagement, the results indicate that this may not always be the case.

Moving forward, it is crucial to test hypotheses thoroughly before implementing them on a larger scale. This can help avoid potential negative impacts on donation rates or other key metrics. Additionally, it is important to analyze the results objectively and be prepared to pivot strategies based on data-driven insights, even if they contradict initial assumptions.

Overall, this experiment highlights the importance of experimentation and data analysis in optimizing donation pages and marketing strategies. It is essential to continuously learn from both successful and unsuccessful experiments to improve future campaigns and drive better outcomes for the nonprofit organization.

experiments.title: How copy and checkbox design drive GDPR consent opt-ins during a high urgency campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that simplifying the GDPR consent process by using a single checkbox with clear privacy language will reduce friction for users and increase the opt-in rate for receiving future communications.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/17/2025 - 04/24/2025
CitizenGO is a global community that uses online petitions and action alerts to defend life, family, and liberty. Their platform enables individuals to influence decision-makers worldwide by leveraging the collective support of like-minded citizens.
This experiment stems from a review of GDPR (General Data Protection Regulation) requirements, which mandate that organizations obtain explicit consent from users before collecting or processing their personal data, including email addresses. As part of this compliance, it’s important to design petition sign-up forms in a way that ensures users are properly informed and opt in to receive future communications.
For this test, we are evaluating different designs for the GDPR consent checkbox on petition landing pages. The current design includes the following two options:
I want to know if this petition wins and how I can help other citizen petitions.
I don’t want to know how this petition or other important petitions are progressing.
The proposed test will compare three variations of the GDPR consent checkbox:
Control – The current checkbox design to track opt-ins.
Treatment 1 – A simplified design with a single checkbox and long privacy copy above it, providing detailed information in accordance with GDPR requirements.
Treatment 2 – A “Christian Lawyer” style design featuring a concise statement next to the checkbox, with a link to additional privacy policy details placed below the petition submission button.
The test will be run on the German petition pages (launching inline with their Stop Digital Euro Campaign) with traffic split evenly across all three variations. This experiment will help determine which design leads to higher consent opt-in rates while maintaining GDPR compliance.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This test ran for 1 week and we observed significant differences in opt-in clicks across the different treatments. This result is parallel to what we saw when we ran this test on Hazteoir petition pages.

Results:
Control: 14,099 samples | 535 opt-in clicks | 6,707 email acquisitions
 	Treatment 1: 14,274 samples | 1,272 opt-in clicks | 6,667 email acquisitions
 	Treatment 2: 14,155 samples | 1,680 opt-in clicks | 6,808 email acquisitions
137.7%
213.7% increase
100% Level of Confidence (LoC)
Control conversion rate: ~47.6%
 	Treatment 1 conversion rate: ~46.7%
 	Treatment 2 conversion rate: ~48.1%
Recommendation:
Treatment 2

In future experimentation, we should consider the familiarity and popularity of different programs or campaigns when determining which call to action to feature prominently. By prioritizing content that is already well-known and loved by our audience, we can potentially increase engagement and drive more conversions. Additionally, we should continue to track and analyze the results of A/B tests to gather insights and optimize our strategies for maximizing donations and achieving our organizational goals.

experiments.title: How adding an image of a well known individual impacts sign-up rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding an image of Dr. Carson to the landing page for Read Across America visitors will achieve an increase in sign-up rate because it enhances the credibility of the pledge through his recognizable and trusted reputation.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 05/29/2025
In an effort to boost email acquisition on ACI’s Read Across America landing page, we opted to test adding an image to the landing page.
We typically include images of Dr. Carson on our landing pages. However, we haven’t ever tested this.
Control: No image
Treatment: Image of Dr. Ben Carson
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that adding an image of Dr. Ben Carson to the landing page for Read Across America visitors did not result in an increase in sign-up rates. In fact, the treatment group saw a significant decrease of 22.8% in Emails Acquired for All Traffic compared to the control group. This outcome was statistically significant at a confidence level of 97.8%.
Moving forward, it is important to carefully consider the impact of visual elements such as images on landing pages. While the hypothesis assumed that Dr. Carson's recognizable and trusted reputation would enhance the credibility of the pledge and increase sign-up rates, the results demonstrated otherwise. It is crucial to test assumptions and hypotheses through experimentation to truly understand what resonates with the target audience.
In future experimentation, it is recommended to continue testing different strategies and elements on landing pages to optimize email acquisition. This could involve testing different images, content layouts, calls to action, or even testing the absence of images altogether. By consistently testing and analyzing results, we can gain valuable insights into what drives engagement and conversion rates, leading to more effective marketing strategies in the future.

experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a donation module for potential donors will achieve a change in conversion rates  because  of the reduction of friction..
experiments.summary:

With the escalation of conflict in Israel in May, Save the Children US was seeing an increase in traffic to their Gaza campaign page. We wanted to test whether adding a donation module to the hero of the page–reducing the amount of clicks donors had to take to make a gift–would lead to a change in conversion rates.

-       +12% statistically significant lift (96%) in overall conversion rate.
-       +60% statistically significant lift (96%) in recurring RPV.
This test proved that the donation module helps reduce friction and improves donor engagement across main pages of the website. The module also elevates the monthly option, making it more visible and more tangible with individual equivalencies, on a page/issue where we would typically see a majority of one-time donors.
Recommendation/Implementation:
We will be implementing the donation module on the Gaza page. We are planning to launch another test on other landing pages which evaluates the placement of the module on the page.

experiments.title: How copy and checkbox design drive GDPR consent opt-ins
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that simplifying the GDPR consent process by using a single checkbox with clear privacy language will reduce friction for users and increase the opt-in rate for receiving future communications.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/05/2025 - 03/12/2025
This experiment stems from a review of GDPR (General Data Protection Regulation) requirements, which mandate that organizations obtain explicit consent from users before collecting or processing their personal data, including email addresses. As part of this compliance, it’s important to design petition sign-up forms in a way that ensures users are properly informed and opt in to receive future communications.
For this test, we are evaluating different designs for the GDPR consent checkbox on petition landing pages. The current design includes the following two options:
I want to know if this petition wins and how I can help other citizen petitions.
I don’t want to know how this petition or other important petitions are progressing.
The proposed test will compare three variations of the GDPR consent checkbox:
Control – The current checkbox design to track opt-ins.
Treatment 1 – A simplified design with a single checkbox and long privacy copy above it, providing detailed information in accordance with GDPR requirements.
Treatment 2 – A design featuring a concise statement next to the checkbox, with a link to additional privacy policy details placed below the petition submission button.
The test will be run with traffic split evenly across all three variations. This experiment will help determine which design leads to higher consent opt-in rates while maintaining GDPR compliance.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This test ran for 1 week, and we observed significant differences in opt-in clicks across the different treatments.

 	Control: 10,980 samples, 481 opt-in clicks, 5,599 email acquisitions
 	Treatment 1 (Long): 11,262 samples, 1,094 opt-in clicks, 5,718 email acquisitions
 	Treatment 2 (Christian): 11,195 samples, 1,387 opt-in clicks, 5,762 email acquisitions

The Treatment 1 (Long) increased opt-in clicks by 121.7% at a 100% Level of Confidence (LoC), while Treatment 2 (Christian) showed an even stronger increase of 182.8% at a 100% LoC. Both treatments outperformed the control, where the opt-in clicks were much lower.

Despite these significant increases in opt-in clicks, there was no impact on email acquisitions. This is fine, as the goal of the test was to increase consent to the terms (via checking the box) rather than directly driving email sign-ups. The conversion rates for email acquisitions were as follows:

 	Control: ~4.38%
 	Treatment 1 (Long): ~4.13%
 	Treatment 2 (Christian): ~4.17%

This indicates that while the treatments successfully increased opt-in clicks, they did not lead to a notable change in email sign-ups. Given the strong results from Treatment 2 (Christian), we recommend implementing this treatment across all petition pages. This approach had the highest increase in opt-in clicks and aligns with the goal of improving user engagement with the petition.

experiments.title: How does adding an exclusive offers slide-up to a landing page impact email acquisition rates?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by presenting users with additional video series options in a slide-up, they will be more likely to find content that resonates with them, which will lead to a higher email acquisition rate. By offering more choices, we expect to reduce friction in the decision-making process and increase the likelihood that users will opt in with their email addresses.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/06/2025 - 03/24/2025
We hypothesized by providing visitors with alternative video series options, visitors would more likely to find content that resonates with them, thereby increasing the likelihood of signing up. The test was not about determining which series resonated more with people but rather whether offering additional options would lead to more email sign-ups.
To test this, we implemented an A/B test in VWO, splitting traffic evenly (50/50) between the Control and Treatment. The Control version of the landing page remained unchanged, while the Treatment featured a slide-up at the bottom of the page displaying three alternative video series options. We tested this on “Proof You Need,” their most successful video series we are running on Facebook.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The experiment ran for 18 days, during which the control acquired 1,249 emails and the treatment acquired 1,155 emails. This resulted in a 6.8% decrease in email acquisition for the Treatment at a 95.6% level of confidence. While we initially hypothesized that offering more options would increase conversions, the results suggest that the slide-up may have unintentionally introduced decision friction. Users who arrived on the landing page were likely already primed to sign up based on the ad they clicked, but the presence of additional choices may have disrupted that intent, leading to hesitation rather than action. Instead of reinforcing their decision, the added options could have created uncertainty, ultimately reducing the overall conversion rate.

Based on these results, we recommend maintaining the current landing page design without the slide-up to avoid introducing additional friction in the sign-up process.

experiments.title: How should we layout the episode pages?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that moving the featured resource CTA higher on the page for the broadcast episode pages will achieve more traffic getting to the donation page because the CTA is more visible.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/01/2025 - 05/02/2025
Each day tons of people go to their broadcast pages to pick through the episodes that they would like to listen to. On the episode pages you can watch the show, read more about the guests, and then towards the bottom of the page you have the opportunity to get the resources that they talked about on the show.
The resources offer leds the user to a donation page where they can get the resource with a gift. We thought, if we rearrange the page to make the resource CTA more at the top we could drive more traffic to the donation page and get more transactions.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that moving the featured resource CTA higher on the broadcast episode pages significantly increased the traffic to the donation page. The treatment group, which had the CTA placed higher on the page, saw an impressive 18.9% increase in CTA button clicks for all traffic compared to the control group. This difference was statistically significant at a confidence level of 100.0%, indicating that the results are very reliable.

In future experimentation, it is important to continue testing the placement of CTAs on the page to maximize engagement and conversion rates. Additionally, it would be beneficial to further analyze user behavior to understand why the higher placement of the CTA led to more clicks. This insight can help in optimizing the design and placement of CTAs across other pages on the website as well.

Overall, the success of this experiment highlights the importance of CTA placement in driving user action. By utilizing this learning and continuing to test and optimize CTAs, Focus on the Family can further improve user engagement and increase donations on their broadcast episode pages.

experiments.title: Will Adding 3rd Party Validation Images Strengthen Trust on the Donation Page?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding 3rd party validation images on the episodic donation page for users to see when they are about to click “donate” will achieve more transactions because the images will reinforce trust and credibility.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/01/2025 - 04/21/2025
For this test, we wanted to see if we could increase transactions on the main episodic donation page by adding 3rd party validator images. These included organizations that partners with the organization.
We added these partnership icons to the bottom of the page right under the donate button where they could reinforce trust right when the user is about to click submit.
Below is what we found.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding 3rd party validation images on the episodic donation page did not increase transactions as hypothesized. In fact, it resulted in a 15.0% decrease in transactions for all traffic with a statistical level of confidence at 95.1%.

In future experimentation, it is important to carefully consider the potential impact of changes to the donation page before implementing them. It is possible that the placement of the images may not have been optimal, as the user as to scroll past the donate button to even see the images, especially on mobile.

We also looked at these results from a new vs. returning user and found that the majority of traffic to this page was from new visitors. Our hypothesis would be that new visitors would be more moved by adding the 3rd party images that those who are returning. However, the data says new visitors are 19% less likely to give at a 98% LoC when the images are added. The majority of new visitors are coming in from a mobile or tablet device where the user would have to scroll past the point of decision, the donate button, to see the images.

experiments.title: TW – Will changing the donation page to be focused on the donor impact affect conversion?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that emphasizing the reader’s personal connection to the devotional and the fact that it is entirely donor-funded for individuals landing on the donation page will achieve more donations because it makes the impact of their gift feel immediate, essential, and personally meaningful—motivating them to take action.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 04/25/2025
Today in the Word is a ministry that offers daily devotionals. We have an acquisition funnel running all the time to get names into their daily email devotional.
For this test, we wanted to see if changing the copy on the donation page for this acquisition funnel would increase the number of gifts.
The control version focuses on the organizational legacy and mission of Moody Bible Institute, emphasizing its long history and continued commitment to spreading the Gospel. The tone is formal, mission-driven, and emphasizes how the gift will be used for broad gospel impact.
The treatment version focuses more directly on the devotional itself and the personal impact it has on readers. It highlights that the devotional is donor-supported, the number of people being reached daily, and positions the donor as a direct enabler of that spiritual impact. The tone is more personal, emotionally resonant, and focused on the reader’s role in expanding access to this devotional.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that emphasizing the reader's personal connection to the devotional and the fact that it is entirely donor-funded significantly increased donations and engagement. By highlighting the direct impact of their gift on expanding access to the devotional and making it feel immediate, essential, and personally meaningful, individuals were motivated to take action and donate.
In future experimentation, we should continue to prioritize personalizing the donor experience and focusing on the individual's role in supporting the mission. By highlighting the immediate and tangible outcomes of their donation, we can continue to drive higher levels of engagement and donations. Additionally, we should consider testing different elements of personalization and emotional resonance to further optimize our acquisition funnels and maximize impact.

experiments.title: Are we presenting the landing page the best way for the Loneliness offer?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that applying a radical redesign to the landing page for ebook offers will achieve more email conversations because the radical redesign will remove friction and increase clarity of the landing page. because  the radical redesign will remove friction and increase clarity of the landing page.

experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that the radical redesign applied to the landing page for ebook offers led to a significant decrease in email acquisition for all traffic. The treatment group experienced an 86.2% decrease in email acquisition compared to the control group, and this result was statistically significant at a level of 100.0%.
These results are specific to desktop only, when we looked at mobile we saw a similar situation with a 14% decrease in emails acquired at a 90% LoC
Based on these findings, it is clear that the radical redesign did not achieve the intended outcome of increasing email conversations. In future experimentation, it is important to carefully consider the potential impact of significant design changes on user behavior and conversion rates. It may be beneficial to conduct user testing or smaller-scale experiments before implementing radical redesigns across all offers.

experiments.title: TW – What happens when we present an email sign up opportunity higher on the page?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that making the email CTA bigger and easier to fill out for the homepage will achieve more emails acquired because it’s more visible .
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 04/25/2025
Today in the Word is a ministry that offers daily devotionals delivered right to your email.
When looking at the Today in the Word site, we noticed that the only sign up for receive emails was in the footer. There is a CTA right below the hero image, but it was very small and easily to miss. With the main goal of this site being to get email address, we wondered what would happen if we made that CTA bigger and easier to fill out right on the page.
This test only showed on the homepage for new visitors
experiments.keyLearnings:
Key learnings from this experiment include:
1. **Visibility is crucial**: By making the email CTA bigger and easier to fill out on the homepage, there was a significant increase in email acquisitions. This indicates that visibility plays a key role in encouraging visitors to take action.
2. **Simplify the process**: Making it easier for users to sign up for emails directly on the homepage likely reduced friction and made it more convenient for them to engage with the content.
3. **Data-driven decisions**: The significant increase in email acquisitions and the high level of statistical confidence in the results emphasize the importance of data-driven decision-making in optimizing website performance.
Moving forward, these learnings can be applied in future experimentation by:
1. **Prioritizing visibility**: Ensuring that important CTAs or conversion points are prominently displayed on the website to attract the attention of visitors and encourage action.
2. **Continuing to simplify**: Streamlining the user experience by simplifying processes such as email sign-ups can lead to higher engagement and conversion rates.
3. **Testing and iterating**: Building on the success of this experiment, it's important to continue testing variations to further optimize the website for conversions and user engagement. Regular experiments and A/B testing can help in refining strategies based on user behavior and preferences.

experiments.title: How an “Undecided” default answer for survey questions affects email conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that defaulting to “Undecided” in dropdown lists for survey answers will achieve an increase in email conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/25/2025 - 03/12/2025
The Heritage Foundation is running a survey in paid Facebook ads and through email prospecting. In a previous experiment, they wanted to know if using a dropdown for the answers instead of radio buttons would impact or increase email conversion rate, resulting in a decrease in email acquisitions.
In the original experiment, the default answer in the dropdown lists was set to a blank value. To further test the dropdown list idea, they wanted to know if setting the default dropdown list answer to “Undecided” would encourage the survey taker to take action and increase email acquisitions.
The control experience showed the original radio button answers and the treatment experience showed the dropdown list answers with “Undecided” or “Not Sure/No Opinion” as the default.
The experiment ran for two weeks and each variant saw 50% of traffic.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that setting the default dropdown list answer to "Undecided" did not have the desired effect of increasing email acquisitions. In fact, the treatment group experienced a 32.9% decrease in email acquisitions compared to the control group, with a 99.9% statistical level of confidence, meaning that the results are very reliable.
In future experimentation, we may consider changing the default answer to "Disagree" or "Agree" to test whether either impacts survey completion rate.

experiments.title: How including how many people downloaded impacted email acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a subheadline of how many have already downloaded the offer for Avila’s landing page visitors will achieve an increase in email acquisitions because people are influenced to download because they see others have done it too.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/17/2025 - 02/05/2025
Avila is an organization that aims to draw Christians worldwide into deeper union with Christ through mystagogically oriented spiritual education and formation.
For this specific test, we tested whether adding the number of people who have downloaded this offer to the landing page headline would impact email acquisition and donations.
We wanted to see if including “Downloaded by 18,377 others!” in the subheadline would engage others. This is known as social proof marketing or the bandwagon effect, where people are influenced to take action because they see others doing it.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding the number of people who have downloaded the offer to the landing page headline had a negative impact on email acquisition for Avila Foundation. The treatment, which included the subheadline "Downloaded by 18,377 others!" resulted in a 24.5% decrease in email acquisition compared to the control group.
In future experimentation, it is important to carefully consider the effects of social proof marketing strategies such as the bandwagon effect. While showing the number of people who have engaged with a particular offer can sometimes increase engagement, in this case, it had the opposite effect. It is crucial to test different variations of social proof messaging and other marketing strategies to understand their impact fully.
People may not have signed up for the offer if the "Downloaded by 18,377 others" didn’t feel relevant to them or it wasn’t clear why they should care. Additionally, if something appears too common or widely available, it can sometimes lose its appeal.
It's important to note that the average gift size in the treatment was $52.50" whereas the control was $13.75. It would be interesting to dig in to see if this was based upon one large gift or if it was a trend.
It is essential to continue using A/B testing and data analysis to make informed decisions about website optimizations and marketing strategies. This experiment demonstrates the importance of testing hypotheses and not assuming that a particular approach will always yield positive results.

This experiment was conducted for the SETI Institute, a nonprofit organization dedicated to scientific research and education in the search for life beyond Earth. experiments.keyLearnings:
Main Takeaway
The test version of the landing page, featuring the updated ebook cover, acquired 19% more emails than the control, with a confidence level of 99.2%. This result demonstrates that the more visually appealing cover design positively impacted user engagement and lead generation.
Observations:

 	The redesigned cover likely improved the user’s first impression of the landing page, making the offer more compelling and increasing trust in the content.
 	The consistent visual alignment between the ad and the landing page may have reinforced the user’s decision to engage with the form.

Next Steps

 	Adopt the New Cover Design: Implement the updated cover design on all landing pages to improve email acquisition rates across campaigns.
 	A/B Test Additional Landing Page Elements: Evaluate other design and content changes, such as CTA language, form placement, or page layout, to identify further optimization opportunities.
 	Monitor Long-Term Impact: Assess the quality of leads acquired through the updated landing page to ensure sustained performance improvements downstream.

experiments.title: How Adding A Progress Bar to the Moody Bible Donation Page Impacted Donor Conversion at Calendar Year End
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a progress bar to the campaign donation page for the cye campaign will achieve a higher rate of donor conversion because  the thermometer would increase urgency during these final days of the year to reach our campaign goal with donors that would have a high motivation to give. because   the thermometer would increase urgency during these final days of the year to reach our campaign goal with donors that would have a high motivation to give.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/28/2024 - 12/31/2024
In this experiment, we aimed to test whether adding a progress bar to the donation page could communicate a sense of urgency more effectively than text alone. To do this, we created a countdown thermometer that tracked the days remaining in the campaign. As time passed, the thermometer gradually filled up, visually showing how close we were to the deadline. Our goal was to see if this dynamic progress bar could boost donor conversion during the critical final days of the year.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding the progress bar to the donation page significantly increased donor conversion rates by creating a sense of urgency. The treatment group, which had the thermometer feature, saw a 23.6% increase in completed transactions compared to the control group with this element present.
In future experimentation, it is vital to continue testing the impact of visual elements on donation pages to drive higher conversion rates during high-urgency campaigns. Experimenting with different visuals, such as countdown timers or progress bars, could further enhance the sense of urgency and motivate more donors to contribute during key campaign days. Additionally, it is crucial to analyze the impact of these elements on different audience segments to tailor the approach for maximum effectiveness.
Overall, the success of this experiment underscores the importance of incorporating visual cues to elicit a desired response from donors and encourages continued innovation and testing to optimize donation strategies.

experiments.title: How adding a countdown timer to an “update your membership information” landing page affects email acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a 10-minute countdown timer for membership update page viewers will achieve an increase in email acquisitions because there is an increased sense of urgency to complete the form when given a set amount of time.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/16/2024 - 11/12/2024
The Heritage Foundation was running a campaign targeting donors who had not yet given a gift in 2024 to update their membership information, including name, email address, phone number, and address on a landing page. They were then redirected to a donation page where they could give a gift in order to renew their Heritage membership.
In an effort to increase email acquisition rate on the landing page, Heritage wanted to test whether adding a 10-minute countdown timer would increase the viewer’s sense of urgency and encourage them to fill out the form more often.
The control page did not have a countdown timer, and the treatment page had a 10-minute countdown timer that started when the viewer started the page session and ended when it reached 0.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding a 10-minute countdown timer to the membership update page did not achieve the desired increase in email acquisitions. In fact, the treatment page saw a significant and valid 11.5% decrease in email acquisitions compared to the control page. This indicates that the sense of urgency created by the countdown timer may have had a negative impact on the viewers, leading to a lower conversion rate. Viewers may have felt put off or pressured by the timer, or there may have been a disconnect in understanding why there was a limited timeframe to update their membership information.

In future experimentation, it is important to carefully consider the potential impact of elements like countdown timers on user behavior. While urgency can sometimes be effective in driving action, it is crucial to test such elements to ensure they are positively influencing the desired outcome. Additionally, it is essential to analyze the results of experiments thoroughly before implementing changes across all marketing efforts.

experiments.title: How a sliding bar on a campaign donation page allowing donors to adjust their impact influences conversion rate and revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that allowing donors to select the level of their impact dynamically will increase revenue during a high-urgency campaign by increasing the perceived clarity and appeal of the donation page.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 12/05/2024
During ACI’s Giving Tuesday campaign, we wanted to increase the tangibility and clarity of the donor’s impact on the campaign’s donation page. The page already highlighted the number of people that would be reached with a donor’s gift. However, we hypothesized that allowing the donors to drag a slider that dynamically changes the number of people reached would increase the clarity of the offer when compared with the current  experience.
We used VWO to create a graphical, interactive slider on the page that dynamically changed the gift amount and the corresponding number of people the gift would reach. We split traffic 50/50 and all campaign sources were directed to the test.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that introducing a graphical, interactive slider on the donation page, allowing donors to dynamically select the level of their impact, can significantly increase revenue during a high-urgency campaign. The treatment group saw a 28.9% increase in revenue for all traffic with a 95.7% level of confidence.
Although we saw a valid increase in revenue, we actually saw a 19.5% decrease in transactions with an 86.6% level of confidence.
In future experimentation, we should continue to explore interactive elements that provide donors with a clear and tangible understanding of the impact of their donations. This includes incorporating dynamic features that allow donors to see in real-time how their contributions will make a difference. Additionally, we should aim to reach the minimum sample size required for statistical significance to ensure the reliability of the results.
Overall, it is crucial to prioritize clarity, personalization, and interactivity in donation pages to engage donors effectively and drive increased contributions, especially during high-urgency campaigns like Giving Tuesday.

experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that allowing donors to select the level of their impact dynamically increased donor conversion significantly during a high-urgency campaign. The treatment resulted in a 37.4% increase in donations for all traffic with a high level of statistical confidence at 97.6%.

Additionally, we increased recurring gifts on the page by 33% at a 90% level of confidence.

We are next going to test this approach on their acquisition offer pages to see what impact the urgency of the campaign had on the results of this experiment.

experiments.title: How removing imagery on a landing page impacted conversion rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that removing the images for Avila’s landing page viewer will achieve an increase in email acquisition because less images will reduce friction and draw attention to the sign-up.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/27/2024 - 10/25/2024
Avila is an organization that aims to draw Christians worldwide into deeper union with Christ through mystagogically oriented spiritual education and formation.
They are currently running an offer, Spiritual Direction and the Discernment of Spirits. We decided to test removing all the images from their landing page to see if this increases conversions.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that removing images from Avila's landing page led to a 20% decrease in email acquisition for all traffic, with a statistical confidence level of 100.0%. This means the control group, which had images on the landing page, performed better in terms of email sign-ups. This led to the treatment showing a 20% decrease in overall revenue. There was also a decrease in the number of transactions from 30 in the control group to 28 in the treatment group. However, this was not statistically valid, so this cannot be a learning but something to test further.

In future experimentation, it is essential to consider the impact of visual elements such as images on user engagement and conversion rates. While the hypothesis that removing images would reduce friction and draw attention to the sign-up form seemed plausible, the results indicate the opposite effect.

Moving forward, it may be beneficial to test different types of images, their placement, or the overall design of the landing page to optimize for conversions. Additionally, conducting user surveys or gathering qualitative feedback can provide insights into the preferences and behaviors of the target audience when interacting with the website.

Overall, this experiment highlights the importance of testing assumptions and being open to unexpected results in order to make informed decisions for optimizing conversion rates in the future.

experiments.title: How a slide-up with gift array vs sticky bar impacts clicks to the donation page during a high-urgency direct appeal
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using a slide-up with a gift array for users on Luther’s Working Preacher site will achieve an increase in clicks to the donation page because it allows the users to already begin the process of giving by selecting a gift amount before they even arrive on the donation page. because  it allows the users to already begin the process of giving by selecting a gift amount before they even arrive on the donation page. because  it allows the users to already begin the process of giving by selecting a gift amount before they even arrive on the donation page. because  it allows the users to already begin the process of giving by selecting a gift amount before they even arrive on the donation page.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/15/2024 - 10/17/2024
Luther Seminary was running a high-urgency campaign to support their Working Preacher site, and one of the web elements on the site was a standard direct appeal sticky bar. We wanted to test whether using a slide-up element instead of a sticky bar would drive more user engagement through clicks to the donation page.
The sticky bar and slide-up both used the same language, but the slide-up presented users with 3 gift options. Clicking a gift option would direct users to the donation page and the form would be pre-filled with their selected gift amount.
Our hypothesis was that the slide-up would drive an increase in clicks to the donation page because it lets users begin the process of giving before they even land on the donation page. We also hoped that this would result in a downstream lift in donations from the slide-up.
We split the website traffic 50/50 between the two variants, and the test ran for ~3 days before we ran out of testing credits and had to end it.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that using a slide-up with a gift array on Luther Seminary's Working Preacher site led to a significant increase in clicks to the donation page compared to a standard sticky bar. The treatment resulted in a 43.6% increase in Clicks for All Traffic with a high statistical level of confidence (99.5%)!
We also found that the slide-up led to a 33.4% increase in donor conversion, but this was only with a 68.3% level of confidence.
From this test we learned that it is important to continue exploring different ways to engage Working Preacher users and streamline the donation process for them. It is clear that providing users with the option to select a gift amount before landing on the donation page can be effective in driving more clicks and potentially increasing donations. For future testing, we want to experiment with the language in the slide-up, as well as the psychology of price anchoring with our gift array amounts.
