experiments.title: How a Badge Icon paired with Tribute Widget Treatments Impacts Donor Conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that social proof and education copy on the Tribute Widget for users who are in the Tribute tab will achieve an increase in donor conversions and revenue because it will address the potential friction of not knowing what this widget is, who the gift is to, or why it has value to the profile.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/14/2024 - 10/17/2024
On CaringBridge profiles, the Tribute widget is a significant source of donations and revenue. A tribute donation is a gift to CaringBridge that a user makes in tribute to the profile they are visiting. This widget appears in a few different locations on the site, but historically one of the highest-performing locations has been on the Tribute page itself. This tab shows a list of tributes that have been made on the user’s profile.
In a previous iteration, we are able to increase donations slightly by simply driving more traffic to this tab using an animated Badge Icon that showed the number of tribute widgets on the page. However, a large majority of what we saw was an increase in clicks that didn’t necessarily translate to more donations.
We hypothesized that by leveraging the increase in traffic brought on by the animated Badge while pairing it with a Social Proof and Education treatment copy on the widget itself would increase both clicks and donor conversion rate.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are:
Incorporating social proof and education copy on the Tribute Widget can significantly increase donor conversions and revenue**: The treatment that included social proof and education copy on the Tribute Widget led to a substantial 204.4% increase in revenue from the widget. This indicates that providing users with more information about the widget and its value can positively impact their decision to donate.
Furthermore, the increase in revenue was statistically significant, with confidence levels of 98.0%. This reinforces the reliability of the results.
In future experimentation, we should continue to leverage the use of social proof and education copy on widgets to increase donor conversions and revenue. Additionally, we could explore other ways to drive traffic to key pages, such as using animated Badge Icons, to further enhance the effectiveness of our messaging. Conducting similar experiments on other high-traffic pages or widgets within the platform could also yield valuable insights and potentially increase overall donations. By continuously testing and optimizing our strategies based on learnings from experiments, we can continue to improve user engagement and drive more donations for CaringBridge.

experiments.title: How adding a video on a landing page impacts email acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a video to the landing page for Hoover’s audience will achieve an increase in email acquisitions because viewers will be more likely to engage and feel connected to the offer they are signing up for.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/12/2024 - 08/19/2024
The Hoover Institution is a public policy research center and think tank located at Stanford University in California, United States. For one of their long-term offers, Dueling Populisms, we wanted to test whether adding a video that we were already running as a YouTube ad to the landing page would make an impact on email acquisition. We hypothesized that adding the video to the page would increase engagement and the viewer would be more likely to sign up for the offer and increase the conversion rate.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding a video to the landing page for Hoover's audience actually resulted in a decrease in email acquisitions by 15.9% compared to the control group. This outcome was statistically significant with a 99.5% confidence level, indicating that the difference in email acquisitions between the two groups was unlikely due to random chance.
In future experimentation, it is important to consider that adding a video to the landing page may not always lead to the desired increase in engagement or conversions. It is essential to test different strategies and elements to see what resonates best with the target audience. Additionally, analyzing the impact of each change on user behavior is crucial before implementing it across the board. By continuing to test and iterate based on the results obtained, the Hoover Institution can optimize their campaigns and strategies for better performance in the future.

experiments.title: How a sticky bar on instant donation pages impacted gift conversions
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by adding a sticky bar for donation pages following acquisition offers will achieve a higher conversion rate because there is added need in that such a small amount of people that receive free resources donate and that there is appeal because of the match.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/29/2023 - 12/20/2023
Pathway to Victory is the broadcast ministry of First Baptist Dallas.
For calendar year end, we decided to test adding a sticky bar to donation pages for acquisition offers to see if it would increase conversions and revenue. The copy for the sticky bar mentioned that only 1% of people who request resources donate to PTV. We also mentioned the CYE match that is in place.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding a sticky bar to donation pages following acquisition offers significantly increased the conversion rate and revenue. The treatment group saw a 50.0% increase in Transactions for All Traffic, indicating that the sticky bar was successful in encouraging more visitors to donate.

Furthermore, the statistical levels of confidence were high, with a 98.0% confidence level for the increase in Transactions for All Traffic, a 97% confidence level for the increase in Revenue, a 97% confidence level for the increase in Transactions, and a 100% confidence level for the increase in Revenue for All Traffic.

Based on these results, it is evident that the sticky bar effectively conveyed the message that only 1% of people who request resources donate to PTV, creating a sense of urgency and the desire to contribute. Additionally, the mention of the CYE match likely provided an added incentive for visitors to donate.

In future experimentation, it is recommended to continue using sticky bars on donation pages following acquisition offers, as it has proven to be a successful strategy in increasing conversions and revenue. Additionally, it may be beneficial to explore other ways to communicate the low donation rate and the impact of the CYE match to further motivate visitors to take action.

Overall, this experiment highlights the importance of targeted messaging and the potential impact of utilizing persuasive elements in fundraising campaigns.

experiments.title: How adjusting a landing page form and image impacted the email acquisition rate

rows.name: Form Adjustments + New Layout + Image Left

experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that the form relocation and aligning the image to the left side for Prayer Calendar landing page visitors will achieve an increase in emails acquired because there will be less friction on the page and the visitor will perceive the form as less work since all content will be above the fold..
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/09/2024 - 08/20/2024
In an effort to boost our email acquisition rate on IMB’s Prayer Calendar funnel, we decided to run an A/B/C split test with traffic equally split between each version.
Treatment 1 – relocated the landing page form the right hand side making the page look like a two column layout
Treatment 2 – relocated the landing page form the right hand side making the page look like a two column layout + making the image left aligned
Our hypothesis that the form relocation would make the landing page appear like a short experience for the user and the image adjustment seems small but the image is angled to face outward which we thought may have been adding unnecessary friction to the page.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that the form relocation and aligning the image to the left side of the Prayer Calendar landing page resulted in a significant increase in email acquisition rate. The treatment led to a 15.4% increase in email acquisition for all traffic with a statistical confidence level of 95.2% meaning that these results are valid and should be rolled out to all traffic.
This result indicates that the hypothesis was correct in assuming that these adjustments would reduce friction on the page and make it appear as a shorter and easier experience for the user. Moving forward, it is important to consider the impact of design elements on user experience and to continue testing and iterating on these factors to optimize conversion rates.
It is important to note that this was technically a multivariate test as we changed both the form location and used a condensed version between the control and treatment 1. This was unintentional, but it is clear that the combination of the form location, condensed fields, and left alignment of the image increased the number of emails acquired which was the final goal.
Future experimentation should focus on further refining the layout and design of the landing page to continue improving the user experience and email acquisition rates. It is also beneficial to involve user feedback and conduct usability testing to gain insights into how visitors interact with the page. By continuously testing and learning from these experiments, we can optimize our conversion funnels and improve overall performance.

experiments.title: How a landing page redesign impacted email acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a landing page redesign for content offer viewers from acquisition will achieve the same email conversion rate because the landing page messaging and experience will mostly stay the same.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/22/2024 - 08/05/2024
With the launch of a new website, we considered moving landing page platforms but wanted to get the data before deciding to transition in case it impacted results. We built a treatment in the new platform and compared how visitors responded in comparison to the old platform. The treatment kept all of the same copy from the control, but had some differences in formatting which may have impacted results. We ran the two versions side by side and split traffic for two weeks.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that the redesign of the landing page for content offer viewers from acquisition led to a significant decrease in email acquisition. The treatment performed 32.3% worse than the control, with a 100.0% statistical level of confidence. This means that the original landing page outperformed the redesigned version. We believe this is due to a few distinct differences in the user experience on the page, including checkboxes, width of form fields, presentation of headline, formatting of page copy, and color and copy on the CTA button. While on the surface the pages were essentially the same, these slight changes in design made a significant impact on results and reduced email conversion.

In future experimentation, it is important to carefully consider the impact of any changes made to landing pages, even if the messaging and content remain the same. Small differences in formatting and design can have significant effects on user behavior and conversion rates. It is also essential to thoroughly test any new designs before fully implementing them, to avoid negative impacts on conversion rates. Additionally, considering running qualitative research such as user testing and surveys in addition to A/B testing to gather more insights into how users are interacting with the landing page and to understand their preferences and behavior better.

experiments.title: How using a ‘reverse funnel’ design that places the form above the fold affects email acquisition rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that moving an email acquisition form above the fold will increase sign-up rates by capitalizing on the momentum created by earlier steps in the funnel and giving the user the option to read more if they choose.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 07/25/2024
If we’re being honest, we don’t think a prospect should have to read several paragraphs of copy to see the value in exchanging their email address for a content offer. It’s generally accepted that 8 out of 10 people will read your headline/subheadline and nothing else, which means your “above-the-fold” copy is, without a doubt, far and away, the most important copy on the page.
Knowing this and working forward from the assumption that someone had to land on our landing page from somewhere — presumably some form of marketing communication that has already, at least, teased the offer …
We wanted to test what would happen if we seized the existing funnel momentum to drive the conversion by placing the form above the fold (directly beneath the headline and subheadline) and moving the ‘explainer’ copy down the page where a user could scroll to read if they so desired.
The treatment kept the headline and subhead copy unchanged and simply moved the body copy ‘below the fold’ where people could continue reading if they wanted to know more about the offer.
**Important note: although they do not show up in the renders below, both the control and the treatment featured 3D renders of the asset being promoted in the upper righthand space of the page
experiments.keyLearnings:
Moving the form above the fold resulted in a statistically valid 56.3% lift in email acquisition rates.
By giving people the option to 'fast-track' the download process, we were able to see more conversions. Importantly, this test is in no way meant to suggest that 'less copy is better.' We did not remove the copy; we simply moved it down the page so that people could forego additional reading if they were already 'sold.'
What this test does show is that your headline and subhead should be strong enough to carry the conversion on their own and that placing the form above the fold may create a more frictionless experience for enough users to make a significant difference in conversion rates.
It also shows the importance of analyzing each asset on its own and as part of the broader funnel. Avoid repeating the same message in your ad, email, landing page, donation, page, etc. Consider the momentum your other awesome assets have already created and capitalize on that to drive the conversion!
In future experimentation, we will focus on testing the headline, subhead, button copy, and any other above-the-fold elements, as this test shows that 'above-the-fold optimization' likely has an outsized impact on conversion rates

experiments.title: How additional body copy and a brighter button color affects email conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that additional copy and a brighter button color for eBook downloaders will achieve an increase in email conversion rate because it will decrease friction in the form submission process.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/12/2024 - 06/25/2024
In an effort to increase the email conversion rate on a landing page for a border crisis eBook offer, The Heritage Foundation wanted to test whether adding body copy explaining what the eBook will help the reader learn, as well as changing the form’s button color, would lead to an increase in email conversion rate.
The control page had three paragraphs of copy and a blue form submit button, and the treatment page had five paragraphs of copy including a more in-depth explanation of the value the reader will receive and an orange button.
This split URL test ran for 13 days.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that the treatment with shorter copy and a brighter button color for the eBook offer did not lead to an increase in email conversion rate. In fact, the treatment resulted in a 6.5% decrease in Email Acquired for All Traffic compared to the control. With a statistical confidence level of 96.0%, it can be concluded that the control page performed better in terms of email acquisition.

The

In future experimentation, it is important to carefully evaluate the changes being made to the landing page. While the hypothesis suggested that shorter copy and a brighter button color would decrease friction in the form submission process and increase conversions, the results showed otherwise. It is essential to test hypotheses thoroughly and be open to unexpected outcomes.

Moving forward, it may be beneficial to further investigate the factors that influenced the lower email conversion rate in the treatment. This could involve conducting surveys or collecting feedback from users to understand their preferences and behaviors. Additionally, conducting more A/B tests with different variations can help in identifying the most effective elements for improving email acquisition on the landing page.

Overall, this experiment highlights the importance of data-driven decision-making and the need to carefully analyze results to inform future experimentation strategies.

experiments.title: How using a ‘pattern interrupt’ in a paid ad affects email acquisition and CPA
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that presenting everyday elements in uncommon contexts will produce higher acquisition rates and lower CPA by using incongruence to “jar the prospect to attention” and entice them to engage with the offer.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 11/03/2023
For our content offers aimed at name acquisition, we’ve traditionally had a difficult time finding a treatment that outperformed a simple 3D render of the resource, especially in terms of CPA—despite the widely parroted best practice of putting value prop copy on the image.
Our hypothesis has always been that the simple presentation of a 3D render on a plain white background worked for 3 primary reasons:
The simple design was more authentic than a heavily-designed ad
The simple design, in its simplicity, stood out from the crowd of busier, more colorful ads and posts
The simple design did not distract from the value of the offer itself
But still, to be convinced of the value of the offer, the prospect had either to read the ad’s “primary text” or click through to the landing page.
And we continued to suspect that if there was a way to prominently feature the value of an offer on the ad image itself, where a user’s attention is naturally drawn, without sacrificing the authentic, human appeal of the 3D render, it may be able to snatch the prospect’s attention, in a unique way, and more effectively.
For this test we pitted our usual control —3D render of the offer as the ad image with value proposition copy in the “primary text” field—against a simple screen grab of a note we created using the Apple Note app on our phone, which communicated the value of the offer, along with common, everyday language used for the primary text.
Because we were testing a new concept, we changed both the ad image and primary text but made sure both fit into our overall hypothesis. We split traffic between the control and the treatment and let the test run until it reached statistical validity and acquired more than 50 conversions.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that presenting everyday elements in uncommon contexts can significantly improve acquisition rates and lower CPA.
The treatment used unexpected juxtaposition to catch the prospect's attention, resulting in a 106.6% increase in email acquisition for all traffic compared to the control. But that's not all. Here are the highlights:
107% increase in email acquisition -- 39 v. 1786.49% increase in CTR -- 64.9% v. 34.8%87.3% increase in outbound clicks -- 103 v. 5556.4% decrease in CPA -- $9.68 v. $22.1946.5% decrease in cost-per-click -- $3.69 v. $6.90
This suggests that by using a creative approach to funnel design, one that subtly blends the ordinary with the unexpected, we can entice prospects to engage by creating an experience beyond just "showing them an ad."
The key to the success of this concept, we believe, is presenting everyday design elements and  language outside of their expected context, using day-to-day, non-promotional urgency, to stoke curiosity and build momentum through to the landing page
What's interesting about this test is that if you stop to think about the treatment logically, it doesn't make any sense. But that does not mean that its "illogical" construction was unintentional. The incongruity of the presentation, we hypothesize, plays a large part in piquing the prospect's interest and pulling them into the funnel.
In future experimentation, we should continue exploring the surprising juxtapositions of elements. It would be interesting to test this concept throughout the entire funnel.
And while we are intrigued and inspired to keep testing this concept, we are also aware of the potential for "fatigue" with the specific application we used in this test. Therefore, while we encourage the "note app" treatment for low-investment to test the concept, and will try it with a few different offers, we believe we will eventually need to test this concept using different tactics and applications.

experiments.title: How video ad creative impacts engagement n compared to static ad creative
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that video ad creative for a direct appeal campaign will be more engaging than an organic image and lead to higher engagement by increasing the visual appeal and clarity of the donation CTA
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/01/2024 - 06/10/2024
Bible League Canada ran a direct appeal campaign on Facebook, inviting users to donate to support children’s Biblical education worldwide. In a prior test, we validated that using “organic” looking ad creative, we could increase engagement and donations for direct appeal campaigns. However, for this campaign, we could use an inspirational short video that demonstrated the need for Christian education worldwide. We hypothesized that this moving video could attract more users on Facebook and lead to better results.
We built two campaigns with matching ad copy; the only change was the ad creative. Our control campaign featured the “organic” looking static image we’d tested in the past against the video ad creative. We ran the test for one week.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Key Learnings:

1. Video outperformed static image: The experiment demonstrated that using a video ad creative for direct appeal campaigns led to a significant increase in engagement, as indicated by the 84.1% increase in Clicks for All Traffic. This result supports the hypothesis that video ads are more engaging than static images for this type of campaign. This in turn led to a 300% increase in donations at a 92% Level of confidence.

2. Visual appeal and clarity: The success of the video ad creative in this experiment may be attributed to its ability to convey the need for Christian education worldwide in a more compelling and emotionally impactful way compared to the static image. The moving visuals likely captured users' attention and prompted them to take action.

It is worth noting that our CPM more than doubled for the video campaign and our cost per click went up 8%. However, the people we were paying to place the ad in front of were far more interested in the content, and thus, our click-through rate and instant donation page conversion rate both increased significantly, leading to the 300% increase in donations. (Our instant conversion rate increased 117%).

We will continue testing the impact of inspirational videos like this in other parts of the funnel and on other campaigns to see their impact.

experiments.title: How Conversion-Centered Design Affects Click Rates On A Landing Page
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that redesigning the NextAfter Institute membership page using conversion-centered design will increase click-through rates by using proven persuasion levers to hold attention and compel action.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 04/12/2024
Conversion-centered design (CCD) aims to apply multiple (a) persuasive design techniques and (b) psychological triggers to get more people to take a desired action. This tactic can be helpful when you feel a page “needs a lot of work” and/or when a page does not receive enough traffic to validate smaller changes.
As we began to drive more traffic to a landing page promoting the NextAfter Institute Membership, we wanted to test how using CCD to make multiple changes would impact conversion rates. For this test, we developed a treatment that tested:
Structuring page to improve “skim-ability” – We used color to create on-page contrast and make it easier for the user to find the information they needed; we also narrowed the copy width to make it more readable
Making social proof more prominent – We embedded a widget that made it easy for users to read and scroll through our many positive reviews, giving them a more prominent placement on the page and placing them in two locations: near the top and near the bottom of the page
Using an outcome-driven headline – The headline is the most important copy on the page, so we shifted from a feature-focused headline to one that reinforced what those features will allow the reader to achieve, reinforcing the “how” with a subhead
Using long-form copy to make the case – At the top of the page, we used outcome-driven copy in big bold font to prompt immediate action but we reinforced that messaging with an in-depth sales letter that uses compelling language to illustrate the value propsotion concisely yet thoroughly
Drawing attention to benefits and outcomes – We used color contrast, huge fonts, bold type, testimonials, highlighted text, short paragraphs, and crossheads to draw attention to the benefits and outcomes of membership
We split traffic between the control and the treatment and ran the A/B test for 30 days.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that implementing conversion-centered design techniques can significantly impact click-through rates.
By making multiple changes to improve the page design from the perspective of conversion rate optimization, we were able to increase click-through rates by 106.6% with a 100% statistical level of confidence.
In future experimentation, we should continue to utilize conversion-centered design principles to optimize our landing pages and drive conversions.
We can now improve on this design by experimenting with different creative combinations to refine our approach and improve our overall conversion rates. Additionally, we should consider applying these learnings to other areas of our website and marketing materials to enhance the effectiveness of our messaging and calls to action.

experiments.title: How does a iPhone Notes graphic impact email sign ups on Facebook
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that changing the image to be notes list of value points for Facebook traffic will achieve a higher email sign up rate because the value is more prominent than the format of the offer.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 05/21/2024
Pathway to Victory is the broadcast ministry of First Dallas.
Pathway Magazine is a digital content offer that PTV offers as a new name acquisition effort. We began to see a decrease in performance for this offer in acquiring name so we decided to run a test on the ad creative.
The control creative is a colorful image with a cell phone mock up of the digital offer along with bullet points of value that the offer contains.
The treatment creative is a mock up of a iPhone notes page with the bullet points of value.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Key learnings from this experiment include:
1. Changing the image format to a notes list of value points resulted in a significant increase in email sign-up rate.
2. The treatment creative, with the notes list of value points, was more impactful in acquiring names compared to the control creative with a colorful image and bullet points.
3. The statistical significance at a 99.6% level indicates a high level of confidence in the results.
In future experimentation, we should consider the following:
1. The format and presentation of the offer play a crucial role in acquiring names. A more straightforward and value-focused approach, such as a notes list, may be more effective than a colorful image with bullet points.
2. Testing different visual formats and content layouts can help identify the most effective design for acquiring names.
3. Continuously monitoring and analyzing the performance of different creatives will help optimize future campaigns and maximize results.
Overall, the success of the treatment creative in this experiment highlights the importance of testing and refining creative elements to enhance performance in name acquisition efforts.

experiments.title: How removing an eBook from Facebook creative impacts complete registrations
experiments.researchQuestion: We hypothesize that substituting the 3D eBook with a scientific image will lead to increased registration rates, as it is expected to captivate the attention of the targeted demographic more effectively.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/03/2024 - 04/28/2024
This experiment was conducted on behalf of a non-profit organization specializing in scientific research, education, and charitable initiatives. The test was carried out as a paid social media advertising campaign on Facebook, comparing the effectiveness of two different creative images. Our objective was to ascertain whether the target audience of this non-profit organization would respond more favorably to a 3D image of an eBook or a photograph. The purpose of this experiment was to determine whether adjusting the creative approach could enhance audience engagement, ultimately leading to a higher rate of completed registrations.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment indicate that the treatment, which involved using a scientific image instead of a 3D eBook image, significantly increased registration rates across various segments of the target audience. The treatment resulted in a 247.9% increase in Complete Registrations for All Traffic with a 100.0% level of confidence.

In addition, the experiment also showed significant decreases in Cost per Click (78%) and Cost per Registration (243%) with high levels of confidence, indicating that the treatment not only attracted more registrations but did so more cost-effectively.

Moving forward, the learnings from this experiment suggest that utilizing scientific images may be more effective in capturing the attention and interest of the target audience for the SETI Institute. Future experimentation should focus on leveraging visually engaging scientific images to drive higher engagement and conversion rates. It is important to continue testing different visual creatives to identify the most effective approach for engaging the audience and achieving the organization's objectives.

experiments.title: How leveraging a small image on a donate button to build affinity and alignment affects donor conversion rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using a small American flag to build affinity for potential donors to The Daily Signal will achieve an increase in conversion rate because the flag image will help align their motivation with the intended action.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 05/09/2024
The Daily Signal is The Heritage Foundation’s multimedia news platform. On their home page, and most other pages, the only call-to-action to donate to support the organization is located at the bottom in the footer section among many other site links.
They’ve previously tested adding a donate button to the main menu of the homepage here: https://app.winstonknows.com/e/K1qG
That experiment resulted in a large increase in traffic to the donation page because we provided an easier, more prominent path to the donation page.
The team had noticed an eCommerce site that, when selling merchandise for a particular team, put that team’s logo on the “add to cart” page to leverage the natural affinity between the buyer and the team.
To continue testing the donate button to increase traffic to the donation page, they decided to test a similar concept of adding an American Flag icon to the button.
They believed the flag image would help align a donor’s motivation with their intended action.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding a small American flag to the donate button on The Daily Signal's website significantly increased the number of clicks to the donation page. The treatment group, which included the flag icon, saw a 53.6% increase in clicks for all traffic with a 99.9% level of confidence.

Additionally, the treatment button saw a 22.8% lift in transactions with a 59.7% level of confidence. Although these results are not valid, they do indicate that overall more traffic to the donation page will increase the percentage of donations made.

Overall, this experiment suggests that incorporating patriotic or visually appealing elements can help build affinity with potential donors and encourage them to take action. In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to continue testing the use of imagery or design elements that align with donors' motivations to see if similar increases in conversion rates can be achieved.

Additionally, it is important to consider the placement of calls-to-action on the website. The success of this experiment may also indicate that strategically placing donation buttons in prominent locations on the site can lead to higher engagement and donation rates.

experiments.title: How does a button on a slide-out impact email acquisitions?
experiments.researchQuestion: We hypothesize that incorporating the form directly into the slideout will streamline the process, reducing friction and the number of steps required, potentially resulting in a higher number of sign-ups. Conversely, a slideout without a form may decrease the perceived commitment, thereby encouraging more sign-ups after users have gained additional information on the landing page
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 04/22/2024
In an effort to enhance email acquisitions for a content offer, an experiment was conducted for WUNC, a non-profit news radio station. This experiment was tested on the homepage of their website to determine whether prospects are more likely to sign up for an offer directly within a slideout or by being redirected to a landing page. The aim is to gain insights into the most effective strategy for maximizing email acquisitions.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are:
1. The treatment, which included a link to direct users to a separate landing page, resulted in a 56.5% increase in Emails Acquired for All Traffic compared to an embedded sign-up form.
2. By requiring users to navigate to a separate landing page to provide their information, the treatment potentially decreased the perceived commitment, leading to more sign-ups. This suggests that after users have gained additional information on the landing page, they are more likely to sign up for a content offer
3. The results of the experiment showed a 100.0% statistical level of confidence, indicating that the increase in email acquisitions was not due to chance.
The key learnings from this experiment can guide decision-making in future experiments. It is recommended to continue testing different sign-up processes to further optimize email acquisitions. Additionally, user convenience and reducing perceived commitment can be important factors to consider when designing and implementing sign-up forms on websites.

experiments.title: How leveraging a small image to build affinity and alignment affects donor conversion rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using a small American flag to build affinity for renewal prospects will achieve an increase in conversion rate because the flag image will help align their motivation with the intended action. because  the flag image will help align their motivation with the intended action.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/26/2024 - 04/08/2024
The Heritage Foundation had been running a slate of experiments on their membership renewal page to try to increase conversion and get more members to renew as monthly donors. In their last experiment, they had tested the arrangement of the giving form tabs to prioritize their monthly ask first.
The team had noticed an eCommerce site that, when selling merchandise for a particular team, put that team’s logo on the “add to cart” page to leverage the natural affinity between the buyer and the team.
The Heritage team wondered if this might be true for donations, and decided to test it on their renewal page. They added a small American flag to appeal to the patriotism of their donors to the “Join the Leaders Club” tab and to the donate button itself, and launched an A/B test to determine a winner.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are:
1. The use of a small American flag on the membership renewal page significantly increased the conversion rate for renewing as monthly donors by 170.4% with a high level of confidence.
2. The treatment version with the flag image and patriotic messaging outperformed the control version by a large margin.
Moving forward, we should consider the following for future experimentation:
1. Including visual elements like the American flag that align with the organization's values can have a significant impact on conversion rates.
2. Personalized and motivational messaging can help create a sense of belonging and purpose, potentially leading to higher conversion rates.
3. It may be beneficial to continue testing different visual elements and messaging strategies to further optimize the membership renewal process and maximize conversions.
4. Consider incorporating similar elements in other areas of the website or marketing materials to maintain consistency in messaging and branding for better results.
Overall, the results of this experiment highlight the importance of using visual cues and persuasive messaging to influence donor behavior positively.

experiments.title: How adding a donate button to a news platform site’s header affects donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a donate button to the top header for site visitors will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate because more people will click to view the donation page.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/02/2024 - 04/10/2024
The Daily Signal is The Heritage Foundation’s multimedia news platform. On their home page, and most other pages, the only call-to-action to donate to support the organization is located at the bottom in the footer section among many other site links.
In an effort to increase the amount of traffic to the donation page from the home page, we previously tested adding a more prominently displayed donate button to the category menu at the top of the page on the desktop version, and in the navigation menu of the mobile version. This experiment resulted in a 42.8% increase in donations compared to the control. However, with a 53.2% level of confidence, these results can’t be relied upon to predict a valid outcome.
In this attempt to increase traffic to the donation page, we wanted to test whether adding the donate button to a more notable location at the very top of the page would lead to an increase in donor conversion rate. We also wanted to test whether adding a gift box icon to the donate button in order to match the “branded” style of the “Search” and “Menu” buttons next to it would lead to an increase in conversion rate.
The regular donate button and “branded” donate button appeared in the top header sitewide for both the desktop and mobile experiences. 50% of traffic saw the control with no button, and the two treatments received 25% of traffic each.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding a donate button to the top header for site visitors led to a significant increase in traffic to the donation page. The first treatment saw a significant 2,694% increase in clicks to the donation page compared to the control group with no button, while the second treatment saw an even higher 2,866.2% increase in clicks at a 99.9% level of confidence. This result suggests that making the donation button more prominent and easily accessible can greatly impact the amount of traffic led to the donation page.
However, in terms of donations, the first treatment lead to a 9.3% decrease in donor conversion rate (at an invalid 14.5% level of confidence), and the second treatment led to a near-valid 64% decrease in donor conversion rate compared to the control.
While invalid, these results may suggest that by more prominently displaying the donate button and increasing traffic to the donation page, people who do click on the button may be less motivated to give than someone in the control experience who seeks out the donation page in order to support the organization.
Future experimentation is needed to understand what motivates visitors of The Daily Signal to make a gift to support their work. Experiment ideas include testing alternative copy on the donation page to motivate more viewers of the site to give, and testing an alternate icon that aligns more strongly with the organization's mission.

experiments.title: How adding a premium impacted donor conversion on an instant donation page
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that highlighting a premium for instant donation page visitors will achieve an increase in average gift because there will be greater appeal to choose a higher donation amount.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/15/2024 - 04/03/2024
When evaluating ways to increase average gift for instant donation page visitors, we wondered if adding a book premium with a minimum gift amount of $50 or more would motivate more people to choose a higher gift amount when donating. We split traffic between a page with no premium against a treatment that included an image of the book and added language that indicated the book was available for gifts of $50 or more.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding a book premium with a minimum gift amount of $50 or more did not lead to an increase in average gift amount from instant donation page visitors. In fact, the treatment group saw a 22.9% decrease in transactions compared to the control group. The difference was statistically significant at a 96.6% confidence level, indicating that the control page without the premium performed better.

In future experimentation, it is essential to carefully consider the value proposition of the premium offered and the minimum donation amount required to receive it. It's possible that the book premium did not resonate with donors or that the $50 minimum gift amount was too high for many visitors.

Moving forward, it may be beneficial to test different types of premiums, adjust the minimum donation amount, or explore other strategies to increase average gift amounts. Additionally, considering the preferences and motivations of the target audience when designing experiments can help ensure more successful outcomes.

experiments.title: How using a plain image in a content offer Facebook ad impacted clicks compared to a designed image using an overlaid CTA
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using a plain image in a content offer Facebook ad will increase clicks by making the ad appear more authentic because it looks like a post from another Facebook user
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/12/2024 - 03/20/2024
Focus on the Family uses free video series content offers to acquire new subscribers and donors. For one of their best-performing campaigns, they hypothesized that they could increase engagement by making the ad look like a post from another user. Users who share content don’t create “ads” with a custom photo with overlaid call to action text. Instead, they often post a plain image with some thoughts about it.
We created an ad treatment with no text or CTA overlaying the image. We used the ad copy itself to promote the free video series to users and invite them to sign up. We ran this against our control ad images that featured overlaid CTAs.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that using an organic-looking Facebook ad design, which resembles a post from another user, can increase clicks significantly compared to traditional ad designs with overlaid call-to-action text. This approach creates a more authentic and engaging experience for the audience, leading to higher engagement with the ad content.
Moving forward, we should leverage this learning in future experimentation by incorporating more organic-looking ad designs in our campaigns. By focusing on creating content that mimics user-generated posts rather than traditional ad formats, we can build a stronger connection with our audience and drive higher click-through rates. Additionally, we should continue to test and optimize different creative approaches to further enhance our advertising strategies and maximize results.
For a future offer, we will test no ad copy and instead use an image with a clear, short call to action on a plain-colored background.
Impact on other metrics:
The treatment decreased the gross cost per click by $0.48 ($1.42 vs $190)There was no statistically significant difference in emails acquired during the experiment.

We wondered if a simplified template or a more designed layout, with imagery and CTA’s along with blurbs about the content, would perform better.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that sending a plain text version of the email to non-donor recipients led to a decrease in email engagement, specifically a 36.9% decrease in Clicks for All Traffic with a 100.0% statistical level of confidence.

Moving forward, it would be beneficial to continue conducting A/B tests with different variations of email layouts to determine the most effective format for engaging non-donor recipients. Additionally, gathering feedback from the email recipients through surveys or direct responses could provide valuable insights into their preferences and help in tailoring email content to better suit their needs and interests.

experiments.title: How removing the fair market value for a premium on a donation page impacted conversions
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by removing the fair market value for donation page viewers will achieve a higher average gift because the perceived value of the premium is not influenced by a low fair market value.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/07/2023 - 12/14/2023
Pathway to Victory is the broadcast ministry of First Baptist Dallas.
PTV uses Anedot as its donation page platform. A feature of the donation page is to show the market value of a premium. When a donation is made, and a premium is given, the tax-deductible amount is the gift amount with the fair market value (FMV) of the premium removed.
PTV frequently self-publishes books as premiums, and therefore, the FMV is typically less than $5. We have observed that the average gift for these premiums is less than professionally published premiums and, therefore, has a more expensive FMV.
We hypothesize that having the fair market value for the premium on the donation is causing the average gift to be lower for self published books because the value of the premium appears to be less.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that removing the fair market value (FMV) for premiums on the donation page significantly increased the average gift and the number of transactions. The treatment group, which had the FMV removed, experienced a 23.2% increase in transactions for all traffic, with a statistical confidence level of 96.3%.
From these findings, it can be concluded that providing the FMV for premiums on the donation page can negatively impact the perceived value of the premium, leading to lower average gifts and fewer transactions.
In future experimentation, it is recommended to continue with the approach of not displaying the FMV for premiums. This can help maximize the average gift and increase the number of transactions, ultimately leading to higher overall donations. It may also be beneficial to explore other factors that could influence donation behavior, such as the design and layout of the donation page, to further optimize the donation process.

experiments.title: How a humanized sticky bar affects donor conversion rates on a CYE donation page
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using a humanized sticky bar on a CYE donation page for potential donors will achieve a higher donor conversion rate because it provides more urgency and a special message from a well-known figure at the organization. because  it provides more urgency and a special message from a well-known figure at the organization.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/29/2023 - 12/31/2023
ACI ran their annual calendar year-end campaign. In the final four emails of the campaign, they wanted to test how an urgent, personalized sticky bar signed by Dr. Ben Carson would perform. Typically, they see higher conversion through funnels and campaigns when messaging is directly from Dr. Carson, so we expected an increase in conversion rate.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that using a personalized sticky bar signed by Dr. Ben Carson did not result in a higher donor conversion rate. In fact, the treatment version of the email had a 19.3% decrease in Transactions for All Traffic compared to the control version, with a 95.1% level of confidence. Additionally, the treatment version experienced a significant 237% decrease in One-Time Donations with a 98% level of confidence.
These results suggest that the personalized sticky bar did not create the urgency and special message that was expected. Instead, it may have had a negative impact on donor conversion.
To use this learning in future experimentation, it is important to carefully consider the messaging and language used in fundraising campaigns. While using a well-known figure like Dr. Ben Carson may normally result in higher conversion rates, it is crucial to ensure that the messaging aligns with the organization's goals and resonates with potential donors. Additionally, it is important to conduct thorough testing and analysis before implementing new strategies to minimize potential negative impacts on donor conversion.

experiments.title: How replacing a button with a link impacts the click rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that replacing button cta with raw link cta for mass communications emails will achieve a higher level of clicks because it will make the email feel more personal.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 02/23/2024
Moody historically has used buttons and very heavily designed emails. We wanted to test if we could increase engagement with their emails by making them look very personal and not like a marketing email. In the first series of this design of experiments, we tested plain text vs. HTML with the plain text resulting in a massive lift in engagement.
For this experiment, we wanted to see if we could continue to make the email more personal by switching out the buttons with raw links.
experiments.keyLearnings:
While the test did result in a valid decrease of clicks, I think we need to take this with a grain of salt.

This email test was meant to be the second test in a series of making the emails feel like a 1-on-1 communication instead of a mass marketing email. The first test was plain text vs. HTML, in which the plain text won with a 99.9% confidence level. This test was intended to be on a plain text template.

I believe we see a sharp decline in this specific format because a link is harder to see in the HTML version.

While this is still a valuable learning to see how the audience responds to this, I believe it will need to be retested to see if it produces the same result as a personal email.

experiments.title: How a fullwidth, plain-text email affects click rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using a full-width text format for our weekly email send will achieve an increase in email clicks because the email will seem more organic, personal, and less like marketing.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/03/2023 - 11/04/2023
Our weekly email sends have been the same format for quite a while. We tested into using plain text format to create a more human-looking email a while ago and were curious if we could take it one step further to  seem even more organic. Typically our email text is centered so that when it’s opened it’s still quite obvious it’s a bulk send, not a one-off email.
The only thing change in the treatment of the email is the format. The copy, subject line, sender, etc. all stayed the same.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that using a full-width text format for our weekly email send can significantly increase the number of clicks. The treatment group, which received the full-width text format, experienced a 27.6% increase in clicks for all traffic compared to the control group.
This indicates that the full-width text format created the perception that the email was sent straight from Gmail rather than a bulk send. This likely caught the attention of recipients and encouraged them to click on the links within the email.
It is important to note that the treatment showed a significant 214% decrease in Clicks to Opens. This means that even though the treatment group had higher click-through rates, fewer people actually took the step to open the email. The treatment would have no impact on this though since the test was run on the email layout, not the content or email "envelope."
To use this learning in future experimentation, we might want to explore different formats that combine the organic feel of the full-width text with elements that entice recipients to open the email.
For example, we could experiment with subject lines that highlight the content or benefits of the email, as well as using personalization techniques to make the email feel even more tailored to each recipient.
Overall, this experiment has shown that the format of our weekly email can have a significant impact on recipient engagement. By continuing to test and iterate on different formats, we can improve our email performance and ultimately achieve our goal of increasing click-through rates while maintaining a high open rate.

experiments.title: How a personalized slide out impacted email conversion rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that making the slide out from an individual for new web visitors will achieve a higher email conversion because  this is a personal invitation and expectations of the offer they will receive .
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 08/02/2023
WUNC is North Carolina’s largest NPR station. In an effort to convert new web traffic into to subscribers we decided to test a more human approach slide out.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that using a personalized slide out from an individual for new web visitors resulted in a significant increase in email conversions. The treatment group, which received the personalized invitation, had a 123.2% increase in acquired emails compared to the control group.
This test only targeting new visitors to WUNC's website so that we are reaching new names and not existing contacts.
This suggests that personalizing the communication and creating expectations of the offer can be highly effective in converting new web visitors into subscribers. This human approach resonated with the visitors and made them more likely to provide their email address.
In future experimentation, it is recommended to continue using this personalized slide out approach for new web visitors. This can be further refined by testing different variations of the personal invitation message, such as the tone and wording, to find the most effective combination. Additionally, it would be valuable to expand this experimentation to other conversion goals, such as donations or membership sign-ups, to see if the same human approach can be successful in those areas as well.

experiments.title: How removing an image in an MMS message affects opt-out rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that removing an image from an MMS message for lapsed donors will achieve a decrease in opt-out rate because it looks less like marketing and more like a text a human would send.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/20/2024
The Heritage Foundation was running SMS and MMS ads as part of their kickoff campaign. They had leveraged texting because of the personal nature of the communication, two-way conversation ability, and high deliverability, and wanted to test a P2P MMS platform However, they wondered if their personal message was undermined by the image they sent alongside the message, since that’s not something that people usually send along with 1:1 communications.
They split their list in half and sent half of the list an MMS without an image to see if it increased conversion rate. This ended up converting the message to an SMS message, which should be noted.
However, they also wanted to monitor other metrics that might be affected by this, including opt-out rate.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that removing an image from an MMS message for lapsed donors resulted in a decrease in opt-out rate.

Based on this experiment, it can be concluded that lapsed donors may be more likely to disengage with a message stream that includes an image. The personal nature of the communication may be undermined by the image, as it might not be seen as a genuine message from a human.

When donors do not see a communication as "marketing", then you have the opportunity to transcend a transactional relationship and open a legitimate communication channel.

experiments.title: How removing images on an email newsletter impacts click through rates.
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that removing images for enewsletter readers will achieve a change in click through rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/25/2024 - 01/26/2024
WUNC is North Carolina’s largest NPR station, and they have a weekly enewsletter featuring some of the top stories of the week that deploys every Thursday.
For this test, we wanted to see whether removing images and making the email look more like a human sent it made an impact on click through rates.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that removing images from the enewsletter resulted in a decrease in click-through rates. The treatment group, which received the enewsletter without images, had a 17.3% decrease in clicks compared to the control group.
Based on this result, it is evident that images play an important role in engaging readers and encouraging them to click on the content. Therefore, in future experimentation, it is recommended to continue including images in the enewsletter. However, it may be worth exploring different types of images or optimizing the placement and design of the images to maximize their impact on click-through rates.
Additionally, it is important to consider other factors that may have influenced the results. The experiment ran for only 1 day, which may not have allowed enough time for readers to adapt to the new format. Conducting experiments over a longer duration could provide more reliable insights.
Overall, the findings suggest that images are a valuable component of the enewsletter and should be used strategically to enhance reader engagement and increase click-through rates.

experiments.title: How an exit intent pop up affects email conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a popup offering a free ebook for users exiting a page that advertises a for-purchase item will achieve an increased acquisition of email because we believe that targeting the same motivation (scripture memory) at a free price point will acquire those who were exiting because of the price.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/21/2023 - 03/30/2023
The Navigators’ Topical Memory System is a for-purchase book that helps the reader memorize scripture. The landing page for this offer briefly outlines what the TMS is, important points, and an ask at the end to purchase the book. We hypothesized that by adding an exit intent pop-up on the page for a free eBook on a similar topic (How to Memorize Scripture) would not interfere with those who are purchasing the TMS book, but would acquire the emails of those who were planning to exit the page.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that offering a free ebook with an exit-intent popup can significantly increase email acquisition. The treatment group saw a 147.9% increase in email acquisition for all traffic, and new visitors, organic search visitors, desktop visitors, social visitors, male visitors, and visitors aged 18-24 saw substantial increases in email acquisition with a high level of confidence.
What does this tell us about users exiting the page? The eBook popup is about the same topic as the TMS page—but where the TMS page is urging users to make a purchase, the popup entices them with a similar offer free of cost. Because this popup is only setup to appear when the user goes to exit the page, this wouldn't detract from those who were going to purchase the book. What it does accomplish is allow The Navigators to acquire the email of someone who would've exited the page and been lost.

experiments.title: How the presentation of the format of a content offer impacts email sign-ups
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that showcasing the digital resource on an iPad mockup on the landing page will lead to an increase in email sign-ups because there is increased clarity on what the person will receive.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 12/05/2023
Each year around Christmas, Dallas Theological Seminary creates an eBook devotional to send to their email list.
This year, they wanted to see how the image on the landing page impacts overall email signups. Historically, they have used a 3d eBook image of the cover of the eBook. They wanted to test how showcasing the devotional as a digital resource by mocking it up on an iPad would impact email conversion.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that showcasing the eBook as a digital resource on an iPad mockup on the landing page led to a significant increase in email sign-ups. The treatment group, which saw the iPad mockup, had a 3.9% higher conversion rate compared to the control group, which saw the traditional 3d eBook image.
This indicates that providing a clear visual representation of the digital resource, such as through an iPad mockup, helps potential subscribers better understand what they will receive. This increased clarity likely contributed to the higher conversion rate.
In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to continue showcasing the digital resource on an iPad mockup or similar visual representation. This can be applied to other landing pages or promotional materials to improve conversion rates and increase engagement. It is important to continue testing and iterating on visual elements to optimize conversion rates and overall effectiveness of email acquisition strategies.

It is possible that this ultimately led the mission to sound more indvidual/Fazale focused rather than broadening to greater impact.

experiments.title: How calling out a most popular gift option on a gift array affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that the inclusion of a “most popular” callout on a gift array  for the $50 option for potential donors will achieve a higher average gift because it leverages the psychological principle of social proof, creating a sense of trust and legitimacy among potential donors.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 10/23/2023
The American Cornerstone Institute is offering a book called “Why America Matters” for a gift of $25 or more through paid Facebook ads. We wanted to test adding a most popular callout to the $50 option on the gift array to see if donors would give a larger amount to receive the book.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that including a "most popular" callout on a gift array for a specific option can significantly increase donations and average gift. The treatment version, which included a "most popular" callout on the $50 option, resulted in a 27.5% increase in donations compared to the control version. Additionally, the treatment saw a $0.35 increase in gift size with a 95.46% level of confidence.
This experiment demonstrates the power of social proof, where potential donors are influenced by the choices made by others. By highlighting a specific option as the "most popular," potential donors are more likely to trust and perceive it as a legitimate choice, leading to a higher average gift.
In future experimentation, we should continue to explore the use of social proof in different ways. For example, we can test different callouts such as "best value" or "preferred choice" to see if they have a similar impact on donor behavior. Additionally, it would be beneficial to explore the effect of social proof in other aspects of the donation process, such as the donation page layout or testimonials from previous donors.

experiments.title: How the placement of a content offer on blog article pages impacts email sign up
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using a slide-out style interrupter to promote an eBook offer on blog article pages will achieve an increase in email acquisition because it will capture the reader’s attention mid-article instead of having to scroll to the bottom.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/10/2023 - 10/30/2023
Care Net has typically run what we call a “dear reader” style ad at the end of their articles. For example, the reader would be on a blog article page, read the entire article and then after, would be presented with what looks like a continuation of the article but starts with, “Dear reader,” and then presents the content offer to the reader.
We were curious to see how many people weren’t actually scrolling to the bottom of the article and therefore, weren’t seeing the content offer. So we decided to develop a slide out that used the same copy and also included the same image of the eBook, but would be presented to the reader once they scrolled through 50% of the article.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are:
1. The use of a slide-out interrupter significantly increased the number of emails acquired for all traffic by 638.1% compared to the traditional "dear reader" style ad at the end of the article.
2. The slide-out interrupter was successful in capturing the reader's attention mid-article, eliminating the need for them to scroll to the bottom to see the content offer.
3. The slide-out interrupter was able to effectively promote the eBook offer, leading to a higher conversion rate.
4. The results of the experiment were statistically significant at a 100.0% level of confidence, indicating a strong likelihood that the treatment was the cause of the increase in email acquisition.
In future experimentation, it is recommended to continue using the slide-out interrupter on blog article pages. This approach has proven to be more effective in capturing the reader's attention and increasing email acquisition. Additionally, it may be beneficial to test variations in the timing or design of the interrupter to optimize its effectiveness further. Furthermore, considering the success of the interrupter in promoting the eBook offer, it could be applied to other content offers or calls to action on the website to improve conversion rates.

experiments.title: How a video-based landing page affects email signup rates for a new YouTube fundraising show
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that replacing a text-based landing page with a video-based landing page will increase registrations by “providing a sample” of the content’s value and showing rather than telling why people should want to tune in.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 07/10/2023
We recently decided to pursue a new way to share what works in fundraising by launching a YouTube show called “Decoding Generosity”.
And to promote this new ‘edutainment’ experience, we wanted to know how using a video on a landing page would affect sign-up rates for a prescreening of the show.
This test was run in the context of a broader promotional strategy testing the concept of creating a unified in-funnel experience using video as a means of “showing” rather than telling about the show.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Using a video-based landing page instead of a text-based landing page produced a significant 57% increase in the registration rate.

We believe there are several reasons that the video-based treatment produced these results:
Show, don't tell. The video-based landing page matched the medium of the content we were promoting, allowing us to preview clips from the show to stoke their desire to tune in and "show" that the claim presented in our offer was genuine
 	Front and center. By reducing the supporting copy, the value-driven headline and subhead were given more visual prominence
 	Quality matters. The high production quality of the video likely played a role in the overall effectiveness of the treatment
 	Context matters. Traditionally, we have seen that text-based landing pages far outperform video-based landing pages. But it's important to consider context. This promotion was run in the context of a B2B promotion (rather than a fundraising context) and the landing page was part of a broader video-based promotional experience meant to "keep people in the story" without feeling like they were in a funnel**

experiments.title: How a stripped down acquisition donation page impacts donor conversion compared to a newer, heavily designed donation page
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a stripped-down acquisition donation page design will increase donation conversion by decreasing friction and increasing the strength of the value proposition.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/11/2023 - 07/26/2023
Buckner International’s top donor acquisition campaign has been their Shoes for Orphan Souls campaign.  In this campaign, donors donated pairs of new shoes and socks to vulnerable children worldwide. The donation page for this offer had been tested and refined for several years. Recently, they launched a radically redesigned version of this page that featured a much more image-heavy and designed “look” than their original page that was stripped down and very text-heavy.
They had no data on how this new page was performing compared to their old page and were unable to test the design since Google Optimize ended. We hypothesized that the new page design may be harming donor conversion because it led with the ask and placed the value proposition for giving shoes beneath the appeal. We also feared that the new design could be distracting to users, increasing friction.
Since we couldn’t test in Optimize, we decided to test the new page against the old on Facebook. We created an AB test of their Shoes campaign and evenly split traffic and budget between both versions.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that the stripped-down acquisition donation page design was highly effective in increasing donation conversion.
In future experimentation, it is important to prioritize simplicity and clarity in the design of donation pages. The stripped-down design reduced friction and made the value proposition more prominent, leading to a higher conversion rate. This suggests that donors respond better to a clear and focused ask, rather than being overwhelmed with excessive imagery and design elements.
Our hypothesis is that the primary issue with the new design is the lack of a compelling value proposition before visitors are asked to make their gift. It is also possible that the competing CTAs of the new design are contributing to the decreased conversion. The original design has one call to action. The new page has multiple, including a subscription CTA in the footer. It is impossible to know from this limited test which elements of the new design were the most distracting to users and had the greatest negative impact on conversion. Once Buckner is able to launch tests on their donation pages again, we will be able to dig into each element's impact on the page.
Also, due to budget limitations in Facebook, we weren't able to get a large sample of gifts through the experiment. However, at no time did we see the new page result in a higher conversion rate than the old per Facebook's campaign data.

experiments.title: How an in-line appeal that becomes a sticky bar with a small donation CTA impacts donor conversion during a Fiscal Year End Campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that an in-line appeal that becomes a sticky bar notifying visitors of the threat of the federal abortion hotline for Care Net’s Pregnancy Decision Line will increase donor conversion by increasing the appeal and fear of loss.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/19/2023 - 06/30/2023
Care Net is an organization that provides support and resources to women and men considering abortion.
Historically, they’ve used a dear reader at the bottom of article pages with messaging around the urgent need in the pro-life space and have tied this urgency back to either a donor goal or a revenue goal. During their fiscal year-end campaign, they often use a sticky bar with a daily donor goal countdown to convert visitors to their article pages into donors.
They wanted to test this against a design inspired by one of Wikipedia’s tests where there is an in-line appeal that becomes a sticky bar when scrolling. The control and the treatment both had the same copy, but the placements were different: the control being at the end of the article and the treatment being at the beginning. On the treatment, once the reader scrolled to continue reading the article, the dear reader would then turn into a sticky bar with the same copy.
Our hypothesis was that the conversational and value-driven copy on the in-line design and sticky bar would increase donor conversion by increasing the appeal and exclusivity of the offer. We would also be tapping into the donor’s desire to not have a federal, taxpayer-funded hotline.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are as follows:
1. The treatment, which consisted of an in-line appeal that became a sticky bar with the same copy when scrolling, performed significantly worse than the control. It resulted in a 70.3% decrease in donations for all traffic, indicating that this design approach did not effectively convert visitors into donors.
2. Different visitor segments showed even larger decreases in donations. Returning visitors had an 828% decrease. These significant drops in donation rates suggest that the in-line appeal with the fear of loss did not resonate well with these specific segments.
Based on these findings, it is clear that the Wikipedia "before article appeal" approach used in the treatment did not effectively appeal to visitors to become donors. The results suggest that a different messaging strategy should be tested in future experimentation.
Instead of relying solely on the placement, other emotional appeals or value-driven messaging could be considered. For example, highlighting how many more people can be reached through the Care Net Pregnancy Decision Line might be a more compelling appeal for potential donors, or showcasing how this new federal hotline could impact people being able to find life-affirming care.
Additionally, it may be worth exploring different placements for the appeal. While the treatment placed the messaging at the beginning of the article, future experiments could test different placements such as the end of the article, in a sidebar, or in a pop-up overlay.

experiments.title: How including a photo of a premium impacted recurring gift conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding an image of a premium for recurring donor prospects will achieve an increase in recurring donor conversion because the clarity and appeal of the offer to give monthly would be enhanced for page visitors.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/07/2023 - 07/03/2023
We were running a monthly upgrade ask to existing donors on the housefile and wanted to offer a special premium for those that chose to convert. We know that sometimes adding images to a donation page can cause additional friction, but we also believed adding a specific image previewing the premium that monthly donors would receive might increase clarity and appeal for this offer. So we split traffic 50/50 between the control page with no image and the treatment page that added an image of the membership card that would be available to those who made a recurring gift on the page.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding an image of a premium for recurring donor prospects had a positive impact on conversion rates. The treatment page, which included an image of the membership card available to monthly donors, resulted in a 79.5% increase in recurring gifts compared to the control page at 80% level of confidence. We believe the visual aid highlighting the benefit they would receive if they said "yes" to the appeal on this donation page helped more people follow through with converting because they had more clarity around what a Servant Leader Membership Card means.
In future experimentation, we should continue to explore the inclusion of images or visual elements on donation pages. While this experiment showed a positive impact, further testing is needed to obtain statistically significant results. It is also important to consider other factors that may affect donor conversion, such as the placement and design of the image, the clarity of the premium offer, and any potential friction caused by the addition of the image.
It is recommended to conduct additional A/B tests with larger sample sizes and optimize the design and placement of the image to ensure that the results are valid and reliable. By testing different variations and analyzing the data at a higher level of statistical confidence, we can make more informed decisions about the inclusion of images on donation pages to increase donor conversion rates.
