experiments.title: How a video impacted email sign ups on a content offer landing page
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by adding a video  for landing page visitors will achieve a higher email sign up rate because viewers receive a preview of the course.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/19/2023 - 07/03/2023
Avila is an organization that aims to draw Christians worldwide into deeper union with Christ through mystagogically oriented spiritual education and formation.
In an effort to increase email sign ups for the Into The Deep course, we tested adding a video the landing page
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding a video to the landing page for the Into The Deep course resulted in a decrease in email sign-ups. This was observed across all traffic, as well as specific segments such as mobile and tablet visitors, new visitors, female visitors, and visitors in the age groups of 35-44 and 55-64. There was also a significant decrease in email sign-ups from paid search visitors.
These findings suggest that the video may not have resonated well with the target audience or may have led to a distraction or disinterest in signing up for the course. It is important to note that these results are statistically significant, indicating a high level of confidence in the findings.
In future experimentation, it is crucial to carefully consider the impact of any changes or additions to the landing page. A thorough analysis of the target audience's preferences, interests, and behavior should be conducted to ensure that any modifications align with their expectations and increase the likelihood of conversion.
Additionally, it may be beneficial to conduct qualitative research, such as interviews or surveys, to gather more insights into the audience's perception of the video and their decision-making process. This can help identify any specific issues or concerns that need to be addressed in order to optimize the landing page for maximum conversion.
Overall, this experiment serves as a reminder that assumptions and hypotheses should always be tested before implementing changes. It is essential to constantly iterate and improve based on data and customer feedback to achieve the desired outcomes.

experiments.title: How testing different Dear Reader survey topics impacted email acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that implementing a dear reader asking for opinions on the polarizing topic of the border crisis in Texas for page visitors will achieve an increase in email acquisition because readers will be more drawn to that topic than of school choice.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/19/2023 - 07/03/2023
TPPF has two active surveys: School Choice and Border Crisis. We want to see which survey topic is most appealing to our reader audience and can collect the most emails, so we’ve separated them via a 50/50 Dear Reader experiment.
These Dear Readers are implemented at the end of policy issue articles, so 50% of readers will be asked to complete the School Choice Survey and 50% will be asked to complete the Border Crisis Survey once they are done reading their respective article.
Let’s see which survey is most appealing to our audience and acquires the most emails.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The implementation of a "dear reader" treatment asking for opinions on the polarizing topic of the border crisis in Texas resulted in a significant increase (99.4% level of confidence) in email acquisition. The treatment group experienced an 80.2% increase in email acquisition compared to the control group.
The treatment also resulted in one downstream donation compared to none to the control.
Based on these findings, it is clear that the topic of the border crisis in Texas is highly relevant and engaging for page visitors, more so than the topic of School Choice. This suggests that future experimentation should continue to explore this topic and its potential impact on user engagement and email acquisition, and that this org should roll out the Border Crisis Dear Reader to 100% of traffic.
It is also worth noting that the experiment ran for 13 days, which may not capture all potential long-term effects. Future experiments could consider running for a longer duration to assess any sustained impact on email acquisition.
In summary, the key learnings from this experiment emphasize the importance of selecting relevant and engaging topics to drive user engagement and email acquisition.

experiments.title: How testing different Dear Reader survey topics impacted email acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that implementing a dear reader asking for opinions on the active topic of school choice in Illinois for page visitors will achieve an increase in email acquisition because readers will be more drawn to the topic of school choice.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/17/2023 - 06/10/2023
IPI has two active surveys: State of Our State and School Choice. We want to see which survey topic is most appealing to our reader audience and can collect the most emails, so we’ve separated them via a 50/50 Dear Reader experiment.
These Dear Readers are implemented at the end of policy research articles, so 50% of readers will be asked to complete the State of Our State Survey and 50% will be asked to complete the School Choice Survey once they are done reading their respective article.
Let’s see which version acquires the most emails.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The Dear Reader pointing readers to our School Choice survey did not perform as well as its counterpart - the State of Our State survey. In fact, the School Choice variant produced a 42.3% decrease in email conversion rate with a valid 99.9% level of confidence.

The results give us valid proof that more page visitors were interested in offering their opinions on the general well being of our state covering a number of topics compared to those who wanted to offer their opinions on a specific topic. Is this evidence that we should boast more general surveys over single-issue surveys moving forward? Not necessarily, as it's possible that school choice simply wasn't a topic that is affecting that many people right now, or that people care deeply about.

The value proposition for each Dear Reader followed a similar template, so without a different approach in value proposition, the results really do boil down to the topic presented.

For our next test, we will swap the School Choice survey for the Illinois Forward survey, which is about the state's fiscal standing and overall responsibility. We'll found out if that topic outduels the State of Our State survey.

It's important to test different dear readers to see which resonates best with our readers and deserves the long standing spot for our standard policy research dear readers. We will continue to test to determine which one has the greatest potential to rack up emails and enhance engagement.

experiments.title: How a donation interruptor including a monthly upgrade ask affects recurring donation rates for previous donors
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that presenting a monthly upgrade ask for one-time, returning donors during the donation process will achieve higher recurring donation rates by increasing its visibility at the decision-making point and prominently communicating its value.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/15/2023 - 03/25/2023
In an effort to increase recurring donor acquisition for a public policy organization during a recent high-urgency campaign, we wanted to measure the effect of interrupting the donation process with a pop-up asking the donor to become a monthly supporter instead of making only a one-time gift.
This pop-up was set up as an A/B test that would only fire for 50% of donors who were in the process of completing a one-time gift under $100, reiterating the value of becoming a monthly donor, both in terms of impact and exclusive benefits, and asking them to a) make a recurring monthly donation of $20 or b) proceed with their one-time gift.
This is a follow-up experiment to a previous donation interruptor test in order to test whether less body copy, and thus a better mobile viewing experience, would lead to an increase in recurring donations.
This A/B test targeted only the member donation page (previous donors of $25 or more).
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that interrupting the donation process with a pop-up asking donors to become monthly supporters instead of making a one-time donation result in a decrease in donations for all traffic.
After running this experiment for 9 days, we saw a 64.6% increase in monthly giving with a 81.6% level of confidence. As a result, this experiment is invalid.
We also saw a 20.9% decrease in overall donations with a 99.9% level of confidence.
We believe that multiple factors contributed to producing this result:
Forcing donors to make another choice after submitting their one-time gift may produce friction. Asking donors to commit to a recurring gift in this case may be too strong of an ask and be perceived as pushy or invasive.Asking donors to join a Leadership Club may produce confusion at what membership entails, how it is different from "renewing" Heritage membership, and seem like too much of a commitment as opposed to a one-time gift to receive a Heritage 2023 member card.Results may be inaccurate due to the experiment recording all page viewers as part of the sample, even before they've seen either the control or donation interruptor experience. The sample size should be adjusted to apply only to those who have clicked the donation form submit button, excluding viewers who land on the page but navigate away before seeing either experience. This will isolate the experiment data only to those who donate or attempt to donate.
Based on these results and the data concerns, we will explore additional testing with added technical feature to record results only when the user clicks on the submit button and sees either experience at that step.

experiments.title: How a radical redesign impacted instant donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that highlighting the need for donors to step up for petition signers will achieve an increase in donor conversion because visitors will have more clarity and visibility into the needs of the organization.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/07/2023 - 06/25/2023
We were seeing consistently low donor conversion for petition signers and we wondered if calling attention to the low conversion would cause more people to stop and consider helping. Our hypothesis was that if all petition signers assume that CTF is receiving plenty of donations they might feel less compelled to give because they believe that someone else will fill in the gap. We rewrote the donation page to draw the visitor’s attention to the fact that only a small portion of petition signers care enough to take the next step and make an impact on the issue they care about from the petition. In the treatment, we also kept one element from previous testing that shares a testimonial to help persuade visitors to give monthly. We split traffic 50/50 for all petitions.
experiments.keyLearnings: After running the experiment for two weeks, the treatment resulted in a 54.8% increase in donations with a 100.0% level of confidence. Additionally, CTF received a significant increase in monthly donors on the treatment. We believe the increased callout to ask the donor to demonstrate if they truly believe in the issue they just signed the petition for by asking them to take the next step and make a donation would make them feel like they were taking a more exclusive action than the "average" petition signer. The treatment copy could have made the donors feel more trust in the organization because of the transparency about the status of the needs and low volume of donations, which may have come as surprising information to some visitors if they believed other petition signers were making gifts so there was not a true need for that individual to also make a gift of support. We also believe this increase could be partially due to the shorter length of copy that puts the donation form above the fold for visitors so they can see the next step more clearly without mistaking the test on the screen as a confirmation page. In future experimentation, CTF should continue to focus on creating a sense of urgency and emphasizing the importance of donor support for achieving its goals.

experiments.title: How reformatting a landing page survey impacts email sign ups
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that simplified formatting through a survey for survey participants will achieve an increase in email sign ups because participants will have an improved experience by being able to more easily read the survey questions and follow along with the survey.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/15/2023 - 06/24/2023
IPI’s Illinois Forward survey is producing a low 27.8% sign up rate. When reviewing the page and taking into account consumer feedback, we attributed the struggling metric to heavy friction through the survey formatting. The existing page includes yellow, all caps survey text that may be difficult for users to read, plus the survey questions are not numbered as surveys customarily are.
We wonder if reformatting the survey to use standard font color and style will make it easier to read, and numbering each survey question will make it easier to follow. You can see the noticeable differences in formatting via the screenshots below.
Let’s see how these simple, user-friendly adjustments impacted survey sign ups…
experiments.keyLearnings:
This test validated in favor of the treatment after running for just 6 days! The treatment produced a 14.1% lift in email conversion rate with a 99.6% level of confidence.
This is a prime example of reducing friction for the user by making their experience more user-friendly and through simplified styling/formatting. Just by making the survey questions more legible and easy to digest, we saw an immediate lift in email sign ups.
We have rolled the treatment out to 100% of traffic. We'll want to apply these changes to future surveys as well.

experiments.title: How a Pop-Up on the Journal Page Increased Follows
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a popup with value copy for readers on a user’s page, but who do not follow them will achieve an increase in follows because the copy helps impart the importance of staying updating and how they can offer support by following.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/29/2023 - 04/07/2023
CaringBridge is a platform where users can update friends and family about their health journey. In the journal view of a user’s page, CaringBridge wanted to capture users who were browsing the user’s entries but did not follow them.  We hypothesized that a simple popup with value copy about staying updating and informed on the user’s health journey would increase the number of follows.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding a pop-up with value copy about staying updated and informed on the user's health journey can significantly increase the number of follows on a user's page. The treatment group saw a 138.8% increase in Follow Site (Journal Pages) for All Traffic with a 100.0% statistical level of confidence. This improvement was seen across all visitor types, including desktop, mobile, tablet, new, returning, direct, email, organic search, and paid search visitors. However, visitors aged 45-54 had a 221% decrease in follow site (journal pages) with a 97% level of confidence.
In future experimentation, it is essential to consider the age group that did not respond positively to the pop-up and adjust the messaging accordingly. Additionally, testing different variations of the pop-up, such as other types of value copy or presenting it at various points during the user's journey on the platform, could reveal even more effective ways to increase follows. Overall, the results of this experiment demonstrate the value of reminding users, not only that they weren't already following, but also the important of how their follow supports the user.

experiments.title: How a multi-step donation process impacted donations for an acquisition offer
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a multi-step donation process broken down into three short steps instead of one long step for donation page visitors will achieve an increase in donations because donors will appreciate the easy flow of the multi-step donation process and will encounter less friction as they complete their donation one simple step at a time.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/26/2023 - 06/09/2023
ACI’s donation platform now has the functionality to set up a multiple-step donation process. In this instance, the standard one-step process would be divided into three steps, one to select the donation amount, one to provide contact/billing information, and one to add credit card information. We want to test the one-step vs. the three-step donation process for a popular content offer to see how donors respond. Would three shorter steps lead to more donation conversions than one long step?
We have set up this experiment for their Cancel Culture Pledge, and traffic will be split 50/50 between the one-step and multi-step process for the duration of the experiment. Let’s see how donors respond.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Donors did not respond to the multi-step donation process as we hypothesized. In fact, the treatment validated with a 51.9% decrease in donations after running for only 14 days. The treatment also produced a valid 72.3% decrease in revenue.
Possible explanations for the negative results could be that most donors are accustomed to the standard one-step donation process and this multi-step process led to increased anxiety and possibly confusion. There is also the possibility that the three-step process invited more friction instead of less, as we hypothesized. Instead of having all the necessary information directly in front of them, donors encountered an uncertain number of steps and also more opportunities to flee the donation opportunity. Perhaps adding clarity to the three-step process would improve the donor experience and save conversions. This could be communicated by noting that this is step 1 of 2 or 2 of 3 as they go through the process.
Some tweaks are clearly needed if we want to try testing this multi-step process once more with our audience.

WUNC is a North Carolina Public Radio Station. To convert web traffic to email subscribers, we tested a slide out with fields to sign up for the weekly What’s Trending Newsletter vs. an ebook offer.
WUNC has struggled in converting web traffic into email subscribers, in a previous test we experimented with presenting a newsletter sign up via a slide-in and saw a 395% increase in emails acquired https://app.winstonknows.com/experiment/6001

The key learnings from this experiment are that offering an ebook is a more effective way to acquire email subscribers for WUNC. In future experimentation, WUNC should continue offering an ebook in their email sign-up prompts and test variations of the newsletter content to optimize email acquisition. Additionally, testing different types of incentives to encourage sign-ups could be a useful experiment to run.

experiments.title: How showing a recurring pop-up to users who are about to make a one-time donation affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a recurring pop-up to the instant donation page for petition signers will achieve a higher recurring donor conversion rate because the donor will be able to make more of an impact with a monthly gift at potentially a lower cost.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/28/2023 - 05/21/2023
MSLF wanted to increase its amount of recurring donors. To test into increasing recurring donors coming in from petitions, they decided to add a recurring pop-up on the donation page once someone hits “donate now” at the bottom of the giving form.
This recurring pop-up calculated the new recurring ask based on how much their one-time gift was:
It will check if the one-time amount they give is less than $1000 then take 60% of the original amount rounded to the nearest $5.
If that is less than $20 then it will set it to $20.
If it’s more than $100, then it will set it to $100.
After they ran this experiment for one month, we found that the pop-up increased recurring giving by .13% with a 84.1% level of confidence. But we also found that it decreased one-time giving by 37% with an 87% level of confidence.
Due to the average gift in that experiment being $26.89, we believed the calculations of the pop-up caused the recurring ask to not be much less or the same as their original one-time gift which deterred them from giving at all.
We decided to retest this recurring pop-up with different calculations to see if the results would change:
Anybody who gave $100 or less, ask for a recurring gift. If they gave more than $100, do not show the pop-up.
$20 – $100 = 60% of what their gift was
$20 or less = Just ask for a recurring gift of whatever their gift amount was
We believe these calculations will better reflect what a recurring gift would look like in comparison to what a donor would give one-time.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The recurring pop-up caused one one-time donor to convert their one-time gift to a monthly gift. There were no recurring donors from the control donation page.

Unfortunately, the recurring pop-up caused a 43.8% decrease in the overall conversion rate with a 96% level of confidence.

We believe this means that most people who were about to make a one-time donation left the page when presented with the option to turn their one-time gift into a recurring gift. The recurring pop-up added friction to the donation process.

experiments.title: How increasing the perceived security and privacy of a donor’s payment information impacts conversion at point of transaction
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that increasing the perceived security and privacy for donors will achieve a positive lift in donor conversion because online security causes anxiety and friction to the giving process.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 05/26/2023
We know that online security and privacy is highly important to people, especially when it comes to entering sensitive data like payment information online. But how important is online security and privacy really and what causes the greatest anxiety and friction around this topic in the giving process? To test into this, we developed an experiment on Save the Children’s main donation page that communicated how seriously we take data security.  Specifically the message said, “We take data and privacy seriously. Your payment information is secure and will be used only to process your gift.” This message was placed just under the donate button.
Additionally, in order to draw attention to the security statement below the donate button, we animated a lock icon to catch the eye of potential donors.
For this experiment, we split traffic evenly between the treatment and the control and ran it for a total of 33 days.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment that displayed a security statement and an animated lock icon resulted in an 11.1% increase in donations with a high level of statistical confidence. The key learning from this experiment is that donors DO have anxiety and friction when giving; even at the point of transacting, but when we can clearly communicate to them that their privacy and security is not only important to us, but tell them exactly what we will do with their payment information, we can remove some of that perceived cost in the donor's mind and increase their likelihood to give.
Other notable data points from the experiment were:
Overall mobile donor conversion was higher for the treatment (+16%) at a statistically valid LoC (95%).Mobile recurring donor conversion was higher for the treatment (+56%) at a statistically valid LoC (99%). Mobile recurring revenue per visitor was higher for the variant (+83%) at a statistically valid LoC (99.8%).
As a whole, we believe that mobile users saw such a significant increase in conversion (more so than desktop) because the animated lock icon drew people's attention to the security statement. This animation and statement on mobile devices is much more prominent resulting in more people seeing it and ultimately converting at a higher rate.
Additionally, the dramatic lift in conversion and revenue for recurring donations only elevates the very real anxiety that people have around data security. For people considering making an ongoing gift to the organization, the weight of that anxiety is heavier as they consider and question what happens with their payment information every month as it gets charged for their recurring gift commitment.
This experiment resulted in an estimated revenue impact over seven figures for recurring donors.
Future experimentation could involve testing different variations of security statements and icons to find the most effective combination. Additionally, it may be worthwhile to test the impact of security messaging on different types of donors or donation amounts to see if the effect is consistent across different segments.

experiments.title: How adding a humanized sticky bar to a campaign donation page affects donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a humanized sticky bar to a campaign donation page for prospective donors will achieve a higher donor conversion rate.

MAF was running a high-urgency campaign for Giving Tuesday. Due to the previous testing of this concept in other high-urgency campaigns, we hypothesized that adding a sticky bar on the donation page itself reiterates the goal and would increase donor conversion. However, we also wanted to humanize this sticky bar by using Aaron Bear’s (CAO and Vice President) image and name so that it seems like it’s a message from Aaron himself. The added layer of using an authority figure within the organization is expected to increase our conversion rate as well.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Overall, we saw a 49.8% lift in donor conversion rate with a 99% level of confidence when adding the humanized sticky bar to the campaign donation page. As hypothesized, we believe that adding a humanized sticky bar to a campaign donation page reiterates the goal of the campaign and personalizes the ask since we are using the face of someone that works at MAF.

Moving forward, we will rollout a humanized sticky bar to all campaign donation pages.

experiments.title: How asking for personal information before payment information on a donation page affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that asking for personal information first for potential donors will achieve a higher donor conversion rate because asking for personal information demonstrates a genuine interest in establishing a relationship beyond just receiving financial contributions, which can build trust and credibility with potential donors.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/12/2023 - 04/20/2023
Prison Fellowship International wanted to test asking for the donor’s personal information first on the donation page rather than asking for the credit card information first.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that asking for personal information first on the donation page led to a significant decrease in donations for Prison Fellowship International. The control group, which had the credit card information form first, performed better than the treatment group by 64.3% with 99.5% statistical confidence. Furthermore, the treatment group had decreased revenue and donations across all types of visitors, such as returning, direct, and organic search visitors, as well as mobile and desktop visitors.

We believe the treatment page performed worse because it was easy for people to provide their personal information first, but by the time they got to the credit card information, they had too much time to think about the process which caused them to bounce from the page. By asking for the credit card information first, you're asking them to get the hard details out of the way and by the time they hit the donate button, it is less transactional.

Although we can pull assumptions from this experiment, we recommend testing this concept again since it only received 19 gifts.

experiments.title: How removing images impacted donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by removing images for main donation page visitors will achieve a higher donor conversion rate because the content of the page is easier to read and understand.

Ended On: 05/05/2023
Avila is an organization that aims to draw Christians worldwide into deeper union with Christ through mystagogically oriented spiritual education and formation.
In an effort to increase donor conversions on the Spiritual Direction donation page, we decided to remove the additional images associated with each giving level.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The A/B experiment conducted by Avila Foundation aimed to increase donor conversions on the Spiritual Direction donation page by removing the additional images associated with each giving level. However, the treatment group witnessed a 60.4% decrease in donations, which indicates that the control group worked better. Therefore, the hypothesis failed, and the key learning from this experiment is that images play a crucial role in donor conversion.
In future experimentation, Avila Foundation should focus on finding better images and ensure that they complement the content of the page. They should conduct more surveys and collect feedback from donors to understand their preferences and expectations better. Also, they should focus on A/B testing with small changes and daily iterations instead of significant changes, which may lead to adverse outcomes. Additionally, they should consider the seasonality, demographics, and location of donors while designing experiments to make them more relevant and personalized. Overall, the experiment highlights the significance of testing and experimentation in driving data-driven decision-making and improving donor conversion rates.

experiments.title: How a monthly pop up during a donation process impacts recurring gifts
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that interrupting the giving experience for those who give one time gifts of $200 or less will achieve an increase in monthly gifts because we are giving the user an opportunity to read through the value proposition for a monthly gift before making a final decision .
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/30/2023 - 04/16/2023
IMB has a multistep donation process within a widget. In an effort to increase monthly donations, we wanted to give people the opportunity to switch their one-time gift to a monthly gift. Our hypothesis is that if we give people the opportunity to switch their gift frequency before they submit their gift, we will see an increase in monthly gifts. The chart of standards used for this experiment is as follows:
If the one-time gift is $25 or less, the monthly ask is $5
If the one time gift is $26 – 50, the monthly ask is $10
If $50 – $100 , then $20 monthly ask
If $101 – $200 , then $25 monthly ask
If the gift is more than $200 then the pop up would not show up.
We ran this experiment on the main donation page which regularly receives a lot of organic traffic.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that interrupting the giving experience for those who give one-time gifts of $200 or less with a pop-up prompting them to switch to monthly donations can potentially increase monthly donations. However, the results cannot be considered valid due to the statistical level of confidence being below 85%. This experiment resulted in a 25% increase in monthly donations with a level of confidence of 33%. We did not generate enough monthly gifts to validate the results of this experiment.

The donor conversion rate for all donations, however, did see a valid increase. It's unclear as to why this is the case considering that the pop up did not show up until the very last step of the giving process. The next iteration of this experiment will move the pop up after the first step of the giving experience as opposed to waiting until the end. That way, the interruption is done right after they have selected "one-time" as their gift frequency.

experiments.title: How a donation interruptor including a monthly upgrade ask affects recurring donation rates
experiments.researchQuestion:
We believe that
presenting a monthly upgrade ask for one-time donors during the donation process will achieve higher recurring donation rates by increasing its visibility at the decision-making point and prominently communicating its value.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/16/2023 - 01/30/2023
In an effort to increase recurring donor acquisition for a public policy organization during a recent high-urgency campaign, we wanted to measure the effect of interrupting the donation process with a pop-up asking the donor to become a monthly supporter instead of making only a one-time gift.
This pop-up was set up as an A/B test that would only fire for 50% of donors who were in the process of completing a one-time gift under $100, reiterating the value of becoming a monthly donor, both in terms of impact and exclusive benefits, and asking them to a) make a recurring monthly donation of $20 or b) proceed with their one-time gift.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running this experiment for approximately 2 weeks, we observed a  93% increase in monthly giving with a 99.2% level of confidence.
We believe that several motivating factors came into play in producing this result:
First, the interrupter pop-up dramatically increased the visibility of the recurring-giving option. Second, it clearly and thoroughly laid out the value of becoming a recurring donor in terms of impact and exclusive benefitsThird, by creating a mental interruption, it may have given donors an opportunity to reset the mental process of completing their donation (transaction forms being as prevalent as they are in the digital space may cue a certain degree of auto-pilot during the transaction process) and allowed them the time to consider their options.And finally, the pop-up treatment positioned monthly giving as an opportunity rather than an ask, giving agency to the donor and casting them in the role of someone who is able to do (and get) more than they would by making a one-time gift.
And while at first glance it appears that the treatment produced a 12.6% decrease in donations and a 16.4% decrease in revenue, upon further examination it appears unlikely that these dips were caused by the pop-up since viewers technically "enter" the experiment upon page-load and the pop-up does not appear until they have already made the decision to give. Additionally, language in the headline of the pop-up indicates to the donor that their donation has not yet been processed to reduce instances of closing the browser window before the donation process is complete.
This leads us to believe that any decrease in donations and/or revenue is more likely a result of the visitor deciding to give when viewing the page, rather than a result of the pop-up itself.
Based on these results, we will explore additional opportunities to globalize this learning.

experiments.title: How adding new video creative to an existing offer affected donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that creating a new video for YouTube viewers will achieve a higher donor conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/21/2023
At Hoover, we had recently been testing video ads with a voiceover with some success. Some of our older ads did not have a voiceover and the cost to acquire a donor with those ads were increasing.  We wanted to test a creative refresh (that included a voiceover) and relaunch the campaign to compare performance side by side.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that creating a new video with a voiceover for YouTube viewers significantly increased the donor conversion rate. The treatment group, which received the new video, saw a 349.7% increase in donations compared to the control group. This result has a 99.7% statistical level of confidence, indicating a high degree of certainty.
Moving forward, it is important to implement the use of voiceovers in our video ads for better donor conversion rates. This experiment demonstrates that the absence of a voiceover in the previous ads was negatively impacting the cost to acquire a donor. By utilizing voiceovers, we can potentially lower the cost per acquisition and attract more donors.
Future experimentation should further explore different elements of video ads, such as the tone and messaging, to optimize donor conversion rates. It is also essential to consistently monitor and analyze performance metrics to identify any further improvements that can be made. By continuously testing and iterating on our video ad strategies, we can maximize our effectiveness in acquiring donors and furthering the mission of the Hoover Institution.

experiments.title: How a Facebook ad radical redesign impacted clicks
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by offering to pray for Facebook viewers will achieve higher click through rate because it is a tangible thing that people may be looking for .
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/20/2023
First Baptist Dallas is a church located in the heart of downtown Dallas, Texas. Each Sunday morning First Dallas uses Facebook ads to share live now ads to invite Facebook ad viewers to join the church on their online campus named iCampus.
Over the last few weeks, Facebook’s ad performance has been declining. In an effort to increase performance, we decided to do a radical experiment by testing the traditional ad with copy about the message and sermon series vs asking if they have a prayer need.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The experiment conducted by First Baptist Dallas involved testing two different types of Facebook ads: one that offered to pray for viewers and another that advertised the message and sermon series. The hypothesis was that the ad offering prayer would achieve a higher click-through rate. However, the experiment's results showed a 15.1% decrease in clicks for all traffic in the treatment group compared to the control group.
The key learning from this experiment is that offering to pray for viewers may not be an effective strategy for increasing click-through rates for Facebook ads. However, we saw increased prayer requests received and interaction with the iCampus site. So while the Prayer ad had a lower click conversion rate, we did see greater engagement through it.

experiments.title: Will a HTML designed email achieve a higher click rate than a plain-text email
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that plain-text emails will achieve a higher click rate than a html design (newsletter style) email.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/09/2023 - 03/15/2023
In effort to test new and valuable content to our active subscribers, we decided to send an email digest of recent experiments we’ve tested to our house file. Since we have over 4,800+ experiments in our library, sometimes it’s easy to lose track of the current experiments.
Since this was a new concept, we decided to test two variants of emails.
Each email contained the same content but one was a html design and the other was a plain-text design. To add context, we normally send plain-text emails to our house file.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Key learnings from this experiment include:
1. Plain-text emails performed better in terms of clicks compared to html design newsletters.
2. Plain-text emails also had a higher open rate and click-to-open rate.
3. The experiment results had a high level of statistical confidence.
Moving forward, we should consider sending more plain-text emails to our house file as it seems to be a more effective approach for engaging our subscribers. We should also continue to test different email formats to see if there are any other changes we can make that will improve our email engagement metrics. Ultimately, we need to prioritize the preferences of our audience and find the best ways to communicate with them effectively.

experiments.title: How adding value proposition language to a direct appeal email impacted donations.
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a value proposition-enhanced email for email recipients will achieve an increase in donations.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/28/2022 - 12/30/2022
As part of a high-urgency campaign for a public policy client, we emailed subscribers to their weekly newsletter, The Policy Shop, asking if they would make a donation in support of The Policy Shop. The email was short and sweet, including a cartoon, a thanks for being a part of The Policy Shop, and an ask for support.
We wondered how a more in-depth, value proposition-focused email would impact donations. The treatment version highlights the problem(s) that The Policy Shop helps solve, provides background on The Policy Shop, provides evidentials on the impact of The Policy Shop, and tells the recipients how their gift in support of The Policy Shop will be used. The treatment also takes a more personal approach by greeting the recipient by name and including a raw donation link instead of a button and graphic.
experiments.keyLearnings: The treatment resulted in a valid 1.3% increase in donations, with six coming from the treatment and none coming from the control. Although the treatment produced fewer clicks, it did produce a far better donor conversion rate, which is more valuable. This proves that the value proposition-heavy email was more valuable. Those who clicked the treatment email were more aware of the impact The Policy Shop has and why it needs support to continue to have such an impact. They had greater clarity on why The Policy Shop is so important. This clarity and motivation gained from the value proposition in the treatment could not be gained from the control. The experiment stresses the importance of value proposition copy and proves that value proposition should be a strong focus in emails going forward.

experiments.title: How a radical redesign impacted Facebook ad clicks
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by asking for prayer requests for the Facebook audience will achieve higher ad clicks because it addresses a felt need of the viewer.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/13/2023
First Baptist Dallas is a church located in the heart of downtown Dallas, Texas. Each Sunday morning First Dallas uses Facebook ads to share live now ads to invite Facebook ad viewers to join the church on their online campus named iCampus.
Over the last few weeks, Facebook’s ad performance has been declining. In an effort to increase performance, we decided to do a radical experiment by testing the traditional ad with copy about the message and sermon series vs asking if they have a prayer need.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The A/B experiment conducted by First Baptist Dallas showed that the traditional ad with copy about the message and sermon series outperformed the ad that asked for prayer requests in terms of clicks for all traffic. However, the results did not reach statistical significance due to a lack of minimum sample size.
The key learning from this experiment is that addressing the felt need of the viewer may not necessarily result in higher ad clicks. It is important to continue testing and experimenting with different ad copy and messaging to determine what resonates best with the target audience. Future experimentation should also focus on achieving a larger sample size to increase statistical significance in the results. Additionally, it may be beneficial to consider other factors such as timing and targeting in the ads to further improve performance.

experiments.title: How images and video of a personality impacted clicks on an eBook ad
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using images and videos of a well-known figure for Facebook ad viewers will achieve an increase in clickthrough rate because this introduces more credibility and authority than using an eBook image alone.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/02/2023
When running ads for an eBook offer, we wondered if the cover of the eBook was appealing enough to drive interest from Facebook ad viewers. In previous testing on other offers, we had found success in using the organization’s founder’s name and photo. We wondered if we could achieve similar success by featuring images and videos in the ads that were relevant to the eBook’s contents. The first treatment had a background of an American flag to serve as a “trigger image” that can help with familiarity as users scroll. The second treatment was a 30-second video quoting Martin Luther King, Jr. and the third treatment was a 30-second video urging the American people to work together and not treat each other as enemies. We launched all versions of the ad and let them run for 30 days.
experiments.keyLearnings: While we saw valid decreases in clickthrough rate for the first and second treatments, the third treatment that included a 30-second video urging the American people to work together and not treat each other as enemies achieved a 44.0% increase in clicks for all traffic with a 100.0% statistical level of confidence. We believe this video saw a lift in engagement and effectiveness due to the relevancy of the messaging that gave a sneak peek into how the topic of the eBook was discussed. The video that produced the lift also had an enhanced sense of urgency compared to the other treatments that could have positively impacted performance. Future experimentation should focus on incorporating relevant and credible images and videos in the ad campaigns, accompanied by concise and compelling messages that resonate with the target audience.

When creating future experiments, we should use this success as proof that implementing two donate buttons with clear and direct language can be effective in increasing donor conversions. Furthermore, we should pay special attention to the messaging associated with each donate button, as this can play a large role in converting visitors into donors.

experiments.title: How condensing copy on a pledge impacted email acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that reducing the length of copy for pledge signers will achieve an increase in email acquisition because they have already been “pitched” the intro copy in the email or ad they clicked over from.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 02/01/2023
When analyzing an email acquisition page for opportunities to improve conversion, we wondered if the length of copy introducing the statement of belief was dissuading visitors from making it all the way through to the email capture portion of the page. We hypothesized that most visitors to this page had enough information about why it was important that they add their name to the statement of belief based on the amount of clarifying copy in the ads and emails that were sending traffic to this page. We wondered if we were getting in the way and needed to reduce the time required to consume introductory copy before getting the visitor to the beginning of the statement of belief for signing.
experiments.keyLearnings: After running the experiment for 30 days, we saw email conversion rate decrease by 8% on the condensed copy treatment with 99% level of confidence which leads us to believe the additional copy was not a problem or interference for visitors arriving to the page. However, we still think the control conversion rate has room for improvement. In future testing, we will isolate the truncated version specifically for mobile visitors so we can reduce the amount of scrolling time required in the user experience to make it all the way through the statement of belief.

experiments.title: How including an image of the non-profit leader impacts donations in email prospecting
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding an image of the non-profit leader will increase donations by building credibility, intrigue, and interest with the audience.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/20/2023
We referenced the following experiment for a public policy client and utilized it for a faith-based client. Since the results of the past experience were affirmative, we wanted to test the same experiment for a different client. https://app.winstonknows.com/experiment/4973
In an effort to see a lift in donations in email prospecting for a faith-based client, we wanted to measure the effect of adding credibility and interest to the offer by means of utilizing an image of the non-profit leader.
The offer deployed a daily devotional direct appeal. The control version used the cover image of the daily devotional, while the treatment version utilized the image of the non-profit leader.
This test was set up in which ~50% of the audience would get the control version and ~50% of the audience would get the treatment version. However, once results were clear to email brokers they only deployed the winning version.
experiments.keyLearnings:
With a 91% decrease in donations (with a 99.9% level of confidence) on the treatment creative, we are able to conclude with clarifying results that our experiment hypothesis for this particular client did not match the results of a similar experiment for a different client. We believe that adding an image of the non-profit leader will increase donations by building credibility, intrigue, and interest with the audience.
The execution of the past experiment was quite similar to this one: We replaced the image of a book/offer premium with the image of the non-profit leader. However, the results were quite different.
It is of importance to note that for this particular client, all other channels tested out of utilizing the image of the leader’s image. We can conclude that the channel email prospecting is no different.

experiments.keyLearnings: This experiment resulted in a valid decrease in donor conversion rate. We saw a 55% decrease in donor conversion rate with a level of confidence of 99%. This was an unexpected result given the previous experiment's results. Our hypothesis is that making the treatment page on the new platform may not look similar enough to the organization's webpages. We will re-run this test on another offer and design the page to more closely match the aesthetics of the control.

experiments.title: How adding a sticky bar to a year-end fundraising campaign donation page affects donation rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a humanized sticky bar to the donation page of a year-end fundraising campaign will increase donation rates by humanizing and further conveying the value of the appeal.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/21/2022 - 01/04/2023
During a year-end fundraising campaign for a faith-based education organization, we wanted to test how adding a humanized sticky bar to the donation page would affect donation rates.
We set this experiment up as a redirect test with ~50% of traffic seeing the standard donation page, sans sticky bar, and the other half seeing a humanized sticky bar from one of the organization’s staff members (including a photo) making a personal appeal and expressing the value of the prospect’s donation.
We ran this test for 10 days, during the last stretch of the fundraising campaign.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running this experiment for 10 days, the treatment featuring a humanized sticky bar achieved a 14.5% increase in donation rates with an 87.1% statistical level of confidence when compared to the control.
In addition, the treatment including the sticky bar increased revenue per visitor by 151% with a 99% level of confidence, indicating that not only did the sticky bar encourage more people to give, it inspired them to give more generously.
We believe that including a sticky bar featuring a staff member of the organization was able to increase donations among page visitors by humanizing and putting a face to the appeal, reminding people that this organization is comprised of real people, just like them, who are trying to make a difference.
And even though we did not receive a large enough sample size to statistically validate these results, we did observe a directional lift that indicates there is low risk associated with implementing this treatment and we therefore feel confident in recommending the inclusion of a humanized sticky bar to 100% of the traffic during their next high-urgency campaign.

experiments.title: How adding a dollar goal to a sticky bar impacts giving
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding the monetary goal in a sticky bar for site visitors will achieve a higher donor conversion rate .
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/15/2022 - 12/31/2022
During a high urgency campaign, RTB ran a sitewide sticky bar that prompted people to give towards their campaign. Mid-campaign the team noted that the stickybar did not state the dollar goal amount and adding this goal may motivate more people to contribute. In order to test this hypothesis we created an experiment where the control (the original sticky bar) did not have the goal amount and the treatment stated the goal amount for the site visitor.
experiments.keyLearnings: This experiment resulted in a valid 32% decrease in donor conversion rate with a level of confidence of 96%. This means that people were less likely to give when given the dollar amount in the sticky bar. This could be because this makes the experience more transactional for the site visitor. Additionally, it could be that the dollar amount was too large for the prospective donor and it made them feel like it was unattainable (and that their donation would not have an impact).

experiments.title: How using a humanized sticky bar during a high-urgency campaign impacts donation conversion rates.

rows.name: Sticky Bar - Humanized with Dan's Photo

experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a sticky bar for website visitors will achieve a change in donor conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/20/2022 - 01/04/2023
During Avila Institute’s year-end campaign, we wanted to capitalize on traffic to one of their most popular websites, SpiritualDirection.com, and run a 3-way experiment to see whether a sticky bar made an impact on donations.
For this experiment, our control was no sticky bar; variant one had a sticky bar with just copy; variant 2 had a sticky bar with a picture of Dan Burke, the founder of Avila. Both variant 1 and 2 had the exact same copy. We ran this sticky bar on all pages on the website.
experiments.keyLearnings:
While we expected a sticky bar to outperform no sticky bar, the results of adding in Dan's image were really interesting. The Dan image version of the stick bar had a 92% increase in conversion rates compared to both the control and variant 1. We believe that this is due to Dan's notoriety within the Avila community; everyone knows who he is, what he looks like, and by adding an image we humanized the message a little more, giving donors the perception that it was really Dan asking for urgent help during the campaign.
Notable changes to other metrics due to the experiment:
Revenue experienced a 89.4% increase with a 94% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How Adding a “Start My Gift” Button to the Top of a Year-End Donation Page impacts donor conversion for mobile traffic
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a “start my gift” button at the top of the year-end donation page will increase donor conversion by decreasing donor friction
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/02/2022 - 12/18/2022
FamilyLife is a Christian ministry serving families worldwide. For their calendar year-end campaign, they wanted to test ways of improving donor conversion, particularly for mobile traffic which has typically had a much lower conversion rate on their site. We hypothesized that the length of the copy on the page might be adding friction to motivated traffic and wanted to see if we could reduce that friction by providing a button that would invite visitors to “start my gift” right at the top of the page. Clicking the button would drop the visitor directly to the donation form.
We ran the experiment on their campaign donation page for almost two weeks.
experiments.keyLearnings:
While we did not achieve a greater than 95% level of confidence for all traffic, mobile validated a lift and all other primary channels showed a strong positive impact with a directional level of confidence. What was surprising was that this button also increased revenue. We excluded any gifts over $750 that would skew the results and still achieved a 237% increase in revenue from the treatment with a 94% level of confidence. We will need to do additional testing to dig into why this impacted revenue so strongly but we will use this button for the remainder of the campaign.
Notable changes to other metrics due to the experiment:
Observations of the impact on other visitor segments in the experiment:
Direct visitors had a 30.6% increase in donations with a 91% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How a radically redesigned donation page for monthly partnership impacted donations.
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that conducting a radical redesign on a monthly donor membership donation page for potential donors will achieve an increase in instant donor conversion rate because potential donors will gain clarity on the benefits of joining the membership club and will have a cleaner, more user-friendly page engagement.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/03/2022 - 12/05/2022
A public policy client has a special membership club for monthly donors. Someone can join this club when making a monthly donation of at least $10, and from there they’ll receive special perks and have an elevated impact on the organization and its cause. The donation page where someone can make a monthly gift to join the club is resulting in a low instant conversion rate. Considering the amount of traffic that reaches the page, and after brainstorming several potential ways to improve conversion, we’ve opted for a radical redesign test to determine its impact on the instant donor conversion rate.
We will test these elements:
Expanding the value proposition of the headline
Moving the inline quote outside of the main copy and into a quote box on the side
Calling out the specific impact of making a monthly gift via bullet points
Moving the (501(c)3) tax language to the bottom of the page below the giving button
Setting the default gift array amount to $10
Changing “Donor Information” to “Your Information” in the giving form
Removing an email subscription opportunity from above the giving button
Removing only on mobile the social proof slide in informing the page visitor that [someone] just joined the membership club
We want to determine if this combination of changes will improve the page/donation experience for the visitor, provide additional clarity on the benefits of joining the membership club, and ultimately increase donor conversions.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The results are in. The treatment (radical redesign) showed a valid 76.5% DECREASE in instant donor conversion rate. The control variant produced 8 conversions to the treatment's 2.
Our swing for the fences resulted in a whiff. The results were negative across segments. Unfortunately, a radical redesign cannot tell us how each change influenced the user experience and donor conversion rate overall. To simplify this test and make more sense of the results, our next test should isolate one or two variants. We may want to go down the list of changes so we can truly determine which change(s) turned potential donors off.

experiments.title: How a radical redesign impacted donations
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by implementing various winning elements for website traffic will achieve an increase in response.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/23/2022 - 12/03/2022
Pathway to Victory is a broadcast ministry that does its primary fundraising through monthly featured premiums.
For Giving Tuesday we decided to do a “radical redesign” to the landing page.
For the treatment, we implemented multiple elements that had won on their own in other scenarios.
These changes included:
Remove Dr. Jeffress’ images
Leading with a solution rather than the match
A conclusion that reinforces how the donor’s gift makes the solution possible
Removed friction of “Important” instructions
And changed the layout of items to be linear.
experiments.keyLearnings:
By removing friction points, clarifying the solution of the gift and making the experience single column, we observed a 112.5% increase in revenue with a 99% level of confidence.
Each of the elements changed in the treatment version had been previously tested on web, email or Facebook for Pathway to Victory and when put together we saw the dramatic increase in conversions.
With these learnings and results from Giving Tuesday, we will be able to implement this approach for year end.

experiments.title: How adding a humanized sticky bar to an instant donation page affects donation rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that including a humanized sticky bar featuring a well-known public figure on an instant donation page will increase donation rates by adding value and credibility to the appeal.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/01/2022 - 12/06/2022
For a content offer for a public policy client’s pledge, we wanted to know how adding a humanized sticky bar including the image of a well-known public figure on a donation page would affect donation rates.
This experiment ran as an A/B test with roughly 50% of traffic being shown the treatment including the sticky bar and roughly 50% of traffic shown the control, sans the sticky bar. No other elements were changed.
Because of the large volume of traffic the donation page received, we were able to validate the results of this experiment after only 2 days.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running this experiment for 48 hours, we observed a 38.6% INCREASE in donation rates, with a 96.9% level of confidence, among traffic who were shown the treatment including the humanized sticky bar, validating our hypothesis that a humanized sticky bar would increase donation rates.
We believe that the increase in donations occurred because the large and colorful sticky bar, featuring a well-known and well-respected public figure among this organization's prospective donors, increased the visibility of the perceived impact for potential donors.
The sticky bar also invites the user to "join" this public figure in achieving the outcome they want to achieve, while additional creating a message match from the pledge page seen before the donation page, as the pledge referred directly to the four values being presented in the sticky bar, creating momentum toward the conversion and leveraging the persuasion principle of "consistency" to inspire the user to follow up on their pledge by taking action.
It is also worth noting that the treatment including the sticky bar produced a ~15% higher average gift, perhaps because the additional value statement shown in the sticky bar increased the perceived value of a larger donation in the minds of donors.
Because of the positive results of this experiment, we will look for other opportunities to include a sticky bar on instant donation pages in future acquisition efforts.

experiments.title: How including an image of a well-known personality affects click thru rate in email prospecting
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding an image of a well-known personality will increase click thru rate by building credibility, intrigue, and interest with the audience.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 12/05/2022
In an effort to increase click thru rate in email prospecting for a public policy client, we wanted to measure the effect of adding credibility and interest to the offer by means of utilizing an image of a well known personality.
The offer deployed was a children’s book premium offer. The control version used the image of the children’s book, while the treatment version utilized the image of the well-known personality who wrote the book.
This test was set up in which ~50% of the audience would get the control version and ~50% of the audience would get the treatment version. However, once results were clear to email brokers they only deployed the winning version.
experiments.keyLearnings:
With a 61.5% increase in click through rate (with a 99.9% level of confidence) on the treatment creative, we were pleased to conclude with clarifying results the validity of our hypothesis. By adding an image of a well-known personality, the image will serve to build credibility, intrigue, and interest with the audience.
Additionally, while not statistically significant, but still significant to note, the relative difference in donors with increased click thru rate was 76.2%.
The control version received 4 donations while the treatment variant received 28 donors—a 76.2% increase. While the level of confidence was only 79.5% and did not meet the 95% threshold to validate, we still believe these results are still of importance to note.
These results are a positive indication that similar tests should be run for other public policy and faith-based clients that can utilize well-known personalities for marketing purposes.

experiments.title: How using a ‘donation interruptor’ affects recurring giving rates during a Giving Tuesday high-urgency campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that interrupting the donation process with a pop-up for donors on Giving Tuesday will improve recurring gift conversion rates by increasing its visibility at the decision-making point and prominently communicating its value.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/28/2022 - 12/03/2022
In an effort to increase recurring donor acquisition for a faith-based client during a Giving Tuesday campaign, we wanted to measure the effect of interrupting the donation process with a pop-up asking the donor to become a monthly supporter instead of making only a one-time gift.
This pop-up was set up as an A/B test that would only fire for 50% of donors who were in the process of completing a one-time gift under $100, reiterating the value of becoming a monthly donor both in terms of impact and exclusive benefits.
We tested this same hypothesis with the same client during last year’s Giving Tuesday campaign, and while we saw an encouraging lift in recurring giving, we were unable to validate our results—so we were eager to see how the same concept would perform in a similar setting during this year’s campaign.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Due to an increase in the number of donors (sample size) over last year's campaign, we were pleased to be able to pull a valid learning from this year's experiment—with our treatment producing a statistically valid 137% increase in recurring gifts with a 98.4% level of confidence.
We believe that several motivating factors came into play in producing this result:
First, the interrupter pop-up dramatically increased the visibility of the recurring-giving option. Second, it clearly and thoroughly laid out the value of becoming a recurring donor in terms of impact and exclusive benefitsThird, by creating a mental interruption, it may have given donors an opportunity to reset the mental process of completing their donation (transaction forms being as prevalent as they are in the digital space may cue a certain degree of auto-pilot during the transaction process) and allowed them the time to consider their options.And finally, the pop-up treatment positioned monthly giving as an opportunity rather than an ask, giving agency to the donor and casting them in the role of someone who is able to do (and get) more than they would by making a one-time gift.
We also observed that the treatment produced a statistically valid increase of 210% in recurring giving among donors using mobile devices, important as we continue to see an increase in mobile giving over time.
These results are a positive indication that a similar treatment may work for other clients in the context of high-urgency campaigns and we will seek to globalize this learning by implementing it for new audiences and new giving contexts, such as content offers or direct appeals.

experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a journal entry step for people registering for a new site will achieve an increase in used sites.

CaringBridge was seeking to get more people to start sites, which keep them connected to loved ones through difficult health journeys. However, they did not just want people to start sites, they wanted them to write their first journal, which constitutes a “used site” and leads to more downstream engagement.
They decided to test placing a “write your first journal” prompt into the process of signing up for a new site, with an optional “skip this step” to get more people to write a journal.
They only tested this with 10% of the traffic to mitigate risk.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment, despite the small sample size, delivered a valid 70.3% increase in "used sites", or sites with a first journal entry. Previously, the "write your first journal" step had been at the very end of the process, and far fewer people had actually taken the final step.
Interestingly, adding this "friction" into the process also

experiments.title: How adding a book as a premium offer increases the average gift on an eBook instant donation page.
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a premium for ebook donors will achieve a higher average gift.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/11/2022 - 10/12/2022
The Avila Foundation has created a website, SpiritualDirection.com, where anyone can go and find answers to questions they might have about their spiritual journey. As part of the efforts to educate the laity through authentic Catholic teachings, Avila has created a series of ebooklets.
For one of these ebooklets, Insights on the Contemplative Rosary, we wanted to test whether adding a premium offer, in this case a physical copy of the book upon which the ebooklet was based, would help increase the average gift. Our hypothesis was that the combination of the two–using the ebooklet as a study guide to the book–would entice donors to contribute more to Avila.
experiments.keyLearnings:
While there was a slight decline in the total number of donations while we ran this experiment (38 to 37), the average gift on the premium treatment page increased by 58%, which also resulted in a 58% increase in total revenue with a 94% level of confidence.
We believe that by positioning the ebooklet as a companion piece to the book, those donors who were brand new, or had not yet gotten a chance to get the book, were convinced to give a gift of a higher amount.
While the average gift for the treatment version was below the $50 we were asking in order to receive a copy of the book, there was enough data to suggest that further refinement of this offer, as well as further testing with other premiums, is warranted.

experiments.title: How adding a $60 giving option to the Lamplighters monthly gift array impacted donations and revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a third monthly giving option as an “in-between” amount  for potential donors will achieve an increase in donations and revenue as it will reduce giving friction.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/19/2022 - 11/19/2022
The PFI Lamplighters program gives people the opportunity to support prisoners in ways that help them become closer to God. The current monthly giving options for Lamplighters are $15 to support one prisoner and $165 to support an entire class. We hypothesized that adding a middle amount as a giving option would reduce giving friction, giving donors an additional option that might nestle in nicely between an amount that is too low or not impactful enough and an amount that is too high.
To test this hypothesis, we added a $60 monthly giving option to support four prisoners.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Adding the $60 giving option to the monthly giving gift array resulted in a 79.3% lift in revenue with a 97.6% level of confidence. Donations saw an 11.8% decrease but only with a 54.4% level of confidence.
The impressive increase in revenue tells us that users responded very well to seeing a third giving option between $15 and $165. The $60 option reduced decision friction to where the donor did not have to think of an amount to settle in between the extremes of $15 and $165 - instead, the option was presented to them! Additionally, the $60 option will bring up our average monthly gift.
It's worth noting that new visitors produced a 114% lift in revenue with a 98.3% level of confidence. This tells us that people that are less familiar with the Lamplighters program appreciate the $60 giving option, as they're likely too new to the program to be willing to make a $165 monthly donation. But they are willing to commit $60/month to the program.

experiments.title: How removing thank you from the header on the instant donation page affects donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that removing thank you from the header on the donation page for course enrollees will achieve a higher donor conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/31/2022 - 11/11/2022
Luther Seminary is offering an online course to study the Book of Revelation. We had previously seen other organizations test the concept of removing “thank you” from the header of the donation page as that typically ends a user’s thought process when signing up for something or completing a purchase. Usually, when you see the thank you confirmation page, you think to yourself – Okay, I’m done now, so I’m leaving the page.
We predicted for this online course that users are leaving the donation page after seeing “thank you” at the very top, so we want to test changing that language so something that lets the user know that they are not done yet.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment page saw a 132% increase in donor conversion rate with a 96% level of confidence. We believe by saying "But before you go, will you consider something?" that the user's thought process is no longer interrupted therefore they are more likely to stay on the page to read the value proposition rather than bouncing as soon as they see "thank you."
Notable changes to other metrics due to the experiment:
Revenue experienced a 140.2% increase with a 97% level of confidence.
We believe revenue experienced such a high lift on the treatment page because we were able to keep double the amount of people on the page. After staying and reading the copy on why they should give, they were more likely to make a donation.

Pathway to Victory is a ministry that leverages great premiums as a way to increase email acquisition. The usual approach to Facebook ad copy was to use an image of the resource and Dr. Robert Jeffress; we wanted to test this against a video that talked about the resource itself.

experiments.title: How a radical redesign affected donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a radical redesign  for potential monthly donors will achieve an increased conversion rate and average gift.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/28/2020 - 11/08/2020
In an effort to increase the donor conversion rate for a monthly donation ask, we developed a treatment for the donation page with new copy, new graphics, and an increased monthly ask amount. We added exclusivity to the headline, increased clarity in the descriptions of the benefits for joining as a monthly donor, and relevance to the intro paragraphs. We also made the photo on the page more congruent with the ad they had seen before arriving on this page and increased the monthly gift ask from $10 to $15. We wanted to see how this would impact the overall conversion rate as well as average gift.
experiments.keyLearnings:
While we saw differing responses from individual segments, overall we saw a 31.6% decrease in conversion rate and turned the experiment off. What we would like to explore next is isolating the copy, image, and monthly gift ask to identify which stand-alone element, if any, was responsible for the decrease. Rather than taking these results at face value, we also need to evaluate if the increase in average gift potentially reflects a greater value than the decrease in conversions to see which is a more beneficial long-term approach. We did not reach statistical significance to have valid results for the increase in average gift to determine its effect on increasing revenue.
Observations of the impact on other visitor segments in the experiment:
Mobile visitors had a 40.2% decrease in donations with a 97% level of confidence.New  visitors had a 44.8% decrease in donations with a 99% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How adding a campaign goal to an acquisition landing page during year-end impacted results.
experiments.organization: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
categoryNames: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a campaign goal for potential donors will achieve a change in conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/14/2021 - 01/28/2022
The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate serve poor and needy people in the United States and more than 70 countries around the world. One of their most popular eBook offers is their Lady of Lourdes Novena.
During their calendar year-end campaign, we wanted to test whether including the year-end campaign goal amount, as well as some specific value proposition language, on the Lourdes instant donation page would help drive a higher conversion rate.
experiments.keyLearnings:
There was a large drop in conversion rates on the treatment, campaign-goal oriented donation page, with a 41% decline in conversion rates and a 99.5% level of confidence.

We believe that this was due to a couple of factors. First, the Lourdes offer was not actively pushed during the campaign; most of the digital messaging on donation forms and the homepage mentioned the year-end goal. Therefore, people that landed on the Lourdes page through a generic Facebook ad might not have been aware there was a campaign going on. Moreover, the campaign goal itself, and why people should donate to it, was not very clear.

For future experiments we will test whether making the value prop clearer and more urgent will have a more positive impact on conversion rates.
