One of the top lead generators for Hope Restored names is the CTA at the bottom of marriage crisis articles on focusonthefamily.com. The copy for the current CTA has been tested into, but now that we have more options for imagery through WordPress, we wanted to see what the addition of an image would do to the conversion rate. We measured this test by clicks alone.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This test affirms that sometimes imagery may distract from the message more than it enhances it. We found that all traffic saw a decrease in conversion for these calls to action on FOTF marriage crisis articles. We will keep the call to action as is, but we want to keep testing to improve conversion from FOTF to Hope Restored.

It is important to note that mobile devices saw the largest negative impact from the image treatment. Mobile traffic had a -12% decrease in clicks with a 98.1% confidence level. We need to be especially careful with mobile users as we decide on creative for CTAs.

experiments.title: How a premium offer impacts donor conversion when compared to a product offer
experiments.researchQuestion: We hypothesized that a premium offer may increase conversion over a product offer.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/01/2022 - 03/21/2022
FamilyLife has a store where they offer products to help you grow in your faith. One of the products that has done historically well is the Resurrection Eggs.
Additionally, over the past year, FamilyLife has found huge success in their paid media acquisition efforts to offer products that are normally found in their store as a “thank you gift” or premium offer when you make a gift of any amount on a donation page. This strategy has changed their acquisition strategy dramatically and is one of the biggest drivers of new donor acquisition. Knowing this, they wanted to see what the impact was on offer the Resurrection Eggs as a product offer from their store vs a premium offer for a donation. What would the difference in donor conversion be between the two experiences.
Traffic was pushed to both of the experiences through paid Facebook ads that promoted the product/premium. The ads were identical for both experiences and funnels.

experiments.title: How a radical redesign of an email appeal and donation page affected donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: How will a radical redesign of both the email and the landing page impact donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/14/2017 - 07/21/2017
The treatment donation flow produced a statistically valid 272.1% increase in donor conversion. This strong leap forward was due to a few identifiable conversion factors:
Reduction of visual friction in the control email led to more readers engaging with the copy.
Personal tone in the email increased motivation to give.
Decreased friction on the landing page increased likelihood to complete a donation
However, since all of these optimizations were made in one fell swoop, we cannot know exactly how each change contributed to the total increase. That said, this experiment provides a new benchmark for email appeal testing for FamilyLife—one that can be optimized in its own right.
experiments.keyLearnings:
FamilyLife had a mature email fundraising program that relied heavily on third-party stories with large images. wanted to test an entirely new approach to their email fundraising that integrated a more personal tone and removed possible sources of friction from the “mental conversation” provoked by the email.
The control email used a template with a large FamilyLife logo at the top, followed by an image, a pull quote and a donate button, before any actual email copy. The email copy made an offer to provide a scholarship for a couple like “Fred and Melissa”, even though that couple had not been introduced yet. This presented clarity issues—who are Fred and Melissa? The couple in the picture? The call to action never really requested a gift, it just suggested a gift by linking text telling the reader what their gift would do. Finally, the email had a donate button at the bottom as well. This email led to a landing page that had the headline “Scholarships for Hardworking Pastor Couples”, which didn’t address the reader. That copy gave more information about Fred and Melissa, and then led into a separate call to action section (visually separated by a green box) that was disconnected from the pastor-centric message: “Help Families Stay Together”. This extra section then led to a donation form that had all the fields stacked on top of each other.
The FamilyLife team started with the email, developing a treatment that stripped out all the visual elements to reduce visual friction and resemble an email that their founder, Dennis Rainey, might actually write. Then they took a new approach with the tone, making it very personal. The treatment copy talked about his wife, their history serving pastors, and the challenges they’ve seen firsthand. The ask was vey clear and direct to the reader, and provided just a single link to the donation page, with no button.
This led to a donation page that led with a headline that extends an offer to the reader: Invest in God’s work by strengthening a pastor’s marriage. This copy removed the story, with the hypothesis that the third-party story with no real connection and anonymized names might detract from the clarity of the value proposition. The treatment added a bulleted list of what the pastor couple would receive at a Weekend to Remember event, and made a clear ask to the reader. The visual separation from the green box was removed, and the donation form was shortened by bringing multiple fields (i.e. first name and last name) onto the same line, which gave the perception of less friction, while still collecting the same information.
They split their file evenly (making sure to evenly split donors and non-donors) and sent one version to each segment. The control email linked to the control page and the treatment email to the treatment page to preserve the integrity of the test throughout the entire appeal flow.

experiments.keyLearnings:
This test affirms that sometimes imagery may distract from the message more than it enhances it. We found that all traffic saw a decrease in conversion for these calls to action on FOTF marriage crisis articles. We will keep the call to action as is, but we want to keep testing to improve conversion from FOTF to Hope Restored.

It is important to note that mobile devices saw the largest negative impact from the image treatment. Mobile traffic had a -12% decrease in clicks with a 98.1% confidence level. We need to be especially careful with mobile users as we decide on creative for CTAs.

experiments.title: How inline acquisition offers in place of ads affected clickthrough
experiments.researchQuestion: Will replacing the mid-roll advertising banner with an inline acquisition increase clicks for this placement?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/30/2017 - 11/09/2017
Focus on the Family currently uses DoubleClick for Publishers to manage ad fill throughout their website.  Most of the ads in rotation are for their own resources, giving opportunities, and some third-party offers. One such placement is a mid-roll banner targeting long-form article pages.  They hypothesized that an inline email acquisition offer that is contextually relevant for their audience may increase clickthrough and help capture more of the high volume of new visitors viewing these articles.
They already had a free online Marriage Assessment which has resonated well with visitors to their marriage and parenting content, so they split the traffic to articles in those categories and delivered an inline offer for the assessment in place of the mid-roll ad from DoubleClick to half the audience.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The inline treatment increased the number of offer clicks by over 1,870%! This learning shows how the alignment of an offer, as well as its treatment, can be put to work to acquire more names. We hypothesize that a large portion of this impact is due to “banner blindness”—a web usability phenomenon where visitors consciously or subconsciously ignore banner-like information.  Banners also limit the amount of value proposition you can effectively communicate, which can decrease perceived value.
With high traffic and known content themes, we plan to test further into this placement—including moving it to the end of the content where we hypothesize we may be able to capitalize on the natural pivot/decision point created by completing the article.  Other potential tests include the headline, button, and value proposition copy as well as the inclusion of supporting graphical elements.

experiments.title: How the Text-To-Image Ratio of an Email Impacts its Open Rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Does increasing the text-to-image ratio of an email by reducing the number of included images impact open rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/14/2018 - 04/24/2018
For several months, email subscribers complained that our emails were being marked “promotional” and assigned to the “promotions” folder of their inbox, instead of showing up in their main inbox. Our hypothosis was that the high text-to-image ratio of our Weekend Update email, which had two images in the body and a large footer image, caused Gmail to treat the email as a marketing message rather than a “personal” one.
experiments.keyLearnings: Increasing the text-to-image ratio of an email significantly increase the number of people who saw the message, increasing the email's open rate by 36%. While images in emails may be more common, they are rarely found in "personal" messages and thus detract from engagement when attempting to send a personalized marketing message.

experiments.title: How adding a premium to an instant donation page impacts revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that offering a physical copy of the ebook for donors who give $50 or more will achieve an increase in average gift .
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/04/2022 - 09/04/2022
Navigators is a ministry organization that runs a variety of eBook offers. We hypothesized that offering a physical version of an already popular offer as a premium on the donation page would increase the average gift amount. This hypothesis stemmed from two ideas: 1.) providing a physical copy is seen as having greater value than the free digital version, and 2.) a premium is a bridge of appreciation to the user. You’ve generously given this, and we want to thank you for something in return. There was $50 gift minimum requirement to get this premium.
experiments.keyLearnings:
We ran the experiment for 30 days and saw a 48.9% decrease in conversion rate with a 99.5% confidence level based upon 75 total conversions. The data surrounding premiums for a donation amount often varies. When offering the premium prior to the actual donation, one theory is that it places the donor in the mindset of exchange. When you attach an item (in this case a book) to a specific number it could cause the donor to shift from the decision-making process of generosity to a more typical evaluation of purchase: is the item worth this?
Consider that the book they just received for free, is now having a "price" of $50 attached to it, this could cause the donor to see less value in what the organization is offering. They've been taken from the mindset of giving to one of consumption, and as a result, the donor is no longer interested.
Observations of the impact on other visitor segments in the experiment:
(Other) visitors had a 55.5% decrease in donations with a 100% level of confidence.Mobile visitors had a 53.1% decrease in donations with a 99% level of confidence.New  visitors had a 37.4% decrease in donations with a 91% level of confidence.Returning  visitors had a 70.3% decrease in donations with a 99% level of confidence.Desktop visitors had a 64.4% decrease in donations with an 87% level of confidence.Female visitors had an 82.0% decrease in donations with a 99% level of confidence.Male visitors had a 100.0% decrease in donations with a 96% level of confidence.55-64 visitors had a 100.0% decrease in donations with a 98% level of confidence.35-44 visitors had a 100.0% decrease in donations with a 96% level of confidence.45-54 visitors had a 100.0% decrease in donations with a 92% level of confidence.18-24 visitors had a 100.0% decrease in donations with an 85% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How adding an in-line dear reader on blog and article pages impacted email acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that an in-line content offer for blog and article visitors will achieve an increase in email acquisition.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/27/2022 - 11/06/2022
Content offers for TGC were being run as display ads on the site. There were also email capture forms in the footer of each page offering people a relevant newsletter subscription based on the content they were consuming. But with high volume traffic to blog and article pages, we wondered if including an in-line free content offer that included the eBook cover image, some light value proposition, and a CTA would increase the number of emails acquired from website visitors. We split traffic for this experiment 25/75 with 75% of visitors receiving the treatment which included a dear reader at the end of the article or blog.
experiments.keyLearnings:
What we found after running this experiment for 1 week was that there was a 27,919% increase in email conversion rate with 100% level of confidence. We saw valid increases across every channel, every device type, and every visitor segment. We quickly learned that web visitors responded well to this free content offer when it was featured in a highly visible placement on the web pages they were visiting. We have rolled the dear reader out to 100% of traffic and have several additional test opportunities to move forward on now that we have installed this feature. In the future, we want to test the impact of embedding the form itself so people can enter their name and email address directly on the site without clicking over to the landing page. We also want to test this against different content offers available so we can have something to offer for returning visitors who have already received this specific ebook.
Our most notable increases were for desktop visitors and returning visitors:
Desktop visitors had a 12,780.6% increase in email acquisitions with a 100% level of confidence.Returning visitors had a 16,432.8% increase in email acquisitions with a 100% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How a layout of premiums impacts donation conversions

rows.name: Call out 1 giving level, with others below

experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by presenting premiums in an alternate layouts for desktop viewers will achieve reduce friction, decision fatigue and therefore increase donations.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/05/2022 - 10/15/2022
Pathway to Victory is a broadcast ministry that primarily generated revenue through monthly premium offers. Typically premium offers will have multiple giving levels that include bundles of various or multiple premiums.
For the month of October 2022, the premium was a brand-new book written by Dr. Jeffress titled, “18 Minutes with Jesus.” Dr. Jeffress traveled the country and did multiple interviews for this premium so the extra traffic provided an opportunity for us to run an A/B/C test.
Historically, Pathway to Victory will present premium giving levels in a grid format however in viewing other sites with multiple offerings we decided to test different layouts of the premiums to see if we could remove friction and help with decision fatigue in choosing a giving level.
experiments.keyLearnings:
In this experiment, we had enough desktop traffic to run an A/B/C test.
For the "linear" version we saw the best conversion rates in donations with an increase of 33% with a level of confidence of 97.1%. For new visitors, we saw a 74.0% increase in donations with a 100% level of confidence.
Our hypothesis is that the linear version is a layout that removes friction for the prospective donor by being a format in one column. Viewers could easily view the options, see differences in the level offerings, and make a well informed decision.
For further testing, we are going to explore a "most popular" call out, test into the language and description of offers to help further drive clarity and remove any decision fatigue the viewer may experience.

experiments.title: How clarity and ordering on a donation form affect donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will ordering the donation steps and clarifying language increase donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/01/2016 - 08/04/2016
The treatment with the ordered copy produced a 12.5% lift in donations, which proved that the user testing had unlocked a powerful principle. While CaringBridge saw their donation process as one big step, the donors responded positively when they were told what it took to complete each step with numerical ordering and increased clarity.
This is a powerful reminder that we must learn to see our donation processes through the eyes of the donor to properly assist them and markedly improve conversions.
experiments.keyLearnings:
CaringBridge had done some user testing in anticipation of launching a new site experience. During that testing, a few users asked questions that suggested that the donation process was unclear and could use clarity and ordering. Based on these responses, they developed a treatment that numbered the header copy in the donation steps, used more personal and descriptive language, and increased perceived security around the donor’s information.
In the first header, they changed “Make your donation” to “1. Choose your donation amount”. Then, they added a number to the next call-to-action, “Write a Message of Support”. Finally, they changed the header “Your Information” to “3. Enter Your Information (It’s secure)”, which was designed to add clarity and reduce anxiety.
They launched an A/B test to quantify the impact.

The stripped-down email (with no template) produced an 80% increase in clickthrough rate. This tells us something about trust — and giving is an act of trust. It may imply that people are more likely to take action when they feel that the email is coming from a person, rather than an organization.

Since the majority of their audience was unfamiliar with the board member, Rik Lalim, they originally created a templated email that was explicitly “CaringBridge” to ease users’ potential anxiety. But they had previously tested emails that had no formatting and emulated “normal” email, and experienced great success. They decided to create a treatment version that stripped away all the formatting and photography.

experiments.title: How security indicators on a donation form affect donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will adding visual elements increase the perception of form security increase donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/21/2016 - 01/12/2017
The treatment with the additional visual indicators of security produced a 14.4% lift in donor conversion, which showed that there was a segment of donors who were convinced to complete their transaction with the extra reassurance that their transaction was secure.
What’s interesting is that the design of the CaringBridge form puts this test far below the “fold” of the page, which indicates that some visitors might have actually started the donation process and “bailed out” because they didn’t feel that their transaction would be secure. This suggests further testing to increase the perception of security earlier in the process.
experiments.keyLearnings:
CaringBridge had run a series of tests on their donation forms to significantly increase conversion over a period of three years. However, they proposed a new test, after reading a research study that showed that the most common reason web visitors didn’t complete transactions online was a perceived lack of security.
They wondered if their donation form was losing potential donors because it didn’t visually represent how secure it was. So, they created a special security box around the credit card section of form and added a padlock into the shaded box to represent that the form was secure. This didn’t make the form more secure—it was already PCI compliant and had the highest levels of security. All these design tweaks did was increase perception of security with the end user. They launched an A/B test to see if it increased donor conversion as they hypothesized.

experiments.title: The effect of a reverse (high to low) gift array on donations
experiments.researchQuestion: Which gift array arrangement will produce the greatest donation rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/26/2018 - 04/02/2018
Gift arrays have always been a point of interest for fundraisers. We’ve tested different amounts, different options, different minimums. We’ve even eliminated the array in favor of an open field. What we have not tested is the order in which to present the amounts.
Many nonprofits online, across all different verticals, when using a gift array choose to show the largest suggested amount first, rather than the lowest suggested amount, likely in hopes that donors will give more.
Does this matter? Especially for CaringBridge donors, who have been quite responsive in helping us understand gift arrays to-date.
How we test this: The control (original) keeps the standard rising suggested amount approach (start with the lowest ($50) on the left (assuming visitors in this case naturally read left to right) and then end with the highest ($250)
The treatment, however, reverses this, by starting with the highest amount first in the eyepath (in this case, left).
experiments.keyLearnings:
By showing the larger amount first, visitors were LESS likely to donate, and LESS likely to give in larger amounts.
But why?
When you think about the donation array, or suggested giving amounts, it all comes down to permission; meaning, what do we, as an organization, want the reader to believe is an ACCEPTABLE amount to give, and thus completely satisfy and receive that warm glow of giving?
By starting with a higher number first, we likely gave visitors the impression that lower amounts are LESS acceptable, despite them being on the list still. (for the same reason “other” is on the list… we won’t refuse their money, but maybe they will imagine us giving them a weird/disappointed look when they do)
Thus, because those visitors perceived their smaller donation was less acceptable, they chose not to donate at all.
Now, as for the drop in average gift approaching validity, this is likely due to the potential upgrade effect that happens when amounts are suggested in that rising order. By starting low, making the low amount feel acceptable, it is possible they feel like they can share a greater feeling of warmth by giving more, whereas in the opposite condition, giving just a little bit more doesn’t seem to mean any more than giving the original amount, due to its presentation.

experiments.title: Does personalizing an email focusing on a premium resource increase clicks for Pathway to Victory?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding personalization for email subscribers will achieve a higher click through rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/14/2021 - 12/28/2021
Pathway to Victory, a ministry of First Baptist Dallas, presented us with an opportunity to test some personalization during their highly-successful December match campaign. As an organization that is very premium-focused, their digital offers throughout the campaign focused around receiving a 2022 Daily Devotional book, along with other assorted premiums based on giving level, coupled with the largest match campaign in the ministry’s history. Despite having a relatively large email file, Pathway to Victory rarely used personalization on their emails. Therefore, we saw an opportunity to test whether adding a Dear First Name salutation to an email, and A/B test that only to the portion of the file that had a first name on file already (approx. 50% of the total file), would increase click-through rates. We tested this approach with an email in the middle of December.
experiments.keyLearnings: While open rates between the two emails were the same, adding the personalized salutation increased the number of clicks to the landing page by 26%. Given that we have seen that adding a simple personalization to emails did not negatively affect any email metrics, we plan to continue testing this, as well as add more than just a personalized salutation in future emails.

PFI’s sent out a Thanksgiving message from their CEO talking about the importance of prison ministry and thanking their constituents for helping change lives. Their emails are typically highly graphically designed, but we wanted to test a plain text email against their usual design. Experience has shown that typically removing design elements increases open rates and click through rates, because those emails are easier to inbox as well as more personal.
experiments.keyLearnings: This experiment was valid in determining that plain text emails have a higher open rate for PFI than highly designed email templates. We know that design elements typically decrease email making it into inboxes. This seems to have held true in this test. We also tested the click through rate for these emails and found an increase, however it did not validate at a 69% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How a premium offer affects donor motivation
experiments.researchQuestion: How does the presence of a premium offer affect donor conversion and revenue?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/10/2016 - 11/14/2016
The treatment with no premium book offer produced a 55.1% increase in donations. Without the “anchor point” of the book, average gift dropped by 34.5%, but there was no discernible impact on revenue.
This indicated something deeper about the motivation of their donors: without the premium offer, the value proposition was entirely centered around supporting the free online courses—and more donors responded. This suggests that DTS prospects are more motivated by an aspirational offer rather than a tactile offer.
The fact that the average gift for the control is right at $75 suggests that people either give to get the book, or don’t give at all. This may imply that people are conflating the offer with the incentive, and the aspirational aspect of the value proposition is clouded by the offer.
Because DTS can offer the premium throughout the course (in a banner) and directly through an email, they will move forward with the treatment. As a result of this experiment, DTS was able to grow their donor file significantly faster without affecting revenue.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Dallas Theological Seminary had released a free online course on the book of Revelation. After visitors registered for the course, they were given the opportunity to give a gift to support the free courses. DTS had traditionally used commentary books as premiums for donations, but wanted to test the impact of removing the premium offer on donations and revenue.
They hypothesized that removing the book might result in more donations, though the average gift might be lower without the “anchor point” ($75, in this case) for the book offer.
They launched an A/B test to determine a winner.

experiments.title: How replacing a static image with a video on a Facebook ad affects click-through rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that presenting an ad in video format will increase click-through rates for Facebook users by better catching their attention and better communicating the value one receives by signing up.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/18/2022 - 10/18/2022
Many of Dallas Theological Seminary’s Facebook ads for their free online courses are similar, using a color-rich background including the names of the courses and the teachers who present them. In an effort to combat low click through rates, we hypothesized that although viewers were seeing ads for different courses, because these ads looked so similar, it was causing viewers to assume they were seeing an ad for the same course time and time again, eventually prompting them to tune the ads out.
We also wondered if the static ads were inadequately communicating the value one could receive by enrolling in one of Dallas Theological Seminary’s free online courses. We wanted to know if we could increase click-through rates by presenting a video ad that would 1) Catch viewers attention and 2) Better communicate the value of registration.
For this experiment, we changed only the ad media, splitting traffic between he control and treatment over a span of 30 days.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running this experiment for 30 days, we observed a 93% LIFT in click-through rates, with a 100% level of confidence.
This experiment validated our hypothesis that a more interactive media format would result in more viewers clicking the ad. We believe that this treatment was successful for two primary reasons:
First, we believe that the new media format broke up the monotony of similarly formatted ads being shown to the same people. A different ad from a familiar organization, we believe, cued viewers that a new offer was being advertised.
Second, we believe that the video allowed viewers to see for themselves the quality of the coursework and the expertise of the teacher (these courses are adapted from on-campus seminary lectures) which increased the perceived value of the offer by improving its credibility and exclusivity, which likely provided more incentive for the viewer to click.
Moving forward, we will attempt to gather more video samples that we can use to promote more courses in their catalog.

experiments.title: How video ads affect email acquisition rate for a free course
experiments.researchQuestion: Will video ads produce an increase in email acquisition rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/03/2017 - 03/03/2017
DTS was promoting their new course, called Can You Trust the Bible?. They had a special video from their Academic Dean to promote it and wanted to test using this video to promote the course. Video ads had shown in some experiments to improve conversion for offers, although DTS had previously used static ads.
They split audiences and tested each ad unit to see which resulted in a better cost-per-acquisition.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The video ads produced a 37% decrease in conversion when tested against the static ads. While a deeper look at the data showed that many more people engaged with the video ads (which means they clicked somewhere on the ad), the video ad was either too distracting or not compelling enough to convince them to take the next step and sign up for the course. This seems to indicate that, in this case, video is more of a distraction than a motivator to take the course.
This doesn’t entirely rule out video ads on Facebook. There have been experiments where video ads produce a lift, and they can be effective at remarketing. DTS will continue to test video as a promotional tool for their free courses.

experiments.title: How advertising imagery affected the clarity of the value proposition
experiments.researchQuestion: Which ad will produce the highest motivated visitors resulting in the highest conversion rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/22/2016 - 08/31/2016
The ads that focused on the images of children had nearly double the email acquisition rate as the ad that just focused on the guide. Our hypothesis is that the images of children served as a reminder of why the prayers were needed whereas the guide was just what would be given. By answering the “why” first, the visitors were more motivated to download the devotional.
The visual aspect of black and white versus color did not seem to make any significant difference.
experiments.keyLearnings:
As part of their Shoes for Orphans Souls campaign, Buckner International created a 7-day digital devotional. The e-book guided individuals in prayer as a means to lift up orphans and other vulnerable children.

As part of the advertising for this campaign, we created Facebook ads to drive visitors to a conversion-focused email acquisition page. For similar organizations, past experiments had proven that showing a 3D version of the guide would produce the best response but with the topic of orphans and vulnerable children, we had a hypothesis that images of kids would provoke a better response. We created multiple versions of this ad and tested it across multiple audiences

experiments.title: How image clarity and ask intensity affected clickthrough rate on a Facebook ad
experiments.researchQuestion: Does an image of the 3D eBook in the ad produce more clicks than an image of children? How do people react to a soft ask vs. a direct ask?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/30/2017 - 05/03/2017
The Buckner team realized that users were 40% more likely to respond to an image including the 3D eBook than one that does not. This stems from the clarity and directness of the image’s alignment with the ad- people know exactly what they’re getting when they click the ad. Copy 2 was also more effective because they’re not asking them to commit to something at the ad level, as in Copy 1. The direct ask in Copy 1 is better suited for people who have already clicked through to the landing page. The softer ask in Copy 2 (asking them to learn more) paired with a direct image led to a 40% more successful click through rate.
They also learned that placing a strong statement (“The foster system is broken”) that people will either agree/disagree with at the beginning of the ad leads to more qualified traffic (and a more likely converter). If the user agrees with the initial statement, then sees the opportunity to learn more paired with a very clear image of what they’re going to receive if they click the link, they’re more likely to continue to the landing page. If they disagree, they’re immediately diverted.
experiments.keyLearnings: Buckner International was promoting a new free ebook to grow their email file targeted towards people interested in the foster care system. They wanted to figure out what made people click through to the landing page from the ad and download the guide. They created four different treatments to test this- 2 images paired with 2 different approaches to copy. One of the images is a photo of the 3D eBook, and the other image is a photo of children. The copy differences hinge on a direct vs. soft ask: “commit to prayer” vs. “learn more about the opportunity”.

experiments.title: How a video impacts the conversion rate of a donation page
experiments.researchQuestion: How will a transcription of a video impact donor conversion rate compared to the video itself?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/15/2016 - 12/23/2016
As part of their calendar year end campaign, Buckner International created a video of their president telling a story from the previous year and making a direct ask for support at the end. The message and imagery of the video was compelling but our concern was that visitors that did not necessarily respond to videos would lose interest before the ask was made. We decided to set up an experiment where we displayed a transcription on the page in place of the video. We then sent an email to their house file to see which would produce the best conversion rate.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The video transcription ended up producing a 560.2% lift to the overall donor conversion on the page. It reached both a statistical level of validity and a valid sample size.

The video was a great way to visually reinforce the messaging of the campaign and we saw high engagement when posted on Facebook but, when it comes to the actual ask, we are much better suited using text to convey the message so that the potential donor does not get distracted from the primary call to action.

experiments.title: How radically redesigning an email affected donation revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a radical redesign of the email that increases the force of the value proposition and introduces images increase response and revenue?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/15/2017 - 11/20/2017
The treatment produced a 123% increase in donations as well as an increase in revenue, which produced a big difference in return on investment. This showed the power of the increased value proposition, with the additional impact of the imagery to help the recipient visualize the offer.
This is a promising result that should be tested on more lists to ensure that there isn’t an anomaly with the first list choice.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The Heritage Foundation created a popular offer for email acquisition with their Digital Guide to the Constitution. After a visitor downloaded the Digital Guide, they were offered membership to Heritage, along with an offer to get the print copy of the Guide to the Constitution for a gift of $100. This offer flow had been quite effective at converting prospects into donors using a text-based approach that had tested well with the house file. But since acquisition mailings reached an audience of people who were not as familiar with Heritage, they created a treatment to test that increased the force of the value proposition and introduced images of the offer.

The control, which introduced the free offer with a casual greeting, focused on the content contained in the Guide. The treatment created an enemy: “Right now, liberals are waging an all-out war against our nation’s founding principles…”. Instead of a resource, it offered a “gift”, and highlighted the hard work that went into its creation, before revealing that it was created for free. The treatment email also included an image of the offer to provide clarity.

Then, Heritage ran an A/B test to determine a winner.

experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/13/2017 - 10/18/2017
When running a Facebook ad campaign for a new online course, we wondered if we needed to add more clarity to the Facebook ad image. In order to more clearly depict the title and purpose of the course, Turning Facebook Likes Into Donors, we added the Facebook logo leading towards a dollar icon on the ad image.
This experiment was conducted with the Network for Good audience ad set.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The new Facebook ad image led to a 68% decrease in course registration, despite appearing to ad more clarity. Since the control ad had previously been running, it’s possible that the additional social proof from likes and shares contributed to it’s better performance; although, likes and share don’t always make that significant of difference.

It could also be that the new imagery, although more accurate and clear, made the course look similar to other marketing courses out there that appear gimmicky or too good to be true.

experiments.title: How additional payment options impacted long-term revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: How will the ACH form impact donor giving?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/20/2016 - 01/10/2017
This test was launched on a recurring donor acquisition campaign for HAFA’s Liberty Brigade. For this campaign, a recurring donation was the pre-selected value on the form.
As far as conversion rate is concerned, there was no significant difference between the two forms (3.99% for the control and 3.94% for the ACH form). This is a good sign since there was concern that the ACH option would add friction to the donation process.
When we analyze the impact on revenue, the value of ACH recurring gifts resulted in an overall 55% lift to long-term revenue (this includes both immediate gifts and gifts based upon the average lifespan of the recurring gift).
What is interesting is that of the recurring donors, only 15% selected the ACH giving option but that 15% led to that significant increase to long-term revenue.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Heritage Action for America recently implemented an upgrade to their donation form. Historically, the online donation forms had only been able to capture credit cards. A recent upgrade provided the ability to also capture ACH transactions (donors could still make credit card gifts if they preferred).

The additional value provided by ACH comes from recurring gifts. The average lifespan of a credit card recurring gift is roughly 12 months. For ACH recurring gifts, the average lifespan expands to be 5 years. However, our concern was that adding in the additional option of ACH may create friction in the donation process which may reduce overall donor conversion. As a result, we decided to test the new form against the control.

experiments.title: How creating a “shortcut” to the donation form affects conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: How will removing the “Donate Now” button on a money bomb impact the number of donations and revenue?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/09/2015 - 09/17/2015
Leading up to their Republican presidential debates, Heritage Action for America ran a money bomb campaign to create more awareness around the event. This campaign was a two week long series with multiple emails all driving to the same landing page.
Previous experiments had proven that longer copy is better at communicating the value proposition to the donor. However, we had a hypothesis that we may be able to create a shortcut for some donors that did not need as much convincing. The idea was to put a “Donate Now” button in the top right of the page that would jump the potential donors to the donation form at the bottom thus reducing friction.
experiments.keyLearnings: By adding a “Donate Now” button to the top right corner of the page, we ended up reducing the number of donations by 28%. Our hypothesis is that this button prematurely tipped our hand on the purpose of the campaign and made the ask before the visitors were ready to see it. While the long page copy may add friction to the giving process, the added benefit that comes with a strong value proposition is enough to overcome that friction.

experiments.title: How the use of an illustrated image over a real photo in a Facebook ad can impact click-through and acquisition
experiments.organization: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
categoryNames: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
experiments.researchQuestion: Which Facebook ad would acquire more email addresses
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/05/2017 - 05/09/2017
People were not only more motivated to click on the illustrated image over the real photo in the Facebook ad, but once they got to the landing page they were more highly motivated to get the offer as well. There was a 66.6% increase in emails acquired with the illustrated image over the real photo. The increase in click-through and acquisition can be attributed to the call-to-action being reinforced on the image, the congruency of the image used in the ad and the imagery on the acquisition page and because the image was more centric to the faith of the organization.
experiments.keyLearnings: The Missionary Oblates developed a Novena prayer in honor of Mother’s Day. They promoted this content offer through paid Facebook ads in an effort to acquire new names. We traditionally use images of real people in Facebook ads, but we wanted to test the image of the ad with something more illustrated. The illustrated image was also the same image used on the cover of the Novena prayers and acquisition page.

experiments.title: How the minimum premium gift impacts donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Can we increase overall revenue by increasing the minimum gift required for a donation premium?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/10/2016 - 06/23/2016
On one of their donor acquisition campaign, The Heritage Foundation was testing the use of a premium to increase conversion and average gift. Previous experiments had proven that the premium increased the likelihood of a donor to convert but we wanted to see if we could also increase to overall revenue coming from the campaign. We decided to try testing the premium at a $125 minimum gift level. The control treatment had the minimum gift set at $100.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The $125 minimum gift was able to lift the average gift by 46% but it decreased the overall conversion by 58%. This resulted in a 39% decrease to overall revenue for the treatment. In this situation, the $100 minimum gift amount was the clear winner between the two.

However, even if this test had managed a slight increase to revenue but still decreased conversion rate significantly, the control would still have been the winner. The purpose of this campaign was new donor acquisition. It would take a significant increase in revenue to justify any loss in the number of donors acquired.

				× 0% increase in average gift

experiments.title: How emphasizing recurring giving impacts overall revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: Which donation page treatment will yield the greatest number of donors and revenue?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/29/2016 - 12/07/2016
In the latest in the series of optimization experiments on the main Heritage donation forms, we implemented a new donation form and value proposition that emphasized the value of recurring giving. In addition to the expanded and more visual value proposition, the new form also laid out a cohesive product-level value proposition on why recurring giving is so important.
With this new treatment, we ran a test across several of the most highly trafficked donation pages to see which would result in more gifts.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This experiment had interesting results. The new treatment resulted in a 54% decrease in the number of donors acquired. However, since the goal of the treatment was to acquire recurring donors given their increased value, we need to analyze the impact on that segment. In the case of recurring donors, the new form does see a 87% increase in acquisition.

Assuming that the average recurring donor will last 12 months, we then factored these two results and discovered that after 12 months, the new treatment would effectively break even on revenue compared to the original control. With a 54% drop in donors, continuing with the treatment would seem to be a pretty risky bet on long-term revenue potential. Based upon this analysis, we decided to end the test and continue with the current control.

experiments.title: How adding a countdown clock to a campaign donation page affects donor conversion rate

rows.name: Campaign Focused Donation Page - Countdown Clock

experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a countdown clock to a campaign donation page for Working Preacher contacts will achieve a higher donor conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/17/2022 - 10/27/2022
Luther Seminary was running its bi-annual Working Preacher campaign. They were sending traffic to this page via mainly campaign emails being sent to the Working Preacher contacts and donors. There was also a significant amount of direct traffic coming to this page from the Working Preacher website.
We wanted to test adding a countdown clock to the deadline of the campaign on October 31st to see if this would increase urgency and make more people likely to give.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The countdown clock resulted in a 20.2% decrease in donor conversion rate with a near-valid level of confidence of 94.9%. Also, revenue experienced a 40.8% decrease with a 99% level of confidence.

We believe this audience did not respond well to the countdown clock because we launched this experiment 14 days prior to the deadline therefore it did not give the audience enough urgency to give at that time. It actually deterred people from giving probably due to the fact that they still a one week to two weeks left to donate.

We would like to test this concept again for this audience but only show the countdown clock on the final days of the campaign to see if the urgency actually increases giving then.

experiments.title: How radically redesigning an email affected donation revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a radical redesign of the email that increases the force of the value proposition and introduces images increase response and revenue?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/15/2017 - 11/20/2017
The treatment produced a 123% increase in donations as well as an increase in revenue, which produced a big difference in return on investment. This showed the power of the increased value proposition, with the additional impact of the imagery to help the recipient visualize the offer.
This is a promising result that should be tested on more lists to ensure that there isn’t an anomaly with the first list choice.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The Heritage Foundation created a popular offer for email acquisition with their Digital Guide to the Constitution. After a visitor downloaded the Digital Guide, they were offered membership to Heritage, along with an offer to get the print copy of the Guide to the Constitution for a gift of $100. This offer flow had been quite effective at converting prospects into donors using a text-based approach that had tested well with the house file. But since acquisition mailings reached an audience of people who were not as familiar with Heritage, they created a treatment to test that increased the force of the value proposition and introduced images of the offer.

The control, which introduced the free offer with a casual greeting, focused on the content contained in the Guide. The treatment created an enemy: “Right now, liberals are waging an all-out war against our nation’s founding principles…”. Instead of a resource, it offered a “gift”, and highlighted the hard work that went into its creation, before revealing that it was created for free. The treatment email also included an image of the offer to provide clarity.

Then, Heritage ran an A/B test to determine a winner.

experiments.title: How different audience motivations affect click rate in a Facebook ad (Part 1)
experiments.researchQuestion: Which ad variation will the nonprofit professionals audience respond to more?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/28/2016 - 08/01/2016
While setting up the advertising and promotions for an upcoming live broadcast, we wanted to determine if our various audience groups for our Facebook Ads would respond differently to various ad treatments. We have 3 main audience groups that we ran this test with: our housefile, housefile lookalikes, and nonprofit professionals.
Our original live broadcast Facebook Ad focused on the speaker and the title, but we thought people may be more motivated by different aspects of the focus topic. For the treatments, we developed an ad that focused on “Growing Your Donor File” and one that focused on “Empathy.”
experiments.keyLearnings: The “Growing Your Donor File” treatment greatly out performed both the control and treatment 2, lifting click rate by 277% with the nonprofit professionals audience. This learning will allow us to increase our total leads for this specific live broadcast. For future live broadcasts, it shows us that it will be more effective to focus our advertising on the learning and results that someone will get out of it, rather than the speaker and their qualifications.

experiments.title: How a text link instead of a button impacted email clickthrough rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using a hyperlinked page URL for prospects will achieve an increase in email clickthrough rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/23/2021 - 02/04/2021
In testing what generated the most clicks when rolling out a new eBook offer to their housefile list, The Fund for American Studies wanted to know whether the approach of using the landing page URL with a hyperlink would definitively be better than using a button in the email promotion.
experiments.keyLearnings:
With a confidence level of 98.5%, we observed a +17.5% increase in email clickthrough rate.

The reality is that when you write an email from an individual, the reader expects the person that wrote them to not use "marketing techniques" (like in this case, a button).

Therefore, the believability that an actual person on the other side of the email actually wrote them goes up when you treat the clickable link in emails like how a real person would send them — in this case, not as a button, but rather as a page URL that is hyperlinked.

When possible, continue to use clickable URL's instead of fancy marketing elements within emails — like a button.

experiments.keyLearnings: People seeing the instant donation page don’t know if they want the whole DVD set yet, because they haven’t taken the course yet. We’re giving people too much before they’re ready, which impacted donations in a negative way.

experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that shortening the copy and simply sharing a link for participants in our NIO cultivation experiment will achieve an increase in clicks to the full post.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/07/2021 - 05/07/2021
In a NIO cultivation email where we were sharing a blog post, we wondered if it would be more effective to preview some key takeaways from the post or simply just share the link with short copy. There are 2 questions here – will a preview of key takeaways make the content seem more valuable? OR will shorter copy create curiosity to read the post?
experiments.keyLearnings: We learned in this experiment that simply sharing the link rather than preview the blog post is much more successful in the click rate to the full post. We can assume that because the control email shared key takeaways and imagery, it gave too much away and made it less necessary to visit the full post.

experiments.title: How the position of the ask impacts email engagement
experiments.researchQuestion: Will moving the call to action up in the email increase overall email engagement?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/28/2017 - 04/03/2017
Moving the call to action closer to the top of the email resulted in a 72% increase in traffic to the donation page. However, despite this increase in traffic, the treatment did not result in a statistically significant increase in the overall number of gifts given.
What this would indicate is that we could shorten our email copy to get the call to action closer to the top and move the additional copy to the donation page. This would let us maintain the level of expressed value proposition while still increasing click through rates. This is a hypothesis that will need to be validated in future email appeals.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Over the last several months, the tone and style of Buckner International’s monthly email appeals had evolved to be longer and more casual in nature. One of the side effects of this evolution was that the call to action and link to the donation page were pushed further down in the copy.

There was a hypothesis that this may be negatively impacting the overall click through and response rates for their appeals. We decided to isolate this variable of the appeal and run an experiment where we moved the call to action up in the email. We then monitored both click through and response rates.

experiments.title: How a visual redesign of a main donate button on the website impacted clicks

rows.name: New - Donate to TGC Green Button

experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that visually emphasizing the donate button for homepage visitors will achieve an increase in clicks to the donation page.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/25/2022 - 03/03/2022
The TGC team was inspired by another experiment they saw in a NextAfter Institute webinar to visually modify the main donate button on their website navigation bar. Rather than rolling out the new version based solely on another organization’s results, we decided to test into the optimal design by splitting traffic 50/50 and measuring the results of how many people clicked on each version of the button. The existing button said “Give” and was visually the same as the other calls to action in the navigation header. The treatment was a green button to help it stand out visually from the rest of the page and the text was revised to “Donate to TGC” to add clarity.
experiments.keyLearnings: After running the experiment for one month, we saw a valid increase in clicks on the new green button that said "Donate to TGC". What was interesting, however, is that donations and revenue recorded on the donation page were actually higher coming from the control version. Because an experiment was simultaneously running on the main donation page that could have been responsible for impacting these results, we will have to take this into account on a future experiment. Overall we learned that the new button design did, in fact, attract significantly more visitors to the main donate page.

experiments.summary:

PFI launched a new digital campaign to acquire sponsorships. Our first hypothesis was that the value proposition on the page was brief and generic to sponsorship rather than specific to the child. So we updated the headline to include more value proposition and then laid out for the reader exactly how they would change that specific child’s life.
experiments.keyLearnings: We had to do a few iterations of this test and it was touch and go for a bit. In the end, the iterations did increase sponsorships by 68%. We posit that showing the direct impact and the value of a monthly sponsorship gift does increase conversion. But we think we can do better and will continue to test.

experiments.title: How a radical redesign of an email appeal and donation page affected donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: How will a radical redesign of both the email and the landing page impact donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/14/2017 - 07/21/2017
The treatment donation flow produced a statistically valid 272.1% increase in donor conversion. This strong leap forward was due to a few identifiable conversion factors:
Reduction of visual friction in the control email led to more readers engaging with the copy.
Personal tone in the email increased motivation to give.
Decreased friction on the landing page increased likelihood to complete a donation
However, since all of these optimizations were made in one fell swoop, we cannot know exactly how each change contributed to the total increase. That said, this experiment provides a new benchmark for email appeal testing for FamilyLife—one that can be optimized in its own right.
experiments.keyLearnings:
FamilyLife had a mature email fundraising program that relied heavily on third-party stories with large images. wanted to test an entirely new approach to their email fundraising that integrated a more personal tone and removed possible sources of friction from the “mental conversation” provoked by the email.

The control email used a template with a large FamilyLife logo at the top, followed by an image, a pull quote and a donate button, before any actual email copy. The email copy made an offer to provide a scholarship for a couple like “Fred and Melissa”, even though that couple had not been introduced yet. This presented clarity issues—who are Fred and Melissa? The couple in the picture? The call to action never really requested a gift, it just suggested a gift by linking text telling the reader what their gift would do. Finally, the email had a donate button at the bottom as well. This email led to a landing page that had the headline “Scholarships for Hardworking Pastor Couples”, which didn’t address the reader. That copy gave more information about Fred and Melissa, and then led into a separate call to action section (visually separated by a green box) that was disconnected from the pastor-centric message: “Help Families Stay Together”. This extra section then led to a donation form that had all the fields stacked on top of each other.

The FamilyLife team started with the email, developing a treatment that stripped out all the visual elements to reduce visual friction and resemble an email that their founder, Dennis Rainey, might actually write. Then they took a new approach with the tone, making it very personal. The treatment copy talked about his wife, their history serving pastors, and the challenges they’ve seen firsthand. The ask was vey clear and direct to the reader, and provided just a single link to the donation page, with no button.

This led to a donation page that led with a headline that extends an offer to the reader: Invest in God’s work by strengthening a pastor’s marriage. This copy removed the story, with the hypothesis that the third-party story with no real connection and anonymized names might detract from the clarity of the value proposition. The treatment added a bulleted list of what the pastor couple would receive at a Weekend to Remember event, and made a clear ask to the reader. The visual separation from the green box was removed, and the donation form was shortened by bringing multiple fields (i.e. first name and last name) onto the same line, which gave the perception of less friction, while still collecting the same information.

They split their file evenly (making sure to evenly split donors and non-donors) and sent one version to each segment. The control email linked to the control page and the treatment email to the treatment page to preserve the integrity of the test throughout the entire appeal flow.

experiments.title: How security indicators on a donation form affect donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will adding visual elements increase the perception of form security increase donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/21/2016 - 01/12/2017
The treatment with the additional visual indicators of security produced a 14.4% lift in donor conversion, which showed that there was a segment of donors who were convinced to complete their transaction with the extra reassurance that their transaction was secure.
What’s interesting is that the design of the CaringBridge form puts this test far below the “fold” of the page, which indicates that some visitors might have actually started the donation process and “bailed out” because they didn’t feel that their transaction would be secure. This suggests further testing to increase the perception of security earlier in the process.
experiments.keyLearnings:
CaringBridge had run a series of tests on their donation forms to significantly increase conversion over a period of three years. However, they proposed a new test, after reading a research study that showed that the most common reason web visitors didn’t complete transactions online was a perceived lack of security.

They wondered if their donation form was losing potential donors because it didn’t visually represent how secure it was. So, they created a special security box around the credit card section of form and added a padlock into the shaded box to represent that the form was secure. This didn’t make the form more secure—it was already PCI compliant and had the highest levels of security. All these design tweaks did was increase perception of security with the end user. They launched an A/B test to see if it increased donor conversion as they hypothesized.

experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/02/2016 - 03/30/2016
The stripped-down email (with no template) produced an 80% increase in clickthrough rate. This tells us something about trust — and giving is an act of trust. It may imply that people are more likely to take action when they feel that the email is coming from a person, rather than an organization.
There were still elements of the treatment email that weren’t entirely personal — the big blue button, and the logo. This prompted further testing to see if the removal of these elements produced an additional lift.
experiments.keyLearnings:
CaringBridge received a matching gift for their spring fundraising campaign from a board member. Since the majority of their audience was unfamiliar with the board member, Rik Lalim, they originally created a templated email that was explicitly “CaringBridge” to ease users’ potential anxiety. But they had previously tested emails that had no formatting and emulated “normal” email, and experienced great success. They decided to create a treatment version that stripped away all the formatting and photography.

They then split their email file and ran a test to see which attracted more responses

experiments.title: How changing the landing page design affects revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: Which landing page design produces the most donor conversions and revenue?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/09/2015 - 06/23/2015
The Heritage Foundation had been using the same landing page design for their search engine marketing (SEM) donor campaigns for the past three years. During those three years, we had run multiple experiments on the main organizational donation page which had gradually evolved into something that looked completely different.
We were in the process of moving the SEM page to the same domain as the main donation form and realized that it would be a good time to validate that the old design was still effective for the SEM audience segment.  We created an experiment by duplicating the page content but displaying it on the two different page designs.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The new design treatment caused an 85.1% drop in revenue by decreasing conversion rate by 67.6% and decreasing average gift by 54.1%. 

The simplified design used in the treatment was developed for a page with minimal copy. When we added the long value proposition text that had been developed for this specific landing page, it became difficult to read.

Landing pages must be designed so that they are easily scannable by the visitor. In this situation, the sub-headlines didn't stand out enough and the lack of page border made the page seem longer than it was.  All of these elements caused enough friction in the mind of the donor to have a negative impact on both conversion rate and average gift.

experiments.title: How “human” imagery affects conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a “human” background image increase conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/02/2015 - 05/26/2015
This image will be tested in other donation experiences as well as providing a guideline for creative moving forward.

experiments.title: How clarity in the design of a Facebook ad can affect conversion rates
experiments.researchQuestion: Which Facebook ad design will acquire more email addresses?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/22/2016 - 09/21/2016
When promoting the Why Should I Give to You? research study on nonprofit value proposition, we came to the realization that our Facebook ads were a little bit hard to read. The yellow text against the light gray background seemed to get lost when scrolling through a Facebook feed.
This led us to the hypothesis that a clearer design would yield at least a greater conversion rate. As a result, we developed a treatment version of the ad that presented the same information, but with a clearer design that would stand out more in the Facebook newsfeed.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment saw a 56.7% decrease in conversion rate. Initially, this was very confusing. How could a design that appeared so much stronger be so ineffective by comparison? We almost didn’t even run a test on this new design because we were so certain it would outperform the control.

Taking a second look, we realized that the slight change of phrasing in order to improve the design aesthetic may have decreased the clarity of the content of the offer.

There are two main learnings we can glean from this test:
There are no marketing experts, design experts, advertising experts, etc. There is only experience. And the only way to know what is more effective is to test
 	Language and copy that makes sense to you doesn’t always make sense to your audience. In this case, the copy in the treatment design made complete sense to us, yet it appears to not have been quite as clear to the Facebook audience.

experiments.title: How personalizing and removing heavy design affects high urgency campaign emails for donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that personalizing an email for alumni and potential donors will achieve a higher donor conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/21/2022 - 09/22/2022
Luther Seminary ran their annual 24-hour Give Day high urgency campaign from 9/21 – 9/22. The emails sent in this campaign were heavily designed and had a marketing feel rather than a personalized, conversation feel.
We wanted to test removing the design from the emails and changing the language to be more conversational from an actual person at the seminary.
We made these changes across the 9 emails for the alumni and all other segments since those were the only two segments large enough to test within.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This experiment saw a 23.1% decrease in conversion rate with a 95.4% level of confidence across the 9 emails sent on Give Day. We concluded that Luther Seminary's audience is accustomed to the highly designed, marketing emails therefore the personalized emails did not increase the likelihood of someone giving a donation.
We believe that the timeline and words of inspiration at the top of the control email actually provided a sense of encouragement and urgency to give that day. Whereas the treatment emails were more focused on making a connection with the potential donor through conversation-like language.
Another notable takeaway from this experiment is that the click-through rates on the emails saw 19.7% decrease with a 98.2% level of confidence from the treatment emails.
We would like to further test this concept in another high-urgency campaign that spans a longer period of time. We believe this will provide more accurate information than a campaign that has 9 emails within 24 hours.

experiments.title: How adding a “give monthly” button to the main navigation menu affects recurring donation rates.
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a give monthly button to a site’s main navigation will achieve higher recurring donation rates .
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 08/23/2022
To increase recurring donation rates for a public policy client, we wanted to know how adding a “give monthly” button to the main navigation menu would impact recurring monthly donation rates. Our hypothesis was that by increasing the visibility of this donation option, more site visitors would decide to give recurring gifts. On desktop this treatment appeared top-of-page, in line with the navigation menu items. On mobile, the give Monthly button was placed at the bottom of a burger menu, directly under the pre-existing donation button.
We ran this experiment for one month, splitting traffic 50/50 between the control and treatment.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This experiment produced a few interesting and unexpected results. First, both the control and treatment (w/ "give monthly" button) failed to produce any recurring gifts, indicating that simply increasing the visibility of the option to make a recurring gift was not enough to motivate prospective donors to do so without a compelling reason why.
Second, while there was not a significant increase in one-time donations for desktop traffic, we did see a valid 144% lift in one-time donations for mobile visitors.
Reviewing these results, we hypothesized that adding a second button increased the visibility of both buttons, not just one. And so we wondered what was causing donors to exclusively choose to give once rather than monthly.
This led us to hypothesize that this increase in one-time giving may have been prompted by the persuasion principle of "reject and retreat". This principle of behavioral psychology states that by "rejecting" a larger ask (a recurring gift), a prospective donor may be more likely to convert when we "retreat" to a smaller commitment (one-time donation).
In this case, by showing two donation options (one representing a larger commitment than the other), donors may have been prompted to reject the larger commitment but then agree to the smaller ask. This may help explain the motivation of choosing one button over the other while so significantly increasing overall giving among people who saw the two-button treatment.
Unfortunately, it is not possible to draw a definitive conclusion one way or the other in this instance, however, this experiment has given us some interesting data points and things to consider about the motivations behind these results.
Since we have discovered that simply adding a monthly donation button provides insufficient motivation for more people to give monthly, we would like to look for future opportunities to increase the visibility of monthly giving, while also conveying why monthly giving is so important and the impact it will have. A sticky bar may be an option, a dear reader, or an exit-intent pop-up. We may also consider drawing attention to the give monthly option + impact on the main donation page. In order to determine the impact of "reject and retreat" on donor motivation, we would need to formulate a more intentional experiment that first asked donors to make a monthly gift, and then followed up with a one-time ask to 50% of traffic. This may be best tested in the form of an appeal that asks specifically for monthly gifts but then follows up with a "retreat" that asks for a one-time gift instead. This might also be tested using a pop-up or some other form of notification. While this may have played a part in the results seen in this experiment, there is simply not enough context to know for sure. But this is an interesting concept that will be worth testing in a different environment.

experiments.title: How adding a humanized sticky bar to an acquisition page affected conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a humanized sticky bar to a digital acquisition donation page for prospective donors will achieve an increase in conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/29/2022 - 08/25/2022
Luther Seminary is offering Enter the Bible: Revelation Study Guide as an acquisition offer through paid Facebook ads and to their housefile through email. We wanted to increase ICR on the instant donation page, so we decided to retest the humanized sticky bar on this page.
We hypothesized that this would increase donor conversion rate by humanizing the donation page with a message (and picture) from Katie Langston who is well known in the Enter the Bible community asking the user for a donation to be able to continue to provide resources like this one in the future.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Adding in a humanized sticky bar actually led to a decrease in conversion rates of 56.4%.
This informs us that this humanized message actually has a negative effect on donor conversion rate and decreases the motivation to give a gift. We believe this is due to the lack of motivation to give throughout the full funnel therefore the presence of the sticky bar does not "convince" people to make a donation.
Further testing on messaging and subjects will be done in the future to see if we can get a lift. This experiment should be run again on an instant donation page that receives a high volume of traffic to the page.
Notable changes to other metrics due to the experiment:
Revenue experienced a 53.7% decrease with a 94% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How strengthening the value proposition with a radical redesign to the copy on an instant donation page affects instant conversion rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that strengthening the value proposition language on the instant donation page for prospective donors will achieve clarity and cause an increase in ICR.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/28/2022 - 08/28/2022
Luther Seminary has been running paid Facebook ads and email marketing for their content offer Enter the Bible: Proverbs Study Guide. While the email sign-up rate was healthy around 48%, we wanted to try and increase the instant conversion rate on the donation page.
We hypothesized that the copy on the control page did not provide clarity on the impact a donation has for Luther Seminary and what the donor’s gift will do for the organization.
We believed that adding more value proposition-focused language to the page would provide clarity to the prospective donor for how their gift will be used in the future.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The results of the experiment were encouraging when it comes to an increase in conversion rate for email traffic: 67.7 increase in conversion rate for email traffic with a level of confidence of 96.9%. When it came to the all-traffic segment, we saw a 59.1 increase in conversion rate with a level of confidence of 96.9%.
When it came to paid social traffic, the change of the language did not provide a valid or directional increase in conversion rate.
We believe the value prop-centric language had the most effect on users who know Luther Seminary well and are very aware of its mission and goals. When it came to new visitors (i.e. traffic from paid social), the language was not motivating enough to convince more people to donate to the Seminary.
And while we are encouraged by these results, importantly, it turns out that we changed an additional variable by changing the confirmation message at the top of the page to be more clarifying following the previous step in the funnel. We will still want to verify these results by running this experiment on another instant donation page while isolating the variables being tested.

experiments.title: How adding a premium to an ebook donation page impacts donor conversion rates and average gift.
experiments.organization: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
categoryNames: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate

rows.name: Gift of any amount for Healing Prayers book

experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a physical premium for donors will achieve a change in conversion rates..
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/26/2020 - 08/27/2020
The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate serve poor and needy people in the United States and more than 70 countries around the world. For this experiment we wanted to test whether adding a premium offer to an eBook instant donation page would impact donor conversion rates and average gifts.
For this treatment version, we added in a premium of offering a 256-page Healing Prayers book as a thank you for a gift of any amount. The control version of this experiment had a $25 ask in order to have a votive candle lit for a donors’ intentions at the Our Lady of the Snows shrine.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Across all metrics tracked, the treatment version of this experiment led to a decrease. There was a 18% decrease in total revenue with a 97% level of confidence, and a 18% decrease in overall donor conversion rates with a 99% level of confidence.
Our hypothesis for this result is that by asking for a gift of any amount, instead of using the premium as an upgrade strategy, the impact was to drive down potential revenue. Moreover, while the premium tied into the acquisition offer itself (Prayers for a Spiritual Life), we believe that since the form copy itself did not give more details about the premium, it negatively impacted conversion rates as well.
For future experiments, we will test different premium-related treatments and strategies.
