experiments.title: How a radical redesign on a donation page affects donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that guiding to prospective donor through their donation process through a radical redesign for donation page visitors will achieve a higher donor conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/11/2022 - 08/15/2022
The Navigators recently moved donation page platforms where the previous donation page template was adjusted. The new template had the gift array at the very top of the page. Our hypothesis is that having the gift array at the bottom of the page gives the prospective donor to read supporting copy which makes them more likely to give. In addition, we made the gift array form a tabbed form (just like their previous template). Our hypothesis is that their previous template would generate more donations. The previous template guides the donor by presenting them a reason to give and making the one-time and recurring giving options easier to identify through the tabbed form. We also removed the navigation at the top of the page as part of this radical redesign.
We ran this experiment on a direct appeal premium offer running on Facebook.
experiments.keyLearnings: This experiment resulted in a 212% lift in donor conversion rate with a level of confidence of 96% - making this a valid increase. This tells us that the changes made to this instant donation page make a better experience for the prospective donor: they're able to read why  they should give before taking action, they are given a clearer selection of a one time vs monthly gift, and they are giving a clearer call to action by removing the external links in the navigation bar.

experiments.title: How adding a header image on an instant donation page affects donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that removing the header image on this instant donation page for prospective donors will achieve a higher donor conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/21/2022 - 05/15/2022
Boy Scouts of America is running the National Scouting Survey as a digital acquisition offer through paid Facebook advertising. This survey was crafted to get the feedback and opinions of BSA from former and current Scouts along with other people that have connections with Scouting.
We ran this experiment on the instant donation page to test if having a header image affects donor conversion rate. BSA previously had header images on all donation pages, so we wanted to see how a page without an image would perform.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The removal of the header image resulted in a 367.1% increase in donations with a 98% level of confidence. This informs us that removing the image removes an element of friction and/or distraction from the page causing users to be more likely to give. We believe by removing the header image that the page seems shorter and less of a time commitment.
Another notable impact from this experiment was for mobile users visiting this page. We saw 201% increase in donor conversion rate with a level of confidence of 84.7%. We believe removing the image provided the mobile user with a better page experience.
All in all, we believe the header image does not provide any value to the donation page, but we would like to further test this on other instant donation pages for more digital acquisition offers.

experiments.title: How adding a security box around payment fields affects instant conversion rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a security box around payment fields on an instant donation page will achieve higher instant conversion rates on a content acquisition offer.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/22/2022 - 08/20/2022
The donation form used by a public policy client, while secure, did not use any visual cues to reinforce this to prospective donors. While our client knew that the form was indeed secure, we hypothesized that the lacking visual cues could potentially be causing friction for donors who were concerned about submitting their payment if the form was not secure. And even if this was not causing friction in the minds of potential donors, we thought that making the form’s security obvious with visual cues could be a motivating factor for people who were ‘on the fence’, by demonstrating that the security of their sensitive data was important to this organization.
We ran this A/B test over a period of one month with traffic split 50/50.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running this experiment for one month, we were able to validate a 49% lift in instant conversion rates with a 97.5% level of confidence.
These results validated our hypothesis that visually reinforcing the security of payment fields on a donation form would reduce friction, reinforce legitimacy, and ultimately lead to an increase in the number of people who completed the donation process.
The positive results seen across many key visitor segments lead us to believe that using visual cues to reinforce the security of a potential donor's transaction plays an important role in reassuring donors that their data is safe, which ultimately influences their willingness to give. When reviewing this experiment, we also observed that the colored security box worked to break the donation process into chunks, potentially reducing friction during the donation process.
Because we were unable to validate these results for mobile users, we may want to test this secure donation form treatment for mobile visitors before rolling it out to all forms.

experiments.title: How adding a visual treatment to a tabbed donation form during a non-urgent campaign season affects donor conversions
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a visual treatment to a monthly giving tab on the main donation page  for visitors to a general donation page will achieve a greater number of recurring gift givers.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/01/2022 - 08/21/2022
Focus on the Family is a global Christian ministry dedicated to helping families thrive. They provide help and resources for couples to build healthy marriages that reflect God’s design, and for parents to raise their children according to morals and values grounded in biblical principles.
While they have a main donation page that is always reachable from the main page, they often run high urgency campaigns that can sometimes skew results for general donations. For the time period of this test, Focus was not planning on having any high urgency campaigns running, so we felt it was a good time to test some things on the main donation page, without the potential results skewing that can sometimes be attributed to high urgency campaigns, where motivations to give can be elevated.
The main donation page has a two-tabbed design that separates a one-time gift from a monthly donation. We wanted to test our hypothesis that by visually drawing more attention to a monthly donation tab on a general donation page, you could increase overall donations (and specifically for recurring giving), even during a period of non-urgency.
For this test, we placed an animated heart that floated above the monthly tab that drew attention to the monthly gift option. The goal of the test was to see if this treatment could get a lift in monthly recurring gifts.
experiments.keyLearnings:
While there was potentially a novelty effect from this experiment, it was very interesting that the treatment for this test validated with a 97.7% level of confidence giving the treatment a 19.3% increase in the total number of donations for all traffic. Again, this experiment ran during a period where there were no urgent campaign factors that could have potentially skewed the results. As for the effect on recurring donations, the treatment experienced a 99.4% increase with a 92% level of confidence.
Of particular note, the experiment received a much larger lift in donations from the desktop, with a 25% lift and a 100% level of confidence. For recurring donations on the desktop, we saw a massive 141% lift in recurring donations with a 94.4% level of confidence (0.1% shy of validating).
On mobile, the inverse was true. We saw an 11% decrease in donations w/ a 50% LoC, and recurring donations on mobile were completely flat, with no change
Key takeaways: Drawing attention to the recurring donation tab on a desktop setting had a positive lift and is worth iterating on to see if we can get an additional lift with variations of the animation effect...seasonality changes, color, etc. As for mobile, we should experiment with more subtle ways to get the same desired effect as the desktop. It was clear that the mobile experience was not working in it's current state, and could use some additional help.

experiments.title: How adding credibility through evidentials on a landing page increased course sign ups
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that increasing authority and evidentials for landing page visitors will achieve an increase in free online course registrations.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/17/2022 - 07/18/2022
A new free online course was taught by the President of the Seminary. We had seen other organizations using evidentials in the form of testimonials to improve email acquisition results and we wanted to test this on the registration page to see if we could increase conversion by adding an element to enhance the visitor’s level of trust and perceived authority of the offer. We built a treatment that included a photo and quote from the online course instructor about what key learnings they would take away from the course by sharing what he personally has learned from studying the book.
experiments.keyLearnings: After running the treatment for one month, we saw a 34.7% increase in email conversion rate with 99.9% level of confidence so the results are valid. We found that adding the President's name, likeness, and personal testimonial and affiliation with the course to the body of the acquisition page gave the offer more credibility by providing evidentials to why a person should sign up for the course and increased the likelihood that a visitor would give away their email address to move forward in signing up for the course. We will want to test this concept using other professors for other courses to see if the credibility holds equal weight across other faculty members or if using the Seminary President gave the offer more credibility. We'll find additional opportunities to promote trust and authority to see if this lift can be replicated for multiple course offerings.

experiments.title: How changing the donation button on website to go directly to a donation form impacted results.

rows.name: Replace The Drop Down with Donation Button

experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that reducing friction on a donation button for a web visitor will achieve a higher conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/26/2021 - 01/27/2022
The Hoover Institution is one of the country’s oldest think tanks, and offers a very wide variety of research to the public. The donation button on their homepage traditionally linked to a ‘ways to give’ page, since Hoover has a variety of ways the public can support their research. We wanted to test whether directing that button directly to a donation form would increase conversion rates. We ran this test from the end of October through the end of January, and while we originally started it as a 50/50 traffic test, during their calendar-year-end campaign we pushed 90% of the traffic directly to the donation form.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Despite the larger sample size (due to shifting more traffic towards the test variant), our hypothesis held true: an increase of 85% in donor conversion rates with a 99.6% level of confidence.
By reducing the amount of clicks, and thus friction, potential donors had to take to support the work of the Hoover Institution, this experiment showed that when someone clicks on a donate/support button, their intention is to make a donation as fast as possible. Adding in different ways to give only increases the friction points.
Moving forward, we will continue to experiment with this, as well as adding in a monthly donate button with the launch of Hoover's new monthly supporter program in early 2022.

experiments.title: How minimized navigation and design affects conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a stripped down page for birthday searchers will achieve an increase in sponsor conversion.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/04/2021 - 02/16/2021
Compassion International had found “birthday search” to be a compelling offer for new donor acquisition. For many years, they had leveraged a stripped down page to present this offer, with confetti and a headline that gave the visitor a reason to give. However, they had never tested this against a similar landing page experience that lived within the general Compassion design. They wanted to understand whether the old template, which was outside of current brand standards, was worth keeping up. They decided to test this through an A/B test.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment, with the stripped down navigation (which removed the Compassion "login" link), produced a 36.9% decrease in conversion. However, it's important to understand exactly what happened here, as there were two different responses based on the device type.
First, mobile visitors had a 45.0% decrease in conversion - with a 100% level of confidence. Since most of the traffic was mobile, this was the deciding factor in the test. However, desktop traffic had a 235.8% increase in conversion - with a 94% level of confidence.
This shows that different designs can produce opposite results with the same page—which stresses the importance of responsive design. It also may hint that something was wrong with the mobile version of the treatment that might be corrected to produce a similar result.

experiments.title: How does adding cues to the donation form affect donation rates?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding conversational cues for donation page visitors will achieve higher conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/01/2019 - 12/14/2019
Heritage wanted to test a way to improve donation rates on their main donation page.
For this experiment, they decided to try putting numbered cues/instructions above each step of the donation form to see if this helped ‘pull’ the donor along the donation process and reduce friction potentially created by the amount of fields necessitated by transaction donation forms.
This experiment was run for 41 days with a 50/50 split in traffic.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This experiment validated the hypothesis by yielding a 19% lift in donation rates with a 99% level of confidence. This experiment also produced a 126% lift in revenue with a 95% level of confidence.

Breaking up the donation form fields with conversational cues helps to break the donation form into more digestible chunks, helping to 'pull' the donor through the process while reducing friction and thus the chance for a donor to reconsider their contribution. 

Due to the positive results of this experiment, it would be worth testing this on instant donation pages and campaign donation pages to futher validate this learning.

experiments.title: How radically redesigning a landing page with clarifying copy and removing navigation effects email acquisition rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that removing the navigation and providing clarity in the page copy for users signing up for this online course will achieve a higher email acquisition rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/16/2022 - 08/04/2022
Luther Seminary had been running their acquisition pages with navigation in the header and footer of the page. They wanted to test what would happen if we removed the navigation from the page to see if this would increase email acquisition.
We tested this concept on their Cosmic Adventure online course landing page. On top of this page having the navigation bars, the offer was not performing well for a consistent amount of time, so they also wanted to test changing the headline and body copy of the page to see if providing clarity for the user as to what they would receive in this course would help increase email acquisition.
The original page language was vague throughout the headline, sub-headline, and body copy. Within the new copy, we wanted to be very specific in the headline and subheadline by stating exactly what this course would do for them – give them answers to questions between science and faith through an informative course. Within the body copy, we wanted to focus on curiosity and clarity.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This experiment (treatment page 2) produced a valid increase in the email sign-up rate of 44.7% with a 96.6 level of confidence. In looking at all three variants, we believe that removing the navigation in the header and footer provided the biggest impact on our sign-up rate. On top of that, changing the page copy to be more clarifying for the user as to what this course will give them ultimately provided us with the valid increase needed.
It is recommended that this be tested on other acquisition pages separately, so we can see the true impact of removing navigation vs adding clarifying copy.
Notable changes to other metrics due to the experiment:
Revenue experienced a 113.8% increase with a 86% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How removing a progress thermometer to a donation page affects donation rates during a high urgency campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that removing a progress thermometer on a campaign donation page will result in lower conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/19/2022 - 08/08/2022
In previous high-urgency campaigns, we’ve had success increasing conversion rates by showing a progress thermometer on the top of the donation page once we were nearing our goal. Knowing this, we wanted to see if we could replicate these results during a recent high-urgency campaign for a public policy client. We hoped to increase conversion rates by inspiring prospects to be one of the donors that helped “fill the bar” and help us meet our goal.
This concept attempts to employ the principles of urgency (we only have X to go), social proof (many others found this cause worthy to support), and unity (belonging to a group) in an effort to inspire donors to act.
We initially planned to show the progress thermometer to 100% of traffic, but then we decided to test this concept again and instead split traffic 50/50 between the control (including the progress thermometer) and the treatment (without the progress thermometer), hypothesizing that removing the thermometer would reduce conversion rates.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Despite previous success using the progress thermometer concept during high urgency campaigns, in this instance removing the thermometer produced a valid lift of 17.1% at a 99% level of confidence.
Interestingly, while removing the progress thermometer increased donations, it also resulted in an 8.7% decrease in revenue. And while we were unable to validate this decrease (75.9% level of confidence), when we combine this finding with the fact that the average gift given by donors increased by 24.8% with the thermometer, it appears that while removing the thermometer increased the amount of gifts received, including the thermometer improved the quality of gifts and ultimately raised more money—presumably by motivating donors to give more so that the goal could be reached and because the amount of money already raised indicated that this was a worthy cause to support.
Because of the mixed results produced by this experiment, it may be worth seeking additional opportunities to test this hypothesis again, focusing on revenue, in an effort to validate the portion of our findings that indicated that including the progress thermometer increased overall revenue during the time this experiment was run.

experiments.title: How creating a security-themed visual enhancement to a secure donation form affects donor conversions
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a visual treatment to the donation form that appears more secure for potential donors entering their credit card info will achieve a greater completion rate and a higher number of donations.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/20/2022 - 06/18/2022
Focus on the Family uses a donation platform that has a fairly standard contact and payment form. The current platform provider uses a form that asks for name and credit card info and is also located on a secure server, but we felt like the form could use some visual enhancement around security features. While the native form was indeed secure, we felt like creating some visual call-outs around the area where you enter your credit card info might actually lead to more completed transactions and thus, more donations. The treatment for this experiment placed a grey box around the credit card info along with a secure lock icon. The control remained the standard layout of a white box with no lock icon. We ran this test on a single content offer that was being promoted on Facebook, so that we could have enough traffic to potentially validate the results.
experiments.keyLearnings: As hypothesized, the treatment with greater perceived security did create a sizeable lift in transaction completitions and total donations. We saw a massive 126% lift in total donations with a 97.8% level of confidence. The perceived increase in security had the effect we were looking for - an increase in form completions and a greater number of donations, but it also was an experiment that had very little potential downside. With this big of a lift, we felt the improvements were drastic enough to roll out the treatment as a standardization across all 1500+ donation forms on Focus on the Family. We started by creating a personalization through Google Optimize that would apply the treatment to all donation forms. Once we saw that the treatment did not have any adverse effects for other known offers with donation forms, we worked with the team at Anedot (the donation platform provider) to open up the source code to allow us to hard code this as a standardized feature for all forms. This has now been implemented as the control going forward. This is a continuation of multiple tactical tests that we completed to improve the performance of a standard donation form for Focus on the Family. Future variants of this test could be testing if background color variations improve conversions or if additional callout text increases form fill completion rates.

experiments.title: How an animated heart that calls attention to monthly giving affects recurring donations
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding an animated heart to the monthly donate tab on the main donation page will increase donor conversion.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/16/2022 - 07/23/2022
FamilyLife is a Christian ministry serving families worldwide. For their main donation page, they use a tabbed donation form allowing visitors to select between a monthly and one-time gift. Based on testing with other organizations, we wanted to see the impact of testing an animated heart on the monthly giving tab that would “float” up when clicked.
Due to limitations with their donor platform’s integration with Google Analytics, we were not able to test the impact on monthly donor conversion, however we were able to test the impact on  overall donor conversion (both onetime and monthly gifts).  We split the traffic 50/50 and ran this for just over a month.
experiments.keyLearnings:
While we were not able to see the impact on only recurring gifts, we did measure a statistically significant impact on conversion for all donations. This shows us that the addition of an animated heart increased the appeal of the overall donation page and drew attention to the form at the point of conversion.
We want to test this approach next on acquisition donation pages to see the impact on conversion for tat audience since new visitors converted so highly in this experiment.

experiments.title: How strengthening the value proposition with a radical redesign to the copy on an instant donation page affects instant conversion rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that strengthening the value proposition on an instant donation page will achieve higher instant conversion rates for a paid acquisition campaign.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/12/2022 - 08/14/2022
We had been running a content offer for a free ebook on CRT produced by one of our public policy clients. At the time of this experiment, the offer had produced a healthy email acquisition rate of 46.7% but suffered from a low instant conversion rate (ICR) of just 1.8%.
After reviewing the instant donation page, we quickly identified an opportunity to inspire more people to give by improving the offer’s value proposition—why a potential donor should give to our client, instead of someone else, or not at all—by improving its appeal, clarity, exclusivity, and credibility.
Since other key metrics were trending positive, we felt that we were reaching the right people with the right offer and that improving the value proposition around an issue our audience cared about presented a significant opportunity to improve the performance of this campaign.
Improving the value proposition for this offer meant we would need to change a number of variables in the way the story was being presented through the copy on the instant donation page.
To improve appeal, we replaced passive, informative language with copy that focused first on the urgency of the problem, then the specific impact the prospect’s donation would have—and the specific things they would help the organization do to fight back. 
To improve clarity, we broke the copy into smaller, concisely written paragraphs that focused on continuing the narrative forward in a logical linear fashion, reducing potential friction caused by the meandering nature of the control. We also put the call to action front and center in a headline to make it clear to the prospective donor exactly why they were there.
To improve exclusivity, we replaced generalized impact language with persuasive bullet points that expressed not only what the organization was doing to solve the problem, but also how, and the impact these actions would have
To improve credibility, we used our last bullet point to present the model legislation that our client wrote and which was already being used by lawmakers in several states to ban CRT from schools—something we felt clearly set them apart from other organizations
experiments.keyLearnings:
The results of our experiment were encouraging: our focus on improving the value proposition produced a valid 25% lift in donations with a 95.2% level of confidence for all traffic and a directional lift of 58.3% directional lift in donations (91.7% LoC) for paid social traffic.
The treatment also produced a 21.3% lift in revenue (87% LoC) for all traffic and a 113% directional lift in revenue for paid social traffic.
And while we are encouraged by these results, importantly, it turns out that we changed an additional variable by personalizing the confirmation message at the top of the page with the prospective donor's first name. We will still want to verify these results by running a subsequent experiment to isolate the impact of first name personalization vs. strengthening of the value prop.

experiments.title: How drawing attention to a monthly gift option through an animated icon impacts conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that increasing attention to the monthly giving option for donation page visitors will achieve an increase in recurring donations.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/07/2022 - 04/23/2022
Monthly giving is a priority to Care Net. Whether through acquisition efforts or converting donors downstream, they are constantly finding opportunities to activate recurring donors. On their primary donation page, we have tested into different tactics to increase this metric and are always looking for new ideas to test on this page. When looking at the current layout we hypothesized drawing attention to the monthly gift option might help improve monthly donor conversion. To achieve this, we created an animated heart on the monthly gift tab where the heart would float up when a donor arrived on the page.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The animated heart increased recurring donations by 97% (directionally). The sample size is small and the level of confidence was 86%, but this shows us that we can potentially increase response rate by applying tactics like this.

Notable changes to other metrics due to the experiment:
Overall donations experienced a 21.6% increase with a 89% level of confidence.
 	Revenue experienced a 68.7% decrease with a 90% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How removing a video thumbnail impacted email engagement
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that removing the video image from the email for ACI subscribers will achieve increased click through rate due to the increased deliverability rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/12/2022 - 05/23/2022
Experimentation with similar organizations has found that the more “stripped down” an email is, the better overall deliverability is which often results in improved engagement. This is due to how providers like Gmail and Yahoo categorize emails with images in them.
With this in mind, we decided to run an experiment for the most recent Cornerstone Conversation email. The entire purpose of the email is to drive engagement for people to view the YouTube video. We wanted to see if removing the YouTube image would improve deliverability enough to drive additional clicks to the video.
experiments.keyLearnings:
By removing the video screenshot from the email, we observed a statistically significant 16.9% decrease in clicks. Additionally, we did not see any increase in deliverability or opens from the removal of the image. In this case, it would appear that the single image didn't impact the deliverability of the email or result in the email being added to the "Promotions" tab which highly designed emails can often do.
In subsequent email, we will be sure to include a screenshot of any videos or even audio recordings to help improve click through rate. Additionally, we'll want to test utilizing Dr. Carson's likeness in emails to see if that helps further drive engagement on emails where a donation is the primary call to action.

experiments.title: How replacing imagery with content impacts donor conversion from email
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that emphasizing the value proposition for email subscribers will achieve increased donations.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 06/29/2021
As American Cornerstone Institute was launching their new organization, they utilized a previously established email file that was created during Dr. Carson’s presidential run. These subscribers had a prior affinity for the ACI Chairman which would indicate they were politically conservative.
In launching their organization, ACI secured an endorsement from President Trump and wanted to promote this to the email file as a way to get people to support the new organization. However, we had the hypothesis that focusing on the value proposition behind the organization would be more impactful than just the single quote. We decided to test this with two treatments: one that expanded on the reason behind ACI and one that did that while also including the quote from President Trump.
experiments.keyLearnings: The most significant learning was that focusing on the "why" behind ACI led to dramatically more donations (478% and 518%). We cannot rely solely on imagery of known political figures to carry the messaging. It is also worth noting that removing the President Trump quote resulted in an increase in donations. It is not statistically significant but it is something that is worth isolating in future experiments.

experiments.title: How a delayed pop-up affects email acquisition rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that triggering a pop-up in a shorter duration for site visitors will achieve a better survey completion/email capture rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/02/2022 - 05/22/2022
We built a National Scouting survey pop-up modal to live on the BSA homepage to drive survey completions. Internal teams were concerned with site UX and asked that we set pop-up to fire after the user was on the page for 15 seconds. We believed a shorter duration of 5 seconds would produce a better result and so we created a split test for half of the users to see a pop-up at 15 seconds and the other half to receive it after 5 seconds had elapsed.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The 5 second pop-up clearly outperformed waiting 15 seconds to trigger the modal. The 5 second variant acquired 53 emails versus the 23 acquired by the 15 second version. The 15 second version underperformed the 5 second version by 56% with a 99.9% confidence rate, proving that the shorted trigger duration was clearly the stronger performing variant.
Hypothesis for this performance has more to do with time on page and were users even on the page for 15 seconds to see the longer triggered variant on the page or had they already navigated elsewhere within the BSA site.
Follow up test will be to shorten the pop-up trigger from 5 seconds to 2 seconds. Test to follow this one will test trigger time versus triggering pop-up upon page scroll.

experiments.title: How a radical redesign on an ebook offer impacted email signups
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a radical redesign  for landing page visitors will achieve an increase in email acquisition.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/29/2022 - 04/16/2022
When reviewing landing page conversion across several offers, we found an eBook that was underperforming. We compared this landing page to higher-performing offers and found a handful of differences that could be optimized for higher conversion. We built a treatment that added social proof – “Downloaded over 2,500 times!” and revised the headline to offer more clarity about the desired action: “Get your FREE copy of Knowing Critical Race Theory When You See It and Fighting It When You Can today!” We also revised the opening paragraph to indicate more relevancy and urgency from “For years” to “Today/right now” and removed the distracting call to action of “And please share it with family and friends” from the last paragraph.
experiments.keyLearnings: We ran the experiment for about 2 weeks and observed a 9.9% decrease in conversion rate with 98.3% level of confidence so the test results are valid. Two different segments saw conflicting results which are important to note: 25-34 aged visitors had a 57.1% decrease in email acquisition with a 100% level of confidence while 55-64 aged visitors had a 22.1% increase in email acquisition with a 93% level of confidence. It is important to keep in mind for future tests that even though the treatment reduced conversion overall, we did see a lift on a key target demographic but for a much smaller sample size. Since the control had low performance and the treatment worsened it, we believe there is a larger issue with the offer copy on this landing page that needs to be re-evaluated and revised for future testing. We will look at ways to enhance the value of the offer and give further clarity on the contents. Because we are seeing the majority of traffic come from the 65+ age range, we will incorporate language that addresses not only children but also grandchildren in the treatment copy on the next experiment.

experiments.title: How a radical redesign of a donation page affects revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that implementing a main donate page radical redesign for site visitors  will achieve an increase in revenue .
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/03/2022 - 04/05/2022
Reasons to Believe moved to a new donation platform and had some limitations in their page design elements. In addition to these complications, there have been adjustments made to their man donation page over time as needs arose within the organization. This meant that elements, fields, and placements were added out of necessity. We also saw that the placement of different page elements were skewed based on the size of the screen. We hypothesized that these changes could be affecting their revenue. We wanted to implement a radical redesign experiment where we could address all these issues.
Traffic to this main donate page came primarily through organic traffic.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The organization experienced a valid revenue lift of 81%. These findings tell us that the initial donation page had a lot of friction for the user. It can be hypothesized that even the layout of the copy (which was crowded and oddly spaced on different screen sizes initially), drew the prospective donor to read the value prop outlined and inspired larger gifts. Adding elements like the box and lock icon over the payment method was also introduced as way to improve results.
It should also be noted that their donation pages were experiencing captcha issues that were stalling out donation submissions. There was also a fix implemented on this page through this experiment. The impact of this fix is unknown.
Next steps include:
testing this new layout on their offers' instant donation pages. Isolating the captcha issue into its own experiment in order to determine revenue/conversion impact

experiments.title: How amplifying the design and repositioning the tribute widget on the personal home page affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that repositioning the tribute widget and amplifying the design for personal home page visitors will achieve an increase in conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/06/2022
CaringBridge’s “personal home page” had been a low-volume, high conversion rate opportunity for tribute gifts. However, their design team noticed that the layout and ordering of the comments section and the tribute widget was confusing and obscured. They proposed two treatments to the current design.
The first repositioned the tribute widget above the comment section and provided an outline and contrast change to the widget itself to allow it to stand out more.
Then, they expanded the comment section beyond the current drop down so that it, too was more prominent. They launched a three-way test to determine a winner.
experiments.keyLearnings: The first, most radical treatment produced a 32.3% increase in conversion rate by bringing more attention to the tribute widget and bringing it higher in the page order. However, the amplification of the design was a positive factor on it's own—the second treatment produced a 6.8% directional lift even with the widget de-prioritized on the page.

experiments.title: How an instant donation page radical redesign impacted gifts from email traffic
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a radically redesigned donation page with clearly outlined value proposition for survey responders from a housefile email will achieve an increase in donor conversion.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/14/2021 - 01/06/2022
Ahead of calendar year-end appeals, we sent a reader survey as a cultivation piece to engage the housefile audience. After they completed their survey, they were redirected to an instant donation page. We wanted to test a radical redesign of the donation page, optimizing various elements to see how potential donors would respond. We removed all navigation from the header/footer, removed the image from the donation page, and replaced the space above the form with value proposition. Throughout the donation page copy we highlight the relevance and urgency of the case for support, use messaging that incorporates Scripture and aligns with the donor’s beliefs, ask for a gift and then explain clearly what their gift will accomplish and give instructions on exactly how to make their gift if interested. We ran an A/B test to split traffic to each version of the page after the survey was submitted.
experiments.keyLearnings:
What we found when we analyzed donations and revenue from email traffic is that we saw a 68.8% lift in donations with 96.5% confidence and a 7.1% lift in average gift on the radically redesigned treatment page. This led to an overall 68% increase in revenue from the new donation page. We learned that potential donors responded well to the donation page copy that acknowledged their survey submission and led into a relevant ask based on their previous action and aligned with what we know about their beliefs and engagement with TGC content. We will continue to introduce value proposition for other donation pages across pathways and channels to improve conversion in the future.
Observations of the impact on other visitor segments in the experiment:
Mobile visitors had an 80.9% increase in donations with an 85% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How a radically redesigned main donation page impacted gifts at calendar year-end
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a radical redesign for a main donation page at calendar year-end will achieve an increase in donor conversion.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/22/2021 - 01/01/2022
Because we saw a lift in donor response in a previous experiment when adding relevant value proposition to a post-survey donation page, we wanted to introduce similar language on the main donation page for TGC during their calendar year-end campaign. In addition to outlining the value proposition in the body copy, we revised the headline to indicate how the donor’s gift would be used and mention the upcoming deadline. We also added a countdown clock at the top of the page to indicate urgency and added a live thermometer at the bottom of the page to show progress toward the revenue goal. Beneath the donation button we added a sentence of supporting value proposition demonstrating the impact TGC donors have had throughout the year. Finally, we removed the navigation bar and alternate conflicting calls to action in the footer to keep the donor on the page and stay focused on moving forward with making their gift.
experiments.keyLearnings: After running the treatment on the main donation page during the last 10 days of the month, we observed an 80% increase in donor conversion with 99% level of confidence. By creating a specific value proposition and highlighting urgency, the treatment outperformed the general main donation page during this timeframe. For future experiments, we will look into which individual elements from this experiment could be re-tested on the main donation page to improve conversion on a more evergreen basis. TGC donors seemed to respond well to the treatment elements but we will want to condense and isolate some of the variables to identify those that can continually move the needle for traffic in general outside of a high urgency campaign.

experiments.title: How a visual redesign of a main donate button on the website impacted clicks

rows.name: New - Donate to TGC Green Button

experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that visually emphasizing the donate button for homepage visitors will achieve an increase in clicks to the donation page.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/25/2022 - 03/03/2022
The TGC team was inspired by another experiment they saw in a NextAfter Institute webinar to visually modify the main donate button on their website navigation bar. Rather than rolling out the new version based solely on another organization’s results, we decided to test into the optimal design by splitting traffic 50/50 and measuring the results of how many people clicked on each version of the button. The existing button said “Give” and was visually the same as the other calls to action in the navigation header. The treatment was a green button to help it stand out visually from the rest of the page and the text was revised to “Donate to TGC” to add clarity.
experiments.keyLearnings: After running the experiment for one month, we saw a valid increase in clicks on the new green button that said "Donate to TGC". What was interesting, however, is that donations and revenue recorded on the donation page were actually higher coming from the control version. Because an experiment was simultaneously running on the main donation page that could have been responsible for impacting these results, we will have to take this into account on a future experiment. Overall we learned that the new button design did, in fact, attract significantly more visitors to the main donate page.

experiments.title: How a visually redesigned landing page impacted email conversion for a content offer
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a visually redesigned landing page for a content offer will achieve an increase in email conversion.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/28/2022 - 02/18/2022
When launching a new acquisition offer, we wanted to test a more highly visual layout that would be more in line with the organization’s branding and design themes against the standard stripped-down acquisition page. The designed layout is similar to past offers TGC had displayed for its visitors, but this was the first time they were using clear value proposition and an optimized call to action so we were curious which version would perform better.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Overall, the treatment produced a 7.6% increase in emails acquired. Looking deeper into the results, however, we see that this actually had a 32% decrease for visitors in the age 55-64 segment. This is important to note as this is a segment that generally has a higher propensity to give. Based on this analysis we could look at targeting visitors based on segment and leading them to a custom landing page based on their likelihood to convert. There are a few elements on the treatment page we would like to further test including moving all value proposition above the form and moving the graphic to the right-hand side of the page that we will build as another experiment and continue to improve email acquisition rates for this page.

experiments.title: How redesigning a landing page affects email sign-ups
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that simplifying the landing page for users will achieve a higher email acquisition rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/30/2022 - 02/19/2022
Cosmic Adventure is an online course by Luther Seminary that discusses how science and faith enhance each other.
We tested two factors on the Cosmic Adventure landing page:
Removing the navigation bar and footer
Being more descriptive in the headline and subheadline
We wanted to test removing the navigation and footer because we’ve found in previous experiments that those items on a landing page can be a distraction causing users to click out of the page.
We wanted to test the headline and subheadline with new copy to be more descriptive as to what the user is actually signing up for. On the control page, the headline says the course name but fails to immediately say what the course is about.
The user experienced the same funnel through Facebook ads and a housefile email with the test being only on the landing page.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Ultimately, in this experiment, the more specific headline on the landing page decreased email acquistion.
We believe that the language used in the treatment landing page was too descriptive and long for the headline and subheadline of the page. The headline and subheadline of the control were short and more eye-catching which increased the appeal of the offer rather than a user having to "learn" and "register" like the treatment page referred to.
This language can also be tested on other acquisition landing pages.
Email acquisitions experienced a 43.7% decrease with a 99.9% level of confidence.

experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that emphasizing the main donation button for homepage visitors will achieve an increase in traffic to the donation page.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 12/30/2021
Leading up to calendar year-end, we know that visitors to the TGC homepage had a high motivation to give. We wanted to make sure that potentially highly motivated donors could easily find where to make their year-end gift.
We rolled out a new button design on the homepage to see if we could make it easier for potential donors to find the donation page, but we wanted to run this as a test against the old version to capture the impact.
The new button (running as the control for this experiment) read “Donate to TGC” and we made it green to stand out for visual emphasis while the old button (the treatment version) simply said “Give” and visually matched all other menu options in the header.
We split traffic to each version.
experiments.keyLearnings:
What we found was the new button (green button) drove more traffic to the donation page. Of these two buttons, the “Donate to TGC” button provided the additional clarity necessary to increase traffic to the page while the white "Give" button saw a decrease in traffic of 20%.
Because we were running a separate test on the donation page, we did not factor in conversion rate or revenue on the donation page from this experiment. We only wanted to measure the impact on clicks through to the donation page. What we learned is that the more visual button made it easier for potential donors to find the next step we want them to take by adding clarity in the messaging and visually leading them where to go.

experiments.title: How giving a user more choice through carousel sponsorship ads affects clickthrough rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that giving more choice through carousel ads for potential sponsors will achieve an increase in traffic.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 08/24/2021
Prison Fellowship International was continuing to promote child sponsorship through Facebook ads that pulled children dynamically from their website into the ad. They wondered if their single-child approach (one child per ad) put too much pressure on the visitor to sponsor that specific child, rather than giving them a choice. Since they had the choice to use Facebook’s carousel ad feature, they decided to test to see if showing multiple children would give the user more choice and increase the potential that they would sponsor a child.
They launched two campaigns in an A/B test to determine a winner.
experiments.keyLearnings: The treatment carousel ads increased link clicks by 27%, which drove much more traffic to the site. However, conversion rate on the site was too low to determine if it drove a more motivated visitor, because the results are statistically significant, we can know that it does increase user engagement with the ad and does drive more clicks per dollar, resulting in a 36% decrease in cost per click.

experiments.title: How including the first survey question in an email impacted clickthrough rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that including the first question of a survey for a housefile email will achieve more clicks to the survey page.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 08/11/2021
We hypothesized that adding the first question of a survey to give people a preview/example of the questions they would be answering and highlighting an easy entry point to begin their survey that we could increase momentum from the email and get more clicks to the survey page. By featuring one of the questions we provide clarity to the reader about what the survey process looks like and decrease anxiety and friction about the amount of time and work required to complete the survey. We also introduce an opportunity to exercise the concept of a “micro-yes” – once people are nodding their heads and saying yes to answering the first question, we can help them continue saying yes throughout the survey and eventually on the donation page.
experiments.keyLearnings: What we found was the treatment with the first question included in the body copy of the email generated a 59.5% increase in traffic with 99.9% level of confidence. Looking further down the process, we also saw a 40% lift in the number of emails acquired through the treatment with a 97.1% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How a radical redesign with simplified copy impacted a housefile digital appeal
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that simplifying the language for a housefile appeal email will achieve an increase in engagement.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 08/11/2021
As a part of the special legislative session, Texas Public Policy Foundation made an appeal around their proposed plan to lower property taxes.
The digital appeals to the housefile had been losing steam to convert donors in recent months, so they decided to test the language of the appeal and see what they could learn about the audience response.
The original email reflected a lot of policy-specific language and when graded in a readability tool, it was projected to be at a 12th grade reading level. So they developed a treatment that dramatically simplified the language and presented at a 5th grade reading level.
experiments.keyLearnings: What we found was the simplified version of the email led to a 54.5% increase in the overall open rate on the email. Diving in deeper, we were not able to secure enough traffic to validate the clickthrough rate but the treatment had a 16% increase on clicks with a 52% level of confidence. This learning can certainly be replicated and applied for future digital appeals, but can also be tested in the acquisition space to see how prospective audiences respond to simplified language compared to the housefile. We can also identify a few donation pages where we may be able to test this simplified language concept.

experiments.title: How new Facebook ad creative impacted donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that aligning identity through copy and graphics for Facebook ad audiences will achieve improved ad performance.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 08/09/2021
As we significantly increased media budget on Facebook ads, we wanted to run treatment versions of existing free online course ads to optimize our spend and avoid creative fatigue for the digital audiences we were targeting. For the first treatment, we developed new copy that was less academic in nature and introduced experiential language that reflected more of the audience identity. We also increased clarity in the offer by outlining what someone could expect: “Through this course, you’ll find encouragement in God’s Word to deal with daily issues with an eternal mindset.” For the second treatment, we kept the copy the same as the control and simply used a graphic of a Bible verse relevant to the course topic to demonstrate alignment of values and identity.
experiments.keyLearnings: What we found after 3 weeks of running all 3 versions simultaneously was the verse image treatment saw a 227% increase in donor version with a 99.4% level of confidence. It also drove the click rate up from .7% on the control to 1.3% with 100% level of confidence. The treatment that had copy changes only did not validate for donor conversion, and actually decreased the click rate to .5% with 100% level of confidence. The next iteration of the test will be to test a version of the ad with the treatment copy paired with the treatment image so that the concept of identity alignment is present in both elements. We are also testing similar graphics on other online course ads to see if these results are consistent across all offers.

experiments.title: How adding check boxes to a statement of belief impacts donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will adding a checkbox to the acquisition form increase name conversion on the pledge?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/13/2019 - 03/15/2020
Billy Graham Evangelistic Association was running an acquisition offer pledge. People would add their name to this pledge if they aligned with it to show support for the cause. In an effort to increase name conversion on the page, we hypothesized adding a checkbox to the form where people would have to mark if they did, in fact, believe in what they were signing. We split this traffic 50/50 with the control.
experiments.keyLearnings:
By adding the single checkbox to the pledge, name conversion on the page increased by 10.6%!
Observations of the impact on other visitor segments in the experiment:
Desktop visitors had a 26.5% increase with a 100% level of confidence.Mobile visitors had a 9.1% increase with a 100% level of confidence.
The checkbox increased the motivation of the user. It brought confirmation to why they were on the page and to their beliefs. This simple tactic can have a significant impact not only on name conversion as we saw with this experiment, but on donor conversion as well since more people will be getting to the instant donation page presented immediately after a person signs the pledge.

experiments.title: How beginning with value proposition on a product page affects conversions
experiments.researchQuestion: Starting with value proposition will lead to more purchase conversions.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 06/03/2021
FamilyLife resources asked us to optimize their acquisition for their small-group kit products. The pages were built to first show the reader the purchase option and a video. After the video it talked more about what was included. So the page led with the ultimate decision point and ended with why they should purchase the item.
We hypothesized that flipping this progression would lead to more acquisitions. So we altered the page to begin with why this kit was valuable, then tell the reader what it includes and then ask them to purchase it.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The new page design led to an 46% increase in conversions, proving the hypothesis true that explaining value before the call to action is key in acquisition.
Future iterations of this test could include, reducing the navigation and the other product options below.

experiments.title: How presenting gift frequency as the first choice affects donor conversion
experiments.organization: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
categoryNames: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
experiments.researchQuestion: Presenting the giving options in two clear and distinct formats will increase giving.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/04/2021 - 07/02/2021
On the Oblates Main Donation Page, we tested a tabbed giving form. The premise behind this was to see if presenting the different giving options, one-time or monthly, in two distinct giving forms would increase donor conversion. We have seen this work well for some organization’s audiences and not for others. So the first step was to ensure that this format did not decrease giving. The second step will be to see how this format affects recurring donation conversion with different calls to action.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This tabbed form increased giving by 15% and increased revenue by 100% at an 89% LOC. Our hypothesis is that by making the giving options more clear, it diminished the decision friction, which in turn allows the donor to feel good about their choice to give. Overall, the treatment saw $9560 more in revenue than the control (according to Google Optimize).
There was no discernible increase in recurring giving.
The next step is testing including more value proposition around the monthly giving option in the body of the copy as well as in a call out above the tab to see if we can increase recurring giving.

experiments.title: How the order of a signup flow affects conversion on desktop devices
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that reversing the sign-in options for desktop visitors will achieve an increase in created users and sites.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 02/27/2021
CaringBridge had two options to create an account: using your email, or using single-sign-on (SSO) options like “Login with Google”, “Login with AppleID”, or “Login with Facebook”. Traditionally, these had been vertically ordered email-first, then SSO options.
The team wondered if the rising popularity of SSO options (primarily for reasons of security) would imply that these options should be listed first. They created a simple test to flip the options to see if the SSO-first approach would result in more signups and created CaringBridge sites.
experiments.keyLearnings: The SSO option produced a significant decrease in conversion for desktop visitors. There was a slight increase in mobile and tablet signups, which leads to a secondary hypothesis about the ease that SSO options provide. However, to avoid a significant downturn in new sites created, they decided to relaunch the experiment just for mobile and tablet users to see if the lift validated.

experiments.title: How a tabbed form increases giving on a main donation page
experiments.researchQuestion: Presenting the giving options in two clear and distinct formats will increase giving.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/05/2021 - 04/04/2021
For their February and March campaigns, we tested a tabbed giving form on FamilyLife’s main donation page. The premise behind this was to see if presenting the different giving options, one-time or monthly, in two distinct giving forms would increase donor conversion. We have seen this work well for some organization’s audiences and not for others. So the first step was to ensure that this format did not decrease giving. The second step will be to see how this format affects recurring donation conversion with different calls to action.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This tabbed form increased giving by 60.5% and increased revenue by 230%. Our hypothesis is that by making the giving options more clear, it diminished the decision friction, which in turn allows the donor to feel good about their choice to give.
Other notable findings in different segments include that this treatment increased giving on all devices, especially mobile. Both returning and new visitors preferred this format. The only channel that saw a decrease in conversion was paid search, with a decrease of 57%. While both traffic from organic and social saw an increase in donor conversion.
The next step is testing the call to action for the monthly giving option to see if we can increase recurring giving.

experiments.title: How having a text based donation page and email vs video affects donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a text based donation page for those that visit the page will achieve a higher conversion rate and more revenue.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 05/20/2021
This was a campaign geared around raising funds to provide relief efforts to those affected by Covid-19 in India. We wanted to test a video recording of e3’s CEO that showed the devastation happening over in India with an ask to help support the relief efforts vs transcribing the video and having just the copy on the donation page. The video was directly on the donation page with the donation form right below the video and the transcribed copy was in the same spot right above the form.
The goal was to test to see if we could get more eyes on what was actually be communicated on the donation page and increase motivation to make a gift by transcribing the video.
experiments.keyLearnings:
We saw a significant lift with the text-based copy with both conversion rate and revenue. The hypothesis is more people are apt to read the copy on a donation page rather than click to watch a 3 min. video. With the text copy, we learned that you don't need video to communicate the devastation/emotion of what was happening in India and move someone to donate.
Here are the overall results including revenue:
Videodonation page
Gifts: 11Revenue: $1,732Avg gift: $157.45
Text donation page
Gifts: 68Revenue: $12,259Avg gift: $180.27
Because of these great results, it helped motivate e3 (who is heavily video-based) to do a resend of the email with just the text-only version.

experiments.title: How minimized navigation and design affects conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a stripped down page for birthday searchers will achieve an increase in sponsor conversion.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/04/2021 - 02/16/2021
Compassion International had found “birthday search” to be a compelling offer for new donor acquisition. For many years, they had leveraged a stripped down page to present this offer, with confetti and a headline that gave the visitor a reason to give. However, they had never tested this against a similar landing page experience that lived within the general Compassion design. They wanted to understand whether the old template, which was outside of current brand standards, was worth keeping up. They decided to test this through an A/B test.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment, with the stripped down navigation (which removed the Compassion "login" link), produced a 36.9% decrease in conversion. However, it's important to understand exactly what happened here, as there were two different responses based on the device type.

First, mobile visitors had a 45.0% decrease in conversion - with a 100% level of confidence. Since most of the traffic was mobile, this was the deciding factor in the test. However, desktop traffic had a 235.8% increase in conversion - with a 94% level of confidence.

This shows that different designs can produce opposite results with the same page—which stresses the importance of responsive design. It also may hint that something was wrong with the mobile version of the treatment that might be corrected to produce a similar result.

experiments.title: How a reintroducing clickable looking text in an ad impacted donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that clickable text in an ad image for prospective donors will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 04/01/2021
In a recent study, Americans for Prosperity tested their way into a simplified ad experience with an ad image promoting a petition funnel that used what appeared like buttons (saying “Yes” and “No” in response to a question).
Unfortunately, this ad image was eventually rejected by Facebook due to it not adhering to ad policy guidelines that specifically prohibit images that have “fake buttons” on them to generate high clickthrough rates.
Therefore, it forced us to try another approach — one that looked like a banner, and another that looked like “clickable text” in response to the question the ad posed.
Which approach would produce the best donor conversion rate?
experiments.keyLearnings:
With a 99.5% level of confidence, we observed a 238% increase in donor conversion rate when using the "clickable text" approach to the question-based ad promotion.
We also observed the following performance against the banner (control) approach:
+345% in ad clickthrough rate+261% in email signup rates
This ad image approach captured most of the essence of the original "fake button" approach we introduced and tested our way into. So long as your ad image is using what doesn't appear to be a button, it appears that you can meet the standards of the Facebook ad policy, while also maintaining the results that "fake button" approach would produce.

experiments.title: How simplifying your ad impacts donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that simplifying the ad experience for prospective donors will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/17/2021
As a part of an open letter (or petition) promotion campaign, Americans for Prosperity wanted to experiment with simplifying an otherwise verbose and complicated open letter promotion.
They thought that by presenting a simple “Yes” or “No” question to the person the ad was being served to, that it could improve clickthrough and email signup rates, subsequently positively impacting donor conversion and revenue for the same dollar spent to advertise the campaign.
experiments.keyLearnings:
With a 96.2% level of confidence, we observed an increase in donor conversion by +64.4% by simplifying the ad experience in such a way that people would be presented with a simple "Yes" or "No" answer to a question, and would be called to take action today if they aligned with the organization (in this case "No").
In fact, we saw increases at every step of the funnel.
Here is the lift that we achieved across the treatment version of the campaign:
Ad CTR: +268.8% (LoC: 100%)Email Signup: +142.4% (LoC: 100%)Donor Conversion Rate: +64.4% (LoC: 96.2%)Revenue: +52.7% (LoC: 98%)
The results achieved with the simplified approach to the ad experience really started by dramatically decreasing the cost per click — showing a -80% decrease in cost per click while nearly quadrupling the number of clicks we saw against the control experience.
This higher volume of clicks for the same expense delivered similar more cost-effective results all of the way throughout the process, which boosted ROI by 100%, and decreased the cost to acquire an email by -69% and the cost to acquire a donor by more than -40%.
There was one more observation worth mentioning, as well. This approach had such an improvement in post engagement that it contributed to the lowering of costs for the outcomes we received. The question/answer-based approach created shares and comments that put this ad extended the reach of these campaigns. Specifically, we observed an 88% increase in post comments (LoC: 100%) due to this approach.
Where possible, experimenting with a very simple approach to issues related to a reader's beliefs in the form of a "Yes" or "No" question can potentially provide dramatic increases in clicks that the efficiency metrics on cost per outcome drop to levels not seen before.
It should be noted that Facebook has rejected some ad images that (like above) look like they have buttons on them. So, further experimentation may be required to use banners or something similar when/if future ads like this are rejected.

experiments.title: How asking a specific question in an ad impacts clickthrough rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that simplified Facebook ads posing a relevant question for potential donors will achieve an increase in traffic to the donation page.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 04/27/2021
As part of a direct ask campaign for monthly donations, the Texas Public Policy Foundation wanted to test if specific policy ads would outperform a general invitation to join a branded recurring donor program. They had seen how other organizations had seen increases in traffic conversion through simplifying ads and wanted to test the concept on their images for the policy-specific ads.
experiments.keyLearnings:
With a 100% level of confidence, we observed a 1,223.4% lift in click rate on these Facebook ads that included a banner with the option to select "Yes" or "No" to indicate if they align with the organization's stance on specific policy areas. Once the visitors clicked on the call to take action, we were able to present them with the recurring donation ask to support TPPF's efforts in that specific policy area they just told us they have a shared belief.
What's interesting is that when we looked at our donor conversion rate on the donation page,  we actually saw an 80.5% decrease with 100% Level of Confidence—which means that now that we've determined how to increase the number of clicks in order to drive a substantial amount traffic to the page, we can now shift our focus to running experiments to improve conversion on the donation page.
While the increase in traffic is not necessarily producing visits from highly motivated donors to the page, we still see this as a worthwhile effort because once they visit the donation page we have the ability to set up additional campaigns designed to retarget them and find other opportunities to reach them distinctly with content related to their policy interests.

experiments.title: How the redesign of a tribute widget affects donations
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that creating more separation between page sections for journal page visitors will achieve an increase in comments with no change to donations.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/06/2021 - 04/24/2021
CaringBridge’s development team had recently begun to collaborate with the design team on new product iterations. Since the most recent redesign, comments had declined slightly. The team wanted to redesign the area surrounding comments, which included the tribute widget—which led to many donations. Since the redesign of the comments section might have an impact on donations, they decided to test it.
The team redesigned the tribute widget to provide more spacing between the comments and the tribute widget, since both asked for a text input. They also added a header and call-to-action to the top of the comments section to clarify the two sections.
Then they split traffic 50/50 to see how comments and donations were affected by this change.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment produced zero impact on comments, but a 13.8% decrease in conversion rate which led to a similar decrease in revenue.
The hypothesis behind why this happened is that the extra spacing provided a visual break that disrupted the mental progression that the prospective donor was making down the page. The extra separation might have pushed the donation ask out of sight, especially for mobile users (where conversion was reduced 18%).
An additional supporting hypothesis to this result is that the header and CTA on the comments section might have provided a "lesser impact bailout" to people who might have otherwise donated.
Either way, the experiment did not produce a lift in the primary metric (comments) and produced a sharp decrease in the secondary metric (donations), which sent the team back to the drawing board for solutions to increase engagement.

experiments.title: The impact of a Yes/No Image Ad on Facebook for Monthly Donor Acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that enabling immediate feedback via the image design for viewers on Facebook will achieve greater monthly donor conversion.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 05/10/2021
When we tested using an image that enabled visitors to feel like they were providing immediate feedback with their “click,” on our volunteer signup page, we greatly increased conversion. So, we tested this on our monthly donor acquisition campaign as well. We tested this new image format against our prior best ad which was a short video that looped with two images and text.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Not only did the new ad format drastically increase donor conversion, it significantly decreased the cost per click on the ad as well. The control had a CPC of $0.63 vs $0.22 on the variation.
We have rolled out this new format and will see how long it continues to perform well and when (if) the novelty wears off.

experiments.title: The impact of a Yes/No Image Ad on Facebook for volunteer recruitment
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that enabling immediate feedback via the image design for viewers on Facebook will achieve greater volunteer conversion rates .
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 04/29/2021
We wanted to test whether using an image that enabled viewers on Facebook to feel like they were providing immediate feedback would increase conversion. So, we designed a static image with the question of our Facebook Survey on it and a big Yes and a big No option for them to “click” on. While not serving as working buttons, we hypothesized that merely simulating a button would be enough to increase conversion rate.
We tested this against a short video ad created using Facebook’s image/video tool.
experiments.keyLearnings: The feedback ad improved conversion on every metric. Not only did we increase volunteer signups by 191%, we decreased the cost per signup 76% (from $37.95 to 8.93) and the cost per click 81% ($0.37 to $0.07). WE are testing this now on our monthly donor campaign to see if it has similar results.

experiments.title: How an offer-focused image in an ad affects email acquisition rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that employing an offer-focused image for prospective audiences will achieve an increase in conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 05/13/2021
The Navigators were promoting their Digital Discipleship Journey offer to a wide variety of familiar and unfamiliar audiences. The image used in the original ad was meant to display the brand value of connection through discipleship, and featured groups of people together. The team wondered if it could increase email acquisition rate on the Digital Discipleship Journey offer by showing the user an image that related to what they were going to receive, rather than people.
This was the only change they made to the ads, though they loaded several “offer-focused” images into Facebook to give it creative options.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The offer-focused ads produced a 211.2% increase in conversion rate—partially fueled by a 136% increase in clickthrough rate.
This large increase from a small change underscores the prospect's need to understand the offer in a short period of time, and how much clarity can be missing when the offer isn't reflected in the image.
Future testing will focus on taking these learnings—both from the image and ad copy—and installing them on the landing page to test congruency.

Timeframe: 10/12/2020 - 10/20/2020
In this experiment, we were running a pre-sign up offer for a year-end fundraising training course. There was quite a lot of copy on the landing page to communicate the value and various components of the training. And we wondered if using design elements could help users understand the copy better and lead to more conversions.
experiments.keyLearnings: The more designed landing page was the clear winner with a 40% increase in conversion at a 99% level of confidence. Going forward, we can be sure that design elements on a long form landing page like this can be used to make the page more scannable, easier to understand, and higher converting.
