experiments.title: How a less designed, text heavy email affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a less designed email lead to more giving?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 05/01/2020
KUOW Public Radio is Seattle’s NPW news station. Their independent, nonprofit newsroom produces award-winning stories, podcasts and events. For a time sensitive fundraising campaign, KUOW decided to test their usual email design against a more simple, stripped down, less designed version to see if it would lead to more donations.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The stripped down, plain text email led to a 28.8% increase in response rate which was the main goal/focus of the experiment and suggests that KUOW should consider this approach again in future email appeals.
A few other interesting findings:
There was a -24% difference in average gift (mostly driven by two larger than normal donations) but is something worth monitoring in future experiments to ensure this approach leads not only to more donations but more overall revenue.The designed email was 28% better at generating clicks or getting people to the donation page (100% confidence) but produced fewer donations overall which means the click to conversion rate of the stripped down email was actually 79.4% greater at converting clicks to donations (100% confidence). This goes to show that often what gets clicks isn't what gets donations or, as is the case here, actually gets fewer donations.
It seems clear that a stripped down, plain text approach can work for KUOW so now they can begin testing other elements within this strategy like the tone, raw links, referencing past giving, the use of images, length of copy, etc.

experiments.title: How hiding gift designations on a primary donation page impacts donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will hiding the 14 gift designations increase donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/03/2019 - 05/26/2019
On BGEA’s primary donation page, they present 14 different designations for people to choose from when making a gift. In looking at this we hypothesized that the significant array of options for people to choose from may be causing friction in the donation pathway. A person would need to look through all of the 14 giving options and select where they’d like to designate their gift towards before moving on to the next step in the donation process. The test our hypothesis, we hid all 14 designations on the donation page and added a link that people could click on if they wanted to see all of the options.
experiments.keyLearnings: To our surprise, hiding the gift designations decreased donor conversion. This result was true for every device type and user segment (new vs. returning). While we saw a negative impact on overall donor conversion, we hypothesize that we may have seen an increase in "where most needed" but a decrease from the other designations. We are dissecting the data now to look into this hypothesis. We also hypothesize that by eliminating all gift designations, we may have removed some of the most used designations resulting in the decrease in conversion. We will now test into this hypothesis in two ways: hiding most of the designations except for the top 3-5, and then also testing a "most popular" designation by visually setting this off and adding value proposition to it.

experiments.title: How a new radical design impacted email acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: Will the design used with the majority of prior online course registration page (including no video and increased value proposition) increase overall email acquisition?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/29/2019 - 07/29/2019
Hillsdale College was launching a new online course that was hosted by Victor Davis Hanson. This new course had increase production values and a well known teaching who’s name would likely resonate with ideal prospects for this course. To match the highly produced look of the video, a landing page was created that focused on the course trailer with minimal copy.
This new landing page, while aesthetically pleasing, was a significant departure from the standard design used with prior courses. To validate this new approach, we decided to set up an experiment that would put the new landing page design against the design and approach used historically.
experiments.keyLearnings: After running for a full month, we found that the standard design resulted in a 16% decrease to email acquisition. It is hard to know whether it was the inclusion of the trailer video, the more modern design, or the minimized copy that helped move the needle given all of the different elements incorporated. However, knowing that this new look produces better results will both improve acquisition efforts and provide ideas for future testing. Subsequent iterations to this test will allow us to isolate the individual variables in order to determine exactly what increased the overall conversion.

experiments.title: How a radical redesign of the main donation form impacted giving
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a radical redesign of the donation form increase overall donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/21/2019 - 08/15/2019
Given the volume of traffic and the average gift size, we have struggled to validate an experiment on Buckner’s main donation form in the past. With this in mind, we decided to test out a radical new donation form that changed multiple elements that we’ve seen work in the past with similar organizations.
Specifically, we made the following changes:
We modified the form headers to be numbers steps instead of just labels.
We removed the gift array which we’ve seen help with organizations that have a large volume of mid-level and major donors
We increased the perceived security near the credit card form
We reiterated the gift size and impact near the donation button
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running for nearly two months, we observed a 41% decrease in overall donor conversion. When we parsed through the data, what we discovered is that the donor decrease was only seen in the segment of donors giving less than $100. The number of donors giving above that level actually increased in volume.
We were also concurrently running a similar experiment on the Shoes for Orphan Souls (SOS) donation page and it observed an increase in giving using similar changes. However, the SOS donation page had a targeted ask for $25 right before the gift amount section.
Based upon these two factors, it is our belief that removing the gift array buttons negatively impacted the small dollar donor conversion since we did not properly frame up the ideal gift size for the audience. Even though the SOS page had the donation buttons removed, it had the targeted ask which effectively replaced the impact of the buttons. It is our recommendation to relaunch this experiment but add the donation buttons back in to validate this hypothesis.

experiments.title: How increased value proposition and the layout of an acquisition opportunity impacts conversion
experiments.organization: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
categoryNames: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
experiments.researchQuestion: Will the layout and a stronger value proposition improve name conversion on the homepage?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 05/21/2019
On the homepage of the Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate website, they placed an email acquisition opportunity for people to sign-up for their newsletter. The acquisition opportunity had a two column approach and minimal value proposition copy. We hypothesized that strengthening the value proposition in the headline and CTA and changing the layout to be one column, we might be able to increase name conversion from the homepage.
experiments.keyLearnings: The treatment increased name conversion by 25%. By changing the layout to be a one column approach instead of a two column, we decreased friction in the acquisition process by improving the line of sight. In addition to this, by strengthening the value proposition and leaning into an exclusivity factor and then reinforcing it in the CTA, we were able to appeal to more people and convert them into new names for the organization.

experiments.title: How an image-heavy design affects email acquisition rate
experiments.researchQuestion: How will an image-heavy design that reduces text and removes social proof indicators affect email acquisition?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/08/2019 - 04/26/2019
Hillsdale College was promoting their new course, Aristotle’s Ethics. The team created a signup flow that matched their control, but wanted to test an image-heavy treatment that pushed the majority of the copy down the page. They decided to test this to see if it impacted email acquisition, as it was a departure from the norm.
experiments.keyLearnings: The new design produced almost 30% fewer donors than the control. There are several hypotheses as to why. First, the new design's images might have unintentionally obscured the value proposition, rather than enhanced it. Imagery can be helpful if it supports the value proposition, but can be harmful if it gets in the way. The new design also removed the headline, which might have been an integral part of the value proposition. Second, the black on white text has been proven to increase reading difficulty, which might have contributed to the drop. Finally, the removal of social proof indicators might have inadvertently taken away a key motivating factor for a user to sign up. This test was insightful as it highlighted factors that might need to be enhanced in the control to increase conversion.

experiments.title: How the design of a donation page and value proposition affect donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: How will a radical redesign of the donation template affect donations?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/26/2018 - 04/09/2018
Leadership Institute saw less than optimal conversion on their donation pages. A review of their main donation page, as well as the donation page for one of their program websites showed that they had minimal value proposition copy, a wide layout, and a two-column form that required a CAPTCHA to complete. They built a new donation microsite that radically streamlined and reordered the donation process, added value proposition copy, removed the required CAPTCHA.
They split traffic between their two main donation pages to determine the impact of this new donation microsite on giving.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The new donation microsite resulted in more than twice as many donations across the two primary pages. Since this was a radical redesign, it’s not clear how each individual element contributed to the lift. But the lift is statistically valid and the opportunity cost is clear.
The new donation microsite also enables future optimization testing, which should begin immediately across multiple donation pages.

experiments.title: Impact of streamlining the layout and copy of a landing page
experiments.researchQuestion: Would streamlining the copy, removing the separate header, and bringing congruency between the header and form increase conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/18/2019 - 04/23/2019
Our free online course, Choosing Life, had not been performing as well as we wanted. We were driving traffic to the landing page through Facebook ads linking to a pledge. After signing the pledge, visitors would see the course signup page. In reviewing the page, I noticed that the template separated the headline from the body. I also noticed that some of the text seemed unnecessary. Furthermore, the headline was not clearly linked to the CTA on the form.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Ensuring that all the text was in the same "box" on the template ensured that visitors wouldn't gloss over the headline. By changing the headline, it was clear from the start what action we wanted them to take. Finally, by streamlining the copy, the value proposition was clearer as well.
Copy length should be long enough to articulate the value of the offer, but short enough to not lose the visitor.

experiments.title: How a radical redesign that reduces friction and increases the force of the value proposition affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a radical redesign that reduces friction and increases the force of the value proposition increase donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/22/2016 - 12/01/2016
Americans for Prosperity wanted to increase the conversion rate on their main donation page. Even though visitors clicked the button “donate” to get there, only two percent of them actually followed through. They hypothesized that there were two factors that reduced conversion rate:
Lack of an effective value proposition. The original page did not offer much reason to give. The headline “Join the fight” could be used by many organizations, reducing its exclusivity. The copy talked a lot about AFP, but did not address the donor’s involvement or have a clear call-to-action.
High friction. The donation page had navigation links in the header and footer that led to other parts of the site, which gave visitors plenty of opportunities to leave. It also had a striking background image that possibly distracted the eye from engaging with the copy. And finally, it had a three-step donation form, which might imply that making a donation takes a long time.
The AFP team created a treatment that removed the potential friction factors by taking away the navigation links, removing the background image, and simplifying the form to a single step. They also significantly boosted the value proposition copy by addressing the donor in the headline, creating a sense of “problem” in the copy that AFP was set up to solve, and providing evidentials for the claims of value.
They launched an A/B test to determine a winner.
experiments.keyLearnings: The radical redesign produced a 134.2% increase in conversion, raising conversion above 6% on the main donation page. The combination of reduced friction and increased force of the value proposition more than doubled donor conversion. However, a 6% conversion rate means that 94% of people still aren’t giving when they arrive at the page, so it prompted future testing to refine and further increase the force of the value proposition.

Hillsdale College had recently redesigned their main website. They wanted to give the site a more modern look and to re-align the content with how visitors explored the site.
The majority of their email acquisition pages still utilized the old website design. We wanted to see what kind of impact this new style would have on email acquisition so we launched an experiment to see.
experiments.keyLearnings: The new layout decreased email acquisition by 13.6%. Our hypothesis is that the multiple sections and multiple calls to action decreased the clarity of the offer which subsequently decreased the likelihood for a visitor to sign up.

experiments.title: How clarity in the design of a Facebook ad can affect conversion rates
experiments.researchQuestion: Which Facebook ad design will acquire more email addresses?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/22/2016 - 09/21/2016
When promoting the Why Should I Give to You? research study on nonprofit value proposition, we came to the realization that our Facebook ads were a little bit hard to read. The yellow text against the light gray background seemed to get lost when scrolling through a Facebook feed.
This led us to the hypothesis that a clearer design would yield at least a greater conversion rate. As a result, we developed a treatment version of the ad that presented the same information, but with a clearer design that would stand out more in the Facebook newsfeed.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment saw a 56.7% decrease in conversion rate. Initially, this was very confusing. How could a design that appeared so much stronger be so ineffective by comparison? We almost didn’t even run a test on this new design because we were so certain it would outperform the control.
Taking a second look, we realized that the slight change of phrasing in order to improve the design aesthetic may have decreased the clarity of the content of the offer.
There are two main learnings we can glean from this test:
There are no marketing experts, design experts, advertising experts, etc. There is only experience. And the only way to know what is more effective is to test.
Language and copy that makes sense to you doesn’t always make sense to your audience. In this case, the copy in the treatment design made complete sense to us, yet it appears to not have been quite as clear to the Facebook audience.

experiments.title: How a radical redesign affects the force of the value proposition
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a radical redesign that reduces friction and dramatically increases the force of the value proposition increase donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/01/2013 - 09/30/2013
The Texas State Historical Association had several initiatives designed to grow their membership base. However, the membership page presented tremendous friction. The experience used a shopping cart, though it was asking for donations. It also presented four different membership options, but did not give distinction between them. And the copy on the page was not very specific, and made unclear and unsubstantiated claims.
The team created a radical redesign with longform copy to appropriately convey the value proposition. They added supporting copy that highlighted exclusivity and credibility, and gave a full explanation of why the visitor should join the TSHA as a member. This page also included evidentials to back up each claim. Finally, the membership levels were quantified and given distinct benefits, with one preferred level preselected.
An A/B test was then launched to determine a winner.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This experiment showed how affecting the force of the value proposition can radically affect donor conversion. Though the underlying value proposition of the TSHA remained the same, the information was presented in a way that caused many more visitors to understand it, believe it, and want to join.
This experiment also revealed that TSHA members are readers, which makes complete sense given their affinity for history. Long form copy gives them more information, refuting the commonly held conventional wisdom that “people don’t read on the internet”.

experiments.title: How the name acquisition design and layout can affect email acquisition

experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/10/2015 - 09/17/2015
For their latest eBook offer, the Texas State Historical Association wanted to find a way to increase the number of email addresses they were acquiring.
The current design was the result of a series of tests that optimized the process of email and donor acquisition for a similar eBook offer.  The design was very simplistic and focused on selling the value proposition primarily through text.
We had the hypothesis that a more modernly designed page may capture visitors’ attention better which would lead to an increased number of conversions. We decided to launch an experiment to find out.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The new landing page ended up decreasing the number of emails acquired by 18.8%. After further review of the new treatment, we would hypothesize this decrease is caused by the combination of several factors:
The headline of the page is about what the visitor has to do (“Download”) instead of what they will receive (“Get”)The form and subsequent call-to-action is placed above the copy that describes why they should download. This can work with when the visitors are already highly motivation but in many situations, the visitors will need a reason to convert prior to being asked.The form is placed out of the eye path of the visitor

experiments.title: How a simple design affects clarity and name acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: Which banner will drive the most traffic and allow for the acquisition of the most email addresses?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/19/2015 - 09/28/2015
When launching their latest eBook offer, the Texas State Historical Association wanted to find the best way to drive the organic traffic that visits their site. Historically they had used banners with a simple headline, a design that matched the landing page, and a button with a clear call to action. On Facebook, however, the banners were a bit different due to the limitations on text amount imposed by the social network.  These banners had been highly effective at driving traffic so we decided to test if this same simple style would have a similar impact on organic traffic.
experiments.keyLearnings:
By simplifying the message and design of the banner, we were able to increase the number of emails acquired by 34%. The banner itself was able to drive 20% more traffic to the landing page and those visitors then converted at a much higher rate.
The more highly-designed banner had all of the elements that we normally look for in ads with a clear headline, simple description, and a button with a clear call to action.  All of these same elements were then repeated on the homepage to reinforce the message.  However, the simple banner was able to communicate this same value proposition much more clearly and concisely.

experiments.keyLearnings:
By simplifying the message and design of the banner, we were able to increase the number of emails acquired by 34%. The banner itself was able to drive 20% more traffic to the landing page and those visitors then converted at a much higher rate.
The more highly-designed banner had all of the elements that we normally look for in ads with a clear headline, simple description, and a button with a clear call to action.  All of these same elements were then repeated on the homepage to reinforce the message.  However, the simple banner was able to communicate this same value proposition much more clearly and concisely.

experiments.title: How page layout can affect visitor motivation
experiments.researchQuestion: Does a single column design increase the number of petition signatures compared to a double column design?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/11/2015 - 08/13/2015
The Media Research Center’s mission is to ensure that the media does not try to shape public opinion through biased reporting. When the first video was released documenting the sale of fetal tissue by Planned Parenthood, the coverage of the video by the major news networks was mere seconds. In order to shed light on this bias by omission, the MRC started a petition to rally public support.
The original landing page for this petition was a two column design with the video and information about it on the left and the petition on the right. Since traffic was being driving by both social media and organic website visitors, we thought it might be better to rearrange the content so that the visitors were first presented with the information before being asked to sign the petition. As a result, we created a single column treatment to test against the control.
experiments.keyLearnings: The single column treatment ended up decreasing the conversion rate of the page by 16.5%. What we learned is that this audience was already educated on the topic and did not need the additional value proposition language before taking our desired action. With the two column layout, those wanting to sign the petition were able to do so right way. The single column layout didn’t let us take advantage of the high visitor motivation.

				gift).

0% increase in traffic
				× 16.5% decrease in conversion rate
				× 0% increase in average gift

experiments.title: How the email presentation impacted click through rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a simplified version of the email increase the number of subscribers getting to the page?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/08/2016 - 12/09/2016
As part of a monthly appeal, Live Action wanted to test out the standard email design that had been in use for the last several months. This email template included the standard Live Action branding and an image of the Live Action president Lila Rose. The hypothesis was that if we could make the email feel more genuine, we could engage a greater proportion of the audience in the appeal.
To test this hypothesis, we stripped out the majority of the design elements and also softened the copy so that it did not have as many of the popular direct mail tactics such as bolding significant portions of text and including an ask array in the email (i.e. “would you consider making a $25, $50, or $100 gift.”).
experiments.keyLearnings:
The simplified design increased the subscriber click through rate by 208%. This increased traffic resulted in a 50% lift to the number of donations made to to the treatment.
This experiment demonstrated the importance of facilitating a one-to-one relationship in mass-marketing emails. It is our belief that people give to people and when they feel marketed to, they are not as likely to engage with an organization. By making this email look more genuine, more people engaged in the message. As we plan for future iterations of testing, this is a concept that we want to continue to explore with further testing.

experiments.title: How a radical redesign of a weekly newsletter affects click rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Does stripping out formatting and imagery and bringing a more personal tone to an email newsletter affect click rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/11/2017 - 12/01/2017
Live Action sends out a weekly email to their housefile with the top pro-life news stories from Live Action News. The current email format is highly branded, comes from Lauren Merz, and includes mainly images with links to stories.
They have had success in the past with emails coming from Lila Rose, the founder. They wanted to test whether a more personal, stripped-down approach to the newsletter would increase click rate in the emails.
experiments.keyLearnings: The stripped-down approach from Lila increased open rates by nearly 70%, however, removing the imagery decreased click rate by 18%. People are drawn to anything with Lila’s name on it, and responded well to receiving these stories from Lila, but clickthrough rate declined without the added pull of an image to tie to each story.

experiments.title: How a radical landing page redesign affects name acquisition rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Does a more evocative landing page with social proof outperform a more straightforward, functional landing page?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/12/2015 - 02/02/2015
Online for Life developed a petition, the Pro-Life Declaration to acquire new email addresses for their house file. Initially, the petition was a flat, content-focused design that looked exactly like…a petition. We wanted to test this against a radical redesign centered around a compelling image combined with the value statement “Life is Precious”. Rather than assume that the site visitor was ready to sign the petition, we assumed that we had to keep selling them once they got on the page. In addition to the new value statement and image, we added a counter at the top to show that by signing the petition, the visitor was joining a much larger group.
experiments.keyLearnings: The radical redesign delivered a 34.7% lift in the number of people who signed the Declaration — a huge boost to the campaign. This lift proved that the landing page must appeal to the donor. It also reminded us that visuals help sell the ultimate conversion, and we are not done selling once we have the user on the page.

experiments.keyLearnings: It would appear that less explanation was needed than we had originally thought.  This may be the result of previous efforts to explain how OFL works or the urgency that comes with year end.  In any case, we will know that less is more for future campaigns.

experiments.title: How a simplified decision process and contextual landing page design affects conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a simplified decision process and contextual landing page design increase sponsorship conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/13/2018 - 07/04/2018
Compassion International receives tens of thousands of child sponsorships every year. Traffic comes to the Compassion homepage from many different sources, with many different motivations, which has made it difficult—if not near impossible to validate homepage tests to increase child sponsorships.
The search process for Compassion.com generally contained four choice options: boy/girl, country, age range, and birthday. Previous tests had shown that prioritizing one (in this case, birthday) as the primary choice could increase “sponsorship starts”, but couldn’t validate a lift after going through their four-step sponsorship process. So with the birthday selection driving more “starts”, the team decided to focus on the next page, which was a grid of children who shared the selected birthday.
There were two primary hypothesized issues with this second page: first, the grid produced an overwhelming number of choices, so many that it required its own “sorter” at the top. Second, the page gave very little context to the action that had preceded it—a birthday selection. The only indication that the children shared the birthday was a single strip of text that read like code: “Showing results for Birth Day: 2, Birth Month: January”.
The team hypothesized that if they could help the user make decisions by reducing decision friction and adding contextual relevance to the page design, they might be able to increase sponsorships.
They crafted a responsive treatment based on a landing page that had produced a lift in sponsorships from banner advertising that featured the birthday search. This new page picked a single child who shared the birthday and featured them at the top, along with contextual design clues (confetti) and a bold headline that declared that the child shared the selected birthday.
They split traffic from the homepage button “search now” to determine if this treatment, combined with the homepage birthday selector, could produce a lift in sponsorships.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment, with reduced decision-making and contextual design that oriented the reader, produced a 14.4% increase in conversion. This test alone resulted in an additional 1,168 sponsorships each year (a 1.7% increase in annual sponsorships)—and generating nearly $2.9 million dollars (based on Compassion's $2,500 lifetime sponsor value). 

This shows the power of simplifying the process—a single decision, instead of four, followed by a single child, instead of an endless grid. This also implies that the customer might not be as choosy as we think, we might just need to help them find and suggest the right child. It also shows the power of contextualized design—if we can help people understand that they are in the right place, then they are more likely to follow through with the intended action.

				× 14.4% increase in conversion rate
				× 0% increase in average gift

experiments.title: How email design affects open rate in a newsletter email
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a designed email newsletter affect open rates?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/14/2019 - 03/18/2019
Email appeal experiments had given us a predisposition to think that email with button, images, and minor design elements are ineffective. But we had really only discovered that to be true in the specific context of a donation appeal.
In this experiment, we wondered if basic design elements could actually lead to greater click-throughs. The control was a plain-text style email in the format we had tested into, and the treatment added section images and clickable html buttons.
But we also wanted to keep an eye on open-rates. We thought it was possible for these design elements to hurt deliverability and inbox placement, and hurt open rates as a result.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This result is a little bit baffling. We expected we might see a decrease in opens, or in the best case scenario, see no change at all. Instead, we saw an 8.1% increase in open rates at a 98% level of confidence.
We thought it was possible that the preview text appeared differently, but that proved to not be the case. Subject lines and senders were also exactly the same. Now, the 98% confidence means theres a 1 in 50 chance that this result may not hold in when tested again. The odds of that are very slim, but seeing that we have no real explanation for why this result occurred, we will test again to be sure.

An organization ran a year end campaign in 2018. They hypothesized that stripping down the “Last chance” emails to look more like Outlook or plain text emails would perform better than the control email which used large banner logos and video screenshots. They tested the outlook styles between two different signers.
experiments.keyLearnings: The combined total of the plain text emails resulted in a 21.4% increase in engagement and donor conversion. This tells us that people give to people, not email marketing machines. This organization should continue to use plain text emails in future fundraising emails.

experiments.title: How shortened copy and design affect conversion rate on an acquisition page
experiments.researchQuestion: Does a radically redesigned approach to an acquisition page affect conversion rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/26/2018 - 07/25/2018
Hoover Institution was trying to increase conversion rate on their Strategika offer, so they tested a few different approaches to radical redesigns. The first was a two-column approach, with evidentials, and the second used shortened copy with a streamlined background. Copy was also different on all 3 versions.
experiments.keyLearnings: The shortened copy with a background image decreased conversion by 25%. This tells us that people are really needing more information than what was provided before they're ready to convert, or that they may have been distracted by the imagery used, instead of using imagery to add value.

experiments.title: How a radical redesign of an instant donation page impacts donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: How would a radical redesign impact donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/09/2011 - 11/13/2018
Focus on the Family offers a free video series for couples on a variety of topics. One of the topics is about how to better cherish your spouse. They sent people to sign-up for this video series through many different channels. After a person signs up for the series, they are immediately given an instant donation ask on the confirmation page. In reviewing the original instant donation page, we hypothesized that a radical redesign – one that focused on the value proposition and how it was presented – might increase donor conversion. To test this, we rewrote the copy on the page, put them in numbered steps, and asked for a specific gift amount attributing the amount back to what it would help do. We then tested this version against the control over four weeks to see the results.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The radical redesign increased donor conversion by 89%. This significant lift can be attributed to a few things:
1. Immediately congratulating people and confirm that they are signed up. This brought clarity to the conversion process. 
2. Created expectations and specific next steps we wanted them to know and do by telling them "there are five important things for you to know before you start the video series" and then numbering them in order.
3. Made a strong case for support that was reinforced through every numbered step.
4. Suggested a gift amount that communicated the specific impact of that gift, "Your gift of $50 today will allow 82 more couples to get signed up for the Cherish Your Spouse video series...".
By communicating a stronger value proposition, reinforcing it, and mentally walking the end user through a numbered/ordered approach, we were able to increase the motivation of the reader and increase donor conversion.

experiments.title: How a radical redesign to an email appeal affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Would an email that looked, felt, and sounded more personal increase conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/12/2018 - 01/23/2018
Traditionally, Focus on the Family sends monthly email appeals that are branded and templates for their organization. They also feature an image or a video with a button for a call-to-action and include a signature of the email sender. They hypothesized that they might be able to increase engagement and conversion of their monthly appeal if they made the email look, feel and sound as if the sender personally sent the appeal himself. To do this, they developed a treatment that stripped the email of it’s branded template to make it look like a plain text email and also wrote the copy in a way that was more conversational. They split their email file in half and tested each version against each other.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment increased donor conversion by 77.5%. This significant increase in conversion can be attributed to the radical approach in testing.
By stripping the email of the organization-centric template and features and changing the tone of the message to be more conversational, we were able to make the email feel like a traditional email sent personally by the sender. People want to feel like they are being personally engaged and that the appeal is only written to them from Jim Daly. People give to people, not mass marketing machines. This test reinforces that idea.
Moving forward, we will test specific elements of this email to gain an even deeper understanding of what motivates people to give.

experiments.title: How course signup page redesign affects email acquisition rate
experiments.researchQuestion: How does adding a more specific value proposition and reducing friction affect email acquisition rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/17/2015 - 07/31/2015
Hillsdale College redesigned their “Great Books 102” course signup page. After assessing the performance of their existing page, they decided to (1) Add a larger, more compelling headline, (2) shorten the body copy, (3) trade the bulleted list for bolded in-line text, (4) add a third party endorsement, and (5) highlight the form box with a bright background color.
They were interested in finding out how a dramatically redesigned version of the signup page might perform against their existing format. They launched an A/B test to find out.
experiments.keyLearnings: The redesigned course signup page produced a 5% relative lift in conversions, showing that the presence of a more compelling headline, third party validation, and an easier-to-spot form box impacted the viewer. This test, though statistically valid, produces more questions than answers for the researcher. Since there were so many variables in play, additional testing is required to determine the precise impact of each variable (and potential combination of variables).

experiments.title: How a radical redesign of an online course signup page impacted email acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a radically redesigned course signup page that uses familiar imagery increase conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/05/2017 - 07/10/2017
Hillsdale College noticed that there was room for optimization on their “Economics 101” online course signup page. After reviewing other sites that offer online courses, they decided to take some design elements that they found and implement it in a treatment version of the page. They attempted to increase the congruency between their Facebook ad and the course signup page by using the same imagery in both. They also were interested in seeing how a “typical” online course signup page would perform against their current control. They launched an A/B test and documented the results.
experiments.keyLearnings:
By emphasizing ad-to-page image continuity over a linear page design with supporting column content, the overall result was a statistically significant decrease in response.
If, however, the layout and supporting content was unchanged, and just the background was changed, we would hypothesize the result to be different/positive.
Despite the disappointing results, Hillsdale did learn a lot from this experiment. They will continue to test various page layouts, specifically on course signup pages.

experiments.title: How radically redesigning an email affected donation revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a radical redesign of the email that increases the force of the value proposition and introduces images increase response and revenue?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/15/2017 - 11/20/2017
The Heritage Foundation created a popular offer for email acquisition with their Digital Guide to the Constitution. After a visitor downloaded the Digital Guide, they were offered membership to Heritage, along with an offer to get the print copy of the Guide to the Constitution for a gift of $100. This offer flow had been quite effective at converting prospects into donors using a text-based approach that had tested well with the house file. But since acquisition mailings reached an audience of people who were not as familiar with Heritage, they created a treatment to test that increased the force of the value proposition and introduced images of the offer.
The control, which introduced the free offer with a casual greeting, focused on the content contained in the Guide. The treatment created an enemy: “Right now, liberals are waging an all-out war against our nation’s founding principles…”. Instead of a resource, it offered a “gift”, and highlighted the hard work that went into its creation, before revealing that it was created for free. The treatment email also included an image of the offer to provide clarity.
Then, Heritage ran an A/B test to determine a winner.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment produced a 123% increase in donations as well as an increase in revenue, which produced a big difference in return on investment. This showed the power of the increased value proposition, with the additional impact of the imagery to help the recipient visualize the offer.
This is a promising result that should be tested on more lists to ensure that there isn’t an anomaly with the first list choice.

experiments.title: How the email design influences clickthrough rate
experiments.organization: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate

experiments.researchQuestion: Will a simplified email design increase the proportion of people clicking to the landing page?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/03/2017 - 03/07/2017
The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate were launching a new digital prayer book to kick off the Lenten season. As part of this launch, they wanted to give their house email file an opportunity to request the resource. This would serve as both cultivation of the subscribers and a new opportunity for them to give a gift once they had requested their PDF.
Historically, the email template used in donor communications was heavily designed with an organizational wrapper. We wanted to see if sending the same email message but in a stripped-down design would have an impact on the percentage of people that clicked on the link to download the prayer book.
experiments.keyLearnings: The simplified email resulted in a 14.8% increase to the percentage of visitors going to the landing page. Our hypothesis is that the lack of design seemed to be more genuine and less “marketing” oriented. As a result, the subscribers were more willing to read and engaged with the email once they opened it.

experiments.title: How a radical redesign on a product page can impact donor conversion and overall revenue
experiments.organization: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
categoryNames: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
experiments.researchQuestion: Would a radical redesign increase conversion on the page?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/10/2017 - 08/15/2017
First, by removing the learn more button and seeing significantly more response, we confirm that the gifts themselves were self-explanatory and did not need explanation.
Second, by having multiple asks in a situation where it is unnecessary, people are less likely to make a decision.
Third, it is possible that when people click learn more, their mindset transitions from a giving mindset into a transaction mindset. When we move someone from a giving mindset into a transaction mindset by diving into the details, they realize that they could probably get the item somewhere else at better quality for significantly cheaper, instead of just quickly selecting a gift to go with their donation, much like grabbing a drink at the checkout line in a grocery store to enhance their overall experience.
experiments.keyLearnings: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate offer religious gifts for people to get if they make a donation to their organization. The religious items are arranged on a product page that shows an image of the item, the name of it and the opportunity to either “Learn More” or “Add to Basket”. We hypothesized that we might be able to increase conversion by strengthening the value proposition for the page and removing an element of friction from the giving process by taking away the “Learn More” button. We developed a treatment that changed those two variables on the page and tested it against the control.

experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/14/2017 - 07/21/2017
FamilyLife had a mature email fundraising program that relied heavily on third-party stories with large images. wanted to test an entirely new approach to their email fundraising that integrated a more personal tone and removed possible sources of friction from the “mental conversation” provoked by the email.
The control email used a template with a large FamilyLife logo at the top, followed by an image, a pull quote and a donate button, before any actual email copy. The email copy made an offer to provide a scholarship for a couple like “Fred and Melissa”, even though that couple had not been introduced yet. This presented clarity issues—who are Fred and Melissa? The couple in the picture? The call to action never really requested a gift, it just suggested a gift by linking text telling the reader what their gift would do. Finally, the email had a donate button at the bottom as well. This email led to a landing page that had the headline “Scholarships for Hardworking Pastor Couples”, which didn’t address the reader. That copy gave more information about Fred and Melissa, and then led into a separate call to action section (visually separated by a green box) that was disconnected from the pastor-centric message: “Help Families Stay Together”. This extra section then led to a donation form that had all the fields stacked on top of each other.
The FamilyLife team started with the email, developing a treatment that stripped out all the visual elements to reduce visual friction and resemble an email that their founder, Dennis Rainey, might actually write. Then they took a new approach with the tone, making it very personal. The treatment copy talked about his wife, their history serving pastors, and the challenges they’ve seen firsthand. The ask was vey clear and direct to the reader, and provided just a single link to the donation page, with no button.
This led to a donation page that led with a headline that extends an offer to the reader: Invest in God’s work by strengthening a pastor’s marriage. This copy removed the story, with the hypothesis that the third-party story with no real connection and anonymized names might detract from the clarity of the value proposition. The treatment added a bulleted list of what the pastor couple would receive at a Weekend to Remember event, and made a clear ask to the reader. The visual separation from the green box was removed, and the donation form was shortened by bringing multiple fields (i.e. first name and last name) onto the same line, which gave the perception of less friction, while still collecting the same information.
They split their file evenly (making sure to evenly split donors and non-donors) and sent one version to each segment. The control email linked to the control page and the treatment email to the treatment page to preserve the integrity of the test throughout the entire appeal flow.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment donation flow produced a statistically valid 272.1% increase in donor conversion. This strong leap forward was due to a few identifiable conversion factors:
Reduction of visual friction in the control email led to more readers engaging with the copy.
Personal tone in the email increased motivation to give.
Decreased friction on the landing page increased likelihood to complete a donation
However, since all of these optimizations were made in one fell swoop, we cannot know exactly how each change contributed to the total increase. That said, this experiment provides a new benchmark for email appeal testing for FamilyLife—one that can be optimized in its own right.
experiments.fluxMetrics:
The Flux Metrics analyze the three primary metrics that affect revenue (traffic, conversion rate, and average
				gift).
				This experiment produced the following results:
0% increase in traffic
				× 272.1% increase in conversion rate
				× 0% increase in average gift

experiments.title: How the design of a fundraising email affects clickthrough rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a stripped-down fundraising email increase clickthrough rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/02/2016 - 03/30/2016
CaringBridge received a matching gift for their spring fundraising campaign from a board member. Since the majority of their audience was unfamiliar with the board member, Rik Lalim, they originally created a templated email that was explicitly “CaringBridge” to ease users’ potential anxiety. But they had previously tested emails that had no formatting and emulated “normal” email, and experienced great success. They decided to create a treatment version that stripped away all the formatting and photography.
They then split their email file and ran a test to see which attracted more responses
experiments.keyLearnings:
The stripped-down email (with no template) produced an 80% increase in clickthrough rate. This tells us something about trust — and giving is an act of trust. It may imply that people are more likely to take action when they feel that the email is coming from a person, rather than an organization.
There were still elements of the treatment email that weren’t entirely personal — the big blue button, and the logo. This prompted further testing to see if the removal of these elements produced an additional lift.
experiments.fluxMetrics:
The Flux Metrics analyze the three primary metrics that affect revenue (traffic, conversion rate, and average
				gift).
				This experiment produced the following results:
80.3% increase in traffic
				× 0% increase in conversion rate
				× 0% increase in average gift

experiments.title: How simplifying the design and expanding the content affects revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: A longer explanation of OFL’s unique process coupled with a simpler page design will allow donors to get a better understanding of their value proposition which will, in turn, increase the overall revenue for the campaign.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/21/2014 - 12/28/2014
For their year end campaign, Online for Life used their unique process for reaching abortion-determined women and the cost that it takes to save a child as the primary value proposition. The question became how much the page needed to explain the details of the process vs. keeping it high level.  We created two treatments to find out.
experiments.keyLearnings: It would appear that less explanation was needed than we had originally thought.  This may be the result of previous efforts to explain how OFL works or the urgency that comes with year end.  In any case, we will know that less is more for future campaigns.
experiments.fluxMetrics:
The Flux Metrics analyze the three primary metrics that affect revenue (traffic, conversion rate, and average
				gift).
				This experiment produced the following results:
0% increase in traffic
				× 50.5% decrease in conversion rate
				× 55.9% decrease in average gift
									78.2% decrease in revenue

experiments.title: How a radical landing page redesign affects name acquisition rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a radical landing page redesign increase name conversion rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/03/2015 - 07/17/2015
Stanford’s Graduate School of Business was promoting a free ebook to build the list for their email publication Insights by Stanford Business. They had launched with a three-column landing page designed to educate the visitor about the book by putting as much information as possible above the fold. While this campaign delivered cost-effective email acquisition, they thought that a page redesign could improve conversion and lower cost-per-name.
They devised a treatment page that “stacked” the information, starting with a headline that advertised what the visitor receives (“Get the free ebook”) rather than what the visitor wants to do (“Build a winning business”). The copy was also reduced dramatically and sharpened to intensify the force of the value proposition. Finally, the form was brought inline with the eye path and the featured contributors were brought up from below to bolster credibility.
experiments.keyLearnings: The new treatment delivered a 10% lift in conversion — which gave us great insight into the construction of the page. Next, we will focus on copy to see if we can further build the value proposition.
experiments.fluxMetrics:
The Flux Metrics analyze the three primary metrics that affect revenue (traffic, conversion rate, and average
				gift).
				This experiment produced the following results:
0% increase in traffic
				× 10.8% increase in conversion rate
				× 0% increase in average gift

experiments.title: How changing the landing page design affects revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: Which landing page design produces the most donor conversions and revenue?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/09/2015 - 06/23/2015
The Heritage Foundation had been using the same landing page design for their search engine marketing (SEM) donor campaigns for the past three years. During those three years, we had run multiple experiments on the main organizational donation page which had gradually evolved into something that looked completely different.
We were in the process of moving the SEM page to the same domain as the main donation form and realized that it would be a good time to validate that the old design was still effective for the SEM audience segment.  We created an experiment by duplicating the page content but displaying it on the two different page designs.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The new design treatment caused an 85.1% drop in revenue by decreasing conversion rate by 67.6% and decreasing average gift by 54.1%. The simplified design used in the treatment was developed for a page with minimal copy. When we added the long value proposition text that had been developed for this specific landing page, it became difficult to read.
Landing pages must be designed so that they are easily scannable by the visitor. In this situation, the sub-headlines didn’t stand out enough and the lack of page border made the page seem longer than it was.  All of these elements caused enough friction in the mind of the donor to have a negative impact on both conversion rate and average gift.
experiments.fluxMetrics:
The Flux Metrics analyze the three primary metrics that affect revenue (traffic, conversion rate, and average
				gift).
				This experiment produced the following results:
0% increase in traffic
				× 67.6% decrease in conversion rate
				× 54.1% decrease in average gift
									85.1% decrease in revenue

experiments.title: How a radical donation page redesign affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a radical redesign that removes friction and strengthens and emphasizes the value proposition increase donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/22/2017 - 08/30/2017
Harvest Ministries had a donation page that produced above-average conversion rates (at nearly 20%). However, they viewed this as an 80% failure rate, and wanted to run an experiment to optimize for donor conversion.
Their control donation page kept the same template as their website (which is common for ministries and nonprofits). This template included navigation that gave many opportunities to leave the page and take other actions. The page copy also started with a premium ask, with little value proposition language to hook the visitor.
The donation form started with radio buttons, which presented possible usability friction for the visitor. The prospective donor was then presented with the choice to make a single gift or a recurring gift, the details of which were located on another page. This introduced possible decision friction into the funnel. Next was a designation. Designations can be helpful for the organization, but research has showed that they present another decision for the user and can bring the donation process to a halt.
Next, the visitor was asked to claim their premium, and fill out the typical information and payment fields, with a question about where they engage with Harvest in the middle.
The treatment removed all the excess navigation from the page and began with a simple, clear value proposition: “Your gift helps reach the lost with the gospel of Jesus Christ”. It then unpacked the value proposition, providing evidentials to each claim of value and connecting the gift to different forms of impact. Then, it repositioned the premium as a thank-you in response to the gift, rather than the reason for the gift.
The gift array was reworked into larger buttons that were easier to click and see which amount was selected. The information and payment fields were moved to the second step of a two-step screen.
Harvest launched an A/B test to discover which donation page flow resulted in more donations and revenue.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment page generated a 44.8% lift in donations — a big feat for a page that was already attracting highly motivated donors. This showed that some combination of all the elements of friction on the page were distracting would-be donors. It also proved that focusing on the clarity of the value proposition (rather than the incentive alone) can increase motivation to give.
This prompted a series of tests to remove more friction from the giving process and further clarify the value proposition.
experiments.fluxMetrics:
The Flux Metrics analyze the three primary metrics that affect revenue (traffic, conversion rate, and average
				gift).
				This experiment produced the following results:
0% increase in traffic
				× 44.8% increase in conversion rate
				× 0% increase in average gift

experiments.title: How updating the layout of the homepage to focus on content impacted conversion rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that providing clearer navigation for website visitors will improve conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/29/2025 - 10/06/2025
Save the Children’s homepage serves multiple purposes and often appears cluttered. In this experiment, we introduced larger image blocks with concise text to guide users to relevant content. The test evaluated the effect of placing navigation-oriented content blocks at the top of the homepage. We hypothesized that this change would streamline access, helping users reach their desired content more efficiently and, in turn, boosting engagement and conversion rates.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This test aimed to determine the impact of adding navigation focused content blocks to the top of the homepage. We hypothesized that this would reduce friction by providing users with an easier way to reach the content they want most. This would then increase engagement and conversion rate.
Key Results:
12% non-statistically significant decrease (94%) in overall conversion rate.
 	18% statistically significant decrease (95%) in overall revenue per visitor.
This test showed that changing the homepage content blocks from news-related to navigational blocks reduced emotional engagement and urgency cues, leading to lower donor conversion. News content activates empathy and a sense of immediacy, prompting spontaneous giving, while navigational blocks encourage exploration rather than action.

experiments.title: Does including an author image improve email performance for non-donors?
experiments.researchQuestion: If we add an image of a credible Hoover fellow to the email, it will increase engagement and gifts. Recognizable and authoritative figures can increase trust and attention, which may make readers more likely to engage with the message and take action.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/13/2026
This experiment aimed to determine whether including an image in an email promoting a free ebook would increase engagement and donations. Conducted for the Hoover Institution, a public policy research organization focused on individual, economic, and political freedom, the test compared two versions of the email. The only variable was the addition of an image featuring a well-known Hoover fellow, while all other elements remained the same.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Control (No Image)
CTR: 1.27%
 	Signups: 1,001
 	Donations: 16
 	Revenue: $920
 	Click to Donate: 1.12%
 	RPM: $8.16
Treatment (Image Added)
CTR: 1.26%
 	Signups: 979
 	Donations: 25
 	Revenue: $965
 	Click to Donate: 1.76%
 	RPM: $8.56

experiments.title: Does Displaying Multiple Ministry Brands Strengthen Donor Confidence?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that displaying the logos of Luther Seminary’s affiliated ministry brands below the donation button for Facebook-driven content audiences will achieve a higher conversion rate and increased revenue, as visible scale and brand familiarity strengthen trust and signal broader mission impact.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 02/23/2026
Luther Seminary operates multiple well-known ministry brands, including Enter the Bible, Working Preacher, and Faith+Lead. Each brand serves a distinct audience while advancing Luther’s broader mission of providing free, high-quality biblical resources and leadership development.
Many donors first encounter Luther through a single resource, such as an Enter the Bible article or a Faith+Lead course, and may not immediately recognize that these offerings are part of a larger network. This raises an important motivational question: Does emphasizing the breadth of Luther’s ministries increase donor confidence and willingness to give?
To explore this, we ran an A/B test on Facebook-driven traffic to two content-based donation pages:
Enter the Bible: Matthew
Faith+Lead: Genesis to Revelation Course
Visitors were randomly split between:
Control: A standard donation page featuring only the originating brand.
Treatment: The same donation page, with the addition of logos representing Luther Seminary’s affiliated ministry brands displayed prominently below the “Give” button.
The donation form defaulted to one-time giving in both versions to isolate the impact of brand presentation.
This experiment aims to answer a central donor motivation question: Does highlighting organizational scale and a family of trusted brands increase giving by reinforcing credibility and perceived impact?
The primary success metric is transactions (both one-time and monthly gifts), with revenue per visitor, one-time, and recurring gifts as secondary measures.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The overall transaction result achieved 89.3% statistical confidence, which does not meet the conventional 95% threshold for declaring a statistically significant winner. Because of this, we cannot definitively claim that the treatment caused the decrease in conversion rate. However, the results are directionally consistent across nearly every metric:
Overall conversion rate declined (-15.14%)
 	One-time giving declined (-15.51%)
 	Recurring giving declined (-34.53%, low volume)
 	Revenue per visitor declined (-7.18%)
When multiple related metrics move in the same direction, it strengthens the directional signal — even if the test does not reach full statistical significance. Directionally, this suggests that displaying multiple affiliated brand logos below the donation button likely introduced distraction or diluted the primary brand connection at the moment of giving. For Facebook-driven, content-based traffic, donors may be primarily motivated by the specific resource or brand they engaged with, rather than by the broader institutional ecosystem. At the point of conversion, clarity and singular focus may be more powerful than signals of scale.

experiments.title: How a past year-end donation page performed compared to the current year-end donation page
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that running last year’s donation page copy versus this year’s donation page copy for CYE donation page visitors will yield no meaningful difference in overall donor conversion, because both pages communicate a sufficiently similar value proposition, urgency, and call to action, motivating giving at comparable levels.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/16/2025 - 12/19/2025
During IMB’s CYE Campaign, we observed unexpected softness in overall performance. While several potential contributing factors were identified, we prioritized validating whether the donation page itself was contributing to the issue.
To isolate that risk, we ran a direct A/B test comparing last year’s donation page copy to this year’s version. Although the test ran for only a few days, the results were directional and clear enough to warrant decisive action during IMB’s largest fundraising season.
This year’s donation page copy converted donors at a higher rate, allowing us to confidently remove last year’s version to protect conversions and revenue at a critical moment in the campaign.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The results indicate that the treatment donation page (last year’s copy) drove a 32.6% decrease in overall transactions (81% LOC). While the test did not reach full statistical validation, the directional signal was clear and unfavorable, reflecting a negative audience response to the treatment.

Given the strength of this directional learning—and the risk of suppressing conversion during IMB’s largest fundraising season—we ended the test.

These findings confirm that this year’s donation page copy is converting donors at a higher rate, indicating that the donation page itself is not the primary driver of the campaign’s softness. As a result, additional testing was required to better diagnose the underlying factors impacting performance.

experiments.title: How radically redesigning a year-end donation page impacts overall donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that the condensed copy, countdown clock, and goal thermometer on the CYE Donation page will increase overall donor conversion by reducing friction in the giving experience and allowing donors—particularly mobile users—to complete the donation process more seamlessly.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/19/2025 - 12/25/2025
During IMB’s CYE campaign, we launched a three-way test designed to introduce meaningful variation and identify elements that could improve donor conversion.
Control: Standard CYE donation page
Treatment A: CYE donation page with countdown clock and goal thermometer
Treatment B: Hyper-condensed copy with countdown clock and goal thermometer
We anticipated Treatment B would outperform the other variations, as it significantly reduced page length—an advantage for mobile donors—while preserving urgency and social proof through the countdown clock and goal thermometer.
experiments.keyLearnings: The results show that Treatment A led to a modest 3.4% decrease in overall transactions (15.2% LOC), while Treatment B drove a substantial 27.8% decrease in transactions with a high level of directional confidence (90.1% LOC). Although neither variation reached full statistical validation, the magnitude and confidence of the decline associated with Treatment B clearly indicated a negative impact on donor conversion. Given the critical nature of the CYE fundraising window and the risk of continued conversion suppression, we have decided to remove Treatment B from rotation. This allowed Treatment A to continue running against the control, enabling additional learning without materially jeopardizing overall conversion or revenue during a high-stakes fundraising period.

experiments.title: How adding an image of the premium in an appeal email affects clickthrough rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding the image of the premium to the appeal email for the housefile will increase the click-through rate because the cover image is unique and would encourage users to click through.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 11/03/2025
Time of Grace offers a new premium each month. They send out appeal emails for this premium. The cover for their October premium was particularly distinctive and stood out to the audience.
Therefore, we wanted to test including the image in the email. We wanted to see how that would affect click-through rate.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key takeaway from this experiment is that adding the image of the premium to the appeal email did not achieve the desired increase in click-through rate. In fact, the treatment group experienced a 25.5% decrease in clicks compared to the control group, with a 91.2% statistical level of confidence. While these results are not considered valid, they are approaching validity, indicating that further testing would be necessary to confirm the effect.
In terms of donations, however, we did see an increase in the treatment, but not with a 95% level of confidence.
Control - 11 Donations
Treatment - 17 Donations
LOC = 74.9%
Future studies should investigate how images in emails influence user behavior. Further testing can reveal whether certain images or situations are more effective. Keep analyzing results to improve email marketing strategies.

experiments.title: How a one-step donation form performs against a 2-step control for a content offer instant donation page.
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that simplifying the steps needed to make a donation for content offer downloaders will achieve an increase in conversion rates because of the reduction in friction on the form generated by the extra steps.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/24/2025 - 10/15/2025
The Hoover Institution is a think thank that is affiliated with Stanford University. For the last couple of years, their donation forms had a 2-step process; the first step was fill out your information and the second step (you had to click on payment information) was add in your billing info. This was a format we had tested into many years ago, but given the data we have seen with other organizations, we wanted to test whether a one-step form would make an impact.
We ran this test on one of Hoover’s more popular content offers, and ran it for 3 months.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that simplifying the steps needed to make a donation for content offer downloaders can lead to an increase in conversion rates. The treatment group, which used a one-step form instead of a two-step form, experienced a 12.7% increase in transactions for all traffic, with a directional level of confidence of 85%. There were no recurring transactions as a result of this test, so the results are for one-time donations.
Due to some platform limitations, the one-step process was still not as 'clean' as we would have liked (you still needed to click on 'show total' on the treatment form to complete the gift), but the level of confidence was high enough were we felt comfortable declaring a winner on this test.
We will continue to test this set-up on different offers to try and pinpoint if indeed it was just the simplification of the form that made an impact on conversion rates, or if it was tied to the offer itself.

experiments.title: Do Visual Elements on a Donation Page Help or Hurt Donor Conversion?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using a simplified donation page design for users visiting a fundraising campaign donation page will achieve an increase in donor conversion because removing competing visual elements will reduce mental friction for the donors.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/18/2025 - 07/28/2025
Bible League Canada typically uses a highly visual donation page style as the default format for all of their fundraising campaigns. These pages often include banners, images, and video elements, along with a FundraiseUp donation form embedded at the bottom. While these pages are designed to create an engaging donor experience, we didn’t know whether these visual elements were helping donor conversion, or adding unnecessary distraction and mental friction.
To explore this question, Bible League Canada tested whether removing visual components from the donation page would reduce friction and lead to more completed gifts.
The Control was their standard visual campaign page, and the Treatment was a simplified FundraiseUp campaign page containing only one image and the donation form. The test was structured as a 50/50 split URL experiment during an active fundraising campaign to determine whether visual richness improves performance or adds unnecessary friction.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Although we hypothesized that a simplified donation page would reduce friction and increase conversions, the original designed page outperformed the simplified version. The Treatment page saw a 24.4% decrease in donor conversion compared to the Control, with an 89.1% level of confidence—just shy of full statistical validation but directionally meaningful.
In addition to stronger conversion, the Control also generated a higher revenue-per-visitor. However, this may have been influenced by one or more larger gifts, which could skew the average and may not reflect a consistent trend.
This result suggests that in fundraising campaigns, donors may benefit from richer context and emotional reinforcement provided by visual elements like images, banners, and video. Rather than creating distraction, these elements may help reinforce the campaign’s importance and build trust. It’s possible that removing them made the experience feel less compelling or authoritative.
Future testing could explore which specific elements (e.g., video, banners, testimonial quotes) contribute most to conversion. Alternatively, testing a hybrid layout—retaining key persuasive visuals while simplifying navigation and layout—might reveal a better-performing balance.

experiments.title: How removing the footer on a donation page affects the conversation rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Removing the footer on a donation page will achieve an increase in the conversion rate because people won’t get distracted by the many page options to visit and allow them to focus on the purpose of the page–to donate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/07/2025 - 03/18/2025
Alliance Defending Freedom is one of the leading Christian law firms committed to protecting religious freedom, free speech, marriage and family, parental rights, and the sanctity of life.
We ran this test on the donation page for one of their grant programs. Traffic came to this page through house file email.
We want to see if removing the footer would stop people from bouncing off the donation page and increase the conversion rate.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that removing the footer on the donation page led to a significant increase in transactions for all traffic. The 70.9% increase in conversions shows that the hypothesis that removing the footer would increase the conversion rate was supported by the data.

However, since the statistical level of confidence is below 95%, these results cannot be considered fully valid. Further testing would be necessary to confirm that removing the footer consistently leads to higher conversion rates.

In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to continue testing the impact of removing the footer on different pages or across different campaigns to see if this trend holds true. Additionally, it would be important to consider other elements on the page that may impact conversion rates and test those as well to optimize donation pages further.

experiments.title: How a goal thermometer on a high-urgency campaign donation page impacts donor conversion metrics
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that the addition of a goal thermometer for FYE donation page visitors will achieve an increase in donor conversion metrics because it creates a sense of social proof by visibly showing real-time progress toward the goal and highlighting that others are actively contributing, we foster a sense of community and shared purpose.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/10/2025 - 06/30/2025
During Moody Radio’s Fiscal Year-End Campaign, we tested the impact of adding a goal thermometer on donor conversion.
The goal of this test was to leverage social proof by emphasizing a shared, communal objective to meet a specific financial need for the ministry, ultimately driving an increase in both transactions and revenue.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are:
The addition of a goal thermometer for FYE donation page visitors did not lead to an increase in donor conversion metrics. In fact, there was an 8.0% decrease in transactions for all traffic, indicating that the control group performed better in terms of transactions.
 	Despite the decrease in transactions, there was a significant increase in revenue by 489% in the treatment group with a 100% level of confidence. This suggests that although fewer people may have donated in the treatment group, those who did contribute gave more on average.
 	The statistical level of confidence for the decrease in transactions is 82.7%, below the generally accepted threshold of 95% for valid results. Therefore, these results should not be considered reliable or used for future decision-making.

experiments.title: Will adding a button on mobile donation pages impact gifts?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that optimizing the donation page for mobile users will achieve a higher donor conversion rate because the button will allow users to jump straight to the donation form instead of having to scroll.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/11/2025
Moody is running their Spring Share campaign which generates a ton of traffic to their donation pages from mobile.
For this test, we wanted to see if we could make the donation page be mobile first by adding a ‘start my gift button’ at the top of the page. When clicked the button will anchor a user down to the form.
Below is what we found
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that optimizing the donation page for mobile users with a 'start my gift button' did not achieve the desired outcome of increasing donor conversions. In fact, the treatment resulted in a decrease of 18.1% in donations for all traffic compared to the control.
