Based on these results, it is clear that the 'start my gift button' did not have the intended effect on donor behavior. It is important to note that the statistical level of confidence was below 95%, indicating that further testing would be necessary to validate these results.

In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to continue testing different strategies to optimize the donation page for mobile users. This could include exploring other design changes, such as simplifying the donation process, improving mobile responsiveness, or testing different call-to-action buttons. Additionally, conducting more rigorous testing with a larger sample size and longer testing period can help ensure more accurate and reliable results.

experiments.title: How a change in layout impacts engagement on a donation landing page
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that changing the layout to a grid for desktop web traffic will achieve a higher engagement with the site because the items are easier to view with less scrolling.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/14/2025 - 02/25/2025
Pathway to Victory is the broadcast ministry of First Dallas.
This year PTV wants to optimize their website to encourage more engagement from site visitors.
This page is where people go when they click the main donation button the PTV website.
We decided to test a grid layout with the hypothesis that the grid layout presents multiple viewing opportunities with less scrolling
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that changing the layout to a grid for desktop web traffic resulted in a 19.7% decrease in donations for all traffic. While the statistical level of confidence was at 92.5%, indicating that the results are approaching validity, further testing would be necessary to confirm the impact of the grid layout on engagement with the site.

In addition, we saw:
11% decrease in time spent on the site for treatment
 	an increase from 1 to 6 Pathway Partner (recurring gifts) between control and treatment

experiments.title: How a redesigned “Post” modal impacts journal entries
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that removing friction in the modal itself versus directing more traffic to the modal for authors will achieve an increase in journal posts because it helps address the point in the funnel where authors are abandoning the process.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/01/2025 - 02/07/2025
In a series of tests to improve Author journal posts, we had largely focused our efforts at the top of the funnel. We presented tests that primarily removed friction at the start: a prompt card in their feed, ghost text options underscoring that posts could be simple updates, etc. In this test, we hypothesized that re-working the modal itself would remove friction at the point of decision and increase journal posts. The changes in this experience were to soften the headline “How are you?,” include an encouraging subhead, ghost text options in the modal itself instead of outside the experience, and update the CTA to “Share My Post.” It was large overhaul, but one where each of the elements were cohesively under the umbrella of making the modal itself more user-friendly and encouraging.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that the changes made to the journal entry modal did not have the intended effect of increasing journal posts. In fact the treatment resulted in a slight decrease, however, the statistical level of confidence is below the acceptable threshold of 95%.

In future experimentation, it may be beneficial to further analyze the user behavior and gather feedback to understand why the changes did not resonate with authors. This can help in crafting more effective interventions in the future. It's also important to continue testing different elements at various stages of the funnel to find the most impactful ways to increase journal posts.

experiments.title: Will adding a heart to the monthly tab increase monthly transactions?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a heart on the monthly tab for the sustainer donation page giving form will achieve a higher number of recurring transactions because the heart will the users eyes to the monthly tab and lead them to giving a recurring gift.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/14/2025 - 02/18/2025
In the past we have tested adding different hearts to elements to catch the users eye and get them to take the action that we are wanting.
In this case, we wanted to see if we could add a heart on the monthly tab on the donation form would increase the number of monthly transactions.
We ran this test on the main sustainer donation page.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that the addition of a heart on the monthly tab for the sustainer donation page giving form resulted in a significant decrease in transactions for all traffic. The treatment group saw a 69.4% decrease in recurring transactions with a 92.4% statistical level of confidence, indicating that the control group performed better in this case.

While the results are not statistically significant at the usual 95% confidence level, they are approaching validity, suggesting that further testing would be necessary to confirm the impact of the heart icon on user behavior.

In future experimentation, it is important to carefully consider the potential impact of visual elements like hearts on donation forms, as they may not always lead to the desired outcome. It would be beneficial to conduct additional tests with different visual cues or design elements to see which ones are most effective in increasing recurring transactions. Additionally, it is crucial to analyze user feedback and behavior to understand the reasons behind the results and make informed decisions for future optimization efforts.

experiments.title: How adding a donor goal sticky bar to the donation page impacts donations
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a sticky bar to the sohl donation page for the CYE campagin will achieve a higher donor conversion rate because the sticky bar will reinforce the need as well as add exclusivity to the page.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/19/2024 - 12/28/2024
For this test we wanted to see if adding a sticky bar that contains a donor goal would increase the appeal to give as well as convey the message that we’re wanting to send to the users. By adding a sticky bar on the family donation page we found that it will increase conversions. Below is what we found for the SOHL audience
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that adding a sticky bar containing a donor goal to the sohl donation page for the CYE campaign resulted in a significant increase in revenue for all traffic. However, the statistical level of confidence was below 95%, which means the results cannot be considered valid for future decision-making.

In future experimentation, it is important to ensure that the statistical level of confidence is at least 95% before drawing any conclusions or making decisions based on the results. It may be beneficial to conduct further tests or experiments to confirm the effectiveness of adding a sticky bar to the donation page. Additionally, it could be helpful to explore other strategies or elements that could potentially increase donor conversion rates.

experiments.title: How $100 Prefill vs a $100 Pre-Select in the Array Impacts Donor Conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a $100 prefill for users on the main donation page will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate because whereas a 3-option array seemed to introduce friction, an open $100 prefill accomplished the desired impact on average gift without introducing more options for the user to choose between..
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/15/2024 - 01/16/2025
On CaringBridge’s main donation page, $100 is the default pre-selected amount. However, how that number has been presented to the user has varied. The standard practice has been to have no gift array, but simply an open field with $100 prefilled. Alternatively, some pages utilized an array of three options with $100 as the preselected amount. It is worth noting that currently, neither of these options were present on the CaringBridge main donation page due to a migration glitch. We hypothesized that when testing between the two options, an open field with $100 prefilled (the way it was originally intended) would improve donor conversion over a 3-option array.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Key learnings from this experiment include:

The treatment of using an open field with a $100 prefill on the main donation page resulted in a 35.3% increase in Revenue for All Traffic compared to the control group. The statistical level of confidence was 92.0%, indicating a high likelihood that the increase in revenue was due to the treatment and not random chance. Furthermore, the hypothesis that a $100 prefill would improve donor conversion rates compared to a 3-option array was supported by the results of the experiment.

Ultimately, these results help enforce two next steps for action items. 1. That CaringBridge should consider testing their other donation pages which have a gift array by default, to see whether an open field pre-fill can also improve conversion rate. 2. CaringBridge should prioritize correcting the current main donation page so that the $100 prefill is represented, rather than the current blank open field (0 is the prefill)

experiments.title: Will adding a sticky bar to the top of a donation page reinforce the need for users to give?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding in a sticky bar that has specific donor goal language for the monthly family campaign giving page will achieve a higher donor conversion rate because the sticky bar will reinforce the need as well as add exclusivity to the page.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/19/2024 - 12/28/2024
For this test we wanted to see if adding a sticky bar to the campaign donation page that contains donor goal language would increase the donor conversion rate on the page. Below is what we found.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are:

1. Adding a sticky bar with specific donor goal language to the monthly family campaign donation page resulted in a significant increase in Revenue for All Traffic by 397.1% with a 91.0% level of confidence.

2. There was also a 48% increase in Transactions - One-Time with a 96% level of confidence.

3. While the results were positive, the statistical confidence level of 91.0% for Revenue for All Traffic falls slightly below the generally accepted threshold of 95% for validity.

4. Further testing is recommended to confirm the effectiveness of the sticky bar in increasing donor conversion rates.

5. Future experimentation should focus on refining the donor goal language in the sticky bar, testing different designs or placements, and running the experiment for a longer duration to ensure reliable results.

6. It is important to continue testing and refining strategies to optimize donor conversion rates and maximize fundraising efforts for the nonprofit "Focus on the Family".

experiments.title: How a goal thermometer on a high-urgency donation page impacts donor conversion metrics
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that the presence of the revenue goal thermometer  for The Navigators CYE Donation page visitors will achieve an increase in donor conversion metrics because it signals to visitors that others are also donating therefore boosting trust on the page. because  it signals to visitors that others are also donating therefore boosting trust on the page.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/06/2025
To improve donor conversion rates during The Navigators’ year-end campaign, we conducted a test by adding a goal thermometer to the donation page.
The experiment ran from December 27 to December 31, 2025, with 50% of site traffic exposed to the treatment.
Our hypothesis was that the thermometer would positively influence donor conversion by serving as social proof, signaling that others are contributing and encouraging more people to donate.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that the addition of a goal thermometer to the donation page did not have the intended effect of increasing donor conversion rates for The Navigators' year-end campaign. In fact, there was a 5.1% decrease in donations for all traffic during the experiment period.
Despite these results, the statistical level of confidence was only 81.0%, which is below the generally accepted threshold of 95% for making valid conclusions. As a result, these results should not be considered conclusive or used for future decision making but they do suggest that The Navigator's audience had a neutral reaction to the goal thermometer.
Moving forward, it is important to understand that not all experiments will yield the expected results, and it is essential to carefully evaluate the data and statistical significance before drawing conclusions. In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to conduct further tests with different variations or factors to determine what truly influences donor conversion rates for The Navigators. Additionally, considering factors such as timing, audience segments, and messaging may also provide valuable insights for optimizing donation campaigns in the future.

experiments.title: How a sliding bar on a campaign donation page allowing donors to adjust their impact influences conversion rate and revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that allowing donors to select the level of their impact dynamically will increase donor conversion rate by increasing the perceived clarity and appeal of the donation page.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 12/09/2024
IMB is running a direct ask offer where people can give to fund Bibles, but we wanted to increase the tangibility and clarity of the donor’s impact on the campaign’s donation page. We hypothesized that allowing the donors to drag a slider that dynamically changes the number of people reached would increase the clarity of the offer when compared with the current experience.
We used VWO to create a graphical, interactive slider on the page that dynamically changed the gift amount and the corresponding number of people the gift would reach. We split traffic 50/50 and all campaign sources were directed to the test.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that the dynamically changing slider for donors to select the level of their impact did not increase donor conversion rate as hypothesized. In fact, the treatment group experienced a 35.8% decrease in donations compared to the control group.

While the results are approaching validity, with a statistical level of confidence of 93.8%, further testing would be necessary to confirm these findings.

In future experimentation, it is important to carefully consider the impact of any changes made to the donation page. It may be beneficial to conduct more qualitative research or user testing before implementing a new feature to ensure it resonates with donors and effectively communicates the impact of their contribution. Additionally, testing smaller changes or variations of the dynamically changing slider could help pinpoint what specifically led to the decrease in donations in this experiment.

experiments.title: How a dynamic thermometer on the donation page impacts donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a dynamic thermometer for users on the Giving Day donation page will achieve an increase in donor conversion because it helps underscore the urgency of this single-day appeal and helps the donor better visual their role in reaching the goal.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 12/03/2024
For RTB’s Giving Day, we hypothesized that including a dynamic thermometer showing the progress of the $15,000 goal during this one-day appeal would help generate greater urgency and improve donor conversion rate.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Key learnings from this experiment include:

1. The addition of a dynamic thermometer showing the progress of the fundraising goal on the donation page had a significant impact on donor conversion rates, with a 80.6% increase in donations for all traffic.

2. Despite the positive outcome, the statistical confidence level of 62.9% is below the commonly accepted threshold of 95%, which means that the results may not be considered completely reliable.

3. It is important to recognize that while the treatment was successful in this particular instance, the results should not be used as a basis for future decision-making due to the low statistical confidence level.

In future experimentation, it is crucial to ensure that the sample size is sufficient to provide a higher level of statistical confidence before making any conclusions or implementing changes based on the results of the experiment. Additionally, it may be beneficial to conduct further tests or iterations of the dynamic thermometer feature to validate its impact on donor conversion rates before making it a permanent fixture on the donation page.

experiments.title: How defaulting to recurring gift with a partner’s benefits call-out on a donation page impacts recurring donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that emphasizing the exclusive benefits of becoming a FamilyLife Partner will increase the number of recurring donations because it provides added value to donors, strengthening their connection to the organization and making the recurring gift feel more rewarding and meaningful.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 11/08/2024
FamilyLife is a Christian organization dedicated to strengthening marriages and families through biblically-based resources, events, and guidance, supporting individuals as they build enduring, healthy relationships. Previously, we tested a recurring gift default on FamilyLife’s premium donation pages, which led to a 0.53% increase in recurring donations with a 99.9% level of confidence. Based on this success, the recurring default has been implemented as a permanent feature across the donation microsite. In this experiment, we aim to test a new approach to the recurring gift call-out, focusing on language that highlights the exclusive benefits of becoming a FamilyLife Partner, to assess if this approach will further encourage monthly giving.
If we emphasize the exclusive benefits of becoming a FamilyLife Partner (e.g., access to insider ministry updates, private events, and a curated library), then users will be more likely to commit to a recurring gift, as the added value and connection to the FamilyLife community will increase their perceived return on investment and loyalty.
The test involved two variations on the donation page. The Control version used a recurring gift call-out that stated, “Equip 140 families with practical insights to strengthen the relationships that matter most.” The Treatment version introduced a new call-out focused on membership benefits: “Receive exclusive benefits: insider ministry updates, invitation-only events, access to our curated library, and membership to our private Partners-only network.” This A/B test was split 50/50 and conducted on their best performing premium donation pages: Seeing Beautiful Again, 52 Weeks in the Word, and the Ferret Flush Couples Pack.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The experiment aimed to determine if emphasizing exclusive benefits for recurring donors would increase the number of monthly donations on FamilyLife’s premium donation pages. However, partway through the test, two of the three offers (Seeing Beautiful Again, 52 Weeks in the Word, and Ferret Flush Couples Pack) were turned off due to budget limitations. As a result, traffic to the test was reduced, which impacted our ability to achieve a conclusive result.
Recurring Donations Results: The Control outperformed the Treatment in terms of recurring donations. The Control had a 0.59% conversion rate (13 recurring gifts from 2,210 samples), while the Treatment had a 0.30% conversion rate (7 recurring gifts from 2,366 samples), representing a relative decrease of 49.7% at an 86.1% level of confidence. This difference did not reach statistical significance, so the results are inconclusive.
Overall Transactions Results: When examining all transactions, the Control had a 4.8% conversion rate (107 total gifts from 2,210 samples) compared to a 4.9% conversion rate in the Treatment (115 total gifts from 2,366 samples). This represents a slight relative increase of 0.4% in the Treatment, with a very low level of confidence (2.4%). This result also did not validate, indicating that the new messaging had no meaningful effect on overall transactions.
Theories on Donor Response:
Mismatch with Motivation: FamilyLife donors may be motivated more by the immediate impact of their giving rather than exclusive, long-term benefits. The Control message, which directly highlighted the impact of equipping families, may have resonated more with donors’ desire to see their contribution create tangible change. The Treatment, which shifted focus to membership perks, may have unintentionally distracted from this primary motivation.Perceived Complexity: The mention of exclusive benefits and a private network may have introduced an element of complexity or commitment that was unappealing. Donors may respond best to messages that feel straightforward and immediately impactful. The “Partners-only network” or “curated library” might not have been clearly valuable in this context and could have made the ask feel less accessible.Different Expectations for Monthly Giving: Some donors may view recurring giving as a simple way to support the organization consistently, without needing additional incentives. This audience might value the ease and simplicity of monthly giving over exclusive access, making the Partner benefits message less motivating.

experiments.title: How a cleaner weekly email design impacts the click rate of readers
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that redesigning Winston’s Weekly for subscribers will achieve a higher email click rate because there will be more information and resources available to them. because there will be more information and resources available to them.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 11/11/2024
We’ve been running Winston’s Weekly for a little more than a year now. The weekly email shares a case study from our experiment library and breaks it down to provide fundraisers with a deeper look at fundraising tests to inspire their own testing and optimization.
Looking at the design, it felt partially designed and partially plain text. We thought that using more design elements might create a cleaner, more readable way to present the information.
Pulling some inspiration from the newsletter Case Studied, we broke each header section into its own blocks in the design. We added a resources section at the bottom that links to the week’s experiment and other relevant resources. We also added a section about our services with a button to learn more.
Unlike the control version, this treatment version provided more opportunities and more reason to click out of the email to our website.
The treatment resulted in an email that was almost twice the length of our typical email but presented the content in a much cleaner way.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The redesigned version of Winston's Weekly did not perform as well as the original version, with a 27.5% decrease in Clicks for All Traffic. The statistical level of confidence for this experiment was 92.4%, meaning the results are approaching validity but cannot be considered completely reliable. The additional information and resources added to the treatment version did not lead to a higher click rate as hypothesized.

I'm interested in doing another email redesign in the control format with just the addition of the resources section and the section about NextAfter's services to see how that would impact clicks.

It could also be interesting to do another email redesign in a plain text format, but removing the gray background and using clearer headings like in this treatment to see if that gets more people to stick around till the end and click.

Future Experimentation:

1. Further testing is necessary to determine the impact of the redesigned layout on subscriber engagement.

2. Consider conducting additional A/B tests with variations in design elements to identify which specific changes lead to improved performance.

3. Use the learnings from this experiment to inform future iterations of Winston's Weekly, taking into account subscriber preferences and engagement metrics to guide design decisions.

experiments.title: Does a missional or initiative ask convert donors?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that having a inititive ask for a high urgency campagin will achieve a higher donor conversion rate because having a tangible ask for a short period of time can be easier for a potential donor to convert on.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 10/31/2024
KCBI runs a Fall Fundraiser each year that brings in traffic to their website.
For this campaign, we wanted to see what type of messessaging converts donors during a high urgency campaign better.. Missional or Initiative.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that having an initiative ask for a high urgency campaign may potentially lead to a higher donor conversion rate compared to a missional ask. While the treatment did show a 52.6% increase in donations for all traffic, the statistical level of confidence was only at 84.9%, which is below the standard threshold of 95% for results to be considered valid.

It's also important to note that donors gave more generously when they saw the treatment of "share 25,000 Bibles". The average gift for the treatment was $205 and the average gift for the control was $123.

experiments.title: How adding a logo at the top of an email impacted clicks
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by adding a logo at the top of emails for subscribers will achieve higher email engagement because the logo creates confidence that the email is legit.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 09/25/2024
Pathway to Victory is the broadcast ministry of First Dallas.
3 years ago we tested out of images within emails, however we decided to retest a logo on email to see if a little branding adds validity to the email and if it can impact clicks and downstream donations.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding a logo at the top of emails for subscribers resulted in a 23.0% increase in Clicks for All Traffic. Although the results suggest potential benefits of including a logo for branding and legitimacy purposes, the statistical level of confidence of 85.0% falls below the accepted threshold of 95% for validity.

In regard to donations we saw a 28% decrease in gifts at a 81% level of confidence

So, while the email saw more engagement, it did not translate to more gifts. 

Therefore, these results should not be used for future decision-making. It is essential to conduct further experiments with a larger sample size or different variations to validate the impact of adding a logo to emails. Additionally, considering other factors such as email content, layout, and targeting can provide more insights into improving email engagement and donations. Ultimately, the key takeaway is to prioritize rigorous testing and analysis to make informed decisions for optimizing email marketing strategies.

experiments.title: How offering a premium version of the content offer impacts conversion rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a premium offer for content offer downloaders will achieve a change in revenue.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/15/2024 - 06/13/2024
Hoover Institution has a quarterly research digest, featuring 30 of their scholar’s work, which is promoted as a free PDF download. We have run experiments in the past where adding in a premium offer–in this case asking for $125 to get all 4 quarterly editions mailed to you–has an impact on conversion rates and average gift.
In this experiment, due to increased mailing and production costs, we want to see whether increasing that ask to $150 would make an impact on response rates.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are as follows:
1. The treatment, which asked for a higher donation amount of $150, resulted in a 23.7% decrease in transactions compared to the control group.
2. The statistical confidence level of 81.8% is below the generally accepted threshold of 85%, meaning the results are not considered valid and should not be used for future decision making.
3. In this specific case, the higher donation ask did not lead to an increase in response rates and could have potentially deterred donors from completing transactions.
4. It is important to carefully analyze the impact of donation amounts on response rates and not assume that a higher request will always result in higher revenues.
In future experimentation, it is important to consider the following:
1. Conduct further experiments with different donation amounts to see the impact on response rates and revenue.
2. Ensure that experiments have a statistically significant confidence level of at least 85% to ensure the validity of the results.
3. Consider other factors such as messaging, timing, and audience segmentation in future experiments to gain a deeper understanding of donor behavior.
4. Continuously analyze and iterate on experiments to optimize donation strategies and maximize revenue for Hoover Institution.

experiments.title: How Adding An Image To an Email Effects Clicks
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding an image for the edu FYE email appeal will achieve a higher click rate because the image will resonate with the user to continue reading the email.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 07/25/2024
Moody Bible Institute runs a FYE campaign each year that consists of a full email campaign to their Education housefile.
For this experiment, we wanted to see if adding an image to the email copy led to more link clicks to the donation page.
experiments.keyLearnings:
For this experiment, we learned that adding an image to the email decreases the click rate if you look at the overall impact. To make this test even, we split the housefile into donors, nondonors, and lapsed donors. Each segment did produce a decrease in clicks, however, the treatment version with the image created a 47% lift in unsubscribes.

Looking at transactions from each email, the control also had a significant increase in transactions and revenue.

experiments.title: Does using a PDF icon on a content offer’s Facebook ad impact signups?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a PDF icon on Facebook creative for Facebook traffic will achieve a higher sign up rate because there is clarity in the type of offer they are receiving.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/02/2024 - 07/31/2024
Pathway to Victory is the broadcast ministry of First Dallas
PTV offers multiple digital content offers in an effort to acquire new names, new donors, and to keep known contacts and donors engaged with content.
One of PTV’s most successful offers is about Billy Graham. In an effort to increase the success of this offer, we decided to test adding a PDF icon on the image.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment include:

The treatment, which involved adding a PDF icon to the Facebook creative, resulted in a 10.0% decrease in Complete Registrations for All Traffic with a 83.8% statistical level of confidence. However, since the confidence level is below the 85% threshold, the results cannot be considered valid for future decision-making

experiments.title: Does Changing the Position of an Image Affect Clicks in Email
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding an image at the bottom of an email for the FYE EDU appeals will achieve a higher click rate because the image will be reinforcing the copy that was just read.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 07/25/2024
Earlier in Moody’s FYE campaign we tested adding an image towards the top of an email to see if it could catch peoples attention and make them want to read more of them email. The results showed that it did the opposite and made people click out or unsubscribe.
For this test we wanted to see if placing an image at the bottom of an email copy could reinforce the message and create a higher click rate in the email.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding an image at the bottom of an email for FYE EDU appeals did not achieve a higher click rate. In fact, the treatment group experienced a significant decrease in clicks compared to the control group. However, if you look at revenue you can see a slight increase in the generosity of the donors when the image is present in the email. Both the control and treatment received the same number of donations, however the gift the average gift is up slightly when compared to the control. It is important to note that the donation size is very small, and the control did receive large outlying gifts that had to be taken out to not skew the average gift.

It would be interesting to test this tactic again to get a larger pool of donors to see if this is something that can be validated.

experiments.title: How zooming in on ad creative impacted cost per registration

rows.name: Hoover Digest Spring 2024 Creative Test Control
Hoover Digest Spring 2024 Creative Test Treatment

experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adjusting the ad creative to show more of the eBook cover  for Hoover’s Facebook audience will achieve an increase in the CTR because it engages the viewer with a highly colorful and visual image and provides consistency throughout the offer funnel with the eBook cover.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/13/2024 - 06/30/2024
The Hoover Institution is a public policy research center and think tank located at Stanford University in California, United States. Currently, the Hoover Institution is running one of its seasonal offers, the Spring Digest. It showed a low CTR. We wanted to test the beginning of the funnel to see how this would impact clicks and, ultimately, registrations. Since the covers for the Digest are visually appealing, we decided to zoom in on the eBook and delete some of the negative space around the eBook.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are as follows:
1. Adjusting the ad creative to show more of the eBook cover resulted in a 7.4% increase in Clicks for All Traffic.
2. The statistical level of confidence for this increase was 81.0%, which is below the standard threshold of 85%. Therefore, the results cannot be considered valid and should not be used for future decision making.
3. The Cost per Click experienced a significant 18.3% decrease with a 100% level of confidence, indicating that the treatment had a positive impact on this metric.
4. The Cost per Registration also experienced a significant decrease of 16.2% with a 99.9% level of confidence, revealing that there was an increase in conversions with a lower cost.
In future experimentation, it is important to:
1. Ensure that experiments are run for a longer period to gather more data and increase the statistical validity of the results.
2. Consider other factors that may have influenced the outcome, such as seasonal trends or changes in the target audience.
3. Continue to test different variations of ad creatives to optimize performance, but always prioritize statistical significance in order to make informed decisions.

experiments.title: How humanizing Facebook Creative impacts complete registrations
experiments.researchQuestion: Utilizing targeted imagery increases engagement and downloads by helping Facebook audiences visualize themselves benefiting from the eBook. It fosters a connection that drives action and boosts conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 07/03/2024
This study was conducted on Facebook for WUNC, a public radio station in North Carolina, to optimize click-through rates and enhance overall conversions for a promotional campaign. The experiment aimed to refine the effectiveness of an advertisement for an eBook about outdoor activities in North Carolina. The experimental design compared the performance of an ad featuring the eBook’s cover image against an alternative ad featuring a person engaged in outdoor recreation.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment on optimizing click-through rates and conversions for an eBook promotion are as follows:

1. The treatment, which featured an ad with targeted imagery of a person engaged in outdoor activities, resulted in a 35.0% increase in Emails Acquired for All Traffic compared to the control ad with the eBook's cover image.

2. The statistical level of confidence for this result was 92.6%, indicating that the effectiveness of the treatment is approaching validity but further testing would be necessary to confirm its impact.

3. The hypothesis that targeted imagery helps audiences visualize themselves benefiting from the eBook, fostering a connection that drives action and boosts conversion rates, was supported by the results of the experiment.

Moving forward, future experimentation should consider the following points:

1. Continue to test and refine the use of targeted imagery in advertisements to enhance engagement and conversions. Further experiments can help strengthen the validity of the findings and optimize the effectiveness of promotional campaigns.

2. Explore different types of imagery and messaging to determine which combinations resonate best with the target audience. This can inform future advertising strategies and improve campaign performance.

3. Implement A/B testing on a larger scale or over an extended period to gather more data and increase the confidence level of results. This will provide a more comprehensive understanding of the impact of different ad elements on audience behavior.

Overall, the key takeaway from this experiment is that targeted imagery can have a significant effect on engagement and conversions. By leveraging this insight and continuing to test and refine strategies, WUNC can enhance the effectiveness of its promotional campaigns and drive better outcomes for future eBook promotions and other marketing efforts.

experiments.title: How humanizing a dear reader impacted email acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a human invitation for website visitors will achieve an increase in newsletter signups because we know that people tend to respond to things that are “alive” in some way.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/17/2024 - 05/07/2024
When launching a newsletter and advertising it to site visitors below blogs/articles on the webpage, we wondered if we could replicate the success we had seen through other orgnization’s testing of effectively increasing email acquisition by humanizing our communications. We developed a treatment that leaned into the humanity of the newsletter offer and that emphasized that you’re going to hear directly from Franco—an insider on the latest happening inside the Canadian government if you sign up for weekly newsletter updates. We ran this experiment for 4 weeks to determine just how adding a clear value proposition and highlighting a familiar face from your organization can improve response.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding a human invitation for website visitors can significantly increase email acquisition for the Canadian Taxpayers Federation's newsletter. The treatment, which emphasized hearing directly from Franco—an insider on the latest happenings inside the Canadian government, led to a 57.6% increase in email signups compared to the control group.

While the results of this experiment are promising, the statistical level of confidence of 90.9% falls slightly below the standard threshold of 95% for validity. This suggests that further testing may be necessary to confirm that the treatment consistently has the intended effect.

In future experimentation, the Canadian Taxpayers Federation should continue to explore the impact of humanizing communications and highlighting familiar faces within the organization to engage website visitors. Additionally, it would be beneficial to test different variations of the human invitation, such as featuring other insiders or providing specific examples of the type of content subscribers can expect to receive. By iteratively testing and optimizing these approaches, the organization can continue to improve email acquisition and overall engagement with its audience.

experiments.title: How including a gift handle with the corresponding impact described in a fundraising email affects revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that including gift handles with the corresponding impact described for potential donors to Eagle Lake will achieve more revenue because we’re providing clear, tangible examples of how different donation amounts will be used .
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 04/22/2024
The Navigators are running their bi-annual Eagle Lake Campaign to raise money for this kids’ camp that operates every summer. In the kickoff email of this campaign, they wanted to test how including gift handles in the email would affect donor conversion rate. Each gift amount described what that amount would provide for Eagle Lake.
This experiment idea came from their direct mail piece which included “tickets” for people to purchase. Each ticket was for a different amount (same as the email) and described what each level of ticket would provide for the kids at Eagle Lake.
To keep the language consistent between the email and the donation page, the treatment donation page also had the gift handles in the body copy.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that including gift handles with the corresponding impact described for potential donors to Eagle Lake can significantly increase revenue. The treatment group saw a 40.2% increase in revenue for all traffic, which indicates that providing clear, tangible examples of how different donation amounts will be used can motivate donors to give more.
However, it's important to note that the statistical level of confidence was only 82.0%, which is below the generally accepted threshold of 85%. This means that while the results show a positive impact, they are not completely reliable and should not be used for future decision making.
In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to continue testing the inclusion of gift handles and impact descriptions in donation appeals to see if the results can be replicated and validated. Additionally, it may be helpful to investigate other factors that could influence donor behavior and revenue generation to further optimize fundraising efforts for Eagle Lake.

experiments.title: How changing the color of form donate buttons impacts donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that changing the color of the donate button for donation forms will achieve a decrease in donor conversion because the color is darker than the previous and is more difficult to read.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/19/2024 - 02/21/2024
Dallas Theological Seminary is celebrating their centennial this year and for it, they had an update to their brand.
For this test, we wanted to see if the updated colors would impact overall donor conversion due to the new yellow/gold being darker than the previous.
We ran this test across all donation pages, including acquisition pages and the main donation page.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Key learnings from this experiment include:

1. The updated color of the donate button for donation forms resulted in a 9.9% increase in One-Time Donations for All Traffic.

2. Although the increase in One-Time Donations was statistically significant at 81.4% confidence level, it did not meet the standard threshold of 95% confidence for valid results.

3. While the darker color of the donate button may have initially been thought to be more difficult to read and potentially decrease donor conversion, the results showed a positive impact on conversions and revenue.

In future experimentation, it is essential to continue testing variations in colors, designs, and elements on donation forms to understand their impact on donor conversion and revenue. Additionally, it is crucial to ensure that experiments are run for a sufficient duration to gather reliable data and reach a higher level of statistical confidence before making conclusive decisions based on the results.

experiments.title: Does a book image or video convert more donors?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that changing the image on donation page to a video for web traffic will achieve a higher conversion rate because the video provides additional details of the book.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/20/2024 - 03/08/2024
Pathway to Victory is the broadcast ministry of First Dallas.
For PTV’s newest book “Are We Living on the End Times” we decided to test swapping the book image for a video promoting the offer.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that changing the image on the donation page to a video for web traffic led to a 40.9% increase in revenue for all traffic. While this result is promising, the statistical level of confidence was only 83.4%, below the generally accepted threshold of 85%.

Therefore, we cannot consider these results as valid and should not make decisions based on this experiment alone. In future experimentation, it is important to ensure that the sample size is sufficient to achieve a higher level of statistical significance. Additionally, it is crucial to continue testing and refining different elements to optimize conversion rates effectively.

experiments.title: How does adding premium language to the donation page affect recurring donor conversion?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding premium language to the donation page for potential donors will achieve an increase in donor conversion because of the additional clarity and continuity between the language used in the email and the donation page.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/05/2024
During the Navigators February Sustainers Appeal campaign, we decided to run a 50/50 split A/B test on the donation page. The test ran for emails 4-8 before we rolled it out for the last two emails of the campaign.
The control focused on the impact of monthly gifts and the way in which monthly sustainers are central to the organization.
The treatment was almost identical to the control other than that it also included the premium of becoming a monthly partner. This premium was included in all of the emails leading up to this. Therefore, our hypothesis was that adding the premium to the donation page would create continuity between the email and the donation page, reinforcing the incentive.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that premium language included on the donation page may have a positive impact on The Navigators audience. The treatment saw a 55.5% lift in recurring donations at an 86.3% level of confidence. While this donation page was a recurring only donation page, it is possible due to privacy concerns and certain browsers that VWO blocked a portion of their audience from the test because we did have some one-time donations also coming through.
We did see a drop in revenue at 38.6% with a 62.9% level of confidence. However, due to the fact that this was a campaign targeted at activating more recurring donors, this drop was anticipated.
Although these results did not validate, the results, in terms of number of recurring donors, were headed in a positive direction. Therefore, we chose to rollout the treatment to the entire audience for the last two emails of the campaign.
While the findings of this experiment are promising in indicating a potential increase in donor conversion by adding premium language to the donation page, further testing with a larger sample size or different variations may be needed to confirm these results. It is also important to consider other factors that may have influenced the results, such as external events or changes in donor behavior.

experiments.title: How does adding premium language to the donation page affect donor conversion?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding premium language to the donation page for potential donors will achieve an increase in donor conversion because of the additional clarity and continuity between the language used in the email and the donation page.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/15/2024 - 02/28/2024
During the Navigators February Sustainers Appeal campaign, we decided to run a 50/50 split A/B test on the donation page. The test ran for emails 4-8 before we rolled it out for the last two emails of the campaign.
The control focused on the impact of monthly gifts and the way in which monthly sustainers are central to the organization.
The treatment was almost identical to the control other than that it also included the premium of becoming a monthly partner. This premium was included in all of the emails leading up to this. Therefore, our hypothesis was that adding the premium to the donation page would create continuity between the email and the donation page, reinforcing the incentive.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that premium language included on the donation page may have a positive impact on The Navigators audience. The treatment saw a 35.6% lift in recurring donations at a 73.3% level of confidence, as well as a 29.3% lift at a 93.1% level of confidence for overall transactions. While this donation page was a recurring only donation page, it is possible due to privacy concerns and certain browsers that VWO blocked a portion of their audience from the test, hence one-time donations also coming through.

We did see a slight drop in revenue at a 10.8% drop at a 32.9% level of confidence. However, due to the fact that this was a campaign targeted at activating more recurring donors, this drop was anticipated.

Although these results did not validate, they were headed in a positive direction. Therefore, we chose to rollout the treatment to the entire audience for the last two emails of the campaign.

While the findings of this experiment are promising in indicating a potential increase in donor conversion by adding premium language to the donation page, further testing with a larger sample size or different variations may be needed to confirm these results. It is also important to consider other factors that may have influenced the results, such as external events or changes in donor behavior.

experiments.title: How removing images and html styling impacted click through rates.
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that stripping html from an email for the Today in the Word Audience will achieve a higher CTR because it will appeal more personal and less spam like.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/08/2024
For this experiment, we wanted to see if we could improve the CTR of an email by changing the layout to make it feel like it is a personal email. To do this we stripped all of the HTML, besides the header logo at the top.
In doing this it makes the email seem less like a marketing email and more like a 1 on 1 email.
experiments.keyLearnings: The key learning from this experiment is that stripping HTML from an email for the Today in the Word Audience can significantly increase click-through rates (CTR). The treatment, which made the email feel more personal by removing all HTML except for the header logo, resulted in a 10.9% increase in clicks for all traffic.

experiments.title: How removing an image in an MMS message affects donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that removing an image in an MMS message for lapsed donors will achieve an increase in conversion rate because it will appear to be more personal and will look less like marketing.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/21/2024
The Heritage Foundation was running SMS and MMS ads as part of their kickoff campaign. They had leveraged texting because of the personal nature of the communication, two-way conversation ability, and high deliverability, and wanted to test a P2P MMS platform However, they wondered if their personal message was undermined by the image they sent alongside the message, since that’s not something that people usually send along with 1:1 communications.
They split their list in half and sent half of the list an MMS without an image to see if it increased conversion rate. This ended up converting the message to an SMS message, which should be noted.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that removing an image in an MMS message for lapsed donors resulted in a significant increase in donations. However, the statistical level of confidence was below 85%, which means that these results cannot be considered valid.

Based on this experiment, it can be concluded that personalization and a less marketing-like approach can have a positive impact on the conversion rate. This suggests that future experimentation should focus on creating more personalized and less promotional messages for lapsed donors.

To ensure more valid results, future experiments should aim for a higher statistical level of confidence. It might be beneficial to increase the sample size or consider other factors that could influence the conversion rate, such as the timing of the messages or the wording of the texts.

Overall, the experiment highlights the potential effectiveness of personalization in communication with lapsed donors, and this insight should be taken into account when planning future campaigns and experiments.

experiments.title: How adding an anchor button on an experience page impacted conversions
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by adding an anchor button at the top of the page for web traffic will achieve a higher conversion rate because it makes the offer more easily accessible.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/30/2023 - 11/22/2023
Pathway to Victory is the broadcast ministry of First Dallas.
For November 2023, PTV released a brand-new children’s book by Julia Jeffress Sadler. To promote this page, PTV built an experience page with tons of graphics, testimonials from readers and images of the interior of the book. At the bottom of the page is where the web visitor can select the offer bundle they would like to receive with their generous donation.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment was a decrease of 11.6% with a 92% level of confidence in transactions.
We believe that the version with the button actually caused web visitors to miss all value proposition for the offer and made it more transactional in nature.

experiments.title: How using an image of the content offer in a housefile email affects click-through rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding an image to the email for potential downloaders will achieve a higher click-through rate because users will be able to see what they’re requesting before clicking out of the email.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/23/2024
The Navigators sent a free content offer to their housefile list through email. The content offer was a free 12-month calendar and users had to provide their name, email, and home address on the landing page to receive a physical copy. Following the landing page, they were redirected to an instant donation page where they were asked to make a gift to support the Navigators work.
Within the email, they wanted to test how adding an image of the calendar would affect click-through rate from the email to the landing page.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding an image of the calendar to the email did not have a positive impact on the click-through rate to the landing page. In fact, the treatment group showed a 3.8% decrease in clicks compared to the control group with an 80.3% level of confidence.
Moving forward, it is recommended to conduct further experiments with a larger sample size and a higher statistical level of confidence to determine the true impact of adding an image to the email. Additionally, other factors such as the content of the email, the subject line, and the timing of the email should be taken into consideration in future experiments. It is also important to continue testing different variables to optimize the email and landing page conversion rates for future campaigns.

experiments.title: How an image of the premium impacted revenue for donors
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by including an image of the book for existing donors will achieve a higher revenue because their is a visual appeal of the new book.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 10/30/2023
Pathway to Victory is a broadcast ministry of First Dallas.
Each month PTV promotes a new premium for the month. For this month we decided to retest using an image of the book to see if it would increase revenue.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that including an image of the book in the promotion resulted in a significant increase in revenue for all traffic. While the statistical level of confidence is not quite at the desired 95%, the results are approaching validity, indicating that the image likely had a positive impact on donor response.

In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to continue testing the inclusion of images in promotions to further validate the results. This could involve testing different types of images, such as a picture of the author or a graphic related to the book's content, to see if they have a similar effect on donor response.

Additionally, it would be helpful to analyze other metrics, such as click-through rates and conversion rates, to gain a more comprehensive understanding of the impact of including images in promotions. This will further inform decision-making and provide a more accurate assessment of the effectiveness of different promotional strategies.

Overall, the results of this experiment suggest that including an image of the book in promotions can have a positive impact on donor response. By continuing to test and refine this strategy, the nonprofit can potentially increase revenue and attract more donors.

experiments.title: How Turning Campaign Graphics into a Plain-Text Design Affects Conversion on Donation page
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using a plain-text donation page rather than stylized campaign graphics for RTB’s FYE campaign  will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate because it positions the copy/value as being from a human..
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/09/2023 - 07/03/2023
For Reason to Believe’s Fiscal Year End campaign, they had created an array of graphics illustrating their goals and the impact of their Scholar Community. We hypothesized that removing these graphics on the donation page in favor of a plain text design would lead to an increase in donor conversion rate. Note that any copy in the images was translated into bullet points for the plain-text treatment.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are:
Plain-text donation pages are more effective in converting donors compared to stylized campaign graphics. The treatment group experienced a 332.0% increase in donations, indicating that the plain-text design resonated better with potential donors. The plain-text design may have created a more personal and relatable experience for visitors, leading to higher engagement and donations.
The statistical level of confidence for the increase in donations is 93.2%, which is approaching the 95% threshold for validity. This suggests that further testing would be necessary to ensure the results are consistent and reliable — the test should also have a greater sample size than the 7 donations for validity.
Based on these learnings, it is recommended to continue testing and refining the plain-text donation page design. Further experimentation can be conducted to validate the results and explore additional factors that may influence donor conversion rates.
Overall, this experiment provides valuable insights for improving donor conversion rates through the use of plain-text design and a more human-centric approach. By continuing to iterate and test, the organization can optimize its donation page for better fundraising outcomes.

experiments.title: How does reinforcing the security of a donation with visual cues affect donation rates?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that including more prevalent security-themed enhancements will increase donations by anticipating security concerns and overcoming hesitancy at the point of completing the transaction.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/01/2023 - 03/18/2023
Care Net is an organization that provides support and resources to women and men considering abortion.
They have been acquiring donors online for many years now but have seen the number of donations/donor conversion rate drop over the last several months.
Many donors feel hesitancy or spend the majority of time in the “consideration” stage of a transaction at or near the button. This is where the momentum most often stops. How would adding a security “lock” icon to the button affect donation rates?
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding a security "lock" icon to the donation button resulted in a significant increase in revenue for all traffic. While the treatment showed a 122.8% increase in revenue, the statistical level of confidence was only at 82.0%. This means that the results cannot be considered valid but can be considered directional with potential for future iterations.
However, the positive impact observed in this experiment suggests that including more prevalent security-themed enhancements may be effective in addressing donor hesitancy and increasing donation rates. Therefore, it would be advisable to continue testing and experimenting with different security enhancements in future campaigns to further validate their impact on donor conversion rates.
It is important to note that future experiments should consider a longer testing period to gather more data and increase the statistical level of confidence. This will ensure that the results are more reliable and can be used for making informed decisions.
Overall, the key learning is that security-themed enhancements have the potential positively increase average gifts and overall revenue. Further experimentation is needed to verify these findings and determine the most effective security enhancements to implement in future campaigns.

experiments.title: How the structure and design of the email acquisition page impacts email signups
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a more designed landing page that presents the reader with a different experience showing components of the course will lead to a higher email sign-up rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/07/2023 - 07/02/2023
Luther Seminary is offering a study guide about the Book of Psalms through paid Facebook ads. We want to increase the email sign-up rate on the landing page therefore we decided to test the design of the landing page.
Historically, we’ve tested into a more simple donation page, so with this treatment, we wanted to see if a more designed approach that presents the characteristics of the study guide in a new way would lead to more people signing up.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are:

1. The treatment, which involved a more designed landing page, resulted in a 9.2% decrease in email acquisitions for all traffic compared to the control page with a 90.4% level of confidence.

2. Desktop visitors had a significant decrease of 216% in email acquisitions compared to the control group. The statistical level of confidence for this result was 99%.

Based on these findings, it can be concluded that the more designed landing page did not lead to a higher email sign-up rate. In fact, it had a negative impact, particularly among desktop visitors.

experiments.title: How a radical redesign impacted monthly giving for membership renewals
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a radical redesign with customized value proposition for returning members looking to renew will achieve an increase in donor conversion because the enhanced appeal and exclusivity focus on what the user wants to do rather than the organization’s internal goals. because  the enhanced appeal and exclusivity focus on what the user wants to do rather than the organization’s internal goals.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/09/2023 - 07/02/2023
For visitors looking to renew their membership from the homepage, we wondered if the existing donation page language for renewals was too focused on the organization. We developed a radical redesign language with customized value proposition for existing members who are already familiar with the organization and had shown historical motivation to make a gift. We hypothesized that increasing the value prop elements of appeal and exclusivity would frame a significantly more attractive way of describing what the user desires. Even if the internal goal is to get a user to renew their membership, the user wants to help protect our nation’s founding principles and keep their membership active. We wanted to communicate this clearly on the page to see if it would increase conversion. We updated the language to recognize that we were talking to visitors who had previously supported the organization and had arrived on this page to make an additional gift to support the organization. We also tested the presentation of the monthly ask, changing it from a checkbox after the gift amount had already been selected to a tabbed form with “Join the Leaders Club” as the text for monthly gifts.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running the experiment for two months, the radical redesign treatment of the donation page with a customized value proposition for returning members looking to renew resulted in a 97% increase in Leaders Club Member conversion with 92% level of confidence. Although the statistical level of confidence is below 95%, the results are directionally approaching validity. This suggests that the enhanced appeal and exclusivity focused on what the user wants to do rather than the organization's internal goals successfully attracted more donors. The treatment version also removed an additional step that asked the visitor to select which area their donation should go towards, so this reduced friction could have also played a role in the increased conversion.
We also saw flat results for one-time donations on this page, so there is opportunity to isolate the value proposition copy and do further testing to increase one-time gifts as well as monthly. We believe future iterations could include additional clarity around the benefits of being a Leaders Club member in addition to the tabbed form design and title to help more visitors choose this option.
Overall, the key learning is to focus on the user's desires and motivations, enhancing the value proposition in alignment with what we already know about the intended audience, in order to increase donor conversion rates.

experiments.title: How adding a premium to a content offer instant donation page impacted donations
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a premium to an instant donation page for potential donors will achieve an increase in donations because it will provide extra incentive for people to make a gift.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/31/2023 - 07/03/2023
TPPF’s School Choice survey is producing a low instant donor conversion rate once users complete the survey and are redirected to an instant donation page. In an effort to increase donation conversion rate, we’ve built a treatment that leverages a premium offer when someone makes a donation of at least $10.
The premium is TPPF’s Pocket Constitution. We’ve seen past success when offering the Pocket Constitution as a free offer, so we want to see if using the content offer as a premium in this instance proves to be successful through an increase in donations.
The only difference between the control and treatment donation page is the closing bold paragraph in the treatment that informs page visitors of the premium opportunity and incudes an image of the premium to the right of the paragraph for visual appeal.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running for a month, the treatment produced a 100% decrease in donor conversion rate with an 84.5% level of confidence. The treatment produced zero donations compared to two to the control.

Despite the nearly valid level of confidence, the outcome of this experiment should be considered lightly due to a total of only two transactions. The recommended number of transactions to confidently review outcomes is 50, so these results are well below that bar.

The traffic sources to this experiment was limited to only a Dear Reader, as we turned the School Choice offer off in paid social due to consistently low performance. Thus, this experiment did not benefit from the healthy amount of traffic it needed to generate more transactions.

The treatment would be worth testing again on another more popular/active offer to gain a better idea of the effectiveness of the premium concept and the specific premium offer.

experiments.title: How a pulsing heart sticky donate button affected donations on homepage
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by adding a side donate button for homepage visitors will achieve higher conversion in donors because the side button has a pulsing sacred heart and is eye catching.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/13/2023 - 07/03/2023
Avila is an organization that aims to draw Christians worldwide into deeper union with Christ through mystagogically oriented spiritual education and formation.
In an effort to increase donations from the Spiritual Directions Homepage we decided to test adding a side sticky donate button with a pulsing heart.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are:
1. The treatment version with the side donate button featuring a pulsing sacred heart significantly increased donations, with a 193.6% increase for all traffic.
2. The treatment version had a higher level of confidence (91.2%) compared to the control version, indicating that the results are approaching validity.
3. When looking at specific visitor segments, the treatment version showed a significant increase in donations for desktop visitors (5903% increase with 97% confidence level) and email visitors (9% increase with 96% confidence level).
4. The changes made in the treatment version, including the addition of the side donate button with a pulsing sacred heart, seem to have a positive impact on donor conversion.
To use these learnings in future experimentation, it would be beneficial to conduct further tests to validate the findings and increase the level of confidence. This could involve testing different versions of the side donate button, such as variations in size, color, or animation. Additionally, exploring other elements on the homepage that could be modified, such as placement or wording of the donate button, could provide further insights into increasing donations. It's also important to consider the specific characteristics of different visitor segments and tailor the donation strategy accordingly, as seen with the significant increase in donations for desktop and email visitors in this experiment.
When results of the blog page experiment is combined with the blog pages results of the same test (https://app.winstonknows.com/experiment/6142) we find a 66% increase in donations with a 94% level of confidence

experiments.title: How a Donation Interruptor Impacts Monthly Giving on an Offer’s Instant Donation Page
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that donation interruptor  for users who pre-select a one-time gift will achieve an increase monthly donation conversion because psychologically it lowers the amount of their original gift, while providing value prop copy about how that smaller amount each month has a far greater impact..
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/26/2023 - 07/02/2023
The Donation Interruptor is an experiment that we’ve tested on IMB’s general donation page in the past. For a greater collection of data, we wanted to test the experience on one of their offers, a Course on Prayer. The tabbed form on the donation page pre-selects One-Time, we hypothesized that a pop-up appearing directly after they submit their gift amount (but before they provide their payment info) will increase monthly gifts. The reason being that the popup will present the user value copy around how a lower amount (but each month) can make a greater impact even though a lower gift upfront.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that implementing the Donation Interruptor on the Course on Prayer offer resulted in a significant increase in monthly donations for all traffic. Although the statistical level of confidence is below the standard threshold of 95%, and the results are approaching validity.
This suggests that the interruptor pop-up, which highlights the impact of smaller monthly donations, has the potential to influence donors to choose the monthly giving option instead of a one-time gift. However, further testing is necessary to confirm these findings and ensure their validity.
One observation is that although this increased monthly gifts, the treatment does appear to lower the total number of gifts. This however, did not validate as a statistical result. It is something to continue observing, while also keeping in mind that repeated monthly gifts can still be an exponential revenue gain even despite a small decrease off in total gifts

experiments.title: How testing gift images in a dear reader helps influence gifts during a high urgency campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding images of the thank you gifts for potential donors will achieve a change in donor conversion rates because people want to see the gifts they will be getting as a thank-you for their donation..
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/25/2023 - 06/04/2023
WUNC is North Carolina’s largest NPR station. During their spring pledge drive, we wanted to run a 3-way test to see whether promoting their premium thank-you gifts using a dear reader style placement would help influence more donations.
We’ve tested dear reader asks in the past, with little lift in direct donations. So this test was designed to see whether having the images, versus not (and no-dear reader at all) would help influence overall online donations.
experiments.keyLearnings:
While we did not see a lot of direct donations that can be attributed to the dear readers, we did see a slight lift approaching a valid level of confidence by using the dear reader overall, with a higher lift in donor conversion rates through using the images of potential thank you gifts.

Further testing of this concept would test the placement of the dear reader, as well as strengthening the language and making the connection between those reading the news articles and the 'why' they need to donate to support the work of WUNC.

experiments.title: How personalizing a sticky bar with an on-air personality impacted donations during a high-urgency campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that personalizing a sticky bar with an on-air personality for pledge donors will achieve a change in donor conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/25/2023 - 05/04/2023
WUNC is North Carolina’s largest NPR station. Since a lot of web traffic during pledge drives is driven by offline sources (mainly radio) we wanted to see whether personalizing a sticky bar with a quote/image from a specific on-air personality, who would be on the air during a specific time and day, would make an impact on overall online donations.
We ran this as a 3-way test, with a control, sticky bar with quote but no image, and sticky bar with quote and image of on-air personality.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The experiment aimed to test whether personalizing a sticky bar with a specific on-air personality would impact overall online donations for WUNC, a nonprofit NPR station. The treatment showed a 13.4% increase in donations for all traffic, with an 87.6% statistical level of confidence, indicating that personalizing the sticky bar had a positive effect on donor conversion rates. However, the results are not entirely valid and require further testing to ensure the intended impact.
While results varied from day-to-day, the overall lift that adding a sticky bar to the homepage during the pledge drive was impactful, with an 87% level of confidence that adding one would help increase overall donations.
Interestingly enough, while the image treatment version had a slightly lower conversion rate vis-a-vis the impact on overall online donations, when digging into the directly-attributed results, the treatment version had 6 gifts compared to the no image version of 3 gifts.
Further testing is definitely warranted, as is trying to quantify the connection between offline (radio) and online traffic.

experiments.title: How a radical redesign for Grace Gone Wild impacted conversion on Facebook

rows.name: PTV DA Grace Gone Wild traditional
PTV DA Grace Gone Wild radical

experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by promoting the book with an exciting creative, that matches the cover for Facebook viewers will achieve a higher conversion of donors because there is excitement around the topic and interest.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/03/2023 - 06/22/2023
Pathway to Victory is a broadcast ministry of First Baptist Dallas. Through radio, television, digital resources, and books, Dr. Robert Jeffress delivers bold, biblical truth to equip the saints in piercing a dark culture.
Grace Gone Wild is a book published by PTV. In the past, this book has not be very successful. Therefore in an effort to increase conversions we decided to test a radical approach to the Facebook creative to see if we could increase conversions.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The A/B experiment was conducted to test whether promoting the book "Grace Gone Wild" with an exciting creative that matches the cover on Facebook would increase conversions.
The results showed that the treatment had an 11.7% decrease in clicks for all traffic, indicating that the control version had a higher conversion rate. However, since the statistical level of confidence was just below 95%, further testing would be necessary to validate the results fully.
Another significant observation was that the cost per click increased by 67% with a 100% level of confidence. This information could be beneficial in future experimentation.
The key learning from this experiment is that an exciting creative alone may not necessarily increase conversions, as evidenced by the decrease in clicks for all traffic in the treatment. The language used in the promotional campaign may also be a factor worthy of testing in future experiments.
