Based on these results, we should continue to conduct further experimentation to explore the most effective messaging and creative strategies for promoting the book, "Grace Gone Wild."

experiments.title: How a Security Callout on a Landing Page Impacts Email Acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that implementing a security callout at bottom of email acquisition form for users on the How to Save a World landing page will achieve an increase in acquisition rate because it relieves the stress and anxiety around how their email will be used.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/16/2023 - 06/03/2023
For Navigators’ How to Save the World landing page, we hypothesized that a simple security callout beneath the form would increase email acquisition rate. This short line of copy reinstates to the user that their information is secure and that they are only providing their email in order to receive the offer.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that implementing a security callout at the bottom of an email acquisition form on a landing page may not have a positive impact on the email acquisition rate. The treatment group resulted in a 12.9% decrease in email acquired for all traffic with an 88.3% level of confidence, indicating that the control group was more effective. In addition, desktop visitors had a significant 730% decrease in email acquisition, suggesting that the callout may have had an adverse effect on this device.
In future experimentation, it is important to conduct further testing to confirm whether the security callout is effective or not. If it does not work, other possible strategies could include using social proof or highlighting the benefits of subscribing to the newsletter. Additionally, it is crucial to consider the impact of the callout on different devices and user segments to ensure that it is not hurting the acquisition rate. Overall, ongoing experimentation and analysis are vital for improving the effectiveness of email acquisition strategies.

experiments.title: How a Wiki-Style Dear Reader impacted donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a Wiki-Style Dear Reader on Hour of Power‘s daily devotional pages will achieve an increase in donor conversion because it is more visually arresting and transforms into a sticky bar if the user scrolls past it.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/24/2023 - 04/30/2023
Hour of Power is a Christian broadcast ministry that shares inspiring sermons, ebooks, and daily devotionals through their media presence and website. They have seen success in increasing email acquisition on their website’s daily devotional pages by adding Dear Readers below the devotion, and for their spring high-urgency campaign we decided to see how their traditional Dear Reader asking readers to become sustaining monthly donors would perform against a Wiki-Style Dear Reader.
The traditional Dear Reader appears on devotional pages underneath the devotion. The Wiki-Style Dear Reader appears closer to the top of the page and is above the devotional content. If a user scrolls down the page and goes past it, the Wiki-Style Dear Reader will disappear and a smaller version of it will appear at the bottom of the screen like a sticky bar.
The traditional Dear Reader only has one CTA button which brings the user directly to the campaign’s donation page, but the Wiki-Style version has two CTA buttons. The first button also leads the user directly to the donation page, but the second button that says “Give a Different Amount” opens up a small gift array within the Wiki-Style Dear Reader. The user can then select or enter the amount they would like to give and then they will be redirected to the donation page with their selection automatically chosen.
Our hypothesis was that new placement and style of the Wiki-Style Dear Reader would be more arresting and eye-catching for users on the page and would thus increase donor conversion.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This experiment did not fully validate, but we saw a decrease in donor conversion on the Wiki-Style Dear Reader with a 90% LOC. What this reveals about Hour of Power's audience is that they do not respond as well to arresting popups/sticky bars that use high-urgency language. Instead, it shows us that their audience is more responsive to CTAs that take a conversational approach.

In future testing we can try using the same kind of conversational language in the Wiki-Style that we use in the Dear Reader to clarify if their audience wasn't responding because of the language used in the Wiki-Style or if it was due the placement/presentation of the Wiki-Style.

experiments.title: How removing a donation page’s header image on mobile affected donations
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that removing a donation page’s background header image for mobile viewers will achieve an increase in instant donation conversion rate because they will be less distracted by imagery and likely to read more of the value proposition copy.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/05/2023 - 05/18/2023
In an effort to increase instant donation conversion rates on a policy organization’s voter fraud survey offer, we created a donation page treatment that removed the background header image on the mobile experience.
We believed that by removing this header image and lessening the amount of negative space above the fold, the viewer would read more of the page’s content and be more likely to donate.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are as follows:
Removing the background header image on the mobile donation page did not have the intended effect of increasing the instant donation conversion rate. In fact, the treatment version saw a 54.1% decrease in donations compared to the control version.The treatment version also resulted in a 73.2% decrease in average gift compared to the control version.
The experiment only ran for two weeks, but the decrease in donations with an 86.3% level of confidence and decrease in average gift prompted us to stop the experiment before results could fully validate.
Reasons for the decrease in donations and average gift could include:
The lack of the header image may have led to visitors thinking there was too much copy to read.The header image of the organization's building may contribute to a feeling of legitimacy that helped prospective donors make the decision to contribute.

experiments.title: How implementing a goal thermometer on a high urgency campaign donation page impacted donations- attempt #2
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that implementing a goal thermometer for donation page visitors amidst a high urgency campaign will achieve an increase in donations because page visitors will be motivated to help contribute to and reach the end goal of the campaign.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/17/2023 - 05/01/2023
As part of a March high urgency campaign for the Illinois Policy Institute, we want to test the impact of adding a goal thermometer on the campaign donation page. The goal is to reach a $50,000 matching gift. The thermometer syncs with GA to track up-to-date campaign revenue, so donation page visitors will see how much money has been raised towards the campaign goal at the time they’re visiting the page.
We’re curious to see what kind of impact the visible progress and end goal seen through the thermometer will impacted donations.
This is our second try at this test. We extended the deadline of the campaign and will re-run this test.
The initial test resulted in a 7.8% increase with a 34.8% level of confidence to the treatment and 9% increase in revenue with a 65.4% level of confidence.
Let’s see how our second test performs.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that the implementation of a goal thermometer for donation page visitors did not result in an increase in donations. In fact, the treatment group saw a significant 46.6% decrease in donations compared to the control group with a nearly valid 83.5% level of confidence.
The treatment went in the wrong direction compared to going in the right direction the first time we ran this experiment. That said, each experiment did produce an increase in revenue through the treatment.
1st test: 9% increase in revenue with a 65.4% level of confidence.
2nd  test: 16.7% increase in revenue with a 56.4% level of confidence.
Oddly, mobile donations through the treatment increased by 58.7% in the first test but saw a 77.5% decrease the second test. The second test did have a significantly smaller sample size, however.
It would be wise to test this again for a future high urgency campaign. An additional hypothesis would be that a more attainable goal would help produce an increase in donations. The goal was not even halfway reached for this campaign, which may have spoiled donor motivation because they felt their gift would not make a big enough dent towards accomplishing the giving goal. Revenue went up in both experiments, so it'd be interesting to see if it would go up the next time this experiment is run as well.

experiments.title: How a sticky donate button on article pages with a slide-out donor appeal impacts donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a sticky donate button with a slide-out CTA featuring additional value proposition copy will increase donor conversion by strengthening the value proposition of the sticky donate button.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/23/2023 - 03/31/2023
FamilyLife is a Christian ministry serving families worldwide. They had previously tested a sticky donate button on article pages that floated on the right-hand side of the page with a pulsing heart and linked to the main donation page. This sticky button improved donor conversion. They wanted to test a new version of that button with an expanded value proposition that would appear in a slide-out when the button was clicked. This they posited would enable visitors to receive a reason to donate before visiting the donation page itself. Visitors could then choose to give or dismiss the message altogether.
For the experiment, they wanted to test two different value propositions. One would focus on FamilyLife’s standard appeal message about making a gift to support families in crisis. The other would focus on the perceived value the visitor received from FamilyLife this year and ask them to make a gift to keep FamilyLife here for them and their family.
Originally these two variations were going to be tested against the prior winning sticky donate button that lacked a slide-out CTA message. Due to a setup error, the control experience lacked a sticky donate button altogether, so it became an experiment between these two competing value proposition slide-outs and no sticky donate button.
experiments.keyLearnings:
We found that inviting visitors to reflect on the value FamilyLife provided them and then asking them to donate to keep FamilyLife there for them and their family was far more motivational than asking them to make a gift to help other families. We hypothesize this is because it is harder for a potential donor to think about the needs of unidentified "families in critical need" than it is to remember their needs and how FamilyLife has been their for them.
We will continue testing this value proposition to further refine it and identify key motivational elements for donor conversion. Our next test will be this slide-out CTA sticky button against the former control experience of just a sticky donate button without a slide-out message.

experiments.title: How a lower ask amount affects revenue on a Wikipedia-style article appeal during a high-urgency campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a lower recurring donation ask on a Wikipedia-style in-article appeal banner will achieve greater revenue by offering a lower commitment, thus increasing donations enough to offset the lower ask amount.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/16/2023 - 03/24/2023
During a high-urgency campaign focused on recurring donor acquisition, we wanted to know if lowering the ask amount on a Wikipedia-style in-article appeal would increase recurring donor rates by offering a lower commitment given that donors would be giving on a monthly basis.
This test followed on the heels of a previous experiment (during the same campaign), where we learned that the Wikipedia-style appeal tactic outperformed both a banner ad and a slide-in appeal when presented on article pages being viewed by prospective donors.
We set this up as an A/B test with roughly 50% of traffic seeing the $30 ask and roughly 50% of traffic seeing the $15 ask in the hopes that donor volume would outweigh the lower ask and provide a net increase in revenue.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running this experiment for 5 days, we were unable to validate our hypothesis that a lower ask amount would lift donations enough to overcome the lower ask and produce a net increase in revenue over an assumed 11-month period.
The treatment was only able to produce one additional recurring gift and the 48% decrease in revenue (81.4% level of confidence) did not justify continuing to run this experiment. Based on these results, we elected to run the $30 ask for the remainder of the campaign since it appeared that the decreased ask amount did little to increase the volume of donors while cutting overall revenue nearly in half.

experiments.title: How increasing urgency by including a dynamic donor countdown to a banner ad affects donation rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a dynamic donor countdown to the emergency banner for potential donors will achieve increased clicks to the donation page because people respond to an urgent message.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/25/2021 - 05/08/2022
If we added a dynamic donor countdown to the emergency banner, donor conversion will increase because donors respond to urgent messaging. We used an emergency alert banner vs. an emergency banner with a countdown as our variable treatment.
Note – Dynamically counts down from 25. After a gift, the number will decrease to confirm with donors that the counter is valid.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Notable Results & Recommendations:
• Test stopped due to lack of significant results.
• Unique banner click rate was higher for the variant (5%) at a non-statistically valid level of confidence (65%).
• Donor conversion was higher for the variant (5%) at a non-statistically valid level of confidence (65%).
• Revenue per visitor was lower for the variant (-5%) at a non-statistically valid level of confidence (65%).
• Recommendation is not to add the dynamic counter to the emergency banner.

experiments.title: How using images of politicians impacted petition signatures
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using images of well-known politicians for petition signers will achieve an increase in signatures because the motivation to sign would be more heavily impacted by emotion by association.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/12/2023 - 01/28/2023
On a petition to repeal alcohol tax, we wondered if instead of using a standard image of the issue itself, how petition signers would respond to images of the politicians in the decision-making seats who were the ones implementing the policies. Our hypothesis was that the treatments that used politician images would invoke more emotion from visitors which might fuel their momentum to complete the petition. We split traffic 3 ways and let the test run for 16 days.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that using images of famous politicians for petition signers did not achieve the intended increase in signatures. The control version of the petition outperformed the treatment with a statistical level of confidence approaching validity. The use of politician images did not motivate visitors to sign the petition, and some categories of visitors, such as social, female, and 25-34 age group, had a significant decrease in signatures with a high level of confidence. Desktop and other visitors had an increase in signatures, but these results were not consistent across all traffic.

In future experimentation, it may be necessary to test the use of images of politicians in a more targeted way, such as using images of politicians who are in favor of repealing the alcohol tax. Also, it may be useful to test different types of emotional appeals, beyond the association with politicians, to motivate visitors to sign the petition. The language used in both versions of the petition was similar, so testing different wording may also be beneficial for future experiments. It is also important to consider the characteristics and preferences of different visitor segments when implementing new designs or strategies.

experiments.title: How a radical redesign impacted gifts on a post-petition donation page
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using more you-focused language for petition signers will achieve an increase in donor conversion because the emphasis is less on the organization and more about what the donor wants to see happen.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/31/2022 - 12/12/2022
When evaluating our standard donation page language presented to someone after they signed a petition, we noticed the language was very organization-centric and we wondered if the value proposition could be reconstructed. The control copy says “Would you consider donating so we can show this petition to people on social media?” and we wondered if this was what the donor actually wanted to do or if we could make a more compelling case for support using more donor-centric language.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The experiment revealed that using more donor-centric language for petition signers resulted in a 15.4% increase in donations with an 89.6% level of confidence. While the statistical level of confidence is below the 95% threshold, the results are approaching validity, implying that further testing is necessary to confirm its effectiveness. Additionally, the (Other) visitors had an 86% increase in donations with a 98% level of confidence.

The key takeaway from this experiment is that a shift towards more you-focused language is likely to increase donor conversion rates. Future experimentation should focus on refining the language to gain further improvements in the results. Based on these insights, Canadian Taxpayers Federation can improve its donation conversion rates by creating more compelling donor-centric value propositions.

experiments.title: How adding a sticky “my cart” button when a user begins a donation affects conversion rates

rows.name: Cart button at top of page

experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a sticky cart button for donors who add an item to their cart (scroll persistent), will achieve a greater number of donor conversions.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/22/2021 - 09/27/2022
Added a sticky cart button that appears when users add an item to their cart (scroll persistent).
experiments.keyLearnings:
Notable Results & Recommendations
• Mobile donor conversion was higher for the sticky cart (42%) at a statistically valid LoC (95%).
• Mobile revenue per visitor was higher for the sticky cart (68%) at a statistically valid LoC (98%).
• Recommendation is sticky cart addition to all gift catalog-related pages.
• Projected yearly incremental lift of $200,000.

experiments.title: How adding a micro-goal slider that stresses urgency to a sponsorship page affects conversion rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a micro-goal slider to the sponsorship page that stresses urgency for potential donors will achieve increase sponsorship conversion  because there is a clear CTA. because  there is a clear CTA.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/20/2022 - 03/28/2022
Adding a micro-goal slider to the sponsorship page that stresses urgency will increase sponsorship conversion because there is a clear CTA. When a user enters the Child List Sponsorship page in the variant, a popup will appear after 3 seconds on the page. It presents a variable number of lifeline children that need to be sponsored. The popup scrolls with the page and can be closed by the user.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Notable Results & Recommendations:
• Desktop donor conversion was higher for the variant (28%) at a statistically valid confidence level (99%).
• Recommendation is to add the slider to all sponsorship-related pages.
• Overall, the variant produced an increase (12%) at a non-statistically valid confidence level (92%).
• RPV was higher for the variant (13%) at a non-statistically valid confidence level (84%).
• Desktop RPV was higher for the variant (34%) at a non-statistically valid confidence level (95%).
• Mobile donor conversion was slightly higher for the variant (2%) at a non-statistically valid confidence level (16%).
• Mobile RPV was slightly lower for the variant (-6%) at a non-statistically valid confidence level (66%).
• Based on 2021 figures (assuming fixed traffic and average gift), we project an incremental lift of $76,000 annually. The net 60-month value of these donors is approximately $1.4M.
• Given the statistical significance in desktop donor conversion (28%) and limited directional downside on mobile, we recommend adding the slider to all sponsorship pages.
• Results may have had an even greater significance if not for the Ukraine emergency. The experiment was close to validating five days in, but highly motivated traffic related to Ukraine evened out the treatments.
• Additionally, we look to test different versions of copy and visual elements employed by the slider to optimize the experience further.

experiments.title: How a callout emphasizing a year-end matching challenges impacts donor conversion on an acquisition donation page
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a callout emphasizing a year-end matching challenge to the acquisition offer donation page will increase donor conversion by increasing the appeal of making a gift.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/20/2022 - 01/01/2023
Focus on the Family is an international ministry that equips families to grow closer to one another and to God. During their year-end campaign, they wanted to test the impact of emphasizing a year-end matching challenge on their acquisition offer donation pages to see if that would increase donor conversion on the page. They hypothesized that the opportunity to “double your impact” would increase the appeal of making a gift and with it the donor conversion on the page. The control page mentioned the matching challenge in the body copy. For the treatment page, they would add a callout box right under the donation button, the point of decision, to emphasize the matching opportunity there as well. The text in the box would read, “Don’t delay. The match expires on Dec 31. Give now to double your impact.”
experiments.keyLearnings:
Contrary to our hypothesis, the callout box did not increase donor conversion. There was an increase in donor conversion on desktop traffic, however since the vast majority of traffic to the page is from mobile devices, there were too few donations in the segment to validate a learning. It seems that the box may have added friction by extending the length of the page on mobile devices which outweighed any of the benefit from reminding the donor of the available match.

Further testing is needed to see if the audience on acquisition offers is as motivated by "year-end" language on the donation page as their housefile audience would be. These visitors are less likely to already be invested in the ministry of Focus on the Family and thus less likely to be motivated to give a "year-end" gift.

experiments.title: How an exit intent impacted donor conversion during year-end
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by adding an exit intent for web visitors will achieve higher donor conversion.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/03/2023
Pathway to Victory is a broadcast ministry with a mission to pierce the darkness of the world’s culture with the light of God’s truth.
For previous high-urgency campaigns, we have tested exit intent popups but have not been able to see a valid response. We decided to test this again with the calendar year-end match campaign.
Previous tests, https://app.winstonknows.com/experiment/4829 and https://app.winstonknows.com/experiment/4417
For Giving Tuesday through December 31st, PTV offered a matching campaign of up to $525k for any donation made.
The exit intent ran on the entire website for anyone that visited a site to make a donation but did not complete the donation.
experiments.keyLearnings:
For this experiment, we were very close to achieving a valid level of confidence. We saw a 4.8% increase in donor conversions with a level of confidence of 92.9%.
Compared to the 2 other times we have run this experiment, this one achieved the highest level of validation for donor conversions.
Due to these results we will move forward with rolling out the exit intent popup during high urgency campaigns and begin testing into copy, CTA and strategy within the popup itsself.

experiments.title: How showing a reminder pop up to users that abandoned the donation process affects revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that showing a pop up reminder to complete a donation for people that have closed out a donation page, without making a donation will achieve a higher amount of revenue than allowing users to remember on their own.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/18/2022 - 09/30/2022
Focus on the Family wanted to see if showing a pop-up with an opportunity to complete a donation (from a previously clicked on amount), would cause users to complete their transaction…instead of abandoning their donation all-together. This experiment was based on abandoned cart functionality often seen in the e-commerce space, but seldom used in the NPO space. Their current donation platform does not natively allow for this functionality, so we built a test using the user’s last click on a donation amount (on an abandoned or closed-out donation page) to launch a pop up with that amount, asking the user to complete their transaction with a simple nudge.
experiments.keyLearnings: This experiment ran for 41 days. While it did not reach statistical significance (only an 87% Level of Confidence), we did have some very interesting learnings. Since we were testing for revenue lift, we wanted to be sure the data was not skewed by abnormally large donation amounts from either the control or the treatment. FOTF often gets large donations that can skew a revenue experiment if we do not normalize the data. After we ran the experiment, we created a custom Google Analytics segment that only counted donations that were less than $1k in the sample size. After doing so, we noticed that the revenue for the treatment was significantly higher - an 85.2% lift in revenue. This took the average gift of this new sample group from $75.54 to $131.86. It is to be noted that prior to removing the gifts over $1k, the control had a sample size much closer to the treatment. Did this weigh into the final results? Are the motivations different for people that come to the main site vs coming from an article page or elsewhere? WHile this test provided some exciting potential lifts, it is still unclear if motivations based on entry point could skew this data. The next step in the process will be to run the same experiment, but split into two different ones, and test if the revenue lift is better or worse when directly visiting via the main page or visiting instead through a secondary page.

experiments.title: How a radical redesign impacted gifts on an instant donation page
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a radical redesign for instant donation page visitors will achieve an increase in donor conversion.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/10/2022 - 11/25/2022
When launching a new content offer, we had similar donation page language in place from previous content offers and we knew that the housefile visitors would likely be returning visitors. We wanted to test a new approach that condensed some lengthy copy on the treatment into 3 specific items the reader should know. We also updated the headline from “Thank you” to “Congratulations” and mentioned the title of the specific offer they had requested. We split traffic equally between the two pages and ran the experiment for 2 weeks before evaluating results.
experiments.keyLearnings: After two weeks, we saw a 25.7% decrease in conversion rate with 94% level of confidence, so the results were indicating a directional decrease. We believe the treatment language in the headline "you're all set" was causing visitors to leave the page before reading the remaining message. We saw valid decreases for many visitor segments, so we decided to end the experiment early and reassess the best treatment to run for these visitors. On future testing for this page, we will reconstruct the headline language that asks people to read the message below before they exit the site. Then, we can better evaluate if the "3 things you should know" approach was the true cause for the decrease, or if there was another element from the radical redesign (in this case, the headline) that was inadvertently causing friction that interfered with the test and prevented people consuming the message.

experiments.title: How placement of a premium link impacts email clicks
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by moving the placement of the link higher in the email copy for email subscribers will achieve an increase in conversions.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 11/04/2022
Pathway to Victory is a broadcast ministry that generates a majority of its revenue through monthly premium offers. Throughout the month, Pathway to Victory will email its file 4-5 times a month about the premium featured.
For the first email about the November 2022 premium, we decided to look at the placement of the link to get the premium. This particular premium is one that PTV has featured before and has seen great success. The success of this offer presented us with the opportunity to test having the link to the premium higher in the email copy.
experiments.keyLearnings:
While we did see an increase in email clicks, 16% with a 92.5% level of confidence, we did not see a significant increase in donations or revenue.

In fact we saw a very slight decrease of donation conversions by 2% with a 6% level of confidence.

 

So while we did see an increase in email engagement this did not translate to an increase in donations or revenue.

 

Further testing ideas for this offer via email include, specific dollar amount ask, other placement tests and images of the premium in the email copy.

experiments.title: How offering a free ebook for email acquisition in the home page footer instead of a general email subscription form impacted email acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that offering a free ebook as an email acquisition method in the home page footer for page vistiors will achieve an increase in emails acquired.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/16/2022 - 10/13/2022
In their home page footer, TGC gives users the opportunity to subscribe to email updates. The section says “Email Updates” and users can enter their email below and sign up. We wondered if offering a free ebook to users when they sign up would acquire more emails than the existing “email updates” sign-up opportunity, so we tested the idea. In the footer, we replaced the email updates section with an offer to receive a popular ebook for free. We included value prop telling the user how the ebook could help them and below was a sign up form including first name, last name, and email, and a button that read “Get my free ebook »”.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The control saw a 0.03% conversion rate compared to a 0.02% conversion rate for the treatment. Ultimately, the treatment had a 26.6% decrease in emails acquired with an 89.1% level of confidence. Users were more likely to sign up for email updates than to receive a free ebook.

Given that TGC offers a ton of content, which users can see as they scroll through the home page and down to the footer of the page, a user's primary intent may be to receive email updates for new content and new articles. So the opportunity to receive only a single free ebook is limiting compared to receiving lots of updates with lots of new content.

We're going to run this same experiment on TGC's Commentaries page to see if/how the user motivation adjusts from the home page to the commentaries page.

It may be a good idea to test adding value prop language to the home page footer in line with receiving email updates. Right now, the sign up opportunity only says "Email Updates" offering no value prop as to why the user might want to sign up for email updates.

experiments.title: How adding a goal thermometer to a campaign donation page affects donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a goal thermometer to a campaign donation page for Working Preacher users will achieve a higher donor conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/27/2022 - 10/31/2022
Luther Seminary ran their bi-annual Working Preacher campaign from October 1st – October 31st. We previously tested a countdown clock on the donation page for a majority of the campaign time, but we saw a valid decrease in donor conversion with the countdown clock.
We decided to test a goal thermometer for their $50,000 campaign goal on the last 4 days of the campaign. The thermometer started at $30,000 which is how much they had raised so far. The thermometer was set to grow every time someone made a donation on that page.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The goal thermometer saw a 19.5% decrease in donor conversion rate with an 89.2% level of confidence. Similar to the countdown clock, we can see from this experiment that the Working Preacher audience did not respond better to seeing the goal thermometer on the donation page.

We would like to test this concept further on another one of Luther Seminary's campaigns that's targeting a different audience so that we can try to understand if this type of "donor goal" experiment only performs negatively for the Working Preacher audience or for the Luther Seminary audience as a whole.

experiments.title: How including branding and fund type in a appeal impacts donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that removing branding and fund language for email recipients will achieve a higher donor conversion rate .
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 06/07/2022
The Navigators were running a Missionary Care campaign where they typically refer to their giving fund by name “Missionary Care Fund.” We hypothesized that by removing the branding and fund type and focusing on the impact of the fund, we would see an increase in donor conversion rate. We believe that donors aren’t motivated by the specific name of a fund but by what their gift is accomplishing.
We tested this hypothesis in an email appeal in their high urgency campaign. Instead of “Missionary Care Fund” our treatment copy used verbiage like ” help missionaries.”
experiments.keyLearnings:
Results for this experiment did not validate. We saw a 55% decrease in donor conversion with a level of confidence of 83.5%. This could indicate a near directional decrease in donor conversion rate when removing the branding and fund language from an email appeal. Upon even closer examination, we noted that the dollar amount in the appeal was supposed to be consistent at $75 and $200 was accidentally left in the control email.

It can be determined that it is likely that the Navigators' audience are familiar with the Missionary Care Fund and are not dissuaded to give by mentioning by name. Additionally, it is surprising that the higher $200 ask in the email resulted in more donations and a higher average gift of $86 vs $31.

Because this experiment did not validate and we actually ended up testing 2 variables instead of the intended one, it is recommended that this concept is retested in a medium that will get more traffic and transactions, like a sticky bar.

experiments.title: How Moving the Global Navigation Donate Button Onto Its Own Menu Line impacts donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that moving the “give now” button onto its own global navigation menu line will increase donor conversion
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/05/2022 - 07/24/2022
KCBI is a large Christian radio station in the DFW area with a worldwide listening audience. In a prior test, they’d found that the addition of a “monthly” donate button to their global site navigation menu increased donor conversion. However, there were some issues with that test that made the results unclear. We had several factors that changed in the experiment (the placement of the “give now button,” the wording on the buttons, and the addition of a monthly donate button) that clouded the results. In order to understand WHICH factor led to the increase in donor conversion, we decided to test simply moving the “give now” button out of the menu line with the other options and onto its own line. We believed this would give it prominence as it would stand on its own in the navigation bar.
Since this would only impact Desktop traffic, that is the only source of donors we examined.
experiments.keyLearnings:
While we didn't wait to reach a 95% or greater level of confidence, there was no period during the test or segment of donors where we were seeing a positive impact and it was clear that moving the button was having a directional decrease on conversion, so we ended the test.
This showed us that donors were not more likely to click the "give now" button when it was in its own menu. Next, we will test the language of the donate button, "give now" vs "donate now" to see which is the most converting.

experiments.title: How adding a humanized sticky bar impacted donor conversions on an acquisition donation page
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a humanized sticky bar to a digital acquisition donation page for prospective donors will achieve a change in conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/16/2022 - 08/01/2022
Luther Seminary was offering a free online course called Cosmic Adventure that explained the relationship between science and faith including the history behind this relationship. They offered this free course through paid Facebook ads and to their housefile through email marketing. This offer was running for almost a year and the conversion rate on the donation page was on a steep decline.
For this experiment, we wanted to see whether adding a humanized sticky bar, featuring a message and image of Andy Root, Professor at Luther Seminary and Chair of Youth and Family Ministry, would have an impact on donor conversion rates from prospective donors coming from this digital acquisition offer.
The message tried to focus the prospective donor’s attention on why they should make a gift.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Adding in a humanized sticky bar actually led to a decrease in conversion rates of 61%, but with only an 86.7% level of confidence. We hypothesize that this is due to the nature of who we included in the messaging, as well as the messaging itself. While Andy Root is well known in his community at Luther Seminary, he might not be that well known to those prospective donors who come onto the file through digital acquisition efforts--they are downloading the content because it appeals to them, and not necessarily because of who it's coming from.
Further testing on messaging and subjects will be done in the future to see if we can get a lift.
Notable changes to other metrics due to the experiment:
Revenue experienced a 59.9% decrease with a 91% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How removing branding and fund type in an appeal impacts donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that removing branding and fund language for email recipients will achieve a higher donor conversion rate .
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 06/07/2022
The Navigators were running a Missionary Care campaign where they typically refer to their giving fund by name “Missionary Care Fund.” We hypothesized that by removing the branding and fund type and focusing on the impact of the fund, we would see an increase in donor conversion rate. We believe that donors aren’t motivated by the specific name of a fund but by what their gift is accomplishing.
We tested this hypothesis in an email appeal in their high urgency campaign. Instead of “Missionary Care Fund” our treatment copy used verbiage like ” help missionaries.”
experiments.keyLearnings:
Results for this experiment did not validate. We saw a 55% decrease in donor conversion with a level of confidence of 83.5%. This could indicate a near directional decrease in donor conversion rate when removing the branding and fund language from an email appeal. Upon even closer examination, we noted that the dollar amount in the appeal was supposed to be consistent at $75 and $200 was accidentally left in the control email.

It can be determined that it is likely that the Navigators' audience are familiar with the Missionary Care Fund and are not dissuaded to give by mentioning by name. Additionally, it is surprising that the higher $200 ask in the email resulted in more donations and a higher average gift of $86 vs $31.

Because this experiment did not validate and we actually ended up testing 2 variables instead of the intended one, it is recommended that this concept is retested in a medium that will get more traffic and transactions, like a sticky bar.

experiments.title: How a radically redesigned donation form impacted gifts
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that an optimized donation form for Giving Tuesday donors will achieve an increase in donor conversion.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/22/2021 - 01/04/2022
Before launching a calendar year-end campaign, we want to test a radically redesigned treatment of Buckner’s donation form to see if we should roll out a new version during year-end. We wanted to first test this for their Giving Tuesday donation form. For the treatment, we made several changes to the form to optimize it. The credit card information was relocated to be immediately underneath the gift array (above donor information) We removed the .00 from the gift array dollar amounts and instead show $25 $50 and $100. We added a tabbed form with a monthly giving option with the header “Can you give monthly?” and added a value prop for giving monthly when the monthly tab is selected – “Show an orphan God’s love all year long with your monthly gift on Giving Tuesday”. We removed optional fields including Title and Phone Number. We revised the email sign-up disclaimer language to Our team will use your email address to share occasional updates with you and communicate the ways your gift is making a difference. And we changed the donate button text to Help a hurting child with my gift »
We split traffic to both versions of the form during their high urgency campaign for Giving Tuesday.
experiments.keyLearnings:
What we found was not what we expected, which is why it is valuable to test and assess results before making changes. Since the campaign was run on a limited window for a 24 hour giving day, we did not receive enough traffic to validate the learnings. But we did notice a trend of a 24% decrease in gifts with an 83% level of confidence. This gave us reason to pause considering implementing the changes to the form for the year-end campaign. Moving forward, we will need to test these elements individually to get a better idea of which piece was responsible for the decrease in transactions.

Notable changes to other metrics due to the experiment:
Revenue experienced a 69.6% decrease with a 95% level of confidence.
Email visitors had a 28.8% decrease in donations with a 93% level of confidence.
 	Returning visitors had a 50.9% decrease in donations with a 96% level of confidence.
 	Direct visitors had a 309.1% increase in donations with an 89% level of confidence.
 	Organic Search visitors had a 48.4% decrease in donations with an 86% level of confidence.
 	Male visitors had a 92.1% decrease in donations with a 100% level of confidence.
 	Tablet visitors had a 300.0% increase in donations with a 100% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How a radically redesigned donation page impacted monthly and one-time gifts
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a radical redesign focused on recurring giving for instant donation page visitors will achieve an increase in monthly recurring donations.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/19/2021 - 01/07/2022
Ahead of their calendar year-end campaign, DTS sent out a digital Christmas Devotional as a cultivation piece to their housefile. The control donation page used their standard appeal language with a paragraph outlining the benefits of monthly giving. To increase recurring gifts, we wanted to test a donation page treatment that specifically asked for a monthly recurring gift emphasizing a microask goal of 10 new daily monthly donors. In addition to the microask goal, we made several other optimizations to the page. We swapped out the headline to reflect that their monthly gift would have an impact 365 days a year and introduced the concept of donor autonomy and value alignment throughout the copy by asking conversational questions such as “Would you consider becoming a Cornerstone Partner?” and “Will you make a monthly gift today to help share biblical truth with the world?” We split traffic 50/50 between the control and the treatment for visitors after they signed up to receive their copy of the digital Christmas Devotional.
experiments.keyLearnings:
While we did not receive enough monthly gifts to validate the experiment's impact on recurring giving, we did see other interesting trends in overall giving as a result of running the test. We did not reach statistical significance overall, but we identified that the treatment page was showing a 29.4% lift in donor conversion with 86% level of confidence. Furthermore, as you'll see below, when we analyzed traffic by channel, mobile visitors saw a valid increase of 116% in donations with a 98% level of confidence. The treatment copy is significantly shorter than the control, so we will need to do additional testing to see if reducing the volume of text and limiting the amount of user scrolling contributed to the lift for mobile visitors. Again, even though we did not see movement in monthly giving as the experiment was intended to produce, we identified learnings to assist in improving conversion rate and overall revenue moving forward for future content offers.
Notable changes to other metrics due to the experiment:
Revenue experienced a 62.2% increase with a 90% level of confidence.
Observations of the impact on other visitor segments in the experiment:
Email visitors had a 33.2% increase in donations with an 88% level of confidence.Female visitors had a 116.0% increase in donations with an 89% level of confidence.25-34 visitors had a 100.0% decrease in donations with a 92% level of confidence.Male visitors had a 53.3% decrease in donations with a 94% level of confidence.Mobile visitors had a 116.9% increase in donations with a 98% level of confidence.New visitors had a 64.4% increase in donations with a 90% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How a radical redesign impacted donor conversion during a high urgency campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that humanizing the donation page with experiential language for fiscal year end donors will achieve an increased donor conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/28/2021 - 08/13/2021
During a fiscal year end campaign, we were seeing lower donor conversion compared to the previous year so we decided to develop a treatment to the donation page for the remainder of the campaign to see if we could improve performance. We modified the headline from “Help DTS reach the world with solid biblical training” to “You can help DTS train students to reach the world with the gospel of Jesus Christ!” We also removed body copy that seemed organization-centric that referenced enrollment trends and their accredited degree programs and instead of featuring the high-caliber quality of the training gave examples of vocations that students pursue after their education and training (counseling, pastoring, etc.) to humanize the institution and presented this as an experiential outcome for how they would “share the good news and strengthen believers” which aligns with what the donor wants to accomplish through their gift. We also introduced copy that highlighted the option to give monthly.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Since the experiment only ran during the final 2 days of the campaign, we would need to run an additional test with similar language to truly test the concept without the existing urgency of the deadline that could be influencing results. The treatment donation page had a 19.1% increase in conversion rate with 93% level of confidence. We want to take this directional lift in gifts on the page and see where else we could apply this learning.
While we did not reach statistical significance to validate the experiment, we did observe some notable changes to other metrics due to the experiment that are outlined below:
Revenue experienced a 47.3% decrease with a 97% level of confidence.
Observations of the impact on other visitor segments in the experiment:
New  visitors had a 39.8% increase in donations with a 99% level of confidence.Email visitors had a 23.9% increase in donations with a 98% level of confidence.Tablet visitors had a 100.0% decrease in donations with an 87% level of confidence.55-64 visitors had an 88.5% increase in donations with a 93% level of confidence.Female visitors had a 70.9% increase in donations with a 96% level of confidence.25-34 visitors had a 76.4% decrease in donations with a 96% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How increased urgency using a countdown clock affects email acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a countdown clock near the call-to-action for landing page visitors will achieve more ebook downloads.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/16/2020 - 01/06/2021
In a previous experiment, we used a sticky bar to try and create a heightened sense of urgency around our ebook outlining strategies to increase giving during the last week of the year. But the sticky bar made no significant difference in conversions.
As a next step, we wondered if using a countdown clock lower on the page ticking down to the last week of the year would be a better means of conveying urgency and lead to more downloads.
experiments.keyLearnings: The countdown clock led to a 10.6% increase in conversion, although it didn't quite reach our desired level of confidence. However, it's a strong indicator that a countdown clock is an effective motivator to increase downloads in this context. We'll continue to use countdown clock on acquisition pages when there is a relevant deadline - in particularly around year-end and the last week of the year.

experiments.title: How a simple text email affects click rates on a content offer email
experiments.researchQuestion: Design elements will reduce open rates and click rates.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 02/10/2021
BRI typically sends emails that have some design elements. So we wanted to test a completely simple text template to make sure that the the design elements weren’t affecting open rates or click through rates. With a recent housefile email offer, we tested the control template against a simple text email.
experiments.keyLearnings: What we found was that the design elements were not harming the conversion rates at any level of statistical validity. The click rates were lower for the simple text by 10%, and the level of validity shows a directional lift. However, the open rate was higher by 1.2% for the simple text, but a level of confidence of 59%, which means that life is entirely inconclusive. Lastly, the donation conversion rate on the simple text was 194.5% higher than the designed email, with a 95% level of confidence. And yet, we only had 34% of the donations needed to really find this test truly conclusive. All in all, this test is a mixed bag and shows that we need to continue to test as the file grows, to see if there are any conclusive effects from this style of email.

experiments.title: How adding a pulsing star icon to a sticky bar impacted clicks

rows.name: Sticky Bar Free Trial w/ Testimonial
Sticky Bar Free Trial w/ Star& Pulsing Button

experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a pulsing icon for website visitors will achieve increased clicks on the sticky bar.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/11/2020 - 10/12/2020
As we looked to increase clicks on a sticky bar to begin a free trial, we developed a treatment that added a pulsing star icon to the sticky bar. All other elements remained the same. We wanted to emphasize this as the primary call to action for website visitors since there are multiple buttons on the page with additional calls to action. We hypothesized that adding an animated element to this specific button would help it stand out more on the page as the preferred call to action.
experiments.keyLearnings:
When looking across all traffic, we did not validate results on the experiment. However, we did see that email visitors had a 272.3% increase in begin free trial signup with a 97% level of confidence, while all other segments indicated a directional decrease. This could be a result of the consistency between the messaging communicated via email and the corresponding offer on the page. We'll continue to do additional testing to introduce other elements to drive free trial signups for additional segments.

Observations of the impact on other visitor segments in the experiment:
Paid Search visitors had a 16.4% decrease in begin free trial signup with a 91% level of confidence.
 	All Traffic visitors had a 9.9% decrease in begin free trial signup with a 93% level of confidence.
 	Desktop visitors had a 14.7% decrease in begin free trial signup with a 92% level of confidence.
 	Tablet visitors had a 24.4% decrease in begin free trial signup with an 88% level of confidence.
 	Returning visitors had a 13.1% decrease in begin free trial signup with a 94% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How simplifying “Dear Reader” ads on news articles impacted donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that simplifying the “Dear Reader” ad experience for newsroom visitors will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/24/2021 - 04/02/2021
As a part of on-site promotions of a recent open letter (petition), Americans for Prosperity wanted to experiment with simplifying their “Dear Reader” ads — which are ads that promote new offers and acquisition funnels at the end of news articles — by replicating the “simplified ad experience” that was so effective within their Facebook advertising campaign.
Would this simplified ad experience also improve results for their on-site visitors to their newsroom pages?
experiments.keyLearnings:
With a nearly valid 89% level of confidence, we observed an increase in donor conversion rate of +390%. Because Americans for Prosperity was looking to maximize results on the limited time they had available to promote this offer, we decided to roll this out pretty quickly.
Notable changes to other metrics due to the experiment:
Revenue experienced a 832.5% increase with a 97% level of confidence.
Observations of the impact on other visitor segments in the experiment:
Email visitors had a 390.8% increase in donations with an 90% level of confidence.Returning  visitors had a 1.0% increase in donations with a 92% level of confidence.
It would appear that using the simplified ad experience (presenting a question, with a "Yes" or "No" button alongside it as a call to action is also as effective in on-site promotions through the "Dear Reader" ads as it was on the Facebook advertising campaign.

experiments.title: How trustmarks on a donation page impacted donations and revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a Charity Navigator trust mark for donation page visitors will achieve increase in number of gifts and average gift size.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/05/2020 - 12/10/2020
In previous experiments, we have found that adding trust marks near the donation button on a donation page can lead to an increase in donations. However, we wanted to take this learning a step further and test how the presence of ANY trust marks and the presence of CERTAIN trust marks would impact revenue.
The control version of the donation page featured two of the organization’s standard trust marks, with the first treatment removing all trust marks and the second treatment containing the standard trust marks and the additional Charity Navigator trust mark.
The Charity Navigator trust mark showed the organization’s score/ranking of 75% with the “Give with Confidence” banner. The placement for all trust marks was directly beneath the donate button at the bottom of the page.
experiments.keyLearnings:
While we did not reach a valid level of confidence for the experiment, what we observed through this experiment was the treatment with no trust marks and the treatment with the added Charity Navigator trust mark were both seeing an overall increase in conversion rate. However, when we analyze revenue and average gift performance for each version, the first treatment with no charity marks saw a slight decrease in revenue compared to the control while the second treatment with the additional Charity Navgiator trust mark was approaching a valid lift in revenue (58.3% with a 91% level of confidence). We also noted a few segments that were impacted directly, with an increase in conversion rate for both tablet and email visitors.

Notable changes to other metrics due to the experiment:
Revenue experienced a 58.3% increase with a 91% level of confidence.
Tablet visitors had a 33.3% increase in donations with a 92% level of confidence.
 	Email visitors had a 55.0% increase in donations with an 87% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How a radically redesigned donation page impacted donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a radically redesigned donation page post-survey increase donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/19/2020 - 07/09/2020
We wanted to increase donor conversion and thought the current donation page copy was causing a disjointed experience for the donor. We changed several elements to create a radical redesign test. Instead of leading with a “Success!” confirmation message upon survey submission, we wanted to draw the reader in further through personal appeal and direct continuation of the conversation. We also updated the copy with enhanced urgency and added a direct ask with a clear outcome “for a tax-deductible gift of $50, we’ll be able to inform 200 people about what’s at stake” rather than a general ask.
experiments.keyLearnings: While we hoped implementing a radical redesign would help us validate results, we did not reach a valid level of confidence and saw a directional decrease in donor conversion rate. Our hypothesis for the decrease is that the new copy did not draw as much attention to evidentials as the control. But for future tests, we will look into testing each variable—headline, evidential points, specific ask, and tone—individually so we can identify where the loss is incurred.

experiments.title: How adding additional clarity through the design affects registrations

rows.name: Event Details Box with Added Clarity

experiments.researchQuestion: Will adding additional clarity to the webinar details lead to more registrations?
experiments.summary:
Ended On:
Prior to this experiment, we had tested into using a time/date bar on the webinar registration page. It hadn’t impacted conversion significantly, but led to less inquires and confusion about the actual start time of the webinar. In this experiment, we wondered if we could use design to bring additional clarity to not just the time and date of the webinar, but to some of the additional perks of registering including live Q&A time and access to a recording afterwards.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The new design led to a 6.4% increase in registrations, although it only reached a 90.9% level of confidence. As the overall conversion rate of this page is very high, it’s increasingly difficult to valid experiments on this page. That said, reaching a 90% level of confidence on a result that’s indicating positive growth gives me the confidence to roll out Version B as the winner.
Even if it makes no difference in conversion, there is now additional clarity on the page that all registrants will get a recording, which is one of the most common questions that registrants ask after the fact.

experiments.title: How a grid layout affects clickthrough on a membership benefits page
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a grid layout lead to more clicks into the checkout process?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/27/2019 - 01/26/2020
In this experiment, we wondered if a grid style layout would be effective at showing visitors the various benefits of our 3 membership tiers, and thus lead to more clickthroughs to the checkout process.
The control used a bulleted list to describe the benefits of each tier. The treatment used a large grid with Xs and checkmarks to visually show what was included with each tier.
experiments.keyLearnings:
While we didn’t reach a 95% level of confidence, the experiment had been running for a full month and has been leaning towards validity. The consequences of being wrong in this case a fairly low. But we will conduct follow-up testing to see if we can confidently improve clicks and conversions in the membership sign-up process.
The results indicate that this more visual approach is more effective at demonstrating the member benefits and showing the value of membership. It’s possible that more info is need on the page to clarify what some of the benefits are. And we also need to ensure in future testing that we can track all the way through to a purchase.
It also looks like a new element was added into the control variant during the test. And it’s likely that this skewed the final result.

experiments.title: How a radical redesign impacted the Shoes for Orphan Souls donation page
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a radical redesign of the donation page increase overall donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/24/2019 - 08/15/2019
Based upon the low traffic volume to the Shoes for Orphan Souls donation page, we knew that we would need to try something radical in order to validate an experiment. With this in mind, we decided to launch a radical redesign of the page in conjunction with a similar experiment on Buckner’s main donation page. Both experiments tested similar elements:
We modified the form headers to be numbers steps instead of just labels.
We removed the gift array which we’ve seen help with organizations that have a large volume of mid-level and major donors
We increased the perceived security near the credit card form
We reiterated the gift size and impact near the donation button
experiments.keyLearnings:
While it did not statistically validate and it had a small number of conversions, we did see a directional lift with the radical redesigned donation page. What is surprising is that the experiment run on the main donation page that mirrored these changes saw a completely opposite result (41% decrease.)
When we analyzed both experiments, what we found was that the decrease on the main donation page was the result of a decrease in donors less than $100. The SOS donation page did not have this same issue (we actually saw an increase on this page.) The key difference is in the final subheadline on both donation pages. The SOS donation page makes an ask for $25 and the main donation page did not suggest any gift amount.
It is our hypothesis that removing the gift array from the main page increased the decision friction for the small-dollar donors. The SOS donation page did not experience the same impact since it had the $25 in the final call to action.

experiments.title: How an additional design element affected registrations from a webinar email

rows.name: Plain Text with Webinar Image and Button

experiments.researchQuestion: Will using an additional design element at the bottom of the email lead to greater webinar registrations?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/12/2019 - 06/14/2019
The majority of our emails are hyper-personal and text only. But recently, we’ve seen digest-style emails perform better using some basic design elements including simple images and HTML buttons. We believe the reason for this is that our emails can be very heavy. And if you’re not reading every line, a design element can quickly show what the offer is and provide a clear call-to-action.
In this case, we wondered if a similar approach could help improve registration rates in our webinar invitation emails.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This experiment was just shy of reaching the desired 95% confidence level. The additional design element led to 20.7% more webinar registrations than the control. This seems to validate the hypothesis that these design element help give people a better understanding of the offer and call to action by adding visual clarity around the title, date, time, and content.
However, it is possible that the novelty of this design itself is leading to an increase. We will need to test again to make sure this increase is an ongoing trend and not a one-off result.

experiments.title: How email design affects click-through in a digest or newsletter email
experiments.researchQuestion: Will additional design elements in a digest email lead to greater click-through?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/14/2019 - 03/18/2019
Email appeal experiments had given us a predisposition to think that email with button, images, and minor design elements are ineffective. But we had really only discovered that to be true in the specific context of a donation appeal.
In this experiment, we wondered if basic design elements could actually lead to greater click-throughs. The control was a plain-text style email in the format we had tested into, and the treatment added section images and clickable html buttons.
experiments.keyLearnings: While this didn’t quite reach our desired level of confidence of 95%, it was very close to validating a 32% increase in clicks. With this type of a result, we’re inclined to test it again to get greater confidence in the learning. There’s the possibility of a novelty effect – it improved clicks simply because it is different, and not necessarily because it is better long term.

experiments.title: How the designation of the donation impacts conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Which value proposition (get more petition signatures vs. work with legislators) will have the greatest impact on donor converison?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/17/2016 - 03/22/2016
Susan B. Anthony List was running a petition against the nomination of a Supreme Court justice. The goal of this petition for the organization was to find email subscribers that aligned around their mission of pro-life legislation. After they signed the petition, new subscribers were shown an instant donation offer. We wanted to test whether people would be more motivated to give to get more signatures for the petition or to advance the core mission of the organization which was to work with legislators on the issue.
experiments.keyLearnings: We were not able to reach statistical validity on the test before the offer was shut off. Spreading the petition seemed to be the more attractive value proposition which is backed up by other experiments but at 85% LoC, we need further testing to definitively be able to state that.

experiments.title: How the landing page layout affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Which layout will produce the greatest number of donations for the year end campaign?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/17/2012 - 01/01/2013
As part of their year end fundraising campaign, Media Research Center wanted to find the best way to communicate their unique value proposition that would lead to increased giving. The standard donation page layout had always been a two column approach in order to get the donation form closer to the top of the page. We wanted to see if that was the best approach to communicate the case for support to the potential donors.
experiments.keyLearnings: By moving the donation form below the form, we allowed the value proposition communicated within the copy to better “sell” the gift. The single column layout was effectively making an ask before adequately explaining why the gift was needed.  The test did not have a high enough level of confidence to be proven valid so further testing would need to be done to fully validate these results.

experiments.title: How the layout of a donation form affects conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Regular buttons will increase the conversion rate given their increased ease of use both for reading and selecting.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/16/2014 - 09/23/2014
The out-of-the-box defaults for Online for Life’s donation system results in giving arrays that consist of radio buttons. Given the amount of text that accompanies each giving option on the form, these radio buttons tend to clutter the form and make it hard to operate on a mobile phone.  We wanted to test a new way of laying out the same information.
experiments.keyLearnings: The new buttons resulted in a 59.9% increase in conversion rate and were also able to increase the average gift by 56.5%. This proved how the new method for displaying the donation options made it easier for donors to understand.  As a result, the gift had more value to the donor resulting in a net increase of 150.2% revenue.

experiments.title: How strengthening the value proposition through a radical redesign affects donations
experiments.researchQuestion: Which page layout and value proposition best presents the value proposition and minimizes friction and anxiety to optimize donor conversion rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/12/2015 - 02/23/2015
Hillsdale College’s popular Constitution 101 online course is supported by donations from individuals, many of whom donate after taking the course. Immediately after signing up for the course (or any of their other courses), registrants are given the opportunity to donate.  The original donation page contained multiple links to other parts of the site, concentrated in two different navigational areas. It also gave them an immediate opt-out opportunity — by clicking the link (in the second paragraph of copy) “I cannot make a donation at this time”.
A single line of copy asked the user to support the course, making the assumption that the visitor already understood the value of Hillsdale College. Additionally, the page focused on a premium book, the Constitution Reader, and used most of the available language to sell the value proposition of that item.
In order to improve donor conversion on this page, Hillsdale wanted to test a simplified design that expanded and highlighted the value proposition of Hillsdale College rather than the premium associated with their gift.
The new treatment page took away all of the navigation menus — creating a “cul-de-sac”, where the visitor either continues through the page or closes the window. The value proposition language focuses on the goal of the course and the intended outcome — educating 50 million Americans on Constitutional founding principles. The Constitution Reader offer is reduced to a secondary, value-added ask.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The two donation forms resulted in nearly identical revenue -- but the new treatment led to a 33.3% increase in conversion rate.
Since the primary goal of the page is the acquisition of new donors, the increased conversion rate made the new treatment the winner. The discrepancy in average gifts between the pages did lead to some interesting observations.  After further examination of the individual transactions, we learned that the new treatment was getting lower average gifts than the control. This led us to believe that the three options in the new treatment's gift array may be too much of a stretch for most people.  As a result, they are using the "Other amount" option.  This is an example of "unsupervised thinking" which is leading to the visitors entering lower gift amounts than we would prefer.
As a result, Hillsdale's next experiment will be a test of the gift array to see if lowering the initial ask can lead to an overall increased average gift.

experiments.title: How a radical redesign impacted the Membership campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: Which treatment will result in the greatest lift to donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/31/2017 - 06/30/2017
Neither of the treatments reached statistical validity. After further analysis, a big takeaway from this experiment can be found in the experiment setup itself. With a radical redesign, the goal is to test a variety of significant factors in order to achieve a bigger lift. However, when we do that, we are unable to identify the specific element that caused the lift.
With this test, we tested multiple factors but they were relatively minor to the conversion process. By testing the headline, background, and a button, there was no cohesive hypothesis that could be isolated and learned from in the future.
For the future, we need to focus on isolating individual factors on a page or making significant modifications to the elements of a landing page that we know influence conversion (copy, donation form, etc).
experiments.keyLearnings: During the Heritage membership campaign, we wanted to test out a radical version of the existing Join Heritage donation page. The new version utilized the size of the Heritage member base as a way to increase credibility of the headline. It also removed the majority of the design elements to try to make the content better flow together. A third version was prepared that also added a “Donate” button to the top that would allow people to jump straight to the form.

experiments.title: How a radically redesigned acquisition page affects conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Does simplifying the process of submitting a story increase conversion rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/01/2017 - 10/31/2017
National Breast Cancer Foundation was running their BCAM campaign and wanted to test a redesign of their Snapshots of Hope acquisition page, which asked for stories from survivors or family members of people with breast cancer.
experiments.keyLearnings: The redesigned page produced a 22% decrease in conversion rate. It’s possible that the removal of the 3-step process produced anxiety in the mind of the submitter or confusion on the process and requirements.

experiments.title: How the page layout impacts visitor engagement
experiments.researchQuestion: How does a sidebar impact visitor engagement?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/30/2017 - 02/21/2017
With the ADF blog, we wanted to find new ways to increase overall visitor engagement. Engagement is defined as anything from donations and email acquisition to visitors exploring the site. One way that the blog increased visitors exploring the site was by listing recent articles at the bottom of each post. We had the hypothesis that this may not be seen by a significant volume of visitors if they didn’t finish each post. To overcome this, we decided to test out “sticky” sidebar that would remain on the right side of the page for desktop visitors.
experiments.keyLearnings: After running for 3 weeks, the sidebar did not produce any significant impact to either emails acquired or visitors exploring the website. The trend seemed to indicate that it may be negatively impacting these metrics so we decided to shut off this experiment in leu of others that we felt may be more impactful.

experiments.title: How design and copy placement affects conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Which design will drive the most donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/23/2015 - 06/01/2015
As part of an appeal for their Reclaim America campaign, The Heritage Foundation wanted to implement a landing page that used the same look and feel as the campaign’s microsite.  This landing page was heavier on imagery than is traditionally used in their appeals so we wanted to ensure the design would be as effective as the standard design style.
We created an experiment using a design from a previous campaign (with minor variations) as the control and the new microsite design as the treatment.  The copy and quotes used were identical between the two pages.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The new microsite design created a 14% increase to donor conversion on the page. After reflecting upon the page further, this may be attributable to both the page design and the placement of the quotes from well-known public figures.  In the control, the quotes are placed in a sidebar while in the treatment, they are in-line with the copy.
For this specific campaign, we rolled out the new design for the remainder of the campaign.  We will want to run future experiments with the placement of 3rd party quotes to validate their potential impact.

experiments.title: How strengthening the value proposition through a radical redesign affects donations
experiments.researchQuestion: Which page layout and value proposition best presents the value proposition and minimizes friction and anxiety to optimize donor conversion rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/12/2015 - 02/23/2015
Hillsdale College’s popular Constitution 101 online course is supported by donations from individuals, many of whom donate after taking the course. Immediately after signing up for the course (or any of their other courses), registrants are given the opportunity to donate.  The original donation page contained multiple links to other parts of the site, concentrated in two different navigational areas. It also gave them an immediate opt-out opportunity — by clicking the link (in the second paragraph of copy) “I cannot make a donation at this time”. A single line of copy asked the user to support the course, making the assumption that the visitor already understood the value of Hillsdale College. Additionally, the page focused on a premium book, the Constitution Reader, and used most of the available language to sell the value proposition of that item.
In order to improve donor conversion on this page, Hillsdale wanted to test a simplified design that expanded and highlighted the value proposition of Hillsdale College rather than the premium associated with their gift.
The new treatment page took away all of the navigation menus — creating a “cul-de-sac”, where the visitor either continues through the page or closes the window. The value proposition language focuses on the goal of the course and the intended outcome — educating 50 million Americans on Constitutional founding principles. The Constitution Reader offer is reduced to a secondary, value-added ask.
experiments.keyLearnings:
