The two donation forms resulted in nearly identical revenue — but the new treatment led to a 33.3% increase in conversion rate. Since the primary goal of the page is the acquisition of new donors, the increased conversion rate made the new treatment the winner.
The discrepancy in average gifts between the pages did lead to some interesting observations.  After further examination of the individual transactions, we learned that the new treatment was getting lower average gifts than the control. This led us to believe that the three options in the new treatment’s gift array may be too much of a stretch for most people.  As a result, they are using the “Other amount” option.  This is an example of “unsupervised thinking” which is leading to the visitors entering lower gift amounts than we would prefer.
As a result, Hillsdale’s next experiment will be a test of the gift array to see if lowering the initial ask can lead to an overall increased average gift.

experiments.title: Can a “Start My Gift” Button on the Main Donation Page Reduce Friction for Year-End Donors?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a “Start My Gift” button near the top of the main donation page for visitors during the year-end campaign will increase donor conversion by reducing friction for highly motivated donors and allowing them to quickly begin the giving process.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/19/2025 - 12/31/2025
During the year-end campaign, many donors arrive on the main donation page after clicking through from ads, emails, and other campaign touchpoints. While the page includes a detailed value proposition explaining the impact of giving, the team was concerned that the longer messaging could introduce friction for highly motivated donors who were already ready to give.
Specifically, visitors had to scroll past the page’s text before reaching the donation form, which could delay donors who simply wanted to give after responding to an appeal.
However, the team also feared that shortening the page would be counterproductive, as many visitors arrive still unconvinced to donate.
To address these concerns, we decided to test a “Start My Gift” button near the top of the donation page. The test variants were:
Control Version: visitors saw the standard donation page experience, where they would scroll down the page to reach the giving form
Treatment Version: a “Start My Gift” button was placed near the top of the page. When clicked, the button automatically scrolled the visitor directly to the donation form, allowing donors to skip the body copy and begin the giving process immediately
The goal of this test was to determine whether reducing friction for highly motivated donors would increase the donation page conversion rate during the year-end campaign. The test ran from December 19 – December 31.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Overall Conversion
Conversion rates were virtually identical between the two versions — 12.9% for the control and 12.8% for the treatment. Adding the "Start My Gift" button didn't hurt overall conversion, suggesting that a faster path to the form didn't undermine the page's persuasive messaging.
Revenue Per Visitor
There was no significant difference in overall one-time revenue per visitor. However, a meaningful shift emerged among larger donors: for gifts of $1,000 or more, the treatment increased revenue per visitor by 34.9% at 93.5% confidence. This suggests the button reduced friction for highly motivated mid-level and major donors who arrived ready to give and simply wanted a faster way to do it.
Monthly Sustainer Gifts
The clearest tradeoff was in recurring giving. The control generated 50 sustainer gifts compared to 31 for the treatment — a 36.8% decline at 95.8% confidence. A likely explanation: the button allowed donors to skip past the page's value proposition, reducing exposure to messaging that might have inspired a monthly commitment.
Mobile vs. Desktop
No meaningful differences emerged between devices. The button performed similarly across mobile and desktop.
Key Takeaway
Reducing friction helps unlock larger one-time gifts without hurting overall conversion — but it may come at the cost of recurring giving. Further testing should focus on whether a fast-access path can be designed in a way that preserves exposure to the monthly giving ask.

experiments.title: How does the placement of an employer match question impact conversion rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that placing an employer match widget for potential donors will improve conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/23/2025 - 06/27/2025
This test aimed to assess the impact of moving the employer match widget on the donation form. We hypothesized that placing the widget near the ask array would motivate people to use it and potentially increase the average gift if the user originally chose an amount not supported by their employer.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This test examined whether moving the employer match widget closer to the gift array would encourage more donors to use it — and potentially increase the average gift size for donors whose initial amount wasn't matched by their employer.
Key Results:
Overall Conversion Rate
+16%

Overall RPV

Conversion and RPV both trended upward, though neither reached the 95% significance threshold. The average gift did not increase. Due to tracking limitations, we weren't able to measure how many donors actually interacted with the employer match widget.

Recurring gifts showed a consistent directional lift in both conversion and RPV, suggesting the widget placement may resonate more with donors already inclined toward ongoing giving.
Next Steps:
To move forward, extend the test duration or increase the sample size to validate the desktop lift. Enhance tracking of widget interactions to determine if the placement change directly impacts employer match usage or if conversion improvements stem from another factor.

experiments.title: How the placement of a featured gift in a gift catalogue impact donation conversions?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that the placement of a featured gift for gift catalogue donors will improve conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/22/2025 - 06/03/2025
Earlier in the year, we tested whether featuring a gift at the top of the gift catalogue affected conversions, and the decrease in conversion rate suggested the featured product actually increased friction in the customer journey. Rather than providing the user with an easy donating option, it added an additional decision point and increased the scroll length of the page..
For this experiment, we wanted to see whether the placement of that featured gift made a difference.
Control: no featured gift
Variant A: The featured gift is at the top of the list. (Similar to the variant in the original experiment)
Variant B: The featured gift aligns with the gifts.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This test explored whether adding a featured product to the all-products page would reduce friction and boost giving. The hypothesis: too many options and constant scrolling were overwhelming donors and impeding gifts.
Key Results:
Overall Conversion Rate
+48%
71%

Overall RPV
+13%

Treatment 1 showed slight lifts in conversion and RPV; Treatment 2 was flat or negative. Neither reached 95% significance, and the sample was too small for rollout.

What the Results Suggest
Treatment 1 performs well overall, but device matters. On desktop, both treatments showed strong lifts, though the sample size was small. On mobile, Treatment 1 held up, while Treatment 2 dropped sharply, showing that the device greatly influences donor experience.
Next Steps
The sample size is too small to make a rollout decision with confidence. Next steps: rerun this test with a larger audience, and analyze results separately by device, given how differently mobile and desktop users responded.

experiments.title: How radically simplifying the email format affects engagement and donations
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that radically simplifying email formatting for an owned-audience email housefile will achieve greater immediate and downstream engagement because readers will perceive greater authenticity and trust, since the email appears to have been sent from an individual rather than a list.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/11/2026 - 02/11/2026
Luther Seminary has been sending emails without many design elements to their housefile for years, believing that the more a message looks as if it was sent by an individual, the greater the trust and engagement will be. While their standard email template does not appear to have many design elements, there is still a bit of extra white space and unconventional line spacing that could subconsciously lead a reader to associate the message with marketing.
We wanted to see if fully simplifying email formatting would boost engagement. For the test, we removed all padding. We matched font, size, and color to a typical personal email.
We determined that click-through rate would gauge impact. We also measured offer sign-ups and donations to assess how a front-end change affected downstream behavior.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Top of Funnel: Email Clicks Radically simplifying email formatting for an owned-audience email housefile did not lead to greater immediate engagement. The treatment actually led to a slight -2% decrease in Clicks for All Traffic compared to the control, though the difference was not statistically significant and therefore unreliable.

Mid Funnel: Offer Sign-Ups. The control email yielded 317 offer sign-ups, and the treatment yielded 316. The radically simplified email format had no impact on downstream offer sign-ups for email subscribers.

Bottom of Funnel: Donations. The treatment email resulted in a 40% increase in donations, a 48% increase in overall revenue, and a 5% increase in average gift, compared to the control, at a 85.8% level of confidence.

Key Takeaway: While the experiment did not yield the top-of-funnel increase in click-through rate as predicted, the impact on downstream donations warrants further testing. The greater authenticity of the radically simplified design could affect donor perceptions of the sender and carry through to important downstream actions. More experiments could demonstrate a clearer link and answer additional questions, such as: How does a radically simplified email format impact engagement in other email types, such as a direct appeal or cultivation message?

experiments.title: Does including an author image improve email performance for donors?
experiments.researchQuestion: If we add an image of a credible Hoover fellow to the email, it will increase engagement and gifts. Recognizable and authoritative figures can increase trust and attention, making readers more likely to engage with the message and take action.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/13/2026
This experiment was conducted for the Hoover Institution, a public policy research organization dedicated to advancing scholarship on individual, economic, and political freedom. For this test, we wanted to see whether adding an image to an email would increase donor acquisition and engagement when promoting a free ebook. Our expected outcome was that the email with an image would result in higher donor responses and engagement than the version without an image. Everything else in the email remained the same; the only difference between the two versions was the addition of an image of Victor Davis Hanson, a Hoover fellow with strong recognition among Hoover’s audience.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Control (No Image)
CTR: 2.27%
 	Signups: 239
 	Donations: 31
 	Revenue: $1,525
 	Click to Donate: 9.69%
 	RPM: $108
Treatment (Image Added)
CTR: 2.13%
 	Signups: 236
 	Donations: 40
 	Revenue: $2,807
 	Click to Donate: 13.25%
 	RPM: $197.86
What we observed
CTR and signups were slightly lower with the image.
 	The image version generated:
 	9 more donations
 	Nearly double the revenue
 	Higher click-to-donate rate
Why do we think this happened?
Donors are already familiar with Hoover’s mission and the work it produces. Because of that, when the content reinforces the type of research and scholarship they support, they may be more inclined to give once they reach the page. While the image did not increase clicks, it may have reinforced credibility and relevance, leading to a higher percentage of donors choosing to give.
Next Steps
Test a Hoover or mission-focused image against an author image to see which better reinforces the work's value.
 	Test language that more clearly connects the scholar to Hoover’s mission, such as framing the author as part of Hoover’s broader research and impact.
 	Test reinforcing this connection on the donation page, helping donors see how the content ties directly to the work their support makes possible.

experiments.title: How changing the donate button link to non-tribute affects donor conversion rate on site profiles
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that changing the donate button links to point to the non-tribute page for site profile visitors will increase the donor conversion rate because the donate buttons leading to the tribute page without the context of the tribute concept could lead to potential confusion and friction with the message. Therefore, leading users to the non-tribute page with more missional language would reduce that confusion and friction, encouraging users to donate. Donation buttons leading to the tribute page without the context of the tribute concept could lead to potential confusion and friction with the message. Therefore, leading users to the non-tribute page with more missional language would reduce that confusion and friction, encouraging users to donate.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/09/2026
On site profiles, all donate buttons usually lead to the tribute donation page for CaringBridge.
For this test, we wanted to change the donate buttons in the header, middle of the page, and the footer to lead to the non-tribute donation page instead of the tribute page. The tribute widget would still lead to the tribute donation page, maintaining that continuity.
This was tested on site profiles and on both mobile and desktop. This was tested when there were no campaigns or matches going on for CaringBridge.
experiments.keyLearnings: The key learning from this experiment is that changing the donation buttons on site profiles from leading to the tribute donation page to the non-tribute donation page resulted in an 8.4% decrease in donations across all traffic. However, the statistical level of confidence was only 53.9%, which is below the generally accepted threshold of 95%. Therefore, these results cannot be considered valid and should not be used for future decision making. In future experiments, it is important to ensure the sample size is sufficient to yield statistically significant results. Additionally, it may be beneficial to consider the timing of the experiment, such as avoiding conducting tests during peak donation periods like the Calendar Year End campaign. It's also important to carefully monitor and analyze the impact of any changes on donation metrics before making broader implementations.

experiments.title: How including an image of the premium in a web asset donation ask impacts engagement
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that the addition of the premium image for Moody Radio website radio player visitors will achieve an increase in engagement and downstream donor metrics because  it visually reinforces the offer and increasing its perceived value to the user.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/03/2025 - 12/14/2025
During Moody Radio’s CYE campaign, we tested the impact of adding a premium image to the website Radio Player.
Both the control and treatment included identical premium language; however, only the treatment featured the premium image.
The test ran for two weeks, with traffic evenly split between both variants.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The experiment showed that adding an image to the Moody Radio Player led to a 20.6% decrease in clicks overall (77.1% level of confidence). Because this outcome was unexpected, we took a deeper look at the data to better understand what may have driven the decline.
To isolate potential drivers, we analyzed click performance by device type.
On mobile, the treatment resulted in a 12.7% decrease in clicks (48% level of confidence).
On desktop, clicks declined by 44.8% (72% level of confidence).
On tablet, clicks dropped by 52.7% (62% level of confidence).
These results suggest that the image had the greatest negative impact on desktop and tablet users, where the decline in engagement was most pronounced.
Transaction data followed a similar pattern. The control generated four gifts, while the treatment generated just one—representing a 75% decrease in transactions (82% level of confidence).

experiments.title: How a dynamic impact statement below the donate button affects transactions
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a dynamic impact statement  for donation page visitors will achieve an increase in transactions because the statement would act as social proof and show the visitor how many other people support this mission, encouraging the visitor to make a donation and join in this mission. because  the statement would act as social proof and show the visitor how many other people support this mission, encouraging the visitor to make a donation and join in this mission.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/02/2025 - 12/02/2025
During Giving Tuesday, we wanted to test adding a dynamic impact statement below the donate button for CaringBridge on their donation pages. The statement pulled the number of donors on the donation page during the day of Giving Tuesday. The statement read as follows –
Your gift’s impact will combine with the X other donors who have also stepped forward to DOUBLE their Giving Tuesday gift alongside the $50,000 match!
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding a dynamic impact statement on the donation page did not have a significant positive impact on transactions during Giving Tuesday. In fact, the treatment group saw a 3.9% decrease in transactions compared to the control group, with a statistical confidence level of 31.1%, which is below the generally accepted threshold of 85%.
This suggests that the dynamic impact statement did not effectively encourage visitors to make a donation based on the number of donors who had already contributed. In future experimentation, it is important to carefully consider the messaging and placement of social proof elements on donation pages to ensure that they resonate with visitors and positively impact their decision to donate.
It is also essential to conduct experiments for a longer duration to gather sufficient data and increase the confidence level of the results before drawing any conclusions or making decisions based on the findings. Additionally, considering different types of social proof elements or adjusting the messaging based on visitor behavior and preferences could be explored in future experiments to optimize donation page performance.

experiments.title: How a double impact slideup affects transactions during Giving Tuesday
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a double impact slide-up for homepage visitors will increase transactions because it highlights the urgency and potential impact the visitor could have, encouraging them to make a donation.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/02/2025 - 12/03/2025
We have seen that slide-ups have been very successful for CaringBridge during high-urgency campaigns. For Giving Tuesday, we wanted to test a regular slide-up vs a slide-up that highlighted the chance for the user to have their gift doubled.
experiments.keyLearnings: The key learning from this experiment is that adding a double impact slide-up for homepage visitors did not yield the expected increase in transactions. In fact, the treatment group saw a decrease in transactions compared to the control group. However, the statistical level of confidence is only 45.7%, which is below the generally accepted threshold of 95%. This means that the results are not statistically significant and should not be used for future decision-making. In future experimentation, it is essential to consider the experiment's duration to ensure a sufficient sample size and sufficient time for the impact of the changes to be accurately measured. Additionally, it may be beneficial to test different variations of the double impact message or explore other ways to create urgency and highlight the potential impact of donations to see if different strategies yield better results. It is also important to consider the overall messaging and timing of the campaign to ensure it resonates with the target audience.

experiments.title: How including a button in an email appeal affects click-through rate for recurring donors
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a button to an email for recurring donors will achieve an increase in click-through rate because it provides a clear, visually distinct call-to-action that reduces friction, draws the reader’s eye, and makes it easier for people to take the intended next step.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 08/26/2025
The Heritage Foundation is running its annual Leader’s Club campaign. The second email of the campaign offered a free ebook called “Flags of Our Fathers.” Within this email, they wanted to test how changing the raw URL to a button would affect the click-through rate to the landing page. This experiment ran on one email, and this segment included donors to The Heritage Foundation.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding a button to an email for recurring donors did not lead to an increase in click-through rate as hypothesized. In fact, the treatment group saw a 13.6% decrease in clicks compared to the control group. The statistical analysis showed a 63.4% level of confidence, which is below the generally accepted threshold of 95% for results to be considered valid.

In future experimentation, it is important to ensure that the sample size is large enough to provide reliable results and that any conclusions drawn are statistically significant. Additionally, it is crucial to carefully consider the design and implementation of changes such as adding a button, as they may not always have the desired effect. It is also recommended to conduct further testing and analysis to understand the reasons behind the unexpected outcome and to inform future decision-making. Experimentation is a valuable tool for learning and refining strategies, and it is important to approach it with a curious and open mindset to adapt and improve based on the findings.

experiments.title: How a two column layout impacts downstream donor metrics on a direct ask email to the housefile
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a two column approach for iMB housefile contacts will achieve an increase in downstream donor metrics because it offers an additional CTA to give.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 07/29/2025
For IMB’s Ghana Training Direct Ask offer, we tested our standard one-column email format vs a two-column format that featured a couple of calls-to-action.
On desktop, the treatment was featured as two columns at about 70/30 between the main content and the additional CTAs. However, on desktop, the “second” column went below the main content of the email. Therefore, it will be imperative to look at gifts based on device type to fully understand the impact of the treatment variation.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that the two-column format with additional calls-to-action in the email for IMB's Ghana Training Direct Ask offer resulted in a 60.0% increase in donations for all traffic. This indicates that providing an extra CTA to give can positively impact donor engagement and conversion rates.

However, the statistical level of confidence for this result was only 59.5%, which falls below the generally accepted threshold of 95% for valid results. Therefore, while the experiment showed a positive outcome, the lack of high statistical confidence means that these results should not be solely relied upon for decision making.

These results indicate that having a secondary CTA to give on desktop did in fact increase transactions, but had no impact on mobile.

In future experimentation, it is important to continue testing different layouts and CTAs in emails to optimize donor engagement. It would also be interesting to test our standard layout against a double CTA within a direct ask email to see if it was truly the secondary column or simply the secondary CTA to give.

experiments.title: How changing the placement of an eBook a landing page impacted email acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that placing the ebook image directly under the headline on the landing page for Hoover’s mobile audience will achieve an increase email acquisition and donation rates because By presenting the offer right away, we reduce friction and maximize clarity, especially for users in scanning mode.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 08/04/2025
This experiment was conducted for the Hoover Institution, a public policy think tank based at Stanford University. Hoover is committed to advancing ideas that promote economic prosperity, individual liberty, and a constitutional government.
For this test, we focused on the content offer “Dueling Populisms”, with a specific emphasis on mobile user behavior.
The core question we wanted to answer was: Does the visual placement of the ebook image impact performance for mobile users?
In the control, the ebook image was positioned further down the page, requiring users to scroll to view it. In the treatment, the ebook image was placed directly beneath the headline, making it immediately visible upon landing. Our aim was to determine if making the offer more visually concrete up front would increase conversions—particularly email acquisitions and donations.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Further Results:

Transactions:
Control: 38 donations
 	Treatment: 37 donations
 	A 2.63% decrease
Control: $1,434
 	Treatment: $1,399
 	A 2.44% decrease
The treatment showed a modest but meaningful increase (+4.4%) in email acquisition by moving the ebook offer to the top of the page. However, this result was not statistically significant. More testing needs to be done to confirm that above-the-fold visibility enhances user engagement for lead-generation offers, particularly when screen space is limited.
While email acquisition increased slightly, there was no significant difference in downstream revenue or transactions. This suggests that while placement can improve micro-conversions (like lead capture), it doesn’t automatically translate into macro-conversions (like donations or purchases). Additional nurturing may be needed.
This experiment reinforces that even small changes to content hierarchy — without altering the offer itself — can lead to improved performance. This is especially important for high-traffic, high-value landing pages like this one.
Test the size or prominence of the CTA button
 	Try shorter copy on just the mobile audience
 	Trigger a popup if the user has gone idle for a bit... to see if you can remind them to signup.

experiments.title: How does a sticky bar on the donation page, calling out the premium, impact donor conversion rate?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a sticky bar on the donation page, calling out that donors will receive a gifted copy of the latest resources with their donation, for will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/13/2025 - 05/27/2025
During Time of Grace’s May Challenge Grant, we hypothesized that a sticky bar calling out the book premium available to every donor would increase the conversion rate.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that adding a sticky bar on the donation page emphasizing the book premium available to donors may have a positive impact on the conversion rate. However, the results of the experiment only showed a 3.2% increase in Transactions for All Traffic with a statistical level of confidence of 20.1%, which is below the 95% threshold necessary for validity.

Originally, the motivation behind this sticky bar was to call out the premium for the reader, in a way that didn't require them to fully read the donation page. However, it's likely that donors arriving on this page from Facebook ads or the Homepage banner may have already been aware of this thank-you gift—making it a non-unique piece of information to have pinned.

experiments.title: How does a radical landing page redesign impact email signups for a video series?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a more structured and visually cohesive landing page design will improve readability and increase email signups.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/23/2024 - 08/23/2024
This organization frequently creates and promotes free video series as a key strategy in their email acquisition efforts. Each video series is housed on a dedicated landing page, but over time, these landing pages have become visually inconsistent and difficult to scan—especially on mobile.
To improve the user experience and increase email signups, we designed a radical redesign of their landing page. The redesigned page includes several key updates aimed at enhancing readability and structure:
A more centered layout with narrowed text width
Addition for the logo
A light background behind the email acquisition form
A distinct background color behind the episode list (or impact statement list)
A new footer that includes a link to the Privacy Policy
Because the formatting of their video series pages varies, we divided them into two separate test groups: Standard pages and Lighthouse pages. For each group, we created two versions of the page. This write-up details results from the Lighthouse landing pages:
Control: The original, existing landing page
Treatment: The redesigned landing page with improved readability and structure
Traffic was split 50/50 between the Control and Treatment in both versions of the test.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are as follows:
The redesigned landing page did not lead to a significant improvement in email signups compared to the original landing page. In fact, there was a slight decrease in email acquisitions when the Treatment page was used.
 	The statistical level of confidence for this experiment was only 47.7%, which is below the 95% threshold needed to consider the results valid. This indicates that the results could be due to random chance rather than a true difference between the control and treatment pages.
 	Given the inconclusive results of this experiment, it is important not to make any definitive conclusions or changes based on these findings. It would be advisable to conduct further experiments with larger sample sizes and potentially different designs to determine the true impact on email signups.
 	In future experimentation, it is important to consider factors such as the layout, design elements, and overall user experience when testing landing page variations. It may also be beneficial to explore different hypotheses and iterate on design changes to find the most effective approach for increasing email signups.

experiments.title: How the placement of an ebook image on a landing page impacts email acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that placing the ebook image directly under the headline on the landing page for Hoover’s mobile audience will achieve an increase email acquisition and donation rates because eading with a visual representation of the offer will capture attention more quickly, establish trust, and reinforce the value of the content before the user scrolls—ultimately making the landing page more persuasive. because  eading with a visual representation of the offer will capture attention more quickly, establish trust, and reinforce the value of the content before the user scrolls—ultimately making the landing page more persuasive.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/12/2025 - 06/30/2025
This experiment was conducted for the Hoover Institution, a public policy think tank based at Stanford University. Hoover is committed to advancing ideas that promote economic prosperity, individual liberty, and a constitutional government.
For this test, we focused on the content offer “Three Keys to Unlock the American Economy”, with a specific emphasis on mobile user behavior.
The core question we wanted to answer was: Does the visual placement of the ebook image impact performance for mobile users?
In the control, the ebook image was positioned further down the page, requiring users to scroll to view it. In the treatment, the ebook image was placed directly beneath the headline, making it immediately visible upon landing. Our aim was to determine if making the offer more visually concrete up front would increase conversions—particularly email acquisitions and donations.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Key Learnings & Takeaways:
Visual Priority May Not Always Drive Action - While it’s often effective to lead with a visual cue—especially for mobile users—the results suggest that immediate exposure to the ebook image did not enhance performance. In fact, conversions were slightly lower in the treatment. It’s possible that the early image displaced other persuasive copy or created a “banner blindness” effect.Perceived Value Comes From Message, Not Just Visual - This experiment suggests that copy structure and headline framing may carry more weight than visuals in persuading users to submit their email. Users might be more compelled by clarity around what they’ll gain from the ebook than the ebook’s image itself.Small Sample Sizes = Direction, Not Decision - With limited traffic and low donation volume, this test doesn’t give us statistically significant answers, but it provides a useful signal that visual-first design changes on mobile should be introduced cautiously and tested against message clarity.
Next Steps & Future Testing:
•	Test Hybrid Layouts: Consider a version where the ebook image is still visible early—but paired more tightly with benefit-driven subhead copy to retain persuasive momentum.
•	Run Similar Tests on Higher-Traffic Offers: This pattern is worth validating at a larger scale, especially on mobile-focused offers with greater reach.

experiments.title: How a plain text appeal email affects conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that sending out a plain text email that looks similar to emails in someone’s inbox for the housefile will achieve an increase in conversion rate because it would look more humanized as it would look like someone you know is sending you an email, encouraging the user to read through the email and donate.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 07/14/2025
Time of Grace sends out appeal emails every month for their new premium. They send out stylized emails with organization branding. For this test, we wanted to see how a plain text email that you see for normal emails in your inbox would affect donor conversion rate.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that sending out a plain text email for donation appeals resulted in a 33.8% increase in donations for all traffic. This indicates that the more personal and familiar format of the plain text email may have resonated better with recipients, potentially leading to higher engagement and conversion rates.

However, it's important to note that the statistical level of confidence for this experiment was only 55.6%, below the generally accepted threshold of 95% for validity. This means that while the treatment showed a positive result, it cannot be considered conclusive or reliable for future decision-making.

We saw an 18% increase in clicks with a 67% level of confidence.

In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to further test the effectiveness of plain text emails for donation appeals with a larger sample size or different variations to see if the results can be replicated and validated with a higher level of confidence. Additionally, incorporating elements of personalization and humanization in email communications could continue to be explored as a strategy for increasing donor engagement and conversions.

experiments.title: How a goal thermometer on a high-urgency campaign donation page impacts donor conversion metrics
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that the addition of a goal thermometer for FYE donation page visitors will achieve an increase in donor conversion metrics because it creates a sense of social proof by visibly showing real-time progress toward the goal and highlighting that others are actively contributing, we foster a sense of community and shared purpose.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/10/2025 - 06/30/2025
During Moody Bible’s Fiscal Year-End Campaign, we tested the impact of adding a goal thermometer on donor conversion.
The goal of this test was to leverage social proof by emphasizing a shared, communal objective to meet a specific financial need for the ministry, ultimately driving an increase in both transactions and revenue.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment is that the addition of a goal thermometer on the FYE donation page led to a 2.4% increase in Transactions for All Traffic, but with a low statistical level of confidence at 19.1%. This means that while there was a positive impact, the results cannot be considered reliable for future decision-making.

However, revenue saw a substantial 400% decrease with 100% confidence, indicating a clear negative impact from the goal thermometer. These results suggest that Moody Bible’s audience may be more inclined to make smaller donations when a goal thermometer is present, potentially due to the high fundraising target set for the FYE campaign. Further testing is needed to refine these insights and draw more definitive conclusions. It may also be worth exploring the use of a daily campaign goal, which could help reduce the perceived pressure of reaching a large target and encourage more frequent contributions.

Moving forward, it is important to take into consideration the statistical significance of the results when evaluating the effectiveness of different strategies. While the idea of leveraging social proof through a goal thermometer showed some promise in increasing donor conversion metrics, further testing and optimization are necessary to draw valid conclusions. It is important to refine the messaging and design of the goal thermometer to ensure it effectively communicates progress towards a communal objective without negatively impacting revenue. Future experimentation should focus on testing variations of the goal thermometer to find the most effective approach for driving donor conversions without compromising financial outcomes.

experiments.title: How a goal thermometer on a high-urgency campaign donation page impacts donor conversion metrics
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that the addition of a goal thermometer for FYE donation page visitors will achieve an increase in donor conversion metrics because is creates a sense of social proof by visibly showing real-time progress toward the goal and highlighting that others are actively contributing, we foster a sense of community and shared purpose..
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/10/2025 - 06/30/2025
During Today in the Word’s Fiscal Year-End Campaign, we tested the impact of adding a goal thermometer on donor conversion.
The goal of this test was to leverage social proof by emphasizing a shared, communal objective to meet a specific financial need for the ministry. Ultimately, the goal was to drive an increase in both transactions and revenue.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that while the treatment version with the goal thermometer led to a decrease in transactions for all traffic, it resulted in 1.5% decrease at a 10.8%level of confidence, which is below the threshold of 95% for validity. These results indicate that Today in the Word's audience didn't respond negatively or positively to the presence of the thermometer.

However, revenue saw a 9.6% increase at a 100% level of confidence, indicating that the goal thermometer was effective at motivating donors to give larger amounts. Therefore, we should find ways to incorporate thermometers during other Today in the Word campaigns.

In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to further explore the impact of the goal thermometer on revenue and donor behavior. It may also be helpful to consider other factors that could have influenced the results, such as the messaging and design of the goal thermometer. Additionally, conducting the experiment for a longer period of time and with a larger sample size could provide more reliable insights.

Overall, the key takeaway from this experiment is that the goal thermometer can be a valuable tool for driving increased revenue and encouraging larger donations, even if it does not directly impact the number of transactions.

Pathway to Victory is the broadcast ministry of First Baptist Dallas.

We also saw a 28% increase in donations from the treatment version.

experiments.title: How adding an image to an email impacted clicks for a nondonor audience
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding an image of the offer for email subscribers will achieve a higher click because there are evidentials of what they would receive. because there are evidentials of what they would receive.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 05/12/2025
Pathway to Victory is the broadcast ministry of First Baptist Dallas.
PTV sends many emails throughout the month and so we decided to test adding an image of the resources included in the bundle.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that adding an image of the offer in the email did not result in a higher click-through rate compared to the control group. Despite seeing a 7.1% decrease in Clicks for All Traffic in the treatment group, the statistical level of confidence was only 49.7%, which is below the accepted threshold of 95% for valid results.

experiments.title: How prioritizing a specific gift on a gift catalogue page impacts conversion rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that prioritizing a specific gift for potential donors will achieve a change in conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/12/2025 - 04/11/2025
This test aimed to assess the impact of highlighting a “priority” product to the top of the gift catalogue all products page for Save the Children. The gift catalogue is something that STC has used over the years to generate support for their mission. In this treatment, we showed one product out of a possible five that we chose to highlight.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that by highlighting a specific gift in the gift catalogue, there was a decrease in donations for all traffic by 18.1%. However, the statistical level of confidence was only 63.0%, which is below the threshold of 95% for results to be considered valid.

Additionally, revenue per visitor also saw a decrease of 14%, but only with a 67% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How redesigning content blocks on the homepage impacted clicks.
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that redesigning content blocks for homepage visitors will achieve more clicks on said blocks.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/06/2024 - 07/24/2024
This test explored the impact of redesigning the content blocks on the Homepage. The goal was to increase clicks (engagement) and conversion by creating a more enticing and easier to navigate look to the content. We changed copy to be in white box overlaying image, with CTAs presented as links and highlight teal when hovering. CTA links always teal on mobile.
Our hypothesis was that redesigning the content blocks on the homepage will make them more engaging for the visitor and will lead to higher clicks and ultimately higher donations.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that the redesigned content blocks on the homepage did not lead to more clicks from visitors. In fact, the treatment version saw a decrease in clicks compared to the control version. Additionally, the statistical level of confidence was only 11.5%, which is below the generally accepted threshold of 95% for valid results.

This experiment also did not impact donor conversion rates:

Control: 5%

Treatment: 4.9%, which was a 1.3% decrease with a 53% level of confidence.

 

The differences between desktop and mobile were also relatively small:

Desktop

Control: 2.4% click through rate; 5.2% donor conversion rate

Treatment: 2.4% click through rate (1.1% increase with a 21% level of confidence); 5.2% donor conversion rate (0.38% decrease, 11% level of confidence).

 

Mobile

Control: 2.6% click through rate; 4.9% conversion rate.

Treatment: 2.5% click through rate (1.6% decrease with a 34% level of confidence); 4.7% conversion rate (2.3% decrease with a 62% level of confidence).

experiments.title: How does a radical landing page (standard) redesign impact email signups for video series?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a more structured and visually cohesive landing page design will improve readability and increase email signups.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/23/2024 - 08/23/2024
An organization frequently creates and promotes free video series as a key strategy in their email acquisition efforts. Each video series is housed on a dedicated landing page, but over time, these landing pages have become visually inconsistent and difficult to scan—especially on mobile.
To improve the user experience and increase email signups, we designed a radical redesign of their landing page. The redesigned page includes several key updates aimed at enhancing readability and structure:
A more centered layout with narrowed text width
Addition of the logo
A light background behind the email acquisition form
A distinct background color behind the episode list (or impact statement list)
A new footer that includes a link to the Privacy Policy
Because the formatting of their video series pages varies, we divided them into two separate test groups: Standard pages and Lighthouse pages. For each group, we created two versions of the page. This write-up details results from the Standard landing pages:
Control: The original, existing landing page
Treatment: The redesigned landing page with improved readability and structure
Traffic was split 50/50 between the Control and Treatment in both versions of the test.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that the redesigned landing page did not perform better than the original landing page in terms of email acquisition for all traffic. There was a 0.7% decrease in email signups with a statistical confidence level of 31.7%, which is below the recommended threshold of 85%. This means that the results of the test are not considered valid and should not be used for future decision making.

In future experimentation, it is important to carefully analyze the results and ensure that the statistical significance meets the minimum threshold before making any conclusions or implementing changes based on the test. Additionally, considering other factors like user feedback, usability testing, and further iterations of the redesigned landing page could help in determining what changes need to be made to improve email signups effectively.

experiments.title: How does a radical landing page (lighthouse) redesign impact email signups for video series?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a more structured and visually cohesive landing page design will improve readability and increase email signups.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/23/2024 - 08/23/2024
An organization frequently creates and promotes free video series as a key strategy in their email acquisition efforts. Each video series is housed on a dedicated landing page, but over time, these landing pages have become visually inconsistent and difficult to scan—especially on mobile.
To improve the user experience and increase email signups, we designed a radical redesign of their landing page. The redesigned page includes several key updates aimed at enhancing readability and structure:
A more centered layout with narrowed text width
Addition of the organization’s logo
A light background behind the email acquisition form
A distinct background color behind the episode list (or impact statement list)
A new footer that includes a link to the Privacy Policy
Because the formatting of their video series pages varies, we divided them into two separate test groups: Standard pages and Lighthouse pages. For each group, we created two versions of the page. This write-up details results from the Lighthouse landing pages:
Control: The original, existing landing page
Treatment: The redesigned landing page with improved readability and structure
Traffic was split 50/50 between the Control and Treatment in both versions of the test.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are as follows:

1. The redesigned landing page did not lead to a significant improvement in email signups compared to the original landing page. In fact, there was a slight decrease in email acquisitions when the Treatment page was used.

2. The statistical level of confidence for this experiment was only 47.7%, which is below the 85% threshold needed to consider the results valid. This indicates that the results could be due to random chance rather than a true difference between the Control and Treatment pages.

3. Given the inconclusive results of this experiment, it is important not to make any definitive conclusions or changes based on these findings. It would be advisable to conduct further experiments with larger sample sizes and potentially different designs to determine the true impact on email signups.

4. In future experimentation, it is important to consider factors such as the layout, design elements, and overall user experience when testing landing page variations. It may also be beneficial to explore different hypotheses and iterate on design changes to find the most effective approach for increasing email signups.

experiments.title: Will adding a donor countdown on the donation page help conversions?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a donor countdown on a sticky bar for the main campaign donation page will achieve more transactions because the donor countdown will provide extra social proof, showing that others are doanting too. It also gives an exact number needed which can lead into exclusivity and ultimately increasing motivation.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 04/03/2025
Moody Radio just wrapped up their annual Spring Share campaign.
For this campaign, we wanted to see if we could include a donor countdown on the main campaign donation page to reinforce the need and provide extra motivation.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that the donor countdown on a sticky bar for the main campaign donation page did not achieve the desired outcome of increasing transactions. In fact, the treatment saw a 4.5% decrease in donations for all traffic compared to the control group.

It is important to note that the statistical level of confidence was only 54.5%, which is below the generally accepted threshold of 95% for results to be considered valid. Therefore, these results should not be used for future decision making.

In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to conduct further tests with a larger sample size or make adjustments to the design of the donor countdown feature before drawing any conclusions. It is also important to consider other factors that may have influenced the outcomes, such as the timing of the campaign, messaging, or external events. Additionally, incorporating qualitative feedback from donors and testing different variations of the donor countdown feature can provide valuable insights for future campaigns.

experiments.title: Will adding a timer on the donation page increase urgency?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a timer to the campaign donation page for the end of the campaign will achieve more transactions because because the added element will increase urgency.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 04/03/2025
Moody Radio recently wrapped up their Annual Spring Share campaign.
This campaign drives a lot of traffic to the donation pages from on-air, paid social and paid search, as well as organic. This campaign is about a month long which can seem like a long time for a donor to have the opportunity to give to where they can push it off.
We wanted to see if we could increase the urgency to give on the donation page instead of waiting by adding a countdown time to the end of the campaign. To make sure this was the most effective, we waited to launch this until the last 4 days of the campaign.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding a timer to the campaign donation page for the end of the campaign did result in a 6.7% increase in donations for all traffic. While this is a positive outcome, the statistical level of confidence was only 42.8%, which means that the results cannot be considered valid or reliable.

In future experimentation, it is important to ensure that the sample size is sufficiently large to generate statistically significant results. Additionally, it may be beneficial to run the experiment for a longer period of time to capture more data and allow for more conclusive findings.

Overall, while the initial results are promising, it is important to take caution in interpreting the results and to continue conducting rigorous experiments to drive decision-making in the future.

experiments.title: Will having headers on the campaign donation page help increase transactions?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding headers to the doantion pages for the spring campaign will achieve more transactions because the headers will make the checkout process easier to follow.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/27/2025
KCBI runs a Spring fundraiser each year where the on air hosts are fundraising and leading traffic to their page to give a gift or to call in.
For this campaign, we wanted to see how we could help increase conversions by adding headers or steps to the donation form. We tested this during CYE, but the results were not concrete. We wanted to see how this could perform during a different season.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding headers to the donation pages for the Spring campaign actually led to a decrease in donations, instead of the expected increase. However, the results were not statistically significant with only a 77.9% level of confidence, meaning that the results cannot be considered valid for future decision making.

With the steep decrease in transactions, we turned this test after just a couple of days. This test inadvertently could have caused more donor friction and anxiety by adding the headers. In future experimentation, it would be worth testing the language used.

experiments.title: Will a side by side layout work better for a donation page?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that changing the form to be a 2 column layout for the Spring Share doantion page will achieve more donor conversions because  the new layout has the potential to make the form easier to read and fill out.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/24/2025
Moody Radio runs a Spring Share fundraiser each year where tons of traffic from digital channels as well as on-air listeners are directed to a donation form.
For this test, we wanted to see if changing the layout of the form had the potential to increase donor conversion on the form
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that changing the form layout to a 2-column format for the Spring Share donation page did not lead to an increase in donor conversion. In fact, the treatment group saw a 7.3% decrease in donations for all traffic compared to the control group. However, when you narrow down the results by traffic we can see that the mobile experience for this test is what caused the overall decrease. Looking at desktop-only results we are seeing a 9.6% increase in conversion at a 57% LoC. While not valid, the results of the desktop do not have a negative impact on conversion.
In future experimentation, it is important to ensure that the statistical level of confidence is at least 95% in order to draw reliable conclusions. Additionally, it would be beneficial to conduct further research to understand why the 2-column layout did not perform as expected. This could involve gathering feedback from users, conducting usability tests, or A/B testing other elements of the donation form to identify factors that may impact donor conversion rates.

experiments.title: Does adding an image of a direct mail envelope impact donor conversion rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding an envelope image for direct mail donors will achieve a change in conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/27/2025
Pathway to Victory is the broadcast ministry of First Dallas.
In March, the direct mail team wanted to test sending a new design of an envelope to donors as a remail strategy. We wanted to target this list with a digital test that looked at whether including the picture of the envelope in the email led to higher conversion rates.
Our hypothesis was that because the envelope was so different from what PTV usually uses, and could easily get lost in the mix, adding an image to the email would drive higher response rates.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding an image of the envelope to the email for direct mail donors did not lead to higher conversion rates. In fact, the treatment group saw a 29.6% decrease in donations compared to the control group.

It is important to note that the statistical level of confidence was only 73.5%, which is below the generally accepted threshold of 95%. This means that the results of this experiment cannot be considered valid and should not be used for future decision making.

It is also important to note that the true impact of this test will be whether it influenced offline giving at all; however, direct mail results won't be available for another month, so we are only reporting on instant digital transactions here.

This experiment also had a similar result when looking at it from a revenue perspective. The treatment version had 38% less revenue per visitor than the control, with an 87% level of confidence.

experiments.title: Are we presenting an acquisition offer in the best way on the landing page?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adjusting the landing page for the Lent offer in MMS will achieve more form submissions because the page will be optimized for a mobile experience.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/12/2025
For this test we wanted to see if we could change the landing page copy for the Lent offer in MMS and see more email sign ups.
We made several changes to the design of the page to make it more mobile friendly in terms of scrolling and skimming.
Below is what we found.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that the adjusted landing page for the Lent offer in MMS did not result in more form submissions compared to the original page. Despite efforts to make the page more mobile-friendly for easier scrolling and skimming, the treatment actually saw a decrease in Emails Acquired for All Traffic.

It is important to note that the statistical level of confidence was only 63.5%, which is below the generally accepted threshold of 95% for valid results. Therefore, these findings should not be used for future decision making.

In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to further test and iterate on the changes made to the landing page. It may be worth exploring different design elements, content, or CTAs to determine what truly resonates with the target audience and drives more form submissions. Additionally, ensuring a higher statistical level of confidence in the results will provide more reliable insights for decision making.

experiments.title: How a grid layout for Watch Now website impacts engagement

We believe that
a grid layout for desktop web traffic will achieve increase engagement because the layout presents multiple viewing opportunities and less scrolling. because the layout presents multiple viewing opportunities and less scrolling.
because the layout presents multiple viewing opportunities and less scrolling. because the layout presents multiple viewing opportunities and less scrolling.</p.

This year PTV wants to optimize their website to encourage more engagement from site visitors.
This page is where people go to watch to past sermons and TV broadcasts.
We decided to test a grid layout with the hypothesis that the grid layout presents multiple viewing opportunities with less scrolling

The key learnings from this experiment are that the grid layout for the desktop web traffic resulted in a 1.9% increase in clicks for all traffic. experiments.title: How an adding a relevant image to a direct ask donation page impacts donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding an image of the anonymous missionaries for Anonymous Missionaries Direct Ask Donation page will achieve an increase in donor conversion metrics because the image provides visual representation as to what an anonymous missionary is—therefore, helping visitors grasp the idea more quickly and evoking a stronger emotional connection.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/12/2025 - 03/03/2025
To improve donor conversion rates on IMB’s Anonymous Missionary Direct Ask, we tested the impact of adding a relevant image to the donation page.
This specific offer can be challenging to communicate effectively, so our hypothesis was that an image depicting “anonymous missionaries” would provide greater clarity to visitors, ultimately leading to higher donor engagement and conversions.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that adding an image of anonymous missionaries to the donation page for Anonymous Missionaries Direct Ask did not lead to the expected increase in donor conversion metrics. In fact, the treatment group saw a significant decrease in donations compared to the control group. However, the statistical level of confidence was below the generally accepted threshold of 95%, meaning that these results cannot be considered valid, but the results are trending in a negative direction.
Due to these results, we looked further into the where the traffic came from to see if it performed better or worse on different types of devices.
Sure enough - mobile users saw an almost valid 71.8% decrease in transactions (94.5% LOC). This indicates that the image negatively impacted potential donors who visited the donation page on their mobile device.  This could be due to the fact that image was somewhat larger and required more scrolling on mobile that desktop.
Desktop users saw an invalid 114.9% lift in transactions at a 47.4% LOC. In future testing, it may be beneficial to only test large images like this on desktop to minimize the amount of friction on the page for mobile users.
In future experimentation, it is important to carefully analyze the results and consider the statistical significance before drawing conclusions. It is also crucial to ensure that the hypothesis is well-grounded and based on solid evidence. Further testing may be needed to determine the true impact of adding an image to the donation page for this specific campaign. Additionally, it could be beneficial to explore other potential factors that may influence donor conversion rates, such as messaging, layout, or incentives.

experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a grid layout for desktop web traffic will achieve increase engagement because the layout presents multiple viewing opportunities and less scrolling. because the layout presents multiple viewing opportunities and less scrolling.

Timeframe: 02/14/2025 - 02/25/2025
Pathway to Victory is the broadcast ministry of First Dallas.
This year PTV wants to optimize their website to encourage more engagement from site visitors.
This page is where people go to watch to past sermons and TV broadcasts.
We decided to test a grid layout with the hypothesis that the grid layout presents multiple viewing opportunities with less scrolling
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that the grid layout for the desktop web traffic resulted in a 1.9% increase in clicks for all traffic. However, the statistical level of confidence was only 8.0%, which is below the generally accepted threshold of 95%. This means that while the treatment group saw a slight improvement, the results cannot be considered valid and should not be used for future decision-making.

However we also saw a 75% increase in time spent on sight for the treatment, implying that more time was spent to read and digest information before taking a click action.

experiments.title: How is the best way to present an ask on the homepage during CYE?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a lower third element for the homepage will achieve a higher donor conversion rate because the lower third element is new, whereas the sticky bar we have relied on heavily in the past and could have banner blindness. The lower third also has additioanl gift options that will help increase donor conversion where as the sticky bar only has a give now button.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 02/18/2025
For this test we wanted to see if having a sticky bar is really the best way to present an ask on the homepage during a high urgency campaign.
This test we took inspiration from the Chicago Sun Times and placed what we’re calling a ‘lower third’ element on the page. This test will only show on the homepage.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that the lower third element displayed on the homepage did not result in a higher donor conversion rate compared to the sticky bar. In fact, the treatment group saw a decrease in donations. However, because the statistical level of confidence is below 95%, these results cannot be considered valid.

In future experimentation, it is important to consider the possibility of banner blindness with the sticky bar and to continue testing new elements to optimize donation conversions. It may be valuable to explore different designs, messaging, and placements to see what resonates best with users. Additionally, ensuring a higher level of statistical confidence in the results will provide more reliable insights for decision-making.

 

Along with the increase in transactions, there was an increase in revenue by $12,000 by showing the lower third sticky bar.

experiments.title: Will adding a countdown timer to a donation page effectively increase urgency?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a countdown timer to the donation page for the last week of the camapgin will achieve more transactions because the countdown timer will reinforce that time is running out and your donation is needed now.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/28/2024 - 01/01/2025
For CYE this year we wanted to see if we could increase urgency on the donation page by adding a countdown timer to when the campaign ends.
We started this test the last week of the campaign when urgency should be at it’s peak.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding a countdown timer to the donation page during the last week of a campaign can potentially increase revenue for all traffic. However, it is important to note that the statistical level of confidence was at 0.0%, which means that the results cannot be considered valid.

In future experimentation, it is essential to ensure that the sample size is sufficient to yield statistically significant results. It may be beneficial to run similar experiments with a larger sample size to accurately determine the impact of adding a countdown timer on donation page conversions.

Overall, while the initial results are promising, it is important to use caution in interpreting the findings and to prioritize statistical significance in making data-driven decisions for future campaigns and optimizations.

experiments.title: Will adding a sticky bar with a donor countdown on the episodic donation page impact transactions?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a sticky bar  for the campaign episodic donation page will achieve more transactions because the sticky bar will increase the urgency and motivation to give.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 02/06/2025
For this test, we wanted to see if adding additional urgency/ motivation on the campaign episodic donation page would impact the number of transactions made.
We tested this on the monthly donation page and found that it did increase transactions and revenue. You can see the results of that test here.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding a sticky bar to the campaign episodic donation page can increase transactions, potentially due to the increased urgency and motivation it provides to donors. However, the results of this experiment are not statistically significant, with a confidence level of only 53.8%.
Similar to the test on the recurring donation page, we also saw an increase in average gift when the stickybar was present on the page. ($288/avg. gift vs. $388/avg. gift).
This campaign was ran towards the end of December when urgency is at it's highest. It's important to note that the treatment was winning up until the 28th (last day this was scheduled to run). In future testing it might be worth testing this approach earlier in the campaign and not at the end with the urgency for most people to give is already there.

experiments.title: How impacted gift array links in email impacts donor conversion during a high urgency appeal
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that impacted gift array links for email users during a holiday campaign will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate and overall revenue because it eliminates friction, pre-filling the selected amount on the donation page, while also reinforcing that their gift is being matched in the copy.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/21/2025
During CaringBridge’s holiday campaign, we hypothesized that using impacted gift array links to drive urgency and eliminate friction during the 2-day match would increase donor conversion rate and overall revenue.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that using impacted gift array links for email users during a holiday campaign led to a decrease in donations for all traffic. Additionally, the treatment saw a dropoff in average gift with $146 in the control versus $65 in the treatment.

Ultimately, the level of confidence was 78.9%, meaning these results are not valid for future decision-making. It would also be worth re-testing this experience for validity outside the window of a high-urgency appeal. Potentially, the treatment may have resulted in too much decision friction at the top of the funnel, whereas the links would have proved more effective simply by increasing clicks and greater traffic to the donation page.

experiments.title: How a humanized sticky bar on a high-urgency campaign donation page impacts donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that the presence of the humanized sticky bar on the CYE Donation page for email 16 visitors will achieve an increase in donor conversion metrics because the personalized message boosts trust and creates a more personalized experience for the potential donor.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/30/2024 - 12/30/2024
During IMB’s CYE campaign, we implemented a humanized sticky bar on the donation page as part of the strategy for Email 16.
This sticky bar ran for a full day, with the test limited to users who accessed the donation page via a link embedded in Email 16.
The sticky bar treatment was shown to 50% of the page’s traffic, allowing us to evaluate its effectiveness in driving engagement and conversions in this specific segment of our audience.
The control featured no sticky bar.
The treatment sticky bar featured Chuck Anderson, sender of Email 16, and had the following copy: “There’s only one day remaining to support IMB missionaries through your year-end gift to the Lottie Moon Christmas Offering®. As a reminder, your gift will play a crucial role in sharing the joy and peace of Jesus with those who have yet to hear His name. – Chuck Anderson”
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that the humanized sticky bar featuring Chuck Anderson on the donation page for Email 16 visitors did not result in an increase in donor conversion metrics. In fact, the treatment saw a 15.0% decrease in transactions for all traffic with a statistical confidence level of 25.6%, indicating that the control group performed better.
It is important to note that the statistical confidence level is below the threshold of 95%, which means that these results are not considered valid and should not be used for future decision making.
In future experimentation, it is important to consider the impact of humanized elements like sticky bars on donor behavior. It may be beneficial to further test different variations of humanized elements or personalize them based on donor preferences to see if this can lead to an increase in conversions. Additionally, ensuring a longer experiment duration and a larger sample size can help in obtaining more reliable and valid results for future decision making.

experiments.title: How will adding a visual cue to a donation page effect conversions?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding in a visual thermomether for the capaign donation page will achieve a higher donor conversion rate because the thermometer would add urgency to the page.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/09/2025
During CYE we know that there is a ton of traffic that make their way to the KCBI donation page on the last 4-5 days of the year. Due to this high volume of motivated donors to give to KCBI, but also to give before the tax deadline of December 31st.
For this test we wanted to see if we could add a visual cue that would show the amount of time left to make a gift.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that adding a visual thermometer to the campaign donation page resulted in a 29.5% increase in donations for all traffic. While this is a positive outcome, the statistical level of confidence was only 47.2%, which is below the recommended threshold of 95%.
Therefore, these results should not be considered valid or used for future decision making. In future experimentation, it is important to ensure that the sample size is large enough to provide statistically significant results. Additionally, it may be beneficial to conduct further experiments or iterate on the design of the visual thermometer to determine its true impact on donor conversion rates.

experiments.title: Does focusing on a physical premium on the donation page increase conversion rate?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believed that by referencing the downloaded content offer and leaning into the electronic versus physical nature of the benefit, the test version would create a more seamless donor experience and increase conversion rates by making the Sustainer benefit more prominent.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/01/2024 - 11/09/2024
This experiment was conducted for WUNC, a public radio station serving North Carolina, to evaluate the impact of tailored messaging on a content offer donation page for “The People’s Pharmacy.” The goal was to determine whether referencing the content of the offer and emphasizing the electronic nature of the benefit would enhance donor conversion rates by making the experience feel more seamless and highlighting the value of becoming a Sustainer.

experiments.title: How using a gift array based on HPC impacted email donations during year-end
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using a gift array based on highest previous contribution for donors will achieve a change in email conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/27/2024 - 12/31/2024
This experiment was conducted for the Hoover Institution, a public policy think tank dedicated to promoting ideas for a free society. During their year-end campaign, we wanted to see whether basing a gift array on a donor’s highest previous contribution made an impact on conversion rates.
The control version had a standard gift array of $100/250/500/Other.
The treatment based the gift array on HPC, and included 1.5X HPC, 2X HPC, 2.5X HPC, and other.
This experiment was run across 3 of the last emails of the year (12/27, 12/30, and 12/31) to active and lapsed donors.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that using a gift array based on a donor's highest previous contribution can potentially lead to an increase in donations. The treatment group in this experiment showed a 23.8% increase in donations compared to the control group, indicating that personalizing the gift array based on donor behavior may impact conversion rates positively.

However, it is important to note that the statistical level of confidence for this result was 78.9%, which is below the generally accepted threshold of 95%. This means that the results cannot be considered valid or reliable for future decision making.

In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to conduct additional tests with a larger sample size to ensure more statistically significant results. It would also be valuable to explore other factors that could influence donation behavior, such as the timing of the donation appeal, messaging, or donor segmentation.

Overall, while this experiment showed a potential impact of personalized gift arrays on donation rates, further testing is needed to confirm these findings and make informed decisions for future fundraising campaigns.

experiments.title: How the placement of a Dear Listener message vs. an Impact Pop-up on a donation page impacts donor conversion during a year end campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using the Impact Pop-up for FamilyLife’s calendar year-end campaign will increase donor conversion better than the Dear Listener message because the pop-up creates a more immediate sense of urgency and engagement, encouraging users to take action right away.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/02/2023 - 12/16/2023
FamilyLife, a nonprofit dedicated to strengthening families through biblically-based resources, ran this experiment as part of their high urgency calendar year-end campaign, which began on December 2nd. Historically, FamilyLife has relied on a “Dear Listener” tactic at the end of their content—an approach that appeals directly to their audience and has proven successful in driving donations during previous high urgency campaigns.
