For this test, however, we sought to explore whether a different tactic could outperform this traditional method in terms of donor conversion.
To determine whether a more interactive and urgent format would yield higher conversions, we tested the Impact Pop-up against the “Dear Listener” message. The “Dear Listener” message was used in the Control group, maintaining the traditional approach where the appeal is made at the end of the content. In the Treatment group, we implemented the Impact Pop-up, which was triggered during the user’s session and emphasized the urgency of supporting the mission. The pop-up also featured an image of a family reading a Bible to reinforce the message and visually engage users.
This experiment was set up as a split A/B test in VWO, with the traffic being split 50/50 between the Control (Dear Listener) and the Treatment (Impact Pop-up). We tested these tactics on their podcast pages. Both versions used similar messaging, but the key differences were the format and the visibility of the appeal.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The experiment ran from December 2nd to December 16th, a two-week period, and although it did not reach the ideal 30-day threshold for full validation, the Impact Pop-up showed a promising 51.8% increase in conversions with a 67.6% likelihood of conversion (LoC) compared to the Dear Listener message. The Dear Listener generated 9 transactions, while the Impact Pop-up generated 14 transactions. While the result is compelling, it was still a bit too early to validate fully, but given the urgency of the campaign, the decision was made to roll out the Impact Pop-up.

The Impact Pop-up likely performed better because it created a sense of urgency by appearing during the user’s session, prompting immediate action. Unlike the Dear Listener message, which required users to scroll down the page to see it, the Impact Pop-up was triggered after 7 seconds of page engagement, ensuring that most visitors to the page would see it. This guaranteed visibility likely contributed to higher engagement and conversion, as users didn’t need to actively seek out the message. The emotional appeal of the image of a family reading the Bible further reinforced the urgency, making the call-to-action feel more relevant and personal for users.

Given the success of the Impact Pop-up in this initial period, we recommend rolling it out as the primary tactic for future high urgency campaigns. However, since the experiment didn’t run long enough to validate fully, we suggest retesting, continuing to monitor its performance closely, and conducting follow-up testing to further optimize the pop-up’s design and messaging. Additionally, testing with a larger sample size and longer duration could help validate its effectiveness across different user segments.

experiments.title: How including compelling images in a high urgency appeal impacts clicks
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that including compelling images in a high urgency appeal for CYE Email 14 will achieve an increase in clicks because the presence of the images allows the reader to visually connect withe the mission of the IMB.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/06/2025
To increase email click rates during IMB’s CYE campaign, we tested the impact of adding compelling images to Email 14. The treatment was presented to 50% of the recipients of this email.
Our hypothesis was that incorporating these visuals would help readers establish a stronger emotional connection with IMB’s mission, thereby increasing the likelihood of clicking the donation link.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that including compelling images in high urgency appeal emails may not necessarily lead to an increase in clicks for donation links. The treatment group, which received emails with images, actually saw a 2.9% decrease in clicks compared to the control group. However, the statistical level of confidence was only 51.5%, which is below the generally accepted threshold of 95%.

Therefore, these results cannot be considered valid and should not be used for future decision making. In future experimentation, it would be important to thoroughly analyze the data and ensure that the findings are statistically significant before drawing any conclusions. It may also be beneficial to test different types of images or messaging to see what truly resonates with recipients and drives them to click on donation links.

experiments.title: How a Slide-Up performs against an Exit-Intent during a Holiday Appeal
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a slide-up with gift array for users on the CaringBridge site will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate and revenue because the experience is less intrusive than the pop-up, and the in-line array pre-fills the donor’s selected amount on the donation page.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/17/2024 - 12/18/2024
During CaringBridge’s holiday appeal, we conducted a re-test of the Slide-Up with gift array vs the standard Exit Intent pop-up. The goal was to determine whether there was a significant impact upon donations and revenue during a high urgency appeal – where motivation is significantly higher.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that the slide-up with gift array did not perform as well as the standard exit intent pop-up in terms of donations during the high urgency appeal. However, it is important to note that the statistical level of confidence was only 55.0%, which is below the recommended threshold of 85%.

However, this test did help troubleshoot an earlier discrepancy between the slide-up and exit intent where traffic targeting was unfairly impacting results. In reality, when distributed to traffic properly 50/50, the two onsites performed similarly.

experiments.title: How using a countdown clock on a donation page in the final days of a high urgency campaign affects donor conversion rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a countdown clock to the donation page will increase the donation conversion rate because it creates urgency by highlighting the time remaining until the deadline.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/27/2024 - 01/01/2025
The American Cornerstone Institute ran a high-urgency campaign for the final month of the year. Within this campaign, they wanted to test the impact of including a countdown clock on the donation for the final 4 days of the campaign.
They conducted an A/B test, split 50/50, with two versions of the donation page: the control version without a countdown clock and the treatment version with a countdown clock. Their hypothesis was that the countdown clock would create a sense of urgency, encouraging more users to make a donation before the deadline (When the countdown clock ran out).
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that the inclusion of a countdown clock on the donation page did not have the intended impact of increasing donation conversion rates. In fact, the treatment group, which had the countdown clock, experienced a 6.6% decrease in transactions compared to the control group.

It is crucial to note that the statistical level of confidence for this result was only 59.0%, which is below the standard threshold of 85% for results to be considered valid. Therefore, these results should not be used for future decision-making.

In future experimentation, it is essential to consider the statistical significance of the results before drawing any conclusions. Additionally, it may be beneficial to test the effectiveness of different strategies to create urgency or incentivize donations, such as limited-time offers or matching donation challenges, to see what resonates best with donors. Conducting further A/B tests with a larger sample size and varying durations may provide more conclusive insights for future fundraising campaigns.

experiments.title: How a homepage takeover affects donations during a Giving Tuesday campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a homepage takeover will increase donor conversion rate by reducing friction for motivated traffic during the Giving Tuesday campaign.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/03/2024 - 12/04/2024
For The Heritage Foundation’s Giving Tuesday campaign, they wanted to test the impact of a homepage takeover that would direct visitors to their homepage on Giving Tuesday to the campaign’s donation page with a link to go to back to their intended destination.
We believed that redirecting visitors to the donation page would reduce friction for highly motivated traffic that came to give a special gift and in turn increase donor conversions and revenue.
This experiment ran for one day. Each variant received 50% of traffic from mostly organic, direct sources to The Heritage Foundation’s home page.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that implementing a homepage takeover to direct visitors to a donation page on Giving Tuesday can potentially increase donor conversions. We saw a 19.6% increase in transactions for all traffic during the one-day experiment. However, with a statistical confidence level of 56.7%, these results are inconclusive and not suitable for future decision making.

These results may indicate that site visitors may not have been highly motivated to navigate to the Heritage homepage in order to give a gift for the Giving Tuesday campaign.

In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to conduct a similar homepage takeover experiment over a longer period of time to gather more data and increase the statistical confidence level. This can be accomplished by running another test over the last few days of the year during the calendar year end campaign.

experiments.title: How a dynamic thermometer on the donation page impacts donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a dynamic thermometer for users on the Giving Day donation page will achieve an increase in donor conversion because it helps underscore the urgency of this single-day appeal and helps the donor better visual their role in reaching the goal.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 12/03/2024
For RTB’s Giving Day, we hypothesized that including a dynamic thermometer showing the progress of the $15,000 goal during this one-day appeal would help generate greater urgency and improve donor conversion rate.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Key learnings from this experiment include:

1. The addition of a dynamic thermometer showing the progress of the fundraising goal on the donation page had a significant impact on donor conversion rates, with a 80.6% increase in donations for all traffic.

2. Despite the positive outcome, the statistical confidence level of 62.9% is below the commonly accepted threshold of 85%, which means that the results may not be considered completely reliable.

3. It is important to recognize that while the treatment was successful in this particular instance, the results cannot be referenced as a basis for future decision-making due to the low statistical confidence level.

In future experimentation, it is crucial to ensure that the sample size is sufficient to provide a higher level of statistical confidence before making any conclusions or implementing changes based on the results of the experiment. Additionally, it may be beneficial to conduct further tests or iterations of the dynamic thermometer feature to validate its impact on donor conversion rates before making it a permanent fixture on the donation page.

experiments.title: How will increasing the readability on the donation page affect conversions?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that increasing the readability for the main donation page will achieve a higher donor conversion rate because it will give a better user expereience.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/21/2024 - 09/03/2024
The statement ‘many donors that make up the bulk of the program are over the age of 65’ reign true.
As we think about being user friendly and making the experience easier for users, it’s important to look at the demographic of those who frequent the donation pages.
For this test we wanted to see what would happen if we increased the readability of the donation page. For this we increased the font size, increased the margins, increased the line spacing, etc..
Here is what we found.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment indicate that increasing the readability of the donation page did not lead to a higher donor conversion rate. In fact, the treatment group experienced a decrease in revenue compared to the control group. However, the statistical level of confidence was below the threshold of 95%, making these results inconclusive and not suitable for future decision making.

Therefore, it is important to be cautious when implementing changes based on assumptions without conducting thorough testing. Future experimentation should continue to focus on user experience and readability but should be done so with careful monitoring and analysis of results before making any significant changes. Additionally, it may be beneficial to consider other factors such as donor demographics and preferences to tailor the donation page for the specific audience.

experiments.title: How including giving handles on a donation page during a recurring giving campaign impacts donor conversion metrics
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that including giving handles for the Recurring Giving donation page visitors will achieve an increase in donor conversion metrics because the gift’s impact will be more tangible for the donor. because the gift’s impact will be more tangible for the donor.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/24/2024 - 11/04/2024
During IMB’s Fall Recurring Giving Campaign, we decided to test including giving handles within the donation page copy.
Both the control and the treatment had the same gift array and the gift array aligned with the amounts within the giving handles. These giving handles ranged from $5 – $75.
This donation page did default to monthly, but one-time giving was still an option.
It is worth noting that this campaign was shut down early, so this test did not get enough traffic to validate.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that including giving handles in the donation page copy led to a 29.3% decrease in Revenue for All Traffic with a 26.4% statistical level of confidence and a 10.1% decrease in Transactions for All Traffic at a 28.3% level of confidence. While neither of these results are trending in the direction we intended, we did see a 210.8% lift in recurring donations at a 93.4% level of confidence.

The recurring donation metrics was extremely close to validating which does suggest that the giving handles resonated well with monthly givers. It is worth testing further into how giving handles can compel IMB's donor base to give.

In future experimentation, it is important to ensure that there is enough traffic to validate the results. Additionally, it may be beneficial to further test the impact of including giving handles with different messaging or positioning to see if there is a way to communicate the impact of donations effectively without negatively impacting conversion metrics. It is also crucial to consider the potential effects of messaging on donor behavior and to continuously iterate and test different approaches to optimize donation page performance.

experiments.title: How a slide out on a landing page impacts email sign ups
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a slide out with supportive copy for authors will achieve an increase in co-author invitations sent because it addresses the felt need and highlights it as a call to action.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/14/2024 - 10/24/2024
For CaringBridge authors, a key call to action is inviting a co-author to help manage your page. In order to increase this, we hypothesized that a slideout  with supportive copy would increase co-author invitations (email sign ups).
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are as follows:

1. The treatment, which involved a flyout with supportive copy for authors, resulted in an 8.9% increase in Co-Author Invites Sent for All Traffic. This indicates that the treatment had a positive impact on the desired outcome. However, the statistical level of confidence for this increase was 62.9%, which is below the generally accepted threshold of 85%.

It may be beneficial to further iterate on the flyout design and messaging to optimize the impact on Co-Author Invites Sent. Continue to monitor the engagement metrics such as Flyout CTA clicked to understand how users are interacting with the flyout.

Overall, while the initial results are promising, it is essential to conduct further experiments with larger sample sizes to validate the effectiveness of the flyout in increasing co-author invitations.

experiments.title: Will adding a sticky bar to a campaign donation page affect donor conversion?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a humanized sticky bar for the Fall Share campaign donation page will achieve a greater donor conversion rate because the need is reinforced on the page in a more eye catching way when somebody lands on the page.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 10/07/2024
Moody Radio runs a Fall Share campaign each year. This campaign draws in a ton of traffic from ads, organic, and on-air listeners.
For this campaign, we wanted to see if adding a stickybar at the top of the campaign giving page would increase donor conversion
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that the addition of a humanized sticky bar to the Fall Share campaign donation page did not lead to an increase in donor conversion rate. In fact, the treatment group saw a decrease in donations compared to the control group.

However, we did see an in the average gift when the sticky bar was present. The control saw a $293 average gift while the treatment saw a $345 average gift!

Although we might not have increased overall transactions, by having the sticky bar present on the page we did see an increase in generosity. More testing needs to be done with this before it can be rolled out.

experiments.title: How an in-line prompt impacts new journal entries (non-clickable)
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that an in-line Create a Post card for authors will achieve an increase in new journal entires because it prioritizes the CTA above others and makes it more visible..
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/27/2024 - 10/04/2024
For CaringBridge Authors, posting a new journal entry is one of the primary calls-to-action. However, currently it is given a similar level of priority as the other CTA’s on the left sidebar: Share, Add a Co-Author, Ways to Help, etc. We hypothesized that by placing this prompt in-line with the feed (where their entry would ultimately go) and it clickable would improve new journal entries — addressing a potential point of friction that authors aren’t sure where or how to post.
This was the first iteration in a testing series which presented an in-line card with a small clickable button – in the next test, the entire card was made clickable. Primarily, the impact of this change upon the first test was a significant decrease in clicks overall to the treatment, indicating that users did not know where to click or interact with the prompt.
experiments.keyLearnings:
While both experiments within this series resulted in a similar increase in journal entries (3%), the non-clickable card impacted this treatment negatively in clicks where users did not know what step to take next.

It is worth noting that making this clickable change didn't impact our end metric ultimately (journal entries) but it did improve the user experience.

experiments.title: How an in-line, clickable “Create a Post” button impacts new journal entries
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a clickable, in-line “Create a Post” prompt for authors will achieve an increase in journal entries because it prioritizes it as a primary call-to-action by making it more visible for the author..
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/14/2024 - 09/24/2024
For CaringBridge Authors, posting a new journal entry is one of the primary calls-to-action. However, currently it is given a similar level of priority as the other CTA’s on the left sidebar: Share, Add a Co-Author, Ways to Help, etc. We hypothesized that by placing this prompt in-line with the feed (where their entry would ultimately go) and it clickable would improve new journal entries — addressing a potential point of friction that authors aren’t sure where or how to post.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are:

1. The in-line "Create a Post" prompt led to a 3.3% increase in New Journal Entries for All Traffic. This indicates that making the journal entry prompt more visible and clickable can encourage authors to post more frequently.

2. However, the statistical confidence level of 79.6% is below the standard threshold of 95%, making the results inconclusive and not completely reliable for future decision making.

We believe that these soft results indicate that the true point of friction hasn't been addressed by this treatment. A similar level of clicks between the cards and a similar conversion rate of journal entires may indicate that "where" to post or "how" is not what's preventing authors from doing so. Rather, it's likely a question of "what" to post. How can we make it easier for authors to share what's on their mind, even if initially they may believe they don't have anything to say.

experiments.title: How adding an image to a premium offer impacts donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by adding the image of the large bundle for email subscribers will achieve a higher donor conversion rate because there is visual reenforcement of what all is included in the bundle.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 09/24/2024
Pathway to Victory is the broadcast ministry of First Dallas
Each month, PTV promotes a new book. Some of the emails also mention an upgrade to purchase a larger bundle of resources.
For this test, we decided to test adding an image of the large bundle
experiments.keyLearnings:
Key learnings from this experiment include:

1. Adding an image of the large bundle for email subscribers resulted in a 6.8% increase in donations for all traffic.

2. The statistical level of confidence for this result was 16.1%, which is below the 85% threshold for validity.

3. Therefore, the results of this experiment should not be used for future decision-making.

Specific to the PS, we saw a 120% increase in clicks to the PS link for the large bundle, however control and treatment each only received 1 large bundle donation.

In future experimentation, it is important to ensure that the statistical level of confidence is high enough to draw meaningful conclusions. Additionally, it may be beneficial to continue testing the impact of visual elements, such as images, in email campaigns to see if they consistently lead to higher donation rates.

experiments.title: How adding an icon to the donation page showing the number of gifts made so far impacts revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a badge icon to the Tribute tab for users on a site profile will achieve an increase in gifts and revenue to the tribute widget because it makes this tab more visible, highlights its importance, and drives more users to the widget..
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/09/2024 - 09/13/2024
On CaringBridge profiles, the Tribute widget is a significant source of donations and revenue. A tribute donation is a gift to CaringBridge that a user makes in tribute to the profile they are visiting. This widget appears in a few different locations on the site, but historically one of the highest-performing locations has been on the Tribute page itself. This tab shows a list of tributes that have been made on the user’s profile, it includes copy “Join the X donors who have made a donation in honor of [name].
Since migration to CB2, CaringBridge has seen low performance on this widget. We hypothesized that by driving more traffic to the tab, it would increase donor conversion rate and revenue. On the old site, this Tributes tab was it’s own designated page and the button to navigate to it included a badge icon with how many tributes were on the profile. We created two treatments, one that’s a static badge icon and another where the same badge icon animates and counts up to the total number of tributes.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that adding a badge icon to the Tribute tab on site profiles significantly increased Revenue - Tribute from the widget by 106.7%. Additionally, Tribute Tab clicks experienced a substantial increase of 9,5881%. These results were statistically significant with confidence levels of 95.6% and 100% respectively.

Moving forward, it is clear that showcasing the importance of the Tribute tab by making it more visible and engaging users with a badge icon can lead to a significant boost in donations and revenue. This insight should be taken into consideration for future experimentation and site optimizations. By continuing to test and iterate on features that drive engagement with the Tribute widget, CaringBridge can further enhance user experience and ultimately increase donor conversion rates.

experiments.title: How a $25 callout on the Non-Tribute donation age impacts revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a $25 callout  for the non-tribute donation page will achieve an increase in revenue and donations because it reinforces the impact of the user’s gift in a tangible way — powering a user’s site..
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/19/2024 - 09/06/2024
For CaringBridge’s non-tribute donation page (main donate) we hypothesized that including a $25 callout over the gift array, leveraging language that’s been used successfully in other locations/widgets on the site, would improve overall revenue and transactions on main donate as well.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that including a $25 callout for the non-tribute donation page resulted in a 3.0% increase in revenue for all traffic but with a statistical level of confidence of only 54%.
It is likely that this callout is not making a significant impact either way, because in both the control and treatment, the average gift is already above the $25 callout.
Moving forward, it is important to note that the results of this experiments hould not be be considered valid. It is essential to ensure that any future experiments have a higher level of statistical confidence to draw reliable conclusions. Additionally, it may be beneficial to further refine the hypothesis and experiment design to potentially achieve more significant and reliable results. Testing different variations, such as adjusting the language or positioning of the callout, could also help to optimize future experiments.

experiments.title: How offering a digital version of Is Christianity the Right Religion impacts email acquisition rates for Pathway to Victory
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that offering a digital download for a Facebook audience will achieve a better email conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/22/2022 - 05/11/2022
Pathway to Victory is a ministry who has built a fundraising program around the offer of premiums—books, pamphlets, brochures, etc. Most of their new name acquisition efforts (not donor acquisition) focused around offering up brochures and pamphlets that were then mailed to the recipient. Because this fulfillment process usually took a month, we hypothesized that offering an instant digital download of the same exact premium, in PDF format, would drive a higher email acquisition rate at a much lower cost to acquire.
This is an extension of an experiment we tested with a single premium in April; for this experiment, we were testing the Is Christianity the Right Religion premium resources.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Pathway to Victory was able to 4.8% increase in email conversion rates; however the level of confidence is only 67%. Moreover, the cost to acquire an email address was 6% higher for the digital premium.

We believe this result is due to a combination of factors:
By offering up immediate gratification through a digital download, versus potentially waiting up to a month to receive your resource in the mail, the audience was much more likely to sign up to receive it.
 	By not requiring a full mailing address for the digital version, we removed some friction in the sign-up process.

experiments.title: How adding a video on a landing page impacts donations
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by adding a video to a landing page for Hoover’s audience will achieve in increase in donations because   they will be more likely to engage and feel connected to the offer they are signing up for.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/12/2024 - 08/21/2024
The Hoover Institution is a public policy research center and think tank located at Stanford University in California, United States.  For one of their most popular offers, Victory and the Savior Generals, we wanted to test whether adding a video that we were already running as a YouTube ad to the landing page would make an impact on donations. We hypothesized that by adding the video to the page, people would become more engaged and connected with the offer and would be more likely to donate.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that adding a video to a landing page for Hoover's audience resulted in a 5.2% increase in Revenue for All Traffic. However, the statistical level of confidence was only 52.8%, which is below the recommended threshold of 95% for valid results.
On the other hand, Email Acquisition experienced a significant 18.2% decrease with a high level of confidence at 98%.
These results suggest that while the video may have positively impacted the revenue, the decrease in email acquisitions raises concerns about the overall effectiveness of the video on the landing page.
In future experimentation, it is important to take into consideration both the positive and negative impacts of the changes made to the landing page. It is also crucial to ensure that the statistical level of confidence is higher before drawing any definitive conclusions. Additionally, further testing and analysis are needed to understand the full impact of adding a video to the landing page and to make informed decisions about its effectiveness in driving conversions.

experiments.title: How bypassing the registration page impacted email signups and donations
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that reducing friction in the course registration process for landing page viewers will achieve an increase in email acquisition because there will be less friction in the process and fewer clicks to achieve the desired action.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/20/2023 - 12/18/2023
After moving to a new course platform, we wanted to test reducing friction in the process by not having visitors have to click an additional button to begin the registration process. However, that page that preceded the checkout option is what communicated the value proposition of the course to the reader so we wanted to test before changing the process entirely. We set up an A/B test and split traffic to the registration page URL and the other 50% of traffic went to a treatment page which bypassed the registration page and went straight to the course checkout page. We ran the experiment for 2 months and observed how visitors behaved on the email acquisition page as well as downstream impacts to guide decisions.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that removing the registration page and reducing friction in the course registration process resulted in a decrease in email acquisition. However, since the statistical level of confidence is below 85%, these results cannot be considered valid and should not be used for future decision making.

To determine the impact of reducing friction on the registration process and email acquisition, it is recommended to conduct further experiments with larger sample sizes and longer durations. This will help obtain more statistically significant results and provide more reliable insights.

Additionally, it would be beneficial to explore other factors that might have influenced the decrease in email acquisition. For example, the treatment page may have lacked important information or a clear value proposition, leading to lower engagement and conversions.

Future experiments can also focus on improving the treatment page by incorporating elements of the registration page that effectively communicate the value proposition of the course. This way, the experiment can gauge whether reducing friction while maintaining the value proposition can positively impact email acquisition. It is important to analyze and understand user behavior and preferences to optimize the registration process and maximize email acquisition.

experiments.title: How adding a thermometer on a recurring campaign donation pages impacts monthly donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a monthly donor counter thermometer for DTS’s North Texas Giving Day donation page will achieve an increase in monthly donor conversion because the thermometer adds clarity on the number of people still needed to become a monthly donor.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/11/2023 - 09/21/2023
Each year, Dallas Theological Seminary (DTS) participates in the local giving day, North Texas Giving Day. During previous years, they’ve focused on increasing their number of monthly donors—Cornerstone Partners.
This year, they continued in this tradition, but wanted to see what optimizations they could make to their donation page to see if they could increase recurring donor conversions.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are:

1. The treatment version of the donation page, which included a monthly donor counter thermometer, resulted in a 23.2% increase in recurring donations compared to the control version.

2. The statistical level of confidence for this result was 64.4%, which is below the accepted threshold of 85%, indicating that the results cannot be considered valid.

3. The hypothesis that adding a monthly donor counter thermometer would increase monthly donor conversions was supported by the observed increase in recurring donations.

4. Future experiments should consider further optimizing the donation page by including a monthly donor counter thermometer as it showed promising results in increasing recurring donor conversions.

5. It is important to have a larger sample size and a higher level of statistical confidence to validate the findings and make informed decisions for future experimentation.

experiments.title: How enhancing the personal value for the user of a free online course impacted email registrations
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that emphasizing the value to the user for potential course takers will achieve an increase in email acquisition because the course is then positioned more uniquely and personally as a solution to the user.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/12/2023 - 10/18/2023
When reviewing a course with low sign up rate, we wanted to propose a landing page treatment that would enhance the value of the course to the individual visitor. We put together copy that focuses more on the historical debates and questions about the return of Christ—addressing the questions in the minds of the visitors that would be answered or solved by signing up for the free online course. We attempt to tap into the curiosity of the visitor, positioning the course as a solution to those uncertainties. We also added clarity in the call to action to make it clear what next step the visitor should take before introducing the button to register. The treatment specifically mentions that by signing up, users will receive their first lecture instantly. This adds a sense of immediacy and instant gratification which might entice visitors to take action.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that emphasizing the value of the course to potential users can result in an increase in email acquisition. This highlights the importance of positioning the course as a unique and personal solution to the uncertainties and questions that potential users may have.

In future experimentation, we should continue to focus on highlighting the value and benefits of the courses we offer. This could involve addressing specific pain points or uncertainties that potential users may have and demonstrating how our courses can provide the solutions or answers they are seeking.

Additionally, it is important to remember the significance of clarity in the call to action. Clearly stating the next steps for the visitor and highlighting any immediate benefits, such as receiving the first lecture instantly, can help to create a sense of urgency and incentivize users to take action.

To validate the findings of this experiment and make informed decisions for future experimentation, it is necessary to run additional experiments with a larger sample size and higher statistical level of confidence. This will provide more reliable and valid results that can be used to guide future decision-making.

experiments.title: How changing the picture of a premium resources impacted donation conversions
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that updating the picture on a premium offer for content offer downloaders will achieve a change in donation conversions.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/13/2024 - 07/06/2024
The Hoover Institution is a public policy research center and think tank located at Stanford University in California, United States.  Every quarter they publish the Hoover Digest, which is a collection of essays from 30 of their scholars. This offer is one of their best performing acquisition offers. We’ve tested into a premium test for the content offer where we ask donors for a gift of $100 or more to get all 4 editions of the Digest in the mail for the next year.
For this test, we wanted to see if changing the image of the Digest itself to something that featured the current cover would make an impact on donor conversions.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that updating the picture on a premium offer for content downloaders led to a 29.6% increase in revenue for all traffic. This implies that the new image featuring the current cover of the Hoover Digest had a positive impact on donor conversions.

However, it is important to note that the statistical level of confidence was only 71.5%, which is below the generally accepted threshold of 85%. This means that the results cannot be considered entirely reliable and should not be used for future decision-making.

In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to continue testing the impact of using updated images or visuals in premium offers to see if they consistently lead to higher conversion rates. It is also essential to ensure that experiments are run for longer durations to gather more data and increase the confidence level of the results. Additionally, considering other elements such as copywriting, call-to-action buttons, and layout design in future experiments could provide further insights into what drives donor conversions effectively.

experiments.title: How using a video on a high urgency campaign donation page impacts giving
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using the transcript of a video on a donation page for email recipients will achieve a higher donor conversion rate .
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 06/17/2022
Reasons to Believe launched their fiscal year end campaign and in their launch email they were leading their email recipients to a donation page with a video. Past experiments with other organizations have shown that a video on a donation page can actually add friction to the giving experience. The reason being that many people are unlikely to watch through a whole video and they’re more likely to read through some copy.
For this email we sent half of their list to a donation page with the video (the control) and the other half of the list to a donation page with a transcript of the content of the video (the treatment). Our hypothesis is that more people would convert on the donation page with the transcript of the video.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This experiment resulted in a 56% decrease in donor conversion rate with a level of confidence of 78%. Something to note is that on the email itself, RTB used different copy on the CTA button. The control had "Watch my video" and the treatment had "Read my note." When looking at the click thru rate between each button we saw a valid 28% decrease in clicks with a level of confidence of 99%. In a sense, 2 variables were tested in this experiment: the call to action and the donation page itself.

It's likely that RTB's audience is conditioned to watch videos for their content and throughout their high urgency campaigns. However it seems as more traffic was pushed to the video donation page because of the different CTA. Watching a video may sound more appealing than to read something.

It's recommended that this is retested in a channel that gets more traffic and that has the same CTA button, like a sticky bar. This will isolate one variable and get us more definitive results.

experiments.title: How adding social proof to a high-urgency donation page through a slide out affects donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding social proof through a slideout for Eagle Lake 2 donation page visitors will achieve an increase in donor conversion metrics because seeing that others are actively engaging and donating will create a sense of urgency and validation, encouraging more people to contribute.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/11/2024 - 07/26/2024
Every year, The Navigators run a high-urgency campaign called Eagle Lake 2 during June and July to raise money for their Eagle Lake camps.
We have seen success running social proof-based tests for other clients, so we were anxious to run it for The Navigators.
For this test, the treatment version displayed a pop-out of a statement that read “FirstName from State just donated $GiftAmount”
All the information displayed was data pulled from donors to the campaign. It is also worth noting, that these contacts were pulled manually due to our lack of ability to install a pixel to track conversions on their donation pages.
Our hypothesis was that the pop-out would increase the amount of social proof experienced by the visitor on the page which would then lead to an increase in our donor conversion metrics by increasing a sense of urgency and need to donate.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding social proof in the form of a slideout for the Eagle Lake 2 donation page did not lead to an increase in donor conversion metrics. In fact, the treatment version saw a decrease in transactions for all traffic compared to the control version.

It is important to note that the results of this experiment are not statistically significant, with a confidence level of only 34.4%. Therefore, these results cannot be considered valid, and it would be unwise to make any decisions based on them. However, we can assume that the addition of social proof may have a negative consequence in terms of donor acquisition metrics. Therefore, we will need to test this concept again to come to a clear conclusion.

In future experimentation, it is essential to ensure that the sample size is sufficient to achieve a statistically significant result. Additionally, it might be beneficial to conduct qualitative research to understand why the  slideout did not have the desired effect. This could provide valuable insights that can be used to design more effective experiments in the future.

experiments.title: How humanizing an Anti-Paywall pop-up can effect donations
experiments.researchQuestion: We anticipate that integrating an anti-paywall pop-up onto the WUNC homepage will inspire prospects to consider donating. By presenting them with a clear value proposition by an actual person and reminding them of WUNC’s commitment to providing free content, we aim to instill a sense of reciprocity and encourage support for the station.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/11/2024 - 07/25/2024
This experiment, conducted for WUNC, a North Carolina-based radio station, aimed to assess the impact of implementing an anti-paywall pop-up featuring a personalized message and photo of an employee on the homepage for new website visitors. The primary goal was to determine if this approach could effectively boost donation rates by immediately engaging visitors upon entry and potentially fostering greater support for WUNC.
We have previously conducted this experiment and wanted to see if we used a different image if it affects donor acquisition rates.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that implementing an anti-paywall pop-up featuring a personalized message and photo of an employee on the WUNC homepage increased transactions for all traffic by 25.6%. While this is a positive outcome, the statistical level of confidence was only 43.1%, falling below the generally accepted threshold of 85%. As a result, these results cannot be considered valid and should not be used for future decision-making.
This experiment did result in a positive outcome when compared to previous experiments, indicating that using this image may be the way to go moving forward.
In future experimentation, it is important to ensure that the sample size is sufficient to produce reliable results with a higher level of statistical confidence. This may involve running the experiment for a longer duration or increasing the number of participants. Additionally, it would be beneficial to conduct further tests with different variables, such as using different images or messages in the pop-up, to determine their impact on donor acquisition rates.
Overall, while the initial results of the experiment were promising, it is important to prioritize statistical rigor in order to draw accurate conclusions and make informed decisions for future strategies aimed at increasing donor support for WUNC.

experiments.title: How prioritizing a child on the sponsorship page impacts conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Can we increase sponsorship conversion by prioritizing a child on the sponsorship page?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/16/2021 - 05/03/2021
Save the Children has thousands of children that people can choose to sponsor at any given time. As users navigate throughout the site, they can sort and filter on certain variables to find the child they want to sponsor: location, gender, age, and by birth month. Many people used the search widgets to filter through children, because the number of children available was overwhelming—even after filtering through multiple searches. In an effort to make the decision process easier for an end user, we hypothesized that prioritizing a single child at the top of the page may increase conversion. To test into this, we developed a treatment that pulled out a single child, that remained static, that used personal language and information of that child. The variation said, “Sponsor Me!” and gave users the ability to quickly add that child to their cart to begin the sponsorship. All sponsorship cart functionality of the variation remained consistent with the control, the variation simply just visually highlighted one child at the top of the page.
experiments.keyLearnings:
While we weren't able to validate the results, we did see a directional lift in conversion from the treatment variation. Additionally, in the initial weeks of the experiment running, the results were, in fact, positively valid. As the experiment continued to run, the results remained positive, but loss the strength of its validity. That said, this experiment reveals something interesting about the sponsors: while we might think that they want more options to choose, they actually are more likely to choose a child when we choose the child for them. The is a result of removing friction to the decision process.

One other result of this experiment is that we also saw a directional lift in overall purchases from this page. Yes, sponsorship conversion is the primary goal of this page, but it is important to ensure that any tactic we would apply to the page to impact that metric doesn't negatively impact the overall conversion.

Because we initially saw valid results with this experiment, which then turned to directional, we believe that this tactic can improve sponsorship conversion, but the principle may need to be strengthened through the value proposition of the prioritized child. This is the next phase of testing for this feature in an effort to validate the directional results.

experiments.title: Will a “Give” button have a higher donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that changing donate to give for the sticky button will achieve a higher donor conversion because give could be more cohesive with the messaging during year end.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/04/2024
For this experiment we wanted to see if changing the Donate sticky button on the side of the web pages to say “Give” would increase donor conversion.
experiments.keyLearnings: The key learning from this experiment is that changing the sticky button from "Donate" to "Give" did not achieve a higher donor conversion. In fact, it resulted in a decrease in donations for all traffic.

experiments.title: How an in-line banner ask on an article page impacted donations
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a wiki style banner for the article pages will achieve a higher donor conversion rate because the additional value prop on the page can motivate people to give.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/12/2023 - 01/01/2024
For a high urgency campaign we wanted to test if having an ask on an article page above the main hero image would impact donor conversion.
experiments.keyLearnings: Having the wiki style banner ask on the articles pages increased the total number of transactions by 6.6% but with only a 41% LOC. While the transactions did increase, the revenue actually saw a decrease by 7.6%. This leads me to believe that there could have been an large gift in the control that should not be taken into account when reviewing the results for this experiment.

experiments.title: How including a staff testimonial video within an email impacts donor acquisition metrics
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that including the staff testimonial video for Eagle Lake 2 email appeals will achieve an increase in overall donor conversion metrics because “people give to people” and the video helps establish a personable connection with the donor.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 07/29/2024
For The Navigators Eagle Lake 2 campaign this year, we wanted to test including a staff testimonial video within an email appeal to see how it impacted donor conversion metrics. The Navigators audience tends to respond more positively to more genuine communication and real stories.
Therefore, for email 7 of this high urgency campaign, we included an applicable video of an Eagle Lake Counselor sharing how God was working within the camp this year. We expected to see an increase in overall donor conversion metrics.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that including a staff testimonial video in email appeals for The Navigators Eagle Lake 2 campaign can lead to a significant increase in donor conversion metrics. The treatment group, which received the email with the video, experienced a 44.5% increase in donations for all traffic. This suggests that the video helped establish a more personable connection with donors, leading to a higher conversion rate.
However, it is important to note that the statistical level of confidence was only 75.0%, which falls below the generally accepted threshold of 95%. This means that the results of the experiment should not be considered valid and should not be used for future decision making.
Further, we saw a 37.2% lift in overall revenue at a level of confidence of 78.2%. While this metric is also not considered valid, it does suggest that The Navigators audience responded positively to the inclusion of the video.
In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to continue testing the impact of incorporating more person to person connection within appeals, but ensuring that the sample size is sufficient to reach a higher level of statistical confidence. It may also be valuable to test different types of testimonial videos or other forms of personal and genuine communication to determine the most effective strategy for engaging donors.

experiments.title: How using a lighter background on a video series landing page impacted clicks
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using a light background for the video series landing page will achieve an increase in clicks to the checkout page because the contrast from the white background will improve readability for the user.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/09/2024 - 04/25/2024
Focus on the Family (FOTF) is a large Christian ministry organization that creates and shares free resources like video series to encourage families. They created a landing page for a new paid video series and wanted to know if the current, black background of the page was harming the user experience.
We decided to test a light-background version of the page against the current dark background. Our hypothesis was that the white background would lead to more clicks to the checkout page because white backgrounds in web design are shown to enhance readability and reduce visual strain.
We ran this as a split URL test in VWO, with a random 50% of the traffic going to the dark background page. The other 50% were instead shown a light background version of the page.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment was that changing the background color of the video series landing page from black to white did not result in the desired increase in clicks to the checkout page. In fact, the treatment group experienced a 2.4% decrease in visits to the Focus Store Checkout Page compared to the control group.

Additionally, the statistical level of confidence was only 15.3%, which is below the generally accepted threshold of 85%. This means that the results of this experiment were essentially flat and shouldn't be considered a valid test result.

Overall, the results of this experiment suggest that changing the background color alone may not be enough to drive increased engagement with the checkout page. Further research and experimentation may be necessary to identify the most effective strategies for improving user experience and encouraging clicks to the checkout page.

experiments.title: How reducing the size of the Express Checkout button on donation pages impacted revenue and donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that reducing the size of the Express Checkout button for visitors to our video series acquisition donation pages will achieve an increase in donor conversion because the smaller button will provide less of a competition for the donor’s attention as they’re making their gift, and this means that the friction will be reduced.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/11/2024 - 06/26/2024
The team at Focus on the Family (FOTF) has been testing various elements of their donation pages to determine which factors could improve donor conversion and revenue. One element that we wanted to test was the Express Checkout button that appears in the donation form below the gift array. Users who click this button can choose to donate through Google Pay and the idea is that this makes the donation process easier and thus increases donor conversion. However, we wondered if this button could serve as a distraction during the donation process and actually negatively affect donor conversion.
FOTF first tested removing the Express Checkout button on their main donation page and the results were very flat. They then tested whether reducing the Express Checkout button’s size would drive a different outcome. This test found that the smaller button size led to a 21.3% increase in transactions, with a 95.7% level of confidence.
The purpose of this experiment is to see if that lift in transactions will occur when we test the smaller button on FOTF’s video series acquisition donation pages.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The results of this test were completely flat; we didn't see any significant changes in revenue or transactions between the control and treatment. This means that, even though we didn't see any statistically-significant improvement on these metrics, the smaller button didn't hurt our donor conversion or average gift.

Because the smaller button saw a positive lift on Focus on the Family's other donation pages -- and did not harm their acquisition donation page conversion -- it's recommended that the smaller button treatment be rolled out on all of their donation pages.

experiments.title: How Adding a Premium to the Winter Digest Affects ICR
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a premium  for donation page viewers will achieve a higher instant conversion rate  because their donors want physical copies of the digests.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/15/2024 - 05/14/2024
The Hoover Institution is a public policy research center and think tank at Stanford University in California. The user gets a free digital version but this test upsells them on 4 physical versions for $100.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that the treatment version, which offered physical copies of the digest for a $100 donation, resulted in a decrease in Transactions for All Traffic by 13.8% compared to the control version. However, the statistical level of confidence was only 49.9%, which is below the generally accepted threshold of 85% for valid results.
Therefore, based on these findings, it cannot be conclusively stated that the addition of a premium for donation page viewers led to a significant impact on conversion rates. Future experimentation should focus on refining the offer and possibly testing different variations to see if there is a more effective way to encourage donations without negatively impacting transaction rates. It is important to ensure that experiments are run for a sufficiently long period with a large enough sample size to obtain statistically significant results that can be used for making informed decisions.

experiments.title: How the Digest Premium Offer Affected ICR on the Trickle Down Donation Page
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that including a premium for donation page viewers will achieve a higher instant conversion rate because their donors want physical copies of the digests.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/08/2024 - 05/17/2024
The Hoover Institution is a public policy research center and think tank at Stanford University in California. The NextAfter team tried this test on the Hoover Digest Donation Page, and it performed well, so it was implemented on other offers to test if it would improve ICR on more than just the Hoover Digest.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that including a premium offer on the donation page can potentially lead to an increase in transactions for the Hoover Institution. In this case, the treatment group experienced a 34.8% increase in transactions compared to the control group. While this result is promising, the statistical confidence level of 74.0% is below the standard threshold of 85% for validity.

Therefore, while the experiment showed a positive outcome, it cannot be deemed as a conclusive result. In future experimentation, it would be advisable to continue testing the inclusion of premium offers on the donation page to see if the trend of increased transactions holds true with a higher level of statistical confidence. Additionally, it would be beneficial to explore other elements such as different types of premiums or messaging strategies to further optimize the donation page and ultimately improve conversion rates for the Hoover Institution.

experiments.title: How the Digest Premium Offer Affected ICR on the Dueling Populism Offer
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that including a premium for donation page viewers will achieve a higher instant conversion rate because their donors want physical copies of the digests.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/08/2024 - 05/17/2024
The Hoover Institution is a public policy research center and think tank at Stanford University in California. The NextAfter team tried this test on the Hoover Digest Donation Page, and it performed well, so it was implemented on other offers to test if it would improve ICR on more than just the Hoover Digest.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding a premium to the donation page for viewers may not necessarily increase the instant conversion rate. In this case, the treatment actually resulted in a decrease in transactions compared to the control group. However, the statistical level of confidence is only 60.2%, which is below the generally accepted threshold of 85% for results to be considered valid.

Therefore, based on these results, it is not advisable to make any definitive conclusions or changes based on this particular experiment. It is important to conduct further testing with a larger sample size and possibly different variations to truly understand the impact of including a premium on donation page viewers.

In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to continue testing different strategies for increasing conversion rates, while ensuring that the sample size is sufficient and the statistical confidence level is high enough to draw reliable conclusions. It is also essential to consider other factors that may influence donor behavior and tailor experiments accordingly.

experiments.title: How a Humanized Letter Treatment on a Donation Page Impacts Conversion Rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a humanized treatment of the donation page that looks like a letter from a Practice Manager for users on the Mother’s Day campaign donation page will achieve an increase in donor conversion because it humanizes and personalizes the appeal to come from a real person who is working at the centers impacted. .
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 05/31/2024
For Human Coalition’s Mother’s Day campaign, we tested a humanized experience in various channels of the campaign. For the donation page, we hypothesized that updating the intro copy and end of the donation page to look like a letter from Amy, a Practice Manager, would help humanize the experience, personalize the appeal, and as a result improve donor conversion. It’s important to note that aside from the intro and sign-off, no other copy was changed on the donation page.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The humanized treatment in the form of a letter from a Practice Manager on the donation page for the Mother's Day campaign resulted in a 27.8% increase in donations for all traffic. Despite the positive outcome, the statistical level of confidence was only 62.1%, which is below the recommended threshold of 85%. While this humanized treatment showed promise in increasing donor conversion, it is essential to conduct further experiments with a larger sample size to validate the results. Other metrics to observe are revenue and recurring.

Revenue saw slight improvements in average gift but below an 85% level of significance:

Control: $1,442 from 26 Gifts | $55 AVG Gift

Treatment $1,669 from 27 Gifts | $61 AVG Gift

Recurring:

Control: 716 Samples | 2 Recurring Donations | .3% Conversion

Treatment 655 Samples | 4 Recurring Donations | .6% Conv Rat | 118% Relative Difference | 63.9% Significance

experiments.title: How removal of an array in favor of a simplified ask in email impacts donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that simplifying an expanded gift array in email for consistent $26 ask in the copy for Human Coalition housefile will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate because it eliminates decision friction and maintains the consistency of the $26 ask presented in all other parts of the email.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 05/31/2024
For Human Coalition’s Mother’s Day campaign, the main ask presented to the donor was that $26 help reach a mom seeking an abortion. However, in various email the reader would also be presented with an expanded array ask that requested $26, $52, $104, or their best gift in the email copy. Our hypothesis is that by simplifying this array to instead continue focus on the $26 ask would improve donor conversion rate.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that simplifying the gift array in email by consistently focusing on a single ask amount of $26 can potentially lead to an increase in donor conversion rate. In this case, the treatment group saw an 18.5% increase in donations for all traffic. However, the statistical level of confidence was only 39.4%, which means that the results cannot be considered valid for future decision making.

Interestingly, it should also be noted that despite the treatment eliminating the expanded array of asks, ultimately average gift remained similar between the control and treatment.

Control: 112,985 Samples | 17 Donations | .1% Revenue Per Visitor | Avg Gift $78.22

Treatment: 112,131 Samples | 20 Donations | .1% Revenue Per Visitor | -.5 Relative Difference | 53.8% Significance | Avg Gift $62.56

Overall, the results of this experiment indicate that simplifying the ask amount in email communications can have a positive impact on donor conversion rates, but further testing and larger sample size is needed for validity.

experiments.title: Will a plain text email achieve a higher click rate vs a HTML style email
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that sending a plain text email instead of a heavily templatized email for HUC sends will achieve a higher level of engagement because the email will feel like it came from a person instead of a mass marketing email.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/11/2024
For our first set of experiments for Moody Radio we wanted to test into a more personalized email instead of a heavily templated email.
For this we came up with several tests that we wanted to do.
HTML vs. Plain Text
Hyperlink vs. Button
1 CTA vs. 2 CTA
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that sending a plain text email instead of a heavily templatized email for house and HUC sends resulted in a decrease in clicks for all traffic. However, since the statistical level of confidence is below 85%, these results cannot be considered valid.

In future experimentation, it is important to ensure that the statistical confidence level is high enough to draw reliable conclusions. Additionally, it may be beneficial to further investigate the preferences of the target audience to understand what type of email format resonates best with them. This can help in creating more personalized and engaging communication strategies in the future.

experiments.title: How replacing a button in an email for a hyperlink achieved a higher click rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that replacing a button with a raw hyperlink in an email for a huc campaign will achieve a higher click rate because it looks more personal and give the illusion of more trust because it feels like a 1-1 email.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/11/2024
For our first set of experiments for Moody Radio we wanted to test into a more personalized email instead of a heavily templated email.
For this we came up with several tests that we wanted to do.
HTML vs. Plain Text
Hyperlink vs. Button
1 CTA vs. 2 CTA
experiments.keyLearnings:
Key learnings from this experiment include:

- Replacing a button with a raw hyperlink in an email for a HUC campaign resulted in a 4.5% increase in Clicks for All Traffic.

- The treatment group showed a higher click rate, which could indicate that using raw hyperlinks in emails may appear more personal and trustworthy to recipients.

- However, the statistical level of confidence was only 50.8%, which means the results are not considered valid for future decision making.

In future experimentation, it would be important to:

- Conduct further tests with a larger sample size to increase the statistical confidence level.

- Consider testing other variables such as email copy, subject lines, or sender name along with the hyperlink vs. button format to understand the impact of each element on click rates.

- Continue experimenting with personalized elements in emails to see if this leads to higher engagement from recipients.

- Keep track of statistical significance to ensure that results are reliable and can be used for decision making.

experiments.title: How a recurring donation interrupter affects recurring donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using a donation interrupter to ask users to convert their one-time gift to a monthly gift for potential donors will achieve a higher recurring donor conversion rate because   they will be presented with the opportunity to know what impact a monthly gift can have.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/23/2024 - 05/09/2024
The Heritage Foundation has a page on their website where people can join the organization as a member by making a one-time or recurring donation, and they wanted to test how a donation pop-up interrupter would perform to increase the amount of recurring donors they get through new memberships.
On the donation page, users have the option to make a one-time or monthly gift, but if the user chooses one-time and makes a gift under $100, they would receive a pop-up after hitting “Make my gift” asking them to convert their gift to a $10 monthly donation.
The pop-up included value proposition as to what impact a monthly gift will have.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that implementing a donation interrupter to ask users to convert their one-time gift to a monthly gift had no effect on conversion rates for monthly or one-time giving. The treatment group saw a 32% decrease in Transactions - Monthly, but this decrease is only based on 5 recurring transactions and only had a 33% level of confidence. This means that the results cannot be considered statistically significant and should not be used for future decision-making.
Additionally, we saw a 15% lift in one-time giving but with only a 38% level of confidence.
The key takeaways are that the recurring interrupter will most likely not hurt one-time or monthly giving conversion rates, but we do not have enough data to know if it will produce a lift of any kind.
In future experimentation, it is essential to continue testing the effectiveness of the donation interrupter with a larger sample size and over a longer period to determine its true impact on recurring donor conversion rates. Additionally, considering other factors such as user experience, messaging, and timing of the interrupter could provide valuable insights for optimizing donation processes and increasing donor engagement.

experiments.title: How including a gift handle with the corresponding impact described in a fundraising email affects donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that including gift handles with the corresponding impact described for potential donors to Eagle Lake will achieve a higher donor conversion rate because we’re providing clear, tangible examples of how different donation amounts will be used .
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 04/22/2024
The Navigators are running their bi-annual Eagle Lake Campaign to raise money for this kids’ camp that operates every summer. In the kickoff email of this campaign, they wanted to test how including gift handles in the email would affect donor conversion rate. Each gift amount described what that amount would provide for Eagle Lake.
This experiment idea came from their direct mail piece which included “tickets” for people to purchase. Each ticket was for a different amount (same as the email) and described what each level of ticket would provide for the kids at Eagle Lake.
To keep the language consistent between the email and the donation page, the treatment donation page also had the gift handles in the body copy.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that including gift handles with corresponding impact descriptions in a fundraising email may not always lead to an increase in donor conversion rates. In this case, the treatment actually resulted in a decrease of 15.1% in donations for all traffic. However, since the statistical level of confidence is below 85%, these results cannot be considered valid or conclusive.

In future experimentation, it is important to continue testing different strategies and elements in fundraising emails to see what resonates best with donors. This could include testing different gift handle amounts, impact descriptions, or even the placement of this information within the email. It's also important to ensure that experiments are conducted with a sufficient sample size to achieve a higher level of statistical confidence in the results.

Overall, the key takeaway is to not solely rely on the results of one experiment, especially if the statistical confidence level is below the recommended threshold. By continuing to test and iterate on different strategies, we can gather more reliable data to inform decision-making for future fundraising campaigns.

experiments.title: How including a gift array impacted clicks on a direct appeal
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that including gift array options for nondonor email recipients will achieve an increase in clicks to the donation page because there is greater clarity around the giving amounts and easier entry point to the page.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 04/01/2024
During a fall recurring campaign, we wondered if introducing the gift array options for monthly donors would drive additional clicks on an appeal by introducing the decision at an earlier stage in the funnel. We split the non-donor file 50/50 and included a clickable gift array for the treatment group.
experiments.keyLearnings: The inclusion of gift array options for non-donor email recipients did not achieve the expected increase in clicks to the donation page. In fact, the treatment group saw a 13.1% decrease in Clicks for All Traffic compared to the control group. The statistical level of confidence for this experiment was only 44.3%, which is below the generally accepted threshold of 95%. This means that the results cannot be considered valid and should not be used for future decision making. For future testing, we will consider testing different variations of the gift array options, such as the layout, wording, or placement within the email, to see if any of these factors may impact the click-through rates.

experiments.title: How adding a banner to a donation page with a button to accelerate the process impacts donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a button to begin the donation for main donation page visitors will achieve an increase in donor conversion because there will be reduced friction present for visitors who are already motivated to give.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/06/2024 - 03/04/2024
For highly motivated traffic coming to a main donation page from the homepage donate button in the top right corner, we hypothesized that giving them a chance to skip over the copy on the donation form and go straight to the gift frequency and gift array selections without having to read the donation page would reduce friction in the process that we know they initiated by arriving on this page. We had tested into having shorter copy on this page to help get the form above the fold, but wondered how else we could escalate the process and reduce the work involved for visitors here.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding a button to begin the donation process for main donation page visitors can potentially increase donor conversion rates. The treatment group saw a 6.7% increase in transactions compared to the control group. However, the statistical confidence level of 72.2% is below the generally accepted threshold of 85%, meaning that the results may not be reliable and should not be solely relied upon for future decision-making.

In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to further explore ways to reduce friction for motivated visitors on the donation page. This could include streamlining the donation process, providing clearer calls to action, or optimizing the layout of the page to make it easier for visitors to navigate and complete their donation. It is important to continue testing and iterating on different strategies to improve donor conversion rates, while also ensuring that the results are statistically valid before implementing any changes permanently.

experiments.title: How a Monthly Pop-Up on a Main Donation Page Impacts Donor Conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that presenting a monthly popup with a lowered gift amount for donors submitting their one-time donation will achieve an increase in monthly gifts because the popup reinforces the impact of a smaller amount than their original gift made on a consistent basis. .
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/03/2024 - 01/12/2024
To increase monthly gifts on CaringBridge’s main donation page, we hypothesized a pop-up that presents the user with an opportunity to turn their original one-time amount into a lowered monthly gift would increase recurring donations. This pop-up would appear when the user submitted their one-time donation. We hypothesized that by making this conversion easy, suggesting a lower amount than what the user was originally going to donate, and reinforcing the impact of that smaller amount on a consistent basis it would improve conversion rate.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are as follows:

The treatment, which involved presenting a monthly pop-up with a lowered gift amount for donors submitting their one-time donation, resulted in a 25.0% decrease in donations for all traffic. However, the statistical confidence level of 77.2% is below the generally accepted threshold of 85%, so these results cannot be considered valid.

Our hypothesis for why this popup not only didn't increase monthly donations but in fact decreased donations overall is due to the way CaringBridge's form is preset. Monthly is already pre-selected as the default giving option, so to submit a one-time gift a user would have to intentionally click it, fill out the form, and press submit. Targeting that audience, who already has a specific motivation for making a one-time donation, with another popup likely made the experience more disruptive rather than presenting them with something they hadn't already considered.
