In future testing, we will focus our efforts on that initial action — selecting one-time rather than monthly — at the top of the form so that we are meeting the donor at the point of consideration rather than at the end where they've already made a decision.

experiments.title: How adding an image of a thank-you gift impacted donations during a one-day fund drive.
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding an image of a thank-you gift for fund drive donors will achieve a change in conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/07/2024 - 02/08/2024
WUNC is one of North Carolina’s largest NPR stations. Most of their fundraising online comes through fund drives, which they have every other month.
February’s fund drive features some great thank you gifts, which we’ve seen through past testing help motivate donors to give to support WUNC. On a specific day during this drive, WUNC offered a box of chocolates and a WUNC-branded mug as the thank you gift, delivered in time for Valentine’s Day. This was a one-day only offer, so we wanted to test whether adding an image of the gift on the donation page made a difference in conversion rates.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that adding an image of a thank-you gift for fund drive donors resulted in a 13.6% increase in transactions for all traffic. However, the statistical level of confidence was only 33.2%, which is below the generally accepted threshold of 85%. This means that the results cannot be considered valid and should not be used for future decision-making.

In future experimentation, it is important to ensure that a sufficient sample size is used to achieve a higher level of statistical confidence. Additionally, it may be beneficial to run the experiment for a longer period of time to gather more data and improve the reliability of the results. This will help in making more informed decisions based on the outcomes of the experiments conducted.

experiments.title: How does receiving a premium compared to receiving personal recognition for their gift affect clicks?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that personal recognition for monthly partners will achieve an increase in clicks and donor conversion because donors would feel more valued by the organization and the personal touch of their name being added to the group.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/04/2024
For The Navigators February Sustainers Appeal campaign, we decided to test whether a premium or personal recognition for their recurring gift would be more effective.
The control focused on the same premium that had been used throughout the campaign, and the treatment focused on a more personal recognition for their monthly gift. The treatment also promised to send the monthly partner a picture of their name on the window with all of the other monthly partners.
We hypothesized that the treatment would greatly outperform the control due to the personal touch and recognition of becoming a monthly partner because recognition is a motivating factor for the majority of people.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that the treatment, which focused on personal recognition for monthly partners, did not outperform the control, which offered a premium for recurring gifts. The treatment actually saw a drop of 3.7% in clicks at a 21.6% level of confidence. These results are inconclusive; however, we also wanted to look into if this test impact donations rather than clicks.

The control had a total of 13 donations, and the treatment had 8 donations. The results in terms of transactions show a drop of 36.1% in transactions at a 71.3% level of confidence. While transactions also did not validate, it indicates that The Navigators audience did not respond well to recognition for their gift.

Moving forward, it may be beneficial to further test the impact of personal recognition versus premiums for recurring donors, ensuring that the sample size is sufficient to draw valid conclusions. Additionally, considering other variables or elements that could influence donor behavior, such as messaging or incentives, could provide a more comprehensive understanding of what motivates donors to contribute.

experiments.title: How including an image of a premium in an email impacted clicks
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that including an image of the premium for email recipients will achieve an increase in clicks because there is greater clarity about the children’s book and its intended audience.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/04/2024
When offering to send a book as a premium for gifts of a certain amount, we wondered if having an image of the book being offered would have any impact on email engagement by generating interest and curiosity about the contents of the book as well as creating congruency between the email and the donation page, which also had the image of the book displayed at the point of transaction. Since the author also writes books for adults, we wanted to provide clarity at the point of decision-making that this book would be best suited for young readers or any children in the visitors’ lives.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that including an image of the premium in email communications might have a positive impact on engagement and click-through rates. Although the treatment group did see a 7.1% increase in clicks, the statistical confidence level of 64.8% is below the generally accepted threshold of 95%.

In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to continue testing the inclusion of visuals, such as images of premiums, in email communications to gauge their impact on recipient engagement. However, it is important to ensure that the sample size is sufficient to achieve a higher level of statistical confidence in the results. Additionally, incorporating other elements such as A/B testing different visuals or messaging strategies could provide further insights into what resonates best with the audience and drives the desired actions.

experiments.title: How using different images for a content offer acquisition funnel affects email conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using an image of the ebook on the landing page for potential downloaders will achieve a higher email acquisition rate because users will perceive the ebook image as higher value.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/14/2023 - 01/16/2024
The Heritage Foundation is offering the “Guide to the Constitution” digital version as a free gift through paid Facebook ads, email prospecting and website traffic.
Heritage originally tested the concept of the laptop image vs the ebook image in a Dear Reader on daily signal. The results showed that click-through rate on the dear reader was 68.6% higher when using the ebook image vs no image at all. The laptop image produced a 30.1% decrease in clickthrough rate compared to the control.
The offer has always had the laptop image on the landing page, so they decided to test changing the image to the ebook to see if it would increase email acquisition rate on the landing page.
They believed it would because the ebook looks to have more value than just a picture of the offer through a laptop or phone screen.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that changing the image on the landing page from a laptop to an ebook did not result in a higher email acquisition rate. The treatment version actually saw a decrease in Transactions for All Traffic.

Although the results showed a decrease in Transactions, the statistical level of confidence was below 85%, which means that the results cannot be considered valid and should not be used for future decision making.

In future experimentation, it would be important to gather more data and increase the sample size in order to achieve a higher level of statistical confidence. It may also be worth considering other elements on the landing page, such as the layout, color scheme, or messaging, to determine if any of those factors have an impact on the email acquisition rate.

experiments.title: How using different images for a content offer acquisition funnel affects email conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using an image of the ebook on the landing page for potential downloaders will achieve a higher email acquisition rate because users will perceive the ebook image as higher value.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/14/2023 - 01/16/2024
The Heritage Foundation is offering the “Guide to the Constitution” digital version as a free gift through paid Facebook ads, email prospecting and website traffic.
Heritage originally tested the concept of the laptop image vs the ebook image in a Dear Reader on daily signal. The offer has always had the laptop image on the landing page, so they decided to test changing the image to the ebook to see if it would increase email acquisition rate on the landing page.
They believed it would because the ebook looks to have more value than just a picture of the offer through a laptop or phone screen.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that changing the image on the landing page from a laptop to an ebook did not result in a higher email acquisition rate. The treatment version actually saw a decrease in Transactions for All Traffic.

Although the results showed a decrease in Transactions, the statistical level of confidence was below 85%, which means that the results cannot be considered valid and should not be used for future decision making.

It may also be worth considering other elements on the landing page, such as the layout, color scheme, or messaging, to determine if any of those factors have an impact on the email acquisition rate.

experiments.title: How does a humanized sticky bar on a donation page impact conversion rate for a direct appeal?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a humanized sticky bar of the email sender for potential donors will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate because the continuity from the email to the donation page will increase the personal connection and credibility of the sender.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/07/2024 - 02/11/2024
For The Navigators February Sustainers Appeal, we ran a 50/50 split in traffic to the donation page, half of which would see the normal donation page, the other half would see a humanized sticky bar featuring the the email sender on the donation page.
Our hypothesis was that individuals who saw the humanized sticky bar of Scott’s face, the email sender, would be more inclined to give a gift because it creates a more personal experience and increases the credibility of the sender.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding a humanized sticky bar of the email sender for potential donors may not always result in an increase in donor conversion rate. In this case, the treatment with the sticky bar actually saw a decrease in transactions of 14.9% at a 38.2% level of confidence, compared to the control group as well as a slight decrease in revenue at 0.13% with a 46.3% level of confidence.

Moving forward, it is important to design experiments with a larger sample size and longer duration to ensure more reliable results. Additionally, it is important to consider other factors that may have influenced the outcomes, such as the timing of the appeal, the messaging, or the overall design of the donation page. It is also important to note that because there was no CTA on this sticky bar our results may be invalid and should not be used for future decision making.

In future experimentation, it would be wise to continue testing the impact of humanized elements in donation appeals, but also to explore different variables and combinations to determine what resonates best with donors. We could potentially try a different email sender to see if a different person from the organization resonates with The Navigators audience more effectively.

experiments.title: How dual donate buttons impacted donations
experiments.researchQuestion: Will adding a second donate button increase donor conversions?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/28/2022 - 12/23/2022
WUNC is an NPR public news and radio station based in North Carolina.
WUNC has a large number of yearly web traffic but very few become donors. In an effort to convert more web visitors into donors, we decided to test a dual donate button.
Our hypothesis is that by adding a second donate button calling out becoming a monthly donor, we will see an increase in conversions, specifically monthly donors, due to the clarity of the ask.
We tested the dual donate button across the wunc.org site.
experiments.keyLearnings:
For this experiment, we could not validate the response as we only saw a 2.8% decrease in conversions at a 68.8% level of confidence for total donations.

For our recurring donor segment, we saw a 5.6% increase in donations with a confidence level of 34.4% so that segment did not see an impact either.

We are looking into alternate tests to encourage web traffic to convert. Future tests include a sticky donate button, dear readers, and content offered within articles.

experiments.title: How an animated donate button impacts conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Can we increase donor conversion with an animated donation button in the primary navigation?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/02/2021 - 02/19/2021
With the high volume of traffic to the Save the Children website, we wanted to discover new ways to increase traffic to the donation page from the donate button in the primary navigation. To do this, we hypothesized that drawing more attention to the button might increase the number of people who would see the button and ultimately click and give. We set up a treatment version of the donate button that was animated. The animation was subtle with a motion going across the button coming from a heart icon. We tested this button against the control.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The animated donation button ran for three weeks. While the goal was to increase donor conversion from the animated donate button - which we weren't able to do with valid results, we did see a significant lift in clicks from it (11%). Essentially, more people clicked to the page but only a minimal amount more donated.

When analyzing the donor conversion, we saw a higher conversion rate from site visitors if they landed on the donation page and saw the animated button versus landing on the homepage.

We also saw the greatest increase in conversion from mobile users. This is most likely due to the button being much more visible on the small device.

After running this experiment and seeing that it was likely not going to validate, we came away with two questions:
Will the subtle animation be a novelty feature that will fade with time?
 	Can we present the subtle animation to people when they are at a point in their experience when they are more likely to convert? This question leads us to our next experiment for this feature where we will trigger the animated donate button at a higher motivation point in their conversion pathway so we can not only get more people to the donation page, but get more people to give.

experiments.title: How dual donate buttons impacted donations
experiments.researchQuestion: Will adding a second donate button increase donor conversions?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/28/2022 - 12/23/2022
WUNC is an NPR public news and radio station based in North Carolina.
WUNC has a large number of yearly web traffic but very few become donors. In an effort to convert more web visitors into donors, we decided to test a dual donate button.
Our hypothesis is that by adding a second donate button calling out becoming a monthly donor, we will see an increase in conversions, specifically monthly donors, due to the clarity of the ask.
We tested the dual donate button across the wunc.org site.
experiments.keyLearnings:
For this experiment, we could not validate the response as we only saw a 2.8% decrease in conversions at a 68.8% level of confidence for total donations.

For our recurring donor segment, we saw a 5.6% increase in donations with a confidence level of 34.4% so that segment did not see an impact either.

We are looking into alternate tests to encourage web traffic to convert. Future tests include a sticky donate button, dear readers, and content offered within articles.

experiments.title: How using an image of the content offer in a housefile email affects donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding an image to the email for potential downloaders will achieve a higher donor conversion rate because users can see the value of the offer at the beginning of the funnel.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/23/2024
The Navigators sent a free content offer to their housefile list through email. The content offer was a free 12-month calendar and users had to provide their name, email, and home address on the landing page to receive a physical copy. Following the landing page, they were redirected to an instant donation page where they were asked to make a gift to support the Navigators work.
Within the email, they wanted to test how adding an image of the calendar would affect click-through rate from the email to the landing page. They also wanted to know if adding the image to the email would affect donor conversion rate since users can see the value of the offer at the beginning of the funnel.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding an image to the email did not result in a higher donor conversion rate. In fact, the treatment group saw a decrease in donations compared to the control group. However, it is important to note that the statistical level of confidence is below the threshold of 85%, which means these results should not be considered valid.

In future experimentation, it would be important to conduct a larger sample size or make adjustments to the experiment design to increase the statistical power and ensure valid results. The Navigators should also consider testing other variables or elements of the email and landing page to identify potential factors that may influence the donor conversion rate. It is important to continue conducting experiments and collecting data to make informed decisions and optimize the effectiveness of email campaigns in the future.

experiments.title: How using different images for a content offer acquisition funnel affects email conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using an image of the ebook on the landing page for potential downloaders will achieve a higher email acquisition rate because users will perceive the ebook image as higher value.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/14/2023 - 01/16/2024
The Heritage Foundation is offering the “Guide to the Constitution” digital version as a free gift through paid Facebook ads, email prospecting and website traffic.
Heritage originally tested the concept of the laptop image vs the ebook image in a Dear Reader on daily signal. The results showed that click-through rate on the dear reader was 68.6% higher when using the ebook image vs no image at all. The laptop image produced a 30.1% decrease in clickthrough rate compared to the control.
The offer has always had the laptop image on the landing page, so they decided to test changing the image to the ebook to see if it would increase email acquisition rate on the landing page.
They believed it would because the ebook looks to have more value than just a picture of the offer through a laptop or phone screen.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that changing the image on the landing page from a laptop to an ebook did not result in a higher email acquisition rate. The treatment version actually saw a decrease in Transactions for All Traffic.

Although the results showed a decrease in Transactions, the statistical level of confidence was below 85%, which means that the results cannot be considered valid and should not be used for future decision making.

In future experimentation, it would be important to gather more data and increase the sample size in order to achieve a higher level of statistical confidence. It may also be worth considering other elements on the landing page, such as the layout, color scheme, or messaging, to determine if any of those factors have an impact on the email acquisition rate.

experiments.title: How using a campaign subject’s story to increase relevancy will affect donor conversion rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that telling the story of Lottie Moon for donation page visitors will achieve an increase in conversion rate because it will give relevancy to the title of the campaign for people who might not be aware.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/18/2023 - 12/27/2023
The IMB was running their annual Lottie Moon Christmas Offering campaign. They knew that they had been growing their new donor file over the past few years, and were curious how they should position “Lottie Moon” (who was a former missionary).  On the campaign donation page, they decided to test their standard value proposition against one that told the story of Lottie Moon, in order to give more relevancy to the name of the campaign.
The control page identified the need, and the treatment page prefaced the need with the story of Lottie Moon. They split the traffic to determine a winner.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that telling the story of Lottie Moon on the donation page resulted in an increase in conversions. The treatment group, which received the Lottie Moon story, had a 27.2% higher conversion rate compared to the control group.
However, it is important to note that the statistical level of confidence for this result was only 76.5%, which is below the recommended threshold of 85%. This means that the results cannot be considered valid and should not be used for future decision making.
In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to collect more data and run the experiment for a longer period of time to increase the statistical level of confidence. Additionally, it may be helpful to make adjustments to the experiment design or implementation to minimize any potential confounding factors or biases.
Overall, while the initial results are promising, it is necessary to conduct further testing to fully validate the impact of telling the story of Lottie Moon on the donation page.

experiments.title: How removing a premium and specific ask amount from a donation page impacted donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that removing the premium of a pledge coin  for visitors will achieve an increase in donor conversion because it will remove unnecessary friction from the donation process.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/28/2023 - 01/04/2024
In an effort to increase our instant donor conversion rate for ACI, we wanted to test removing the premium of their 2023 Pledge Coin which required a $100 donation. We felt that the high ask amount and the premium was causing unnecessary friction for the user, causing them to give lower amounts or not at all.
Therefore, our hypothesis was that be removing the premium all together, potential donors would have a clearer idea of what was being asked of them due to the clarity now being provided.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that removing the premium of the pledge coin for visitors resulted in a 11.9% increase in the number of transactions. However, due to the statistical level of confidence being below 95%, these results cannot be considered valid.
The treatment saw a negative impact in revenue at a 51.7% drop with a 16.4% level of confidence, also not considered valid.
In future experimentation, it is important to conduct experiments for a longer duration to collect more data and increase the statistical confidence level. While you should not conduct future decision making off of these results, we can observe how donors perceived this change. That is, more people were willing to give when there was no premium, but the average gift fell significantly. Therefore, it would be crucial that you consider these results from a strategic standpoint of focusing on donors or donations.
Overall, this experiment highlights the potential of removing unnecessary friction in the donation process to improve donor conversion. It is recommended to continue exploring different strategies to simplify the donation experience and increase revenue for the American Cornerstone Institute.

experiments.title: How removing a signer’s image in an email footer signature impacted clicks

rows.name: Control - With Image in Footer Signature
Treatment - No Image in Footer Signature

experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that removing a photo from the email signature for housefile recipients will achieve an increase in clickthrough rate because the email will appear more human and personalized.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/06/2023 - 12/14/2023
When sending a course registration email, we wondered if the signature image was causing the email template to appear more like marketing and less like a personalized email coming from a real human. We split the file 50/50 and tested each version to measure engagement.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that removing a photo from the email signature for housefile recipients resulted in a 7.5% increase in clickthrough rate. However, the statistical level of confidence was only 79.8%, which means the results cannot be considered valid.
We will want to re-test this concept in future experiments to fully measure the impact of removing the photo from the email signature.

experiments.title: How a large image on a landing page affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that removing the hero image for Holy Lands donation page visitors will achieve an increase in conversion rate because it will reduce visual friction on the page.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 10/31/2023
The IMB was running a campaign to raise funds for mission work in the Holy Lands during the Israel-Hamas conflict in 2023. Motivation to give was high for the campaign, reflected in high conversion rates even from direct acquisition.
When looking for opportunities to optimize conversion, the team wondered if the image on the landing page — although striking — might actually be pushing the rest of the content too far down on the page. It was large on the desktop version, and pushed all the copy, value proposition, and donation form below the initial “fold”.
They quickly mocked up an experiment that removed the image and launched an A/B test to determine a winner.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that removing the hero image from the Holy Lands donation page resulted in a 9.5% increase in donations. However, the statistical level of confidence is below the recommended threshold of 85%, so these results cannot be considered valid and should not be used for future decision making.
Based on this, it is important to recognize that the experiment did show a potential impact on conversions by removing the hero image. This suggests that reducing visual friction on the page could be beneficial in improving conversion rates.
To further validate these findings and make informed decisions, future experimentation should be conducted with a larger sample size and higher statistical level of confidence. This will help ensure the accuracy and reliability of the results.
Additionally, it is recommended to explore other potential optimizations on the landing page to further improve conversion rates. This could involve testing different layout designs, call-to-action placements, or content positioning to find the most effective approach for encouraging donations.
Overall, the learnings from this experiment highlight the importance of conducting rigorous and statistically valid experiments to make informed decisions in future optimization efforts.

experiments.title: How adding a gift array in the body of an email appeal impacted clicks
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that introducing a sample gift array for a direct email appeal will achieve an increase in engagement because there will be additional cognitive momentum to continue the process to the next step.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/27/2023 - 11/28/2023
In a high-urgency campaign, we typically keep the decisions isolated per each step. In the email step, we help the reader decide “Do I want to click?” and then at the donation page step we help the reader decide “How much do I want to give?” In one email, we wanted to test if adding gift array options at the email level would help more people begin thinking about both questions at once. The treatment version added a gift array of buttons, letting the reader know they could click one of the buttons to choose their donation amount and begin the process of making a monthly gift.
experiments.keyLearnings: After splitting the email between the control and the treatment, we observed a 1.2% lift in clicks at 9.4% level of confidence for the treatment version which is not statistically significant so the experiment results are not valid. The engagement remained flat, even when we added buttons with gift array amounts in the body of the email. We will want to look downstream and see if there is any significant difference in completed donations from the treatment group that would lead to more meaningful results to see if moving the gift array into the body copy helped produce more motivated visits from email traffic who had already decided what amount they would be interested in giving and continue the cognitive momentum in the process when they arrive on the donation page.

experiments.title: How adding a second link in an email impacted conversion for nondonors
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by adding a second link for email subscribers will achieve a higher conversion rate because there are more opportunities to click . because there are more opportunities to click .
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/31/2023 - 11/03/2023
Pathway to Victory is a broadcast ministry of First Baptist Dallas.
This is an experiment we ran for PTV a couple of years ago, but we wanted to test again to see if the results still held.
We tested a second link in the email to see if it would increase engagement and down funnel donations.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are as follows:

1. The addition of a second link in the email did not result in a higher conversion rate. In fact, the treatment group saw a decrease in clicks for all traffic, implying that subscribers did not engage more with the email when presented with an additional link.

2. The control group, which did not have the second link, outperformed the treatment group in terms of clicks. Although this result is not statistically significant, it suggests that the original email format may be more effective in driving engagement.

3 Of those who received the treatment only 37% clicked the first link and 63% clicked the second link.

4 Donations saw a 0.9% increase with a level of confidence 4.5%.

 

Based on these results, the presence of a second link did not impact engagement or donations.

experiments.title: How adding an image of the book impacted revenue for nondonors
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by including an image of the book for nondonors will achieve a higher revenue impact because of the visual appeal of the book.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 10/30/2023
Pathway to Victory is a broadcast ministry of First Dallas.
Each month PTV promotes a new premium for the month. For this month we decided to retest using an image of the book to see if it would increase revenue.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that including an image of the book for non-donors did not achieve a higher revenue impact. In fact, the treatment group saw a slight decrease in revenue compared to the control group. However, since the statistical level of confidence is below 85%, these results cannot be considered valid.

In future experimentation, it is important to ensure that the sample size is sufficient to achieve a higher level of statistical confidence. Additionally, it may be helpful to further explore the reasons behind the decrease in revenue for the treatment group. This could involve conducting qualitative research or gathering feedback from participants to better understand their preferences and reactions to the image of the book.

Overall, the learnings from this experiment suggest that including an image of the book may not be the most effective strategy for increasing revenue. It would be beneficial to consider other potential variables or elements to test in future experiments in order to optimize revenue impact.

experiments.title: How a recurring tab impacted conversions
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by adding a monthly option for web donors will achieve higher conversion rate because there is additional give options and clarity in the type of gift.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/10/2024 - 01/26/2024
Pathway to Victory is the broadcast ministry of First Dallas.
PTV’s offers are primarily various books and premiums with donations. Our January emphasis was to increase the number of recurring donors. With this in mind, we decided to try adding a recurring tab to normal offers to see if we could passively acquire recurring donors by just having the option available.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Note: treatment generated 0 recurring gifts
The key learning from this experiment is that adding a monthly option for web donors did not achieve a higher conversion rate. In fact, the treatment group saw a decrease in transactions compared to the control group. However, it is important to note that the statistical level of confidence is below 85%, meaning the results are not considered valid and should not be used for future decision-making.
Based on this, it is necessary to conduct further experiments with a larger sample size or different variations to determine the effectiveness of adding a recurring tab for web donors. It is also important to consider other factors that may have influenced the results, such as the timing of the experiment or the specific offer being presented.
Future experimentation should focus on gathering more data and conducting rigorous A/B testing to determine the impact of different variables on conversion rates. It may also be beneficial to analyze other aspects of the donor journey, such as the effectiveness of different messaging or marketing channels, to gain a deeper understanding of donor behavior and preferences. Additionally, seeking feedback from donors and analyzing their motivations and decision-making processes can provide valuable insights for future experiments.

experiments.title: How the presentation of a digital resource on a landing page impacts email sign ups
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that presenting the resource in a digital form for landing page viewers will achieve an increased email sign up rate because they have clarity in the way they will be able to use the digital resource. .
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/08/2023 - 09/21/2023
Dallas Theological Seminary has participated in North Texas Giving Day each year. From previous testing, they know that priming their housefile with a content offer has historically lead to increased donors and revenue. This year, they wanted to test if presenting the eBook in a different method on the landing page would impact email sign ups.
Historically with eBooks, they have created an image that represents the eBook as a 3D eBook preview—more like an actual book. They decided to test this against a mock-up of the eBook on an iPad.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that presenting the resource in a digital form on the landing page resulted in a slight increase in email signups compared to the traditional 3D eBook preview. However, it is important to note that the statistical level of confidence is only 40.1%, which is below the acceptable threshold of 85%.
Based on these results, it is not advisable to make any definitive conclusions or decisions regarding the effectiveness of presenting the eBook in a digital form. The results of this experiment should not be used for future decision-making.
In order to obtain more reliable and valid results, it is recommended to conduct further testing with a larger sample size and a higher statistical level of confidence. By doing so, the nonprofit can gather more accurate and conclusive data to determine the most effective method of presenting the eBook on the landing page.
DTS will be testing this again during Calendar Year End on their Christmas Devotional, which has historically gotten a significantly higher rate of traffic and should be able to give us a better sample size.

experiments.title: How removing the main menu navigation bar from a donation page effected conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that removing the main navigation menu from the page for potential donors will achieve a higher conversion rate because this would reduce decision friction so the donor motivation would not break by having too many decisions to make.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/20/2023 - 07/02/2023
This ministry client offers different ministries that a donor can choose to give towards. In this experiment, this donation page contributes to providing biblical resources to international prisoners. We were interested in testing decision friction on the donation page. We believed that removing the main navigation menu at the top of the page could reduce decision friction. Decision friction gives the donor too many options to consider which breaks the train of thought they had for giving. This is something we wanted to reduce and could potentially lead to a higher conversion rate.
Between the control and treatment donation page, the only difference was that the control page had the menu navigation whereas the treatment page did not. This test ran as a split traffic 50/50 A/B test.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that removing the main navigation menu from the donation page did not lead to an increase in conversions. In fact, the treatment page saw a decrease in donations compared to the control page. However, the statistical confidence level of 23.3% is below the acceptable threshold of 85%, so these results did not validate.

Based on these findings, the presence of the menu may not have been a significant source of decision friction for donors, and removing it may have actually hindered their experience on the page.

For future experimentation, we could focus on other elements of the donation page that could be optimized to increase conversions. This could include testing different calls-to-action or simplicity the donation page copy to enhance the persuasiveness of the page. It is important to conduct further experiments with larger sample sizes and stricter statistical confidence thresholds to obtain valid and reliable results.

experiments.title: How a sticky bar with a daily donor goal countdown impacts donor conversion on a high urgency campaign donation page
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a sticky bar with a daily donor goal countdown on the donation page will increase donor conversion during a high urgency campaign
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/01/2023 - 05/20/2023
During FamilyLife’s Spring Recurring Campaign, they rely on a daily donor countdown in sticky bars and website calls to action to increase urgency and drive traffic to their donation page. We hypothesized that reinforcing this countdown on the page itself would add urgency and increase donor conversion.
The message would indicate how many new monthly donors were still needed before midnight that night.  When the number was reached, it would change to say, “We still need new monthly partners before 11:59pm to be here for you and other families in need.”
We split traffic evenly between both versions of the page and ran it for almost three weeks.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding a sticky bar with a daily donor goal countdown on the donation page did not increase donor conversion during the high urgency campaign. The treatment group actually saw a decrease in FamilyLife - Recurring Donations compared to the control group.
While we could not validate this decrease, it was a sharp enough decline in conversion that we ended the experiment early so we wouldn't harm donor conversion during the critical campaign. It is possible that the message was more of a distraction than provided additional urgency to the page.

experiments.title: How does personalizing copy for new names impact donor conversion rates during a match for Pathway to Victory?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that referencing the way someone came onto the file for new names will achieve a change in donor conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/01/2023 - 07/04/2023
Pathway to Victory (PTV) is the international ministry for First Baptist Dallas. They have 2 major match campaigns throughout the year-one during December, the other in June.
Part of the overall strategy for PTV is to acquire new names onto their file, both from new donors as well as people that sign up for some of their acquisition offers. New names tend to have a very low instant conversion rate; for this match campaign, we wanted to test whether adding language in the body of the email that referenced how they came onto the file would impact conversion rates.
This test ran across 4 different emails.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that referencing the way someone came onto the file for new names may have an impact on donor conversion rates. The treatment group, which included language referencing how they raised their hand to seek spiritual help, saw an 8.8% increase in donations compared to the control group.
However, it is important to note that the statistical confidence level of the results is only 27.8%, which is below the desired threshold of 95%. This means that the results cannot be considered valid and should not be used for future decision making.
In future experiments, placement of the copy, as well referencing the name of the offer they signed up for, will be an interesting test.

experiments.title: How an animated lock on a donation page affects giving
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that reinforcing security on the donation page for prospective tribute donors will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate  because there is a perceived increase in security and reduces anxiety for the prospective donor. because  there is a perceived increase in security and reduces anxiety for the prospective donor.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/23/2023 - 07/02/2023
CaringBridge already had an existing image of a security lock on their tribute donation pages.  We wanted to reinforce the perception of security even more by drawing attention to this part of the giving form. We made the lock an “animated” lock where it opened/closed every few seconds and we additional copy to remind the prospective donor that they are making a secure donation.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This experiment resulted in a 4.9% increase in donor conversion rate with a level of confidence of 71%.
Reinforcing the perception of security on the donation page can lead to an increase in donor conversion rate. An animated lock and additional copy reminding the prospective donor about the secure donation process can help reduce anxiety and increase donations. It's likely that because the security lock already existed on the page, security was already sufficiently highlighted for the end user.

experiments.title: How a humanized sticky bar on a campaign donation page affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a humanized sticky bar to a campaign donation page for users who are familiar with this organization will achieve a higher donor conversion rate because Jake is a familiar face to these subscribers which will increase the humanization of the donation ask.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/13/2023 - 07/01/2023
This ministry client was running a campaign for their fiscal year-end. We’ve seen humanized sticky bars increase donor conversion for other organizations when used for high-urgency campaigns, so we decided to test this on their main campaign donation page which received traffic from their housefile list and Facebook ads.
The sticky bar included a message from Jake Hennes, someone who the file is very familiar with and who they receive cultivation emails from often. We wanted the urgent, important message to come from someone they recognize. Jake is also the one who sent a majority of the campaign emails.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are as follows:

1. The addition of a humanized sticky bar featuring a familiar face can significantly impact donor conversion rates, especially for users who are already familiar with the organization.

2. The treatment resulted in a 25.6% increase in donations for all traffic, suggesting that the humanization of the donation ask through the use of a familiar face resonates positively with donors.

3. Female visitors, in particular, showed a significant increase in donations, with a whopping 956% increase and a 95% level of confidence. This suggests that the humanized sticky bar was especially effective in engaging and persuading female donors.

4. While the results of the experiment showed promising improvements in donor conversion rates, it is important to note that the statistical level of confidence was below the recommended threshold of 85%, but due to the volume of gifts and difference in donor conversion, we believe this concept should be used in future campaigns.

experiments.title: How a Radical Redesign Impacted Staff Donation Pages
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that re-positioning the donation page and better highlighting one-time vs monthly gifts for staff donors will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate because moving the gift array to the bottom allows the user to read the copy and see the value first before being given an opportunity to give. .
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/11/2023 - 04/06/2023
The Navigators’ saw tremendous success with a radical redesign of their instant donation pages that implemented a tabbed form and moved the gift array to the bottom of the page instead of the top. We hypothesized that creating the same treatment for Staff Donation Pages would result in a similar lift to conversion rate.
experiments.keyLearnings: Ultimately, we saw neither a valid increase nor a decrease in this experiment. The relative difference in conversion rate was only 2.8% with a low level of confidence at 27.7%. A learning here is that simply because a treatment worked on one page doesn't mean that it will generate the same results on another. The minimal difference also urges consideration on how this treatment was being applied on a very different page to an audience with potentially very difference motivations than in the original test. For Staff Donation Pages, users are more likely to already know in advance who they'd want to support. It's possible that because they are coming to the page with an intentional motivation of who they are or are not giving to, this treatment did very little to affect this audience's decision.

experiments.title: How an animated lock impacted donations
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that an animated lock  for donation page visitors will achieve a higher donor conversion rate because the lock is eye catching and increases perceived security.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/01/2023 - 07/03/2023
Pathway to Victory is a broadcast ministry of First Baptist Dallas. Through radio, television, digital resources, and books, Dr. Robert Jeffress delivers bold, biblical truth to equip the saints in piercing a dark culture.
In an effort to increase donations, and therefore revenue, we decided to test an animated lock on the donation page for the America is a Christian Nation donation pages.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Based on the A/B experiment conducted on the America is a Christian Nation donation pages, the key learnings are as follows:
1. Animated lock impact: The treatment group, which had an animated lock on the donation page, showed a 2.5% increase in revenue for all traffic. This indicates that the animated lock had a positive effect on donor conversion rates. However, the statistical level of confidence of 58.5% is below the recommended threshold of 85%, rendering these results invalid.
2. Audience segmentation: When the results were further analyzed based on visitor types, returning visitors showed a significant 720% increase in revenue with a 96% level of confidence, and other visitors had a 355% increase in revenue with a 96% level of confidence. This suggests that the impact of the animated lock was more pronounced for repeat visitors and other visitor segments.
Based on these findings, it is clear that there is potential for using an animated lock on the donation pages to increase donor conversion rates. However, to draw stronger conclusions and make informed decisions for future experimentation, the statistical validity of the results needs to improve by achieving a higher level of confidence.

experiments.title: How adding a humanized sticky bar to a donation page impacts donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a humanized sticky bar for donation page visitors will achieve a higher donor conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/15/2023 - 07/03/2023
TPPF’s Keep Texas Texan petition is producing a low instant donor conversion rate after users sign the petition. In an effort to increase donor conversion rate on the donation page, we decided to run a test where we would would humanize the experience for the prospective donor. So, we added a humanized sticky bar with their CEO’s picture and a message directing the reader’s attention to the donation opportunity below and reminding them of the impact that they can make. We believe this message coming from a person and prominent figure in the organization will lead to more donations.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding a humanized sticky bar with the picture of a prominent member of the organization and a personal message from that person can significantly increase the donor conversion rate - the power of personal touch. The treatment version of the donation page, which included the humanized sticky bar, saw a 164.8% increase in donations compared to the control version.

However, the statistical level of confidence is only 63.5% which is below the recommended threshold of 85%. This means that the results cannot be considered valid and should not be used for future decision making. This experiment had to end after 15 days, preventing the necessary data to reach validation. It also recorded only 4 total donations between the two variants, which is below the recommended 50 transactions to reach confident results.

experiments.title: How a Security Callout on a Landing Page Impacts Sign-Up Rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a security callout at the bottom of the form for users on the Course on Prayer landing page will achieve an increase in email acquisitions because it removes anxiety around giving up an email. because  it removes anxiety around giving up an email.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/25/2023 - 06/25/2023
The IMB’s Course on Prayer offer was experiencing a low sign-up rate on the landing page. We hypothesized that eliminating anxiety with a simple security callout at the bottom of the form would reassure users by clarifying what their information will be used for. We believe a reminder to the user of why their email is being requested (to receive their course lessons) will increase the number of emails acquired.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The results of this experiment should not be considered valid due to the statistical level of confidence being below 85%. Ultimately, a very small lift with a low level of confidence shows that this treatment did not cause much of a difference for users arriving at this landing page. More testing is required, but this also prompts us to explore what other factors could be causing anxiety/friction to email acquisition.
In future experimentation, it is crucial to have a larger sample size and a higher level of confidence before making any decisions based on the results. It may also be beneficial to test alternative forms of security callouts or messaging to determine which resonates best with visitors.

experiments.title: How a donation exit intent popup on an instant donation page impacted donations
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that an exit intent popup prompting a return to the donation page will increase donations because potential donors will be brought back in by the ask and simplified value prop, and ultimately convert.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/15/2023 - 06/17/2023
TPPF’s monthly donor conversion funnel is producing a low instant conversion rate. In an effort to increase conversion, we’ve implemented an exit intent popup that appears when someone tries to exit the page.
The popup will hit donation page visitors with one more ask with condensed value proposition copy in hopes that it will convince them to return to the donation page and complete a donation.
This approach could be effective in a number of ways:
It’s unconventional and it literally “pops out” to users
The popup will catch all users that try to exit, but could be especially impactful to the users that would bounce from the page immediately without reading the value proposition copy and realizing the donation ask
Perhaps the exit intent popup will help make the mind up of anyone that is on the fence about donating
Prompting visitors to second guess their decision to leave the page could lead some to return and convert – but will it be enough to produce a valid lift in conversion?
experiments.keyLearnings:
The experiment did not produce the results we had hoped for. In fact, it only produced eight conversions in total and they were split between the control and the treatment (exit intent popup). Ultimately, the treatment had a 1.9% lift with only a 2.12% level of confidence.
This approach is worth testing again, but on donation funnel that has ample traffic and overall conversions. We simply don't have enough data from this experiment to prove any effectiveness or lack of effectiveness.

experiments.title: How adding a relevant image to an instant donation page impacted donations
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that implementing a relevant image on an instant donation page for potential donors will achieve an increase in donations because potential donors will appreciate the image and gain inspiration from it leading them to make a gift to receive the book.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/04/2023 - 06/03/2023
In an effort to boost instant donor conversion rate through ACI’s Red, White, and Blue direct ask funnel, we want to test adding a relevant image to the donation page body section. The image is of Dr. Ben Carson reading the Red, White, and Blue: Our Flag Matters to Me and You book that he wrote to an audience of American children. We selected this image because it encompasses his image and likeness, his influence and care towards educating and engaging the American youth, and the book he is reading is the book someone can make a donation to receive on this donation page.
We believe that implementing this warm, inspiring, and relatable image as potential donors read through the value proposition will encourage more people to give.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The addition of the image within the value proposition copy did not provide a boost in donor conversion like we hypothesized it would. In fact, the treatment led to a 14% drop in donor conversion with a 68.2% level of confidence. We saw a steeper 22% decrease in revenue with a nearly valid 91% level of confidence.
This is our second attempt at adding an image of Dr. Carson and the Red, White, and Blue book and both attempts led to a decrease in donations. It seems our audience does not respond well to images, or perhaps disruptions, as they are reading the value proposition. They may arrive at the page already motivated and the image could simply get in their way as they're working their way to the donation form. They may arrive at the page and are turned off by the image, as they may associate images with ads or blogs, or something that may turn them off when they're online.
It may be time to transition away from image implementation and try experimenting with other elements of the page.

experiments.title: How adding a goal thermometer to a campaign donation page affects donor conversion

rows.name: Campaign Focused Donation Page - With Thermometer

experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a goal thermometer to a campaign donation page for Working Preacher subscribers will achieve a higher donor conversion rate because the goal thermometer on a donation page leverages psychological factors such as visual progress, social proof, competitiveness, accountability, and psychological commitment and engages potential donors on an emotional level and provides them with a compelling reason to contribute to the cause.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/01/2023 - 05/26/2023
Luther Seminary is running its bi-annual Working Preacher campaign. They wanted to test whether adding a goal thermometer to the donation page would increase donor conversion.
We added the thermometer to the treatment page from the start of the campaign and showed it to 50% of traffic. Users came to this donation page through email.
experiments.keyLearnings:
We found that adding a goal thermometer to a donation page for Working Preacher subscribers resulted in a 4.6% increase in donations with a 42.1% level of confidence. While the experiment suggests that adding a goal thermometer may be effective in increasing donations, further testing is needed to confirm this.

We believe this did not validate as an increase in donor conversion because the Working Preacher subscribers are already comfortable and emotionally driven to support Working Preacher. Therefore they did not need the extra push that the thermometer provided.

experiments.title: How adding a relevant image highlighting an authority figure to an instant donation page impacted donations
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a relevant image highlighting an authority figure to an instant donation page for potential donors will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate because seeing the authority figure in action and in a natural, relatable manner will make them want to support the organization and receive the premium offer.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/06/2023 - 04/29/2023
ACI’s Red, White, and Blue book instant donation page is producing a low instant donor conversion rate. This is a direct ask funnel where users go directly from an ad to the donation page, where they can receive this book when making a donation of a certain amount. We wondered if implementing an image of the author of the book and the face of the ACI organization – Dr. Ben Carson – would help improve donor conversion rate. The book is written for children and the image shows Dr. Carson signing the book for a young kid. Will the relevance of the image and the inclusion of Dr. Carson make potential donors appreciate the opportunity more and convince them to make a donation to receive the book?
Let’s find out.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding an image highlighting an authority figure to an instant donation page may not always result in an increase in donor conversion rate. The treatment (instant donation page WITH the image) resulted in a 7.5% decrease in donations with a 42.8% level of confidence.
Revenue did however produce a 33.7% increase with a 90.5% level of confidence. Additionally, new visitors produced an 86.4% increase in donations with a 78.% LOC compared to returning visitors producing a 5% decrease in donations.
The overall results tell us that there is potential with this concept. Overall donations did not increase like we expected, but revenue did and so did donations among new visitors. Potential deterrents could be the treatment page layout: The layout includes two images, both of Dr. Carson. The images split up the page copy and could really disrupt the flow of value proposition copy. We may want to try using only one image on the page or right aligning the book image in the main body to avoid disrupting the reader's flow.
And perhaps this specific image is not our most effective. We may want try implementing a different image but with the same intention. It may be beneficial to test different variations of images to see which resonates best with potential donors.

experiments.title: How does a stickybar with a countdown affect sponsorship acquisition during a campaign?
experiments.researchQuestion: A countdown that increases urgency and expands on the value prop will increase sponsorships acquired.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/10/2020 - 12/31/2020
During their calendar year end campaign to acquire sponsors, we tested a sticky bar with a countdown to see if it would increase sponsorships. The control had no sticky bar. The test counted down both days left to acquire sponsors and the children left who needed sponsors. It was placed on sponsorship pages to increase motivation and reinforce value proposition.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This experiment did not validate due to sample size that was too low and not enough of a disparity between control and treatment. Our hypothesis is that this did not validate for two reasons:
The countdown clock did not create enough urgency or movement.
 	The high motivation during calendar year end typically decreases the effectiveness of a test, because those receiving the control are typically more motivated to give.
Organic Search visitors had a 481.1% increase in child sponsored with a 93% level of confidence.
 	Email visitors had a 10.5% increase in child sponsored with an 87% level of confidence.

experiments.title: Will adding a sponsorship countdown slider increase donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that if we add a count down clock to the slide out for potential donors it will achieve a greater response rate and higher conversions because we are evoking a sense of urgency.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/10/2022 - 07/07/2022
We decided to test a slide out on the main website to determine if adding a countdown clock vs. our original it would increase the conversion rate with our audience.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Notable Results & Recommendations:

• Test stopped due to lack of significant differences in donor conversion and RPV.

• Donor conversion was slightly higher for the variant (+7%) at a non-statistically valid LoC (48%).

• Revenue per visitor was slightly higher for the variant (+5%) at a non-statistically valid LoC (61%).

• We recommend not changing the sponsorship slider to include a countdown clock.

experiments.title: How addressing the felt need of the ebook recipient impacts account creations
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using language that addressed the felt need for ebook downloaders will achieve an increase in site creation rate because the “why” of a site creation would be established before being asked to create one, thus reducing friction.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/15/2023 - 04/09/2023
In oder to increase the conversion rate for site creations, we ran a test where we added language that addressed the felt need of the ebook recipient.
This test was ran on an ebook funnel where the confirmation page for their ebook had a call to action to create a site with CaringBridge. The control for this experiment invited the individual to “start a CaringBridge site” and was an explicit as to why someone would need a CaringBridge site. We believed that changing the language to address the felt need would decrease friction the person would experience in being asked to create a site right away.
This was tested on one ebook funnel and traffic was primarily driven by paid Facebook ads.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment version of the call to action saw a 7.6% increase in website creations for all traffic with a 21.0% statistical level of confidence. However, since the statistical level of confidence is below 85%, these results cannot be considered valid for future decision making. The results indicate that changing the language to address the felt need had little to no impact on the site creation rate. It is likely that the change in copy was not significant enough to sway the recipient to take action.
For future experiments, it is recommended to continue testing language that addresses the felt need of the audience for increased website creations. Perhaps a complete rewrite may be more impactful.

experiments.title: How an exit-intent popup affects overall donation rates on a donation page during a recurring-giving campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that showing an exit-intent popup with a one-time ask for traffic attempting to exit a recurring-giving donation page will achieve higher overall donation rates by presenting a lower-commitment offer to leverage the reject and retreat principle of persuasion.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/07/2023 - 03/17/2023
During a high-urgency campaign focused on acquiring new monthly donors, we wanted to know how an exit-intent popup would affect overall donation rates.
As the name applies, the exit-intent popup would populate only as a prospect attempted to leave the donation page without completing a donation. The specifics of the popup were designed to leverage the persuasion principle of reject and retreat. This principle is based on the idea of reciprocity and states that when someone is presented with a perceived large commitment and rejects the offer, they may be more likely to accept a subsequent offer if it has a perceived lower level of associated commitment—due to the fact that the prospect will be motivated to reciprocate the retreat by accepting the smaller offer.
In this case, the exit-intent popup would provide prospects who are attempting to leave the page without becoming a recurring donor the opportunity to still make an impact by instead giving a one-time gift of the same amount.
For this experiment, 50% of donation page traffic was shown the exit-intent popup while the other 50% did not see anything when they closed the page.
experiments.keyLearnings:
While the treatment showing the exit-intent popup did produce a slight lift in overall donations, we are unable to draw any definitive conclusions about this experiment based on how the results were tracked.

Because the experiment session "fired" based on page load (rather than popup load) we are unable to determine whether it was the popup that persuaded more people to give or if these results occurred merely by chance. We were also only able to achieve a 39% level of confidence in these results. This does not mean that the tactic itself is invalid, just that in order to retest we will want to make sure the experiment "fires" only once someone see the popup and attempt to obtain a larger sample size to validate the results.

experiments.title: How implementing a goal thermometer on a high urgency campaign donation page impacted donations
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that implementing a goal thermometer for donation page visitors amidst a high urgency campaign will achieve an increase in donations because page visitors will be motivated to help contribute to and reach the end goal of the campaign.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/22/2023 - 03/31/2023
As part of a March high urgency campaign for the Illinois Policy Institute, we want to test the impact of adding a goal thermometer on the campaign donation page. The goal is to reach a $50,000 matching gift. The thermometer syncs with GA to track up-to-date campaign revenue, so donation page visitors will see how much money has been raised towards the campaign goal at the time they’re visiting the page.
We’re curious to see what kind of impact the visible progress and end goal seen through the thermometer will impacted donations.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment (showing the thermometer) resulted in a 7.8% increase in donations but with a level of confidence of only 34.8%. The treatment produced 64 donations to the control's 54. Revenue saw a 9% increase through the treatment with a level of confidence of 65.4%. The increases cannot confidently be rolled out for future high urgency campaigns, but it's definitely worth testing again to see if the experiment can reach a confident result.
It'd be interesting to see how the campaign goal factors into results. Would a campaign that nearly reaches the campaign goal be more motivating for users to give? In this case, the total revenue of the campaign was less than half the matching gift goal. So perhaps users weren't as motivated to give because they felt the goal was out of reach. The goal for the next campaign could be adjusted based on historical results to be more achievable.
Interestingly, the experiment produced contrasting results through desktop and mobile. Desktop saw a 24.3% decrease in donations from the treatment (86% loc) compared to a 58.7% increase in donation on mobile (91% loc). We should test this concept again, hopefully during a campaign that lasts longer than 11 days.

experiments.title: How positive messaging and imagery on a donation page affects conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that removing the dark photo and showing direct photos of multiple children for potential donors will achieve a higher conversion rate because we are sharing more descriptive and specific information about the children who are being served.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/08/2023 - 03/06/2023
Prison Fellowship International works to transform the lives of prisons, their families and victims through a global network of community partners. In recent months we have been showcasing the support a child program for the children of prisoners that is more general that 1:1 child sponsorship. Programmatically it costs less to maintain while allowing more children to participate. The ads were not converting as many donors as we wanted to see. In an effort to increase the donations and revenue, we redesigned the donation page to remove a darker image of a child alone in a house and replaced it with child photos of them looking at the camera and gave details about children in the program.
experiments.keyLearnings: The key learning from this experiment is that removing a darker image of a child alone in a house and replacing it with more upbeat photos of children and sharing details about their lives did not result in a higher conversion rate. In fact, the treatment saw a decrease in donations for all traffic. We learned that a significantly large gift had been given in error to the treatment, which means that the conversion rate was actually .60% which is a decrease of 25.8% with a 40% LOC and not .70% as shown above. It is important to note that there was only a 2 gift difference between the control and treatment and when the large gift is removed the treatment received a $30.80 average gift compared to $12.14 shown in the control.

experiments.title: How does removing an inline image from an instant donation page affect donation rates?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that removing an inline image on an instant donation page will increase donation rates by eliminating distractions, reducing friction, and focusing the reader’s attention on the content of the appeal.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/08/2023 - 03/09/2023
This organization has traditionally included inline images on its instant donation pages. We wanted to know if this might be creating friction by distracting the prospective donor from the appeal and interrupting their momentum to make a donation—or if the image was effectively working as a supporting element to add context and value to the appeal.
We wanted to test this hypothesis on a single offer to see if we could obtain a large enough sample size to attain statistically valid results before applying it to other offers. This experiment was set up as an A/B test with both the control (with image) and treatment (sans image) receiving roughly 50% of the traffic.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running this experiment for 30 days, we observed a 24% lift in conversions by removing the inline image. However, these statistical results are deceiving because that 24% lift accounted for only one additional donation since the sample size was so low. The low sample size also prevented us from validating our hypothesis, producing only a 35% level of statistical confidence in these results.
Nevertheless, it is worth noting that desktop visitors had a 5.6% increase in donation rates, with a 96% level of confidence, which suggests that removing the inline image may be more effective for desktop users. Conversely, removing the image resulted in a 40% decrease in donations for mobile users, with a 61% level of confidence.
Due to the very small sample size achieved during this experiment, and because the inclusion of an inline image on instant donation pages is something this organization applies across the board, we are for now going to leave the image in place and test this hypothesis at a later date in the context of a revamped strategy to test our hypothesis across all instant donation pages in an effort to produce larger sample sizes and obtain more statistically reliable results.

experiments.title: How a lock icon on a donation page impacts conversions
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by adding the lock icon for prospective donors will achieve higher donor conversion because the perceived security has been increased.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/17/2023 - 03/13/2023
The Hoover Institution is the public policy think tank for Stanford University.
In an effort to increase donor conversion on donation pages, we decided to test the presence of a lock icon.
experiments.keyLearnings: The key learning from this experiment is that adding a lock icon to the donation page can lead to an increase in donor conversion. However, the statistical level of confidence is only at 43.2%, which is below the threshold of 85%, indicating that the results are not valid for future decision making. Therefore, it is necessary to run further experiments with an adequate sample size to validate these findings. Additionally, future experiments should consider testing other elements on the donation page that can lead to an increase in donor conversion.

experiments.title: How relevant images impacted petition signatures
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that removing an image from the landing page for petition signers will achieve an increase in signature conversions because there would be less friction on the page by removing a distracting or emotional element.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/10/2023 - 01/15/2023
On a petition to repeal beer tax, we wondered if the image on the landing page was distracting or dissuading visitors from completing the petition. We created a treatment that removed the image altogether, and developed a second treatment that portrayed the politician the petition would be submitted to. We split traffic between the three versions and let the experiment run for 5 days.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that removing an image from the landing page did not result in an increase in signature conversions. In fact, there was a slight decrease in the number of sign-ups. However, this result cannot be considered valid due to a low statistical level of confidence. It is also important to note that there were significant differences in sign-up rates among different devices and traffic sources. Desktop visitors showed a significant decrease in sign-ups, while tablet and email visitors showed a significant increase.

In future experimentation, it would be important to examine these differences among devices and traffic sources and to tailor the landing page accordingly. It may also be useful to test different messaging strategies to see if there is a specific type of language or tone that resonates better with potential signers. Overall, this experiment highlights the importance of careful testing and analysis to ensure that decisions are informed by valid and reliable data.

experiments.title: How humanizing an appeal by presenting a specific child’s name, age, location and image for sponsorship affects conversion rates

rows.name: Slider with generic # of sponsors needed

experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that the featured child will outperform the slider  for users because they will identify with specific child details, more than a generic number of children in need of a sponsor, which will achieve a greater number of donors.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/18/2022 - 10/12/2022
We wanted to test a slide out with a specific number of children left in need of sponsorship by midnight vs. a (rotating) specific child above the search functionality that a potential donor could choose to sponsor.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Notable Results & Recommendations:
• Test stopped due to declines in donor conversion and RPV.
• Donor conversion was lower for variant (-7%) at a non-statistically valid level of confidence (77%).
• Revenue per visitor was lower for the variant (-33%) at a statistically valid level of confidence (99.9%).
• We recommend not changing the sponsorship slide.

experiments.title: How increasing perceived security at the point of transaction affects donation rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that increasing the perceived security at the point-of transaction on a main donation page (by explicitly stating how their payment details will be used) will increase donations by anticipating security concerns and increasing trust/transparency by reassuring potential donors that their sensitive information will be kept private.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/07/2023 - 03/03/2023
We know that online security is highly important to general web users, especially when it comes to entering sensitive data like payment information online. So we wanted to test how increasing perceived security at the point of transaction would impact donation rates on a main donation page for a family ministry organization.
In order to draw attention to the security statement below the donate button, we animated the lock icon to catch the eye of potential donors.
We also included a more detailed security statement to communicate that the donation is not only secure but that the donor’s sensitive payment information will also be kept private and will not be used for any other reason than to process the donation.
