For this experiment, we split traffic evenly between the treatment explained above and the control which featured a static lock icon and a brief security statement, “Your information is secure.” for a duration of 21 days.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning gained from this experiment is that making security assurances more visible can significantly increase donations, validating our hypothesis that online security is highly important to web users, especially when it comes to entering their payment information.
This experiment shows that people need to feel secure when making donations and that focusing on reinforcing the security of their transaction will result in higher donation rates. We believe that the treatment anticipated and preemptively addressed a common objection among potential donors, thereby increasing trust and the desire to give to an organization that cares about donors by taking data privacy seriously.
After 21 days, this experiment saw a 26.3% increase in overall donations (99% level of confidence), with a 74.1% increase in revenue.
The treatment also produced a 37.4% increase in donations among desktop visitors (100% level of confidence)--who make up the majority of this organization's donor base.
Significantly, we also observed a 28.2% increase in donations among new visitors (96% level of confidence) and a 31% increase in donations among visitors referred from organic search (98% level of confidence), highlighting the importance of online security to the general population and among traffic that may be less familiar with the organization.
We are unable to draw a precise conclusion about whether the animated lock icon or the more detailed security statement played a more significant role in these results, however both variables apply to the stated strategy of "increasing perceived security" so we do not feel this invalidates the learning.
That being said, future experiments could focus on isolating these two variables and then continuing to refine the design of the donation page to make donors feel confident and secure when donating.

experiments.title: How replacing an “emergency” banner with an “emergency” slide-in affects conversion rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding an emergency slider with a clear CTA to the homepage for potential donors will achieve a greater response than the emergency banner  because it moves the page and dynamically loads an equivalent goal.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/04/2022 - 03/09/2022
Adding an emergency slider with a clear CTA to the homepage will outperform the emergency banner because it moves the page and dynamically loads an equivalent goal.
Numbers in the slide-in module are dynamic and reset every hour. If a user clicks “donate now” and then returns to the homepage within the hour, the number will be lower than what they saw previously. The slide-in module is sticky and stays in view when the user scrolls down the page and can be removed by clicking on the “x” in the top right corner, similar to the emergency banner. Both of these CTA’s are driving to an undesignated GAF form. Copy approved by legal.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Notable Results & Recommendations:
• Overall increase in donor conversion on the homepage for the variant (2%) at a non-statistically valid level of confidence (50%).
• Overall increase in RPV on the homepage for the variant (4%) at a non-statistically valid level of confidence (80%).
• Undesignated donor conversion was lower for the variant (-11%) at a statistically valid level of confidence (99.8%).
• Undesignated RPV declined for the variant (-5%) at a non-statistically valid level of confidence (81%).
• Emergency donor conversion was higher for the variant (21%) at a statistically valid level of confidence (99.9%).
• Emergency RPV was higher for the variant (14%) at a statistically valid level of confidence (98%).
• A few days into the test, there was an uptick in call center donors who requested their gifts be assigned explicitly to Ukraine.
• We shifted 100% of test traffic to the variant, as these users were more likely to give an emergency gift (potentially reducing call center calls). Shortly after, we shifted from Ukraine designated CEF, which made it difficult to measure the impact of the shift.

experiments.title: How a premium affects recurring conversion rate on a main donation page
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that including a premium for main donation page visitors will achieve an increase in recurring donations because it provides an incentive to give.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/05/2023 - 02/02/2023
CaringBridge saw strategic value in growing their recurring donor program, and wanted to run an experiment on their main donation page. Through a partnership with Bicycle cards, they had the chance to offer a unique deck of playing cards in exchange for a recurring gift.
They wanted to test this concept to see if it would increase recurring gifts.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The experiment ran for 27 days and the results indicate that overall conversion saw a significant increase of 3.4% when adding the premium incentive. However, this result is too small to be considered statistically valid due to a 5.9% level of confidence.
Overall, the key learning from this experiment suggests that recurring donors do not respond positively when provided with incentives. Future experiments should focus on better understanding the motivation as to why people want to give recurring gifts, attempting to tailor incentives in a way that resonates most with their movement. This, in turn, will lead to sustainable increases in conversions and eventually, customer loyalty.

experiments.title: How adding a privacy statement below the donate button on an instant donation page affects donation rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a privacy statement below the donation button for instant donation page visitors will increase donations by reducing anxiety around sharing sensitive data online.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/20/2023 - 02/10/2023
For a faith-based media organization, we wanted to know how including a privacy statement below the donation button would affect donation rates. We know that privacy continues to be a growing priority for users online and we wanted to test whether or not anticipating any reluctance to submit payment information online at the point of giving would reduce anxiety and encourage more people to give.
The privacy statement below the button on the treatment reads: “We take data and privacy seriously. Your information will be kept safe and will be used to process your gift.”
~50% of traffic received the control and ~50% of traffic received the treatment.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running this experiment for just over two weeks, we observed that the inclusion of a privacy statement had very little impact on potential donors' motivation to give. Furthermore, the low sample size of donations prevented us from achieving an adequate level of statistical confidence in the experiment's results.
While the treatment produced a 5.3% decrease in donations (10% level of confidence), this amounted to just one less donation than the control. Because of the flat and inconclusive results of this experiment, we can determine that the statement's inclusion was not a significant enough motivating factor to move the needle, perhaps because the security of the transaction is already adequately conveyed with a visual indication shown around the payment fields.
Based on these results, we will look for other test ideas that may have a larger impact on potential donors' motivation to give.

experiments.title: How adding a premium to an instant donation page affects average gift
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that offering a premium of Navigating Genesis for users who already downloaded OT questions for free will achieve a higher ROI.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/15/2022 - 01/26/2023
RTB was running a free offer for their Old Testament Questions eBook, in an effort to improve the ROI we tested a premium offer for another ebook, Navigating Genesis on the instant donation page for Old Testament Questions. For a gift of any amount, the donor would receive a copy of this premium, Navigating Genesis.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Instead of an increased ROI, we actually saw a directional decrease in the average gift but with a total of only 9 donations, this experiment does not validate. A potential area to test is the exact wording of the premium offer. The options are either to be sent the Navigating Genesis offer, OR what is pre-selected is "No, I'd like to maximize my gift." My thought is that this creates conflict in the donor's mind of what their action should be.
On one hand, there's this generosity of the organization saying "we want to thank you with a gift," but also insinuating that they shouldn't accept it if they actually want their donation to be maximized. As a result, the donor begins weighing what they should actually do, deliberating over it too much and just deciding to exit due to the friction.
Observations of the impact on other visitor segments in the experiment:
Desktop visitors had a 75.4% decrease in revenue with a 92% level of confidence.Returning  visitors had a 73.0% decrease in revenue with a 91% level of confidence.Email visitors had an 83.3% decrease in revenue with an 88% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How giving is impacted by the use of buttons in an email appeal
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that removing donation buttons from an email appeal for email recipients will achieve a higher donor conversion rate .
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/17/2023
RTB historically uses multiple “Give Now” buttons in email appeals. During calendar year end we decided to run an experiment where we removed the buttons from the email appeal. The idea is that the removal of the buttons would actually humanize the communication more, make the email seem less promotion-like, and encourage people to read the entire appeal/case for support before deciding to give. This in turn, has the potential to increase donor conversion rate. We chose one email in the campaign to test this concept.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Results in this experiment were flat. We saw .35% decrease in donor conversion rate with a .68% level of confidence. The average gift across both the control and treatment were also negligible (control: $250, treatment: $238) so the humanized treatment did not inspire people to give more either. A concern in removing the buttons from the email was that it would negatively impact click thru rate. However, results indicate that the removal of the buttons has no effect on click thru rate either. Click thru rate stayed the same at .4%.
Where we did see a noticeable difference was the impact on new donors. New donors experienced a 497% increase in donor conversion rate with a level of confidence of 94%.  More non donors were activated to give through the treatment (no buttons) than the control. While it's not a large number of transactions, the organization should consider using this new layout (no buttons) knowing that it will not impact giving overall but may active more new donors. It's likely that non donors saw the email as less transactional/promotional and decided to read more of the appeal and were therefore more motivated to give.

experiments.title: How adding a humanized sticky bar to donation pages during a year-end campaign impacts conversion rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a personalized sticky bar for potential donors will achieve a change in conversion rates because it gives the organization a “face” which makes it more relatable.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/13/2022 - 12/23/2022
During Avila Institute’s year-end campaign, we wanted to capitalize on traffic to one of their most popular websites, SpiritualDirection.com, and see whether adding a humanized sticky bar to a donation page would impact conversion rates.
This sticky bar ran on all donation pages for 10 days; following this period we launched it across the entire site.
Our hypothesis was that by adding the personalized note from Dan, we would see a change in conversion rates.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This experiment actually led to a decrease in conversion rates on the donation form, with 16.8% less donations and a 68% level of confidence. We believe that this was caused by a number of factors:
The placement of the sticky bar was not optimalThe copy on the sticky bar did not tie into the overall messaging during the year-end campaignIt potentially added a friction point for those donors who were ready to donate (since they already landed on the donation form)
Future iterations of this test will experiment with whether different variations of a sticky bar, placement, and styles, will have a more positive impact.

experiments.title: How does adding a personalized sticky bar to the donation page affect donation rates during a year-end campaign?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a humanized sticky bar featuring a public personality to the donation page of a year-end fundraising campaign will increase donation rates by increasing credibility and humanizing the appeal.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/05/2022 - 12/28/2022
During a year-end fundraising campaign, we wanted to know how adding a humanized sticky bar to the campaign donation page would affect donation rates for a public policy organization, especially considering that the personalized message on the sticky bar would be coming from a public personality who is well-known and trusted by the organization’s target audience.
For this experiment, we created a treatment featuring a personal message and photograph of a public figure asking users to ‘stand with me’ by making a donation to the year-end campaign. We set this experiment up as an A/B test wherein ~50% of traffic would see the control (no sticky bar) and ~50% of traffic would see the treatment (including the sticky bar). The experiment ran for 23 days, throughout the bulk of the year-end campaign.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running this experiment for 23 days, the treatment featuring a humanized sticky bar achieved a 12.7% increase in donation rates (with a 79% level of confidence) when compared to the control.
We believe that including a sticky bar featuring a well-known public figure who is widely trusted by the target audience was able to increase donations among page visitors by humanizing and building credibility into the appeal. This may also have increased the believability of the outcomes being promoted on the donation page.
And though we did not receive a large enough sample size to validate this increase, we know that there is a relatively low risk associated with implementing a treatment that has shown an increase in targeted metrics, so we decided to roll this treatment out to 100% of traffic to wrap up the campaign.

experiments.title: How a countdown timer on a donation page affects donation rates during the end of a year-end fundraising campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a countdown timer to the donation page for the last few days of a year-end fundraising campaign will increase donation rates by increasing the urgency for the prospect to respond to the appeal.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/29/2022 - 01/04/2023
During a year-end fundraising campaign for a public policy organization, we wanted to know how adding a countdown timer to the donation timer during the final four days of the campaign would affect donation rates. We hypothesized that the countdown timer would increase urgency and fear of missing out and encourage more prospects to give before time ran out.
For this brief 4-day experiment, we ran an A/B test with ~50% of traffic seeing the control (no timer) and ~50% of traffic seeing the treatment (countdown timer).
experiments.keyLearnings:
Due to the small sample size entering this experiment, we were unable to draw statistically valid conclusions about the efficacy of using a countdown timer in the context of a high-urgency campaign. Showing the timer produced a 5.4% decrease in conversion rates with only a 38.2% level of confidence.
Although this experiment did not validate, the results suggest that we may not be able to assume that adding a countdown timer to a donation page has a significant impact on donation rates. This could be because it is overdone in the nonprofit fundraising space (thus decreasing its impactfulness), or perhaps because it distracts from the content of the donations page. But due to the low statistical confidence of these results, these are only additional hypotheses and we are unable to draw definitive conclusions one way or the other.
Knowing that there is risk involved with implementing a treatment that produces a decrease in conversions, even if the result is not statistically valid, we will want to test this concept again in an effort to receive an adequate sample size to validate the efficacy of a countdown timer before rolling it out to all traffic during future high-urgency campaigns.

experiments.title: How adding a thermometer to a sticky bar affects donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a thermometer to a sticky bar for site visitors will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate .
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/29/2022 - 12/02/2022
During Giving Tuesday, RTB wanted to include a progress bar on the on-site sticky bar. We wanted to test adding this thermometer in order to determine if it motivates more people to give through the sticky bar. The results of this experiment also determined if the organization would invest time and labor to manually update a thermometer in the sticky bar during the rest of the calendar year end experiment. If we saw significant gains by adding the thermometer, we would invest the time in manually updating the thermometer.
This sticky bar was live across the RTB site. Traffic was primarily driven organically.
experiments.keyLearnings: We initially believed that the thermometer would provide a visual representation of the organization's goal and would motivate more people to give. Results indicate that the thermometer did not have a significant impact. The experiment resulted in a 42% decrease in donor conversion rate but with only a level of confidence of 73%. There weren't many transactions through this experiment because it only ran for a couple dates for Giving Tuesday. However, we determined that because we saw a decrease (though invalid), we would not move forward in adding this feature in the rest of the campaign. It's probable that the goal seemed too large to the site visitor and ultimately made the goal feel unattainable by seeing such a large amount in the thermometer.

experiments.title: How remove Dr. Jeffress’ image impacted donations
experiments.researchQuestion: Will removing Dr. Jeffress’ image from the year-end landing page increase donor conversions?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/05/2022 - 12/16/2022
PTV is a broadcast ministry with a premium-based fundraising program.
Dr. Jeffress is the host and preacher for PTV therefore his image is prominent in most promotions, landing pages, and websites.
Though his face is known to his TV audience, his radio, email and newly acquired names may not be familiar with his image.
For the year-end campaign, we decided to test the landing page without his image to see if this impacted donor conversion.
experiments.keyLearnings:
While we did not see a statistically valid increase of donations, we also did not see any harm with removing Dr. Jeffress' image.
On mobile, we had expected to see more of an impact of the treatment version. With the control version on mobile, Dr. Jeffress' image takes up the entire screen between the paragraphs of copy and the donation level options. So with the treatment version having the image removed, we expected an improved mobile experience and less friction however the data did not end up supporting that.
We will continue testing on this landing page to improve donor conversion as we are seeing higher than normal web traffic given the year end final push.

experiments.title: How an urgent popup with a sponsorship goal and a 24-hour countdown impacted sponsorship conversion on children sponsorship pages.
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that an urgent popup noting a daily sponsorship goal and a 24-hour countdown clock for people visiting child sponsorship pages will achieve an increase in sponsorship conversions because the popup will create urgency and motivation, and stand as a special nudge to get people interested in sponsoring to sponsor.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/23/2022 - 12/06/2022
In an effort to increase sponsorship conversions on children sponsorship pages for a child sponsorship organization, we’re testing a popup that sets a daily sponsorship goal counting down from three and includes a countdown (hours, minutes, seconds) for the day. It also includes value prop as part of an urgent message. This popup does not include a CTA and does not redirect to another page when clicked. It’s tested to add urgency and motivation while a potential sponsor is already scrolling the children sponsorship pages. We’re running the popup only on pages that catalog the children available for sponsorship.
We believe that the popup will act as a special nudge to get people who are already considering sponsoring a child to convert into sponsors.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running for two weeks, the experiment did not gain much traction. The treatment resulted in a 16.8% decrease in child sponsorships with a 37.9% level of confidence. This tells us that the popup did not carry much weight in visitors' decisions to sponsor a child. The logic here is that they are already on the children page, so their intent is already established. Whether they saw the urgent popup or not, their mind was already made up when they reached the page.
We have another experiment running in which the popup appears on all website pages except for the children pages, and includes a call-to-action button that takes users to the child sponsorship page. In this case, we hypothesize that the popup will convince people who did not come to the website with the intent to sponsor a child to click the popup CTA, redirecting them to the child sponsorship page and converting them into a sponsor.
We will keep an eye on the experiment and see how it compares to this one in regards to motivation, urgency, and conversion.

experiments.title: How adding an image of an incentivized item to an email impacted clicks
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that providing an image of the special CATIS coin in the welcome series email for potential CATIS members will achieve an increase in clicks because they’ll be impressed with the coin and gain a true feeling of possessing the coin.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/03/2022 - 12/02/2022
As part of TPPF’s welcome series, new names are sent an invitation email to join the Come and Take It Society (CATIS) by making a monthly gift. When joining, members receive exclusive perks. One perk is receiving a special CATIS coin. This specific perk is mentioned in the email, however, the email does not include an image of the coin to give potential new members an accurate, tangible impression of the coin.
We hypothesized that adding an image of the coin would further pique the interest of email recipients, giving them a preview of the special coin they would receive, and therefore encouraging them to click through the email to the CATIS donation page.
experiments.keyLearnings: The treatment resulted in an 89.9% lift in clicks - a 6.3% click rate compared to 3.3% for the control. Our hypothesis proved correct: Those who saw the coin were more likely to click within the email. The coin image gave these people a preview of the coin and provoked visions of what it would be like to possess the coin. The mere mention of it can only go so far. By just mentioning it, email recipients don't know what the coin looks like and if it'd be worth essentially paying for as part of a monthly donation commitment. They could envision the coin as appearing tacky and poorly made. Showing an image of the coin promotes something tangible and takes some of the guesswork out of the person's mind.

experiments.title: How a tabbed donation form affects recurring giving rates on an acquisition offer
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a tabbed donation form will increase recurring giving rates on the donation page of an acquisition offer by increasing the visibility of the option to give monthly.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/27/2022 - 12/02/2022
In an effort to increase the recurring giving rate on an instant donation page of an acquisition offer, we wanted to know how increasing the visibility of the monthly giving option earlier in the donation process, with a tabbed donation form (which in the past has shown success in this regard), would affect recurring monthly giving.
We ran this experiment for 30 days as an A/B test splitting traffic 50/50 between:
A. the control that features a monthly giving checkbox beneath the gift array, and
B. the treatment that removes the checkbox in favor of a monthly giving tab above the gift array
In addition, the impact statement featured below the monthly giving checkbox in the control was moved above the monthly donation form in order to keep it in close proximity to the monthly giving option.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running this experiment for 30 days, the treatment featuring the tabbed donation form produced a 16.6% DECREASE in recurring giving rates with a 47.7% LoC. While the low statistical validity of this experiment prevents us from drawing definitive conclusions about these results one way or another, we did observe that perhaps the checkbox in the control may have offered a more logical "flow" during the donation process, prompting users to first select their donation amount, then select how often they want to give, then view the impact that monthly donation will make.
But because the results of this experiment were well below the 95% LoC that would make them statistically valid, and because we have seen tabbed donation forms produce lifts in recurring giving for other clients in the past, we will want to look for opportunities to test this treatment on a donation page that has an opportunity to produce a larger sample size and validate the results one way to the other.

experiments.title: How adding a humanized sticky bar to a donation page affects donations during a Giving Tuesday Campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a humanized sticky bar featuring a well-known public figure during a high-urgency campaign will increase donation rates by adding value and credibility to the appeal.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/29/2022 - 12/03/2022
During a Giving Tuesday campaign for a public policy client, we wanted to know how adding a humanized sticky bar including the image of a well-known public figure would affect donation rates.
This experiment ran as an A/B test for one day with roughly 50% of traffic being shown the treatment including the sticky bar and roughly 50% of traffic shown the control, sans the sticky bar.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running this experiment for the length of the Giving Tuesday campaign—one day—we observed a 20.6% DECLINE in donation rates, with a 61% level of confidence, among traffic who were shown the treatment including the humanized sticky bar. Because we did not receive a large enough sample size to validate these results, it is not possible to draw reliable inferences about this experiment.
However, observationally, we can hypothesize that by being large and colorful, the sticky bar may have actually created friction by attracting users' eyes to the top page, rather than the headline and then the body copy, and then the donation form, creating friction during the conversion process.
Because we were unable to obtain a large enough sample size to validate these results, we will want to deploy a similar experiment during a longer-term high-urgency campaign in an effort to validate the results one way or the other.

experiments.title: How a slide up CTA with a daily donor goal impacts donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a slide-up CTA with a daily donor goal will increase donor conversion
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/26/2022 - 10/31/2022
Buckner International is a Christian nonprofit that has been serving children and families for more than one hundred years. They wanted to find ways of getting more visitors to become monthly donors. They had used sticky bars before and found success with these at acquiring donors from visitors to their website. They hypothesized that using a different style message, one that slid up from the bottom of the page, and that included a daily donor goal would increase donor conversion. They hoped that this message would, if not leading to more monthly donors acquired, at least lead to more donors acquired.
They used a bright yellow color design to catch the eye and a low appeal threshold of just $15/month.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running the experiment for just over a month, we were unable to validate an impact on donor conversion from the slide-up. Our sample size of gifts was just too small. We do however want to test micro goals presented in either sticky bars or slide up CTAs during future campaigns, particularly during seasons of high urgency when we are more likely to get enough data to validate the result.

It is possible that a transition to iDonate that took place during this test may have caused us to be unable to track donations made on the main donation page that were influenced by the popup rather than made through the popup.

experiments.title: How showing an abandoned donation pop-up to users based on entry point affects revenue – homepage version
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that visitors entering the site on the main page will be prompted to complete their donation when presented a pop-up reminder and we will generate more revenue than not showing a pop-up.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 11/11/2022
Focus on the Family wanted to see if showing a pop-up with an opportunity to complete a donation (from a previously clicked on amount), would change based on the entry point of a visitor. The thought was that user intentions might be different for visitors coming directly to the home page vs visitors entering from other pages.
Prior to this experiment, we first tested if showing a pop-up to visitors (regardless of entry point) would increase overall completed transactions. We built this initial test with a single pop up that was shown to all visitors that visited a donation page, clicked on an amount to give, but abandoned the process after that. The pop-up would show to that visitor as they navigated around on the FOTF site.
For this test, we split the test into two separate tests. One test focussed on visitors to all non-homepages. The pop up was shown only to folks that came through anywhere but the main page, hit the donate button, clicked an amount…then abandoned the process.
This particular test focused on visitors to the main home page. The pop up behaved exactly the same, except that it only showed to people that visited through the homepage page first. The idea was that people coming through main page would be more familiar and thus more likely to complete a transaction, but they may need more reminders if they happened to go through the donation pathway…and possibly the abandoned donation pop up would serve as that gentle nudge and cause a completed transaction, and thus more revenue.
experiments.keyLearnings:
By showing a pop-up reminder to complete the transaction, our theory was that those that came to the main page to make a donation, but abandoned for some reason, would potentially be swayed by this gentle nudge. Just like the test on the non-homepage version, When we pulled transaction data, we found quite a few gifts that skewed the results of both the control and the treatment. We decided to remove any donation of $2k or more from both the treatment and the control in order to remove any outliers that might be skewing the results. When we did this, we found the data to be inconclusive with an 11% decrease in the treatment, but with only a 53% Level of Confidence. There is no real positive or negative effect on the results. It is also important to note that experiments for FOTF that run outside of high-urgency time frames behave much differently than during campaign periods.

The key takeaway is that when running transaction experiments that involve revenue, we cannot take the results on face value. We must dig into the numbers deeper, look for any anomalies that can skew results, and then evaluate for lift. In this case, while we did not see a real difference either way (after removing outlier data), we also did not see a potentially harmful outcome. While we cannot recommend that this current variation become the new control, we can recommend to keep testing different variations of the same experiment. Next up is a reduced ask (60% of the originally clicked donation amount), but only on the non-homepage version.

experiments.title: How emphasizing the ‘I’m new’ button for new website visitors impacted email acquisition rates for First Baptist Dallas
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that highlight a specific section for new visitors will achieve a change in click throughs.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/15/2022 - 09/16/2022
As one of the largest Baptist churches in the country, First Baptist Dallas drives a lot of new website traffic to it’s site every day. For this experiment, we wanted to test whether highlighting the ‘I’m New’ navigation menu in the header menu for new visitors only made an impact on email sign-ups on that specific page.
Our hypothesis was that by highlighting this section, any new visitor to the site would easily find information that was more relevant to them, instead of spending time searching through the site. New visitors were also asked to fill out a form on that page to receive more information; by reducing the amount of friction to find that form, our hypothesis was that email acquisition rates would be higher.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Highlight the 'I'm New' header navigation menu for new visitors to the site led to a small, 1.7%, increase in email acquisition rates, with only a 48.8% level of confidence.

While this shows a slight directional lift in email rates, this test warrants further refinement. For example, the form where people are asked to fill in their information is actually a plan your visit (for Sunday service) form; not all of the new visitors to the homepage went there with the intention of attending a Sunday service. The form itself is also at the bottom of the 'I'm New' page; further testing would place this form higher, potentially above the fold, and make it more appealing to new visitors by offering more than just Sunday service information in exchange for an email sign-up.

experiments.title: How adding a premium offer to an ebook instant donation page impacts revenue.
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a premium for donors will achieve a change in average gift..
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/10/2022 - 11/11/2022
The Hoover Institution is the public policy think tank for Stanford University. Part of its outreach for the public is making available ebooks on specific topics available for download.
Every quarter, Hoover publishes a collection of essays and articles in the Hoover Digest. There is a digital version of this that Hoover offers as an email acquisition tool, and there is a physical version of the Digest that gets mailed to donors who make a gift over $100. For this experiment, we wanted to test whether including getting access to the physical version all four quarters of the Digest, for a gift of $100, would increase the average gift.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This experiment brought in a slightly higher average gift than the control version, with an increase of 17% and a 77% level of confidence. This result is in line with previous premium-focused experiments we've run on the Hoover Digest.
Our hypothesis is that the positioning of the physical versions of the Digest is not as appealing to digital donors as more traditional offline donors. Further testing might including positioning the Digest premium test as providing a 'gift' for friends and family.

experiments.title: How adding urgency with a sticky bar affects donation rates during a high urgency campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that including a sticky bar that conveys urgency during a fundraising campaign will increase donation rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/21/2022 - 11/04/2022
During a special high-urgency campaign for a public policy client, we wanted to know how adding a sticky bar with a high-urgency message from the director of the project and including his image would affect donation rates.
The treatment including the sticky bar was run during the entirety of the campaign and was shown to 50% of prospective donors who clicked through to the donation page
experiments.keyLearnings: After running this experiment for just over 30 days, we observed a 5.3% decline in donation rates ... but with only a 49.4% level of confidence. Because we did not receive a large enough sample size to validate these results, it is not possible to draw reliable inferences about this experiment. However, we can hypothesize that the sticky bar either created friction by attracting the viewer's eyes back up the page, rather than progressing down the copy to the donation form. It could also be possible that the particular language chosen for this sticky bar "this can't wait" was a bit too forceful and created resentment in viewers. But in either case, we cannot know for sure.

experiments.title: How a give now button at the top of the donation page anchored to the form impacts donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a give now button at the top of the donation page anchored to the form will increase donor conversion
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/14/2022 - 10/07/2022
KCBI is a large Christian radio station in the DFW area with a worldwide listening audience. They wanted to see if the length of their donation page was causing friction for donors already motivated to give and simply looking for the donation form on the page. During their Fall Campaign, they decided to test the impact of a “give now” button at the top of the page that was anchored to the form. Those coming to the page motivated by the email they read or the on-air appeal they heard, could click that button and drop straight to the form. They hypothesized that this could increase donor conversion.
experiments.keyLearnings:
We ran this experiment for three weeks and were unable to validate the impact of the anchor button. When digging into the results by channel (note: the T1 space in the results window above is actually the control without the anchor button), we found that there was a 17% or so decrease in conversion on direct traffic, but an 11.7% increase on email traffic conversion. While neither of these segments were statistically valid, we hypothesized that there is a possibility that email was showing a positive result because the email traffic was primed to give by the appeal they'd just read and thus wanted the button and the decreased friction it provided. We saw that returning traffic was also showing a higher conversion rate (though not valid) and we believe it would be for the same reason.
We will not roll out a give now button but may test a similar approach on a future offer.

experiments.title: How showing an abandoned donation pop-up to users based on entry point affects revenue – non-homepage version
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that visitors entering the site outside of the main page will be prompted to complete their donation when presented a pop-up reminder and we will generate more revenue than not showing a pop-up.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 11/03/2022
Focus on the Family wanted to see if showing a pop-up with an opportunity to complete a donation (from a previously clicked on amount), would change based on the entry point of a visitor. The thought was that user intentions might be different for visitors coming directly to the home page vs visitors entering from other pages.
Prior to this experiment, we first tested if showing a pop-up to visitors (regardless of entry point) would increase overall completed transactions. We built this initial test with a single pop up that was shown to all visitors that visited a donation page, clicked on an amount to give, but abandoned the process after that. The pop-up would show to that visitor as they navigated around on the FOTF site.
For this test, we split the test into two separate tests. One test focussed on visitors to the main page. The pop up was shown only to folks that came through the main page, hit the donate button, clicked an amount…then abandoned the process. This particular test focused on visitors to all other pages. The pop up behaved exactly the same, except that it only showed to people that visited through non-homepage pages first. The idea was that people coming through main page would be more familiar and thus more likey to complete a transaction, where as visitors that came to article pages or via a SEO search, may need more reminders if they happened to go through the donation pathway…and possibly the abandoned donation pop up would serve as that gentle nudge and cause a completed transaction, and thus more revenue.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This test provided some interesting early data. Our hypothesis was that motivations for people visiting the site outside of the main page would be lower to donate than those from main site visits. By showing a pop-up reminder to complete the transaction, our theory was that those that had less motivation would potentially be swayed by this gentle nudge. After about 10 days of running the experiment, the test actually validated (with a greater than 95% LoC) based on revenue. The test results also showed that average gift for the treatment was substantially higher (over $25 greater for the treatment). While these initial results were very encouraging, whenever we run a test on revenue lift, we always need to look for outlier donations that could potentially skew the data results. When we pulled transaction data, we found quite a few gifts that skewed the results of both the control and the treatment. There was a $20k gift on the control, and several $5k plus gifts on the treatment. We decided to remove any donation of $1k or more from both the treatment and the control in order to remove any outliers that might be skewing the results. When we did this, we found the data to be inconclusive. There is no real positive or negative effect on the results. It is also important to note that experiments for FOTF that run outside of high-urgency time frames behave much differently than during campaign periods.
The key takeaway is that when running transaction experiments that involve revenue, we cannot take the results on face value. We must dig into the numbers deeper, look for any anomalies that can skew results, and then evaluate for lift. In this case, while we did not see a real difference either way (after removing outlier data), we also did not see a potentially harmful outcome. While we cannot recommend that this current variation become the new control, we can recommend to keep testing different variations of the same experiment. Next up is a reduced ask (60% of the originally clicked donation amount).

experiments.title: How changing the landing page design affects revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: Which landing page design produces the most donor conversions and revenue?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/09/2015 - 06/23/2015
The Heritage Foundation had been using the same landing page design for their search engine marketing (SEM) donor campaigns for the past three years. During those three years, we had run multiple experiments on the main organizational donation page which had gradually evolved into something that looked completely different.
We were in the process of moving the SEM page to the same domain as the main donation form and realized that it would be a good time to validate that the old design was still effective for the SEM audience segment.  We created an experiment by duplicating the page content but displaying it on the two different page designs.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The new design treatment caused an 85.1% drop in revenue by decreasing conversion rate by 67.6% and decreasing average gift by 54.1%. 

The simplified design used in the treatment was developed for a page with minimal copy. When we added the long value proposition text that had been developed for this specific landing page, it became difficult to read.

Landing pages must be designed so that they are easily scannable by the visitor. In this situation, the sub-headlines didn't stand out enough and the lack of page border made the page seem longer than it was.  All of these elements caused enough friction in the mind of the donor to have a negative impact on both conversion rate and average gift.

experiments.title: How Calling Out The Name of a Monthly Giving Program on Tabbed Donation Form Affects Monthly Giving Rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that replacing generic monthly donation form tab copy with program-specific copy will increase recurring donor rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/13/2022 - 10/02/2022
In an effort to combat ‘messaging fatigue,’ we wanted to know how replacing the generic monthly donation tab copy with language that specifically calls out the name of their recurring giving program will affect monthly giving. We replaced “Recurring monthly gift” with “Become a Cornerstone Partner” to see if we could increase exclusivity and a feeling of community by calling out the program by name. We ran this experiment during DTS’s annual North Texas Giving Day campaign, and split traffic 50/50
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running this experiment for 16 days, we saw a 155% increase in monthly donation rates with a 75% level of confidence. Although there was a low volume of monthly gifts (restricted by the shorter length of a campaign), we were encouraged to see a significant lift in recurring giving via the treatment which included the "Become a Cornerstone Partner"--the name of Dallas Theological Seminary's monthly giving program.
We believe that calling the monthly giving program out by name was able to add value in the eyes of donors by conveying exclusivity and a sense of community/belonging. We also believe that breaking away from more generic 'give monthly' language helped the monthly giving option stand out by reducing "messaging fatigue" and thus doing a better job of catching the donor's attention.
Based on these results, we will look to validate this hypothesis by rolling the same treatment out to higher-traffic donation pages that are not as restricted by the truncated timeframe of a high-urgency campaign.

experiments.title: How strengthening the value proposition with a radical redesign to the copy on an instant donation page affects average gift
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that strengthening the value proposition language on the instant donation page for prospective donors will achieve clarity and cause an increase in ICR.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/22/2022 - 09/18/2022
Luther Seminary has been running paid Facebook ads and email marketing for their content offer Enter the Bible: Revelation Study Guide. While the email sign-up rate was healthy around 48%, we wanted to try and increase the instant conversion rate on the donation page.
We hypothesized that the copy on the control page did not provide clarity on the impact a donation has for Luther Seminary and what the donor’s gift will do for the organization.
We believed that adding more value proposition-focused language to the page would provide clarity to the prospective donor for how their gift will be used in the future.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The results of the experiment were encouraging when it comes to lifts in revenue for this acquisition offer. We saw a 39.1% increase in average gift with a lift of 75.4% level of confidence. This experiment had been running for over a month and had not been validated therefore we moved traffic to the treatment page with confidence that the value prop copy will increase the giving amount on this page.
While we saw a lift in average gift, the results for donor conversion rate were flat at 4.3% lift with a 7.34% level of confidence.
We believe that the value proposition copied inspired potential donors more to give a higher gift, but was not enough to convince someone to give if they already decided against it. Although it did not negatively affect the decision to make a gift.
And while we are encouraged by these results, importantly, it turns out that we changed an additional variable by changing the confirmation message at the top of the page to be more clarifying following the previous step in the funnel. We will still want to verify these results by running this experiment on another instant donation page while isolating the variables being tested.

experiments.title: How adding a visual call-out to for a monthly gift ask impacted conversion rates.
experiments.organization: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
categoryNames: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a visual monthly call out for potential donors will achieve a change in conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/01/2022 - 05/16/2022
The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate serve poor and needy people in the United States and more than 70 countries around the world. The recruitment of monthly donors makes up a large part of their digital program; new monthly donors are offered the opportunity to have a candle lit at the shrine of our Lady of the Snows every month.
For this experiment, which we ran during the Easter season, we wanted to test whether having a visual call-out for monthly gifts made an impact on monthly donor sign-ups.
experiments.keyLearnings:
For this experiment, there was a decrease in the number of monthly gifts that came in during this testing period of 28%, with a 78% level of confidence. While the total number of gifts is just barely over the number we like to have to validate an experiment (50), there were a couple of differences in the two forms that contributed to mixed testing results.
On the control version, there is a check box one needs to select in order to make your gift become monthly; for the treatment version we not only introduced the visual call out, but also added in a tabbed form. We believe that this contributed to the change in conversion rates.
For future tests, we will try and isolate just a single change on the form to get a cleaner test result.

experiments.title: How adding a ‘most popular’ treatment to a specific gift amount impacted revenue.
experiments.organization: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
categoryNames: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that highlighting a most popular gift amount for potential donors will achieve a change in conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/01/2022 - 04/07/2022
The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate serve poor and needy people in the United States and more than 70 countries around the world. As part of the run-up to their Easter fundraising campaign, we wanted to test whether adding a graphic treatment for the ‘most popular’ amount would have an impact on conversion rates and average gift.
experiments.keyLearnings: This experiment resulted in a slight increase in conversion rate with the most popular treatment option, as well as a slight increase in average gift. However, since it did not reach the level of confidence needed for a valid test result nor can provide any directional results, we will need to continue testing this approach to see if it has a long-term impact on donor behavior.

experiments.title: How would ordering the giving tabs with the monthly giving option showing first (the left tab) impact recurring giving?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that swapping the giving tabs to show monthly giving first for page visitors will achieve an increase in monthly giving.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/28/2022 - 08/26/2022
With the goal to increase recurring giving to PFI’s Lamplighters program, we conducted an experiment in which the monthly giving tab (“Monthly Gift”) would show as the left tab and the one-time giving tab (“Give Once”) would show as the right tab on the Lamplighters giving widget. This order is the reverse of the existing setup (control variant) in which monthly giving shows on the right and one-time giving shows on the left. We ran the experiment on the Lamplighters landing page giving widget.
Lamplighters historically receives a lot of one-time gifts, so we want to see if this change will result in an increase in monthly gifts, as monthly gifts are much more valuable over time. The idea is that page visitors would read and notice the “Monthly Gift” option first, indicating greater importance and making it resonate with them ahead of the one-time giving option – prompting them to make a monthly gift.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment variant resulted in a 34.4% increase in recurring giving conversions for all traffic with a level of confidence of 64.8%. Strictly for desktop users, the treatment resulted in a 187.2% increase in recurring giving conversions with a level of confidence of 89.4%.
Our hypothesis proved correct, that visitors noticing and reading the monthly giving option first instead of one-time giving would prioritize monthly giving in their minds and result in more monthly giving conversions.
Interestingly enough, total donations (one-time and monthly) showed 21 donations through the treatment and 18 through the control. So, despite the treatment placing a greater focus on monthly giving, users still made more donations overall when seeing the monthly giving tab on the left and the one-time giving tab on the right.
This concept is certainly worth testing elsewhere given the results and the long-term impact that recurring giving has on revenue.

experiments.title: How a radical redesign of an instant donation page impacts donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a radical redesign on an instant donation page for those who sign up for a course will achieve a higher donor conversion rate .
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/07/2022 - 06/03/2022
Reasons to Believe initially ran a radical redesign experiment on their main donation page that provided a significant lift in donor conversion rate. We wanted to see if this same radical redesign applied to an instant donation page to an offer, would result in the same type of lift. The motivation behind someone giving on a main donation page (someone who comes to the page with the intention to give) differs from someone who comes across a donation page after acquiring a resource. We applied this radical redesign to a funnel that was already getting a high instant conversion rate (Listen & Learn, an online course).
Most of the traffic directed to this page was through paid Facebook ads.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The results for this experiment did not validate even after running for a significant amount of time. We saw a 51% increase in donor conversion rate but with only a level of confidence of 63.9%. Because this has already validated on the main donation page and because it is a better experience for the end-user, we decided to role out the treatment to 100% of the audience anyway. It could be that this funnel was not getting enough traffic through the sign up page in order for this experiment to validate.

Observations of the impact on other visitor segments in the experiment:
Returning visitors had a 186.2% increase in donations with a 93% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How an exit intent popup on a donation page affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that employing an exit intent modal for Lamplighters page visitors will achieve an increase in emails without a decrease in conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/06/2022 - 07/22/2022
Prison Fellowship International was running a lot of paid traffic to generate donations for their Lamplighters offer to share the gospel with prisoners. They knew that conversion rates were about 4%—and though they wanted to increase the conversion rate, they also wanted to capture the emails of people who were coming to the site but not giving so they could “waste fewer marketing dollars”.
However, they wanted to make sure that their exit intent popup didn’t reduce conversion, as it can sometimes be triggered when people are moving their cursor around without the intent to exit the page.
They built a popup in Unbounce and used Google Optimize to trigger it for half the visitors to determine the impact.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Observations of the impact on other visitor segments in the experiment:
Desktop visitors had a 32.9% increase in donations with an 86% level of confidence.
 	Mobile visitors had a 14.3% decrease in donations with an 85% level of confidence.
 	Male visitors had a 42.4% decrease in donations with a 98% level of confidence.
 	45-54 visitors had a 52.0% decrease in donations with a 92% level of confidence.
 	Email visitors had a 145.5% increase in donations with a 97% level of confidence.
 	65+ visitors had a 46.5% decrease in donations with an 86% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How adding a humanized sticky bar to a main donation page impacts donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a personalized sticky bar for main donation page visitors will achieve a higher donor conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/08/2022 - 07/31/2022
In efforts to increase the donor conversion rate on the main donation page, IMB added a humanized sticky bar at the top of their page. This sticky bar would not have a call to action and would just be a preemptive “thank you” from Paul Chitwood (IMB’s President). Our hypothesis was that adding a message from Paul Chitwood would remind the prospective donor that they’re hearing from a person, not just an organization. This would in turn, increase donor conversion rate on this page.
experiments.keyLearnings: After running for 20 days, the treatment made no impact on conversion rate. This experiment resulted in a 6% decrease in conversion rate but with only a level of confidence of 57%. Meaning, half the the time it will result in a 6% decrease, and half the time it will not. Our conclusion is that those who are coming to the main donation page already have a high level of motivation. They're deliberately coming to this page to make a donation and therefore may not be impacted by a thank you message from Paul Chitwood. As a follow up test, we'd like to add a message from Paul Chitwood with value proposition-based language on the actual donation page.

experiments.title: How moving a premium offer above the fold impacted donations for Pathway to Victory

rows.name: Kids book above the fold on website

experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that moving an offer above the fold for website visitors will achieve a change in donor conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/31/2022 - 06/06/2022
Pathway to Victory is a ministry who has built a fundraising program around the offer of premiums—books, pamphlets, brochures, etc. They have 2 main match campaigns throughout the year- one in December, and one in June. This year’s June match campaign focused on the launch of a brand new children’s book, coupled with a $500,000 challenge match.
Traditionally, the homepage of Pathway to Victory only shows an image of the featured monthly premium as a small image above the fold, and a larger focus below the fold. For this experiment, we wanted to see whether moving the image and description of the kids book above the fold would make an impact on donor conversions.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Moving the image of the new kids book above the fold had a meaningful impact on donor conversion rates and revenue, despite not reaching a 100% level of confidence. The 32.6% increase in donation conversions, with a 78% level of confidence, indicates that placing the image on an area where most of website visitors will see will lead to a change in donations.
Our hypothesis is that because the majority of traffic to the homepage is driven through radio and TV offers where they reference the premium offer, placing the image above the fold made it easier for potential donors to make a donation without having to search for where they can find the book.
Since Pathway to Victory is a premium-focused organization, this is a test we will continue to run with every offer.

experiments.title: How a radically redesigned donation page impacted gifts during a high urgency campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a radically redesigned donation page for fiscal year end campaign visitors will achieve an increase in donor conversion.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/26/2022 - 08/01/2022
DTS was running their annual fiscal year-end campaign and we wanted to optimize the standard donation page that is typically used during this campaign. We developed a treatment that incorporated the following elements: we updated the headline to include urgency by referencing the deadline of “before midnight on June 30”. We also used bullet points to demonstrate opportunities for impact in the body copy. The language also gave specific evidentials of how the donor’s gift would be used, stating that for every $75 you give, you can offer our online courses to 15 people. We also introduced some donor autonomy and re-highlighted the urgency of the deadline in the subheader leading into the donation form “If you want to support…please make your gift using the form below:”
experiments.keyLearnings: What we found after running the test for the duration of the campaign was that the radical redesign treatment had a 5% decrease in conversion rate with 44% level of confidence so the results are not valid. Our hypothesis for the flat results is that 1) the donation page copy is longer than the control which could have introduced additional friction to the experience for those in the treatment group and 2) the donation page copy used in the treatment is still organization-centric and not donor-focused. All 4-5 paragraphs begin with the word "DTS" or "we" and the bullet points are organization-centric in terms of being presented as "here's what we are already doing" rather than posed as "what your gift will help accomplish". We also feel that the urgency could have been better communicated by asking for a gift "now" or "today" rather than any time before June 30. We need to better compel visitors to make their gift at the time of their visit to the page. There is no clear need or problem presented in the copy in the treatment for the donor to address or solve, which is something we will want to consider framing out for future tests.

experiments.title: How splitting out a monthly donate button impacted monthly donor donations
experiments.organization: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
categoryNames: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that calling attention to a monthly donor program for potential donors will achieve a change in donor conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/18/2022 - 05/16/2022
The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate serve poor and needy people in the United States and more than 70 countries around the world. The recruitment of monthly donors makes up a large part of their digital program; new monthly donors are offered the opportunity to have a candle lit at the shrine of our Lady of the Snows every month.
For this experiment, which we ran during the Easter season, we wanted to test whether splitting out a donate and monthly-donate-specific button would have any impact on new monthly donor conversions.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This experiment had a 23% increase in the number of new monthly donors who were converted using the monthly call out button, but only had a 46% level of confidence given the lower total number of new monthly donors that came in during that time period.
Despite this experiment running during what is a traditionally a high-impact giving season (Easter), the number of donations was not enough to give us a valid result.
Nevertheless, this is a concept that we will continue testing on the Oblate's main website, especially given the importance of new monthly donors on their program overall.

experiments.title: The impact of a floating shoe icon compared to a floating heart icon on donor conversion for traffic to the website
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a floating heart icon will convert as many or more donors as a floating shoes icon.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/26/2022 - 06/17/2022
Buckner International is a Christian nonprofit that has been serving children and families for more than one hundred years. They’ve been testing into ways to convert more of the traffic to their website into donors. Recently, we tested the impact of a floating “shoes” icon that when clicked, led donors to their donation page to provide needy children with a pair of comfortable shoes and socks. We found that the floating icon did improve donor conversion. This led to a question about whether a heart icon leading to their general donation page would be more or less effective than the shoe icon.
We wanted to be able to drive traffic to their general page rather than just their campaign specific shoes page and hoped that this would be as effective or better than the shoes icon.
experiments.keyLearnings:
As expected, we were not able to validate any statistical difference on donor conversion between the two floating icons. The primary impact on donor conversion is not limited to a specific design, but rather the fact that there is a floating, eye-catching donation button on the page.
This allows Buckner to use a floating "heart" icon to drive traffic to the general donation page throughout the year and then use the shoes icon during high urgency campaigns raising money for shoes.

experiments.title: How a radical redesign impacted gifts on a survey confirmation page
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a radically redesigned donation page for survey takers will achieve a lift in donor conversion.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/09/2022 - 06/17/2022
Email conversion rates on a survey offer were performing exceptionally well but the instant donor conversion was sufferably low. We wanted to produce a radical redesign treatment to take advantage of the high traffic coming to the page and see if we could improve conversion. We added a personalized headline, and brought in recency and urgency to the timeline of the issue throughout the body copy. We also introduce the concept of donor autonomy by asking “Would you consider…” and saying “If you make a gift today…” and “Will you join?” Rather than using bullet points to highlight principles of belief like the control, we instead outline the ways their gift will be used in these bullet points demonstrating the impact.
experiments.keyLearnings: After running the experiment we observed a 9.8% lift in donations with a 27.5% level of confidence so the test did not validate. When reviewing the results, we hypothesized that both the control and the treatment copy are long and required a lot of scrolling on the page to actually reach the donation form itself that could be causing unnecessary friction. For the next test on this page, we will develop a treatment that has shorter copy and leaves the donation form in view.

experiments.title: How a radical redesign of an instant donation page impacts donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a radical redesign on an instant donation page for those who sign up for a course will achieve a higher donor conversion rate .
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/07/2022 - 06/03/2022
Reasons to Believe initially ran a radical redesign experiment on their main donation page that provided a significant lift in donor conversion rate. We wanted to see if this same radical redesign applied to an instant donation page to an offer, would result in the same type of lift. The motivation behind someone giving on a main donation page (someone who comes to the page with the intention to give) differs from someone who comes across a donation page after acquiring a resource. We applied this radical redesign to a funnel that was already getting a high instant conversion rate (Listen & Learn, an online course).
Most of the traffic directed to this page was through paid Facebook ads.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Observations of the impact on other visitor segments in the experiment:
Returning visitors had a 186.2% increase in donations with a 93% level of confidence.
 	Female visitors had a 100.0% decrease in donations with an 86% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How a radical redesign of an instant donation page impacts donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a radical redesign on an instant donation page for those who sign up for a course will achieve a higher donor conversion rate .
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/07/2022 - 06/03/2022
Reasons to Believe initially ran a radical redesign experiment on their main donation page that provided a significant lift in donor conversion rate. We wanted to see if this same radical redesign applied to an instant donation page to an offer, would result in the same type of lift. The motivation behind someone giving on a main donation page (someone who comes to the page with the intention to give) differs from someone who comes across a donation page after acquiring a resource. We applied this radical redesign to a funnel that was already getting a high instant conversion rate (Listen & Learn, an online course).
Most of the traffic directed to this page was through paid Facebook ads.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The results for this experiment did not validate even after running for a significant amount of time. We saw a 51% increase in donor conversion rate but with only a level of confidence of 63.9%. Because this has already validated on the main donation page and because it is a better experience for the end-user, we decided to role out the treatment to 100% of the audience anyway. It could be that this funnel was not getting enough traffic through the sign up page in order for this experiment to validate.
Observations of the impact on other visitor segments in the experiment:
Returning  visitors had a 186.2% increase in donations with a 93% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How a radically redesigned landing page impacted email acquisition for a free course
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that increasing clarity and credibility through imagery for landing page visitors will achieve an increase in online course subscribers.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/14/2022 - 05/21/2022
On a past eBook offer, we had seen a highly visual landing page increase email conversion so we wanted to test the same hypothesis on an online course offer. We developed a treatment that highlighted an image of Tim Keller, one of the instructors featured in the course, to add some clarity and credibility to the offer. We split traffic between the plain-text control vs. the radically redesigned layout of the treatment.
experiments.keyLearnings: After running the experiment for one month, we saw there was a 3.5% decrease in conversion rate with just a 23.3% level of confidence so the test is not valid and the results were flat. One interesting thing to note is when we looked downstream one step further into the funnel, donations experienced a 68.2% decrease with a 86% level of confidence in the treatment, indicating a directional decline. We believe this is because the highly designed landing page doesn't have enough synergy with the plain-text donation page that follows it, making the control landing page more congruent with the experience for each step. Next, we will test adding the Faith & Work course graphic on the plain-text page to see if adding a visual element will help indicate to visitors they are taking the correct next step to get the offer and increase email acquisition.

experiments.title: How changing the layout of a campaign donation page affects donations
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a 2 column layout on a campaign donation page for potential donors visiting the campaign donation page during a HUC will achieve a greater number of donations.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/22/2022 - 04/20/2022
During a high urgency fiscal year end campaign, e3 Partners wanted to test a 2 column layout on a campaign donation page to see if there was an appreciable difference in total donations. The control would have a single column layout with a donation form at the bottom. The treatment would have a donation form on the right side of the copy. It is worth noting that the mobile version would default to a single column layout with a donation form at the bottom to provide a better user experience.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Key takeaways from this experiment were:
The timeframe was too short to get any statistical significance A small increase in conversions was not enough to make a judgement either wayIt is worth continuing to experiment with layouts
Observations of the impact on other visitor segments in the experiment:
Tablet visitors had a 23.1% increase in donations with a 95% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How a landing page layout redesign impacted email acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that changing the layout for landing page visitors will achieve an increase in email acquisition.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/01/2022 - 04/15/2022
Through previous testing, we had found a winning version of the landing page but we identified a few changes we could implement to further optimize and test to achieve a higher conversion rate on the page. We developed a treatment that moved the eBook image to the right side of the page, relocated the supporting value proposition text/”reasons to download” from the bottom of the page into bullet points above the form, and added this copy leading into the form: “Tell us where to send your free copy now:”
experiments.keyLearnings: We ran the experiment for one month and only achieved a 23.2% level of confidence that there was a 0.67% decrease in email acquisition on the page so the results are not valid. However, we did observe a valid increase in acquisition with some specific segments in the experiment that are worth noting: Email visitors had a 52.2% increase in email acquisitions with a 100% level of confidence. 45-54 aged visitors had an 18.3% increase in email acquisitions with a 96% level of confidence, and 55-64 aged visitors had a 34.6% increase in email acquisitions with a 100% level of confidence. Overall, what we learned was that putting the form lower on the page by increasing the text ahead of the call to action could have been causing an adverse effect on conversion for some visitors. For future testing, we will shorten the copy ahead of the form to see if the length of the copy is interrupting the experience and causing friction, and distracting from the intended call to action.

experiments.title: Does adding a donor’s last gift into a supporter card renewal appeal increase renewals?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that reminding a donor of their last gift date for members of AFP will achieve higher click throughs and donor conversion rates..
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/19/2022 - 03/05/2022
Americans for Prosperity has a long-standing membership card, which is promoted through mail and digital channels. For this year’s renewal campaign, we wanted to test whether including the image of the card, with a donors’ information overlaid on top of that image, coupled with a shorter copy, would lead to a higher click rate and higher donor conversion.
experiments.keyLearnings: Our treatment version with image and donor information performed better than the control version, with a 10% increase in click throughs. While not a valid result, it also resulted in 800% more donations than the control version.

experiments.title: How a radically redesigned calendar year-end donation page impacted donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a radical redesign for a calendar year-end donation page will achieve an increase in donor conversion.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/28/2021 - 01/04/2022
Because we saw a lift in donor response in a previous experiment when adding relevant value proposition to a post-survey donation page, we wanted to introduce similar language on the calendar year-end donation page for TGC to capture the additional responses from email appeal traffic. In addition to outlining the value proposition in the body copy, we revised the headline to indicate how the donor’s gift would be used and mention the upcoming deadline. We also added a countdown clock at the top of the page to indicate urgency and added a live thermometer at the bottom of the page to show progress toward the revenue goal. Beneath the donation button, we added a sentence supporting a value proposition demonstrating the impact TGC donors have had throughout the year. Finally, we removed the navigation bar and alternated conflicting calls to action in the footer to keep the donor on the page and stay focused on moving forward with making their gift.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Overall, we did not receive enough traffic to this particular page to validate the experiment. However, we did capture additional learnings by running this same test on the main donation page. Those learnings can be viewed in the write-up for that experiment which is linked here.

Observations of the impact on other visitor segments in the experiment:
Referral visitors had an 80.6% increase in donations with a 93% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How a radical redesign impacted donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that providing additional clarity for donation page visitors will achieve a higher conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/11/2020 - 07/29/2020
We saw a low conversion rate on an instant donation page following a high-value offer (a free 30 day devotional eBook), so we created a treatment to the donation page. While the control copy included relevant Scripture and had ordered steps that were clear to follow, we realized it did not make a clear enough or strong enough ask. The copy was heavily centered around getting the donor to say “yes” to specific prayer requests, and the final call to action was “if you’d like to make a gift today,” which did not indicate urgency or need.
