The treatment copy was written to introduce the felt needs that people were experiencing and give more details about why a potential donor’s support was needed to produce resources like this devotional eBook. We also put a tangible, specific ask in the treatment copy outlining exactly what a gift of a certain amount could accomplish: “Your gift of $50 will allow us to get this resource into the hands of 20 more people who are in need…” and added two clear calls to action “Will you make a gift” and “Please make a gift” to prompt the donor.
experiments.keyLearnings: Unfortunately, the experiment did not receive enough traffic to validate. While we did not reach statistical significance, we did have a few observations to note. We saw a 90% increase in conversion rate with a 72.6% level of confidence. On the flip side, even though the treatment copy asks very specifically for a gift of $50, we saw a sharp drop in the average gift on the treatment page. Additionally, mobile visitors had a 2.3% conversion rate while desktop visitors were converting at a 9.1%, so additional tests to optimize this page specifically for mobile traffic could be explored moving forward.

experiments.title: How a radical redesign of a landing page with a seasonal offer impacted email conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a radical redesign for landing page visitors will achieve an increase in email conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/12/2021 - 04/08/2021
In the weeks leading up to Easter, a sticky bar on the homepage was pointing visitors to a landing page where they could sign up for a free resource offer with Easter-focused family activities. We wanted to test a radical redesign of the landing page to see if we could increase overall email acquisition on this offer. We developed a treatment that removed the traditional footer with links to alternate pages on the site to avoid distraction from the intended call to action, which was to submit the email acquisition form and sign up for the free PDF.
We also revised the header on the page from “Adventures in Odyssey: Easter Faith Building Activities!” to “Get access to your free copy now and help your family celebrate Easter together with this free PDF of fun ideas for activities and devotionals to prompt meaningful conversations!” in an effort to make the offer less organization-focused and more focused on the page visitor.
The third element we changed for the treatment version of the page was enhancing the overall value proposition and added in language that helped address the why behind someone might benefit from the contents of the free guide rather than only sharing what was inside. We spoke directly to the felt needs of the audience and the emotions they might be experiencing as a parent and how this resource could alleviate some of the stress that often accompanies the anticipation of the Easter holiday.
Finally, we changed the CTA on the form from “To get your FREE download of Easter Faith Building Activities, fill out the form below” to “Tell us where to send your FREE copy of Adventure in Odyssey’s Easter Faith Building Activities:” and switched the button copy from “Download Here” to “Send my free copy »” to make this portion feel like less work for the user. We set up the control and the treatment to run as an A/B test and split traffic to each version.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Since this was a limited time offer, we didn't have enough run-time to acquire the number of visitors to the page needed to validate results and we only reached a 35% level of confidence on the experiment. However, what we saw was a few observations on specific visitor segments that are outlined below. Even though we did not reach statistical significance overall, we can use these learnings to guide the development of language for future tests that target display/tablet visitors and speak more relevantly to females and those who are 65+. We could also use this knowledge to our advantage by keeping this audience in mind for introducing social proof on a landing page by adding a testimonial quote from a 65+ female who found the resource valuable. We can also test each of these items from the radical redesign individually on other free offers in the future to have more insight into which changes led to these increases and decreases per segment.
Desktop visitors had a 31.7% decrease in outbound link click with a 100% level of confidence.
 	Display visitors had a 42.1% increase in outbound link click with a 100% level of confidence.
 	Email visitors had a 34.9% decrease in outbound link click with a 94% level of confidence.
 	Tablet visitors had a 33.1% increase in outbound link click with a 100% level of confidence.
 	Organic Search visitors had a 28.5% decrease in outbound link click with a 95% level of confidence.
 	Referral visitors had a 32.0% decrease in outbound link click with a 100% level of confidence.
 	Female visitors had a 39.5% increase in outbound link click with a 98% level of confidence.
 	Direct visitors had a 100.0% decrease in outbound link click with a 99% level of confidence.
 	Social visitors had a 100.0% decrease in outbound link click with a 97% level of confidence.
 	25-34 visitors had a 72.0% decrease in outbound link click with a 100% level of confidence.
 	35-44 visitors had an 86.2% decrease in outbound link click with a 100% level of confidence.
 	65+ visitors had a 1,211.5% increase in outbound link click with a 100% level of confidence.
 	18-24 visitors had a 34.0% increase in outbound link click with a 100% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How a redesign of a newsletter offer impacted clicks from the homepage
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding value proposition for homepage visitors will achieve more clicks to sign up for a newsletter.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/07/2021 - 08/02/2021
As we evaluated the Adventures in Odyssey homepage, we wanted to find a way to increase the number of people who were clicking on the link to sign up for the newsletter. We wanted to develop a treatment that increased clarity about what the visitor would receive through the newsletter, emphasized the appeal by highlighting the benefit and value of getting access to the content rather than not signing up at all, and indicated the offer was unique, exclusive, and why it is distinct from signing up for any other newsletter. We took the control headline “Sign up for our Newsletter” and replaced it with “Get access to the latest resources for your family”. We also revised the supporting body copy from “Sign up to receive the monthly Adventures in Odyssey newsletter directly to your inbox” to “We’d love to share our most recent and helpful resources with you and your family! Be inspired to grow in your faith through these exclusive updates you won’t find anywhere else!”. And the final element we changed was the button text which read “Subscribe Now” while the treatment version said “Get Access Now”. We split traffic to each version 50/50 and let the experiment run.
experiments.keyLearnings:
We were not able to reach statistical significance on the experiment, but the control saw a 20.8% increase with a 72.5% level of confidence when compared to the results of the treatment. What's particularly interesting is when reviewing the segments, we had a significant portion of samples that were visiting from mobile and who were labeled as "new visitors" but both of these segments show lower conversion rates, so future tests for this page could be more strategically designed to address these types of visitors since they make up the majority of the traffic volume. It also appears that returning visitors are more likely to click to sign up for the newsletter so we could potentially develop additional prompts such as a pop-up on someone's second or third visit that highlights the offer more prominently once we know they have shown a continued interest in the content. Phase two of this experiment involves testing the landing page copy where people end up once someone has clicked the button to subscribe so we can find out if people are actually converting and entering their email address to get the newsletter on the second step of the process after they have clicked through from the homepage.
Observations of the impact on other visitor segments in the experiment:
Tablet visitors had a 100.0% decrease in newsletter clicks with a 95% level of confidence.New visitors had a 100.0% decrease in newsletter clicks with a 100% level of confidence.Returning visitors had a 131.9% increase in newsletter clicks with a 100% level of confidence.(Other) visitors had a 100.0% decrease in newsletter clicks with a 97% level of confidence.Direct visitors had an 83.4% decrease in newsletter clicks with a 100% level of confidence.Organic Search visitors had a 100.0% decrease in newsletter clicks with a 100% level of confidence.Referral visitors had a 100.0% decrease in newsletter clicks with a 100% level of confidence.Social visitors had a 100.0% decrease in newsletter clicks with a 92% level of confidence.

experiments.title: FY21 – CYE Notecard Image Test
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that changing the image of the notecard for all donors will achieve higher net revenue.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 02/08/2021
The test was run during December 2020 in the notecard, which landed in-homes around 12/15. The election was a prominent topic in the news at the time, most receiving it just after the electoral college cast their votes. We wanted to see if changing the image on the outer envelope made a difference. Three versions were tested:
1) The control was a stark, wintery image of SCOTUS
2) The first test was an image of a family with young kids walking in a snowy forest
3) The second test was an image of a wintery barn with an American flag
experiments.keyLearnings: First, image selection matters. Second, the image of SCOTUS, which has been our control, was the better route to go. This is likely because our donors care deeply about our work at the Supreme Court and it felt most like ADF.

experiments.title: How aligning the image and value proposition for a content offer affect email acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that aligning the image with the offer and increasing the clarity of the value proposition for landing page visitors will achieve an increase in emails acquired.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/01/2021 - 06/12/2021
The Navigators were promoting their Digital Discipleship Journey resource through Facebook ads. Their cost-per-name was above what they projected, and they wanted to see how they could optimize their results. A previous test had shown that an image that was more specific to the offer greatly increased conversion rate and CPA, so they wanted to apply that to the landing page as well. Additionally, they wanted to test the content on the landing page to see if increasing clarity of the offer and removing vertical space could increase conversion.
They created two treatments and ran an A/B/C test to determine a winner.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Ultimately, neither landing page made a significant difference in conversion rate. There was directional evidence that the new image provided a slight bump to conversion rate, but it wasn't near significance.
The 29% conversion rate on the landing page is still low compared to other Navigators offers, but this test suggests that the issue is upstream from the landing page—perhaps in the targeting or in the advertising.

experiments.title: How the order of a signup flow affects conversion on desktop devices
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that reversing the order of login options for mobile visitors will achieve an increase in user signups.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/27/2021 - 04/03/2021
CaringBridge had two options to create an account: using your email, or using single-sign-on (SSO) options like “Login with Google”, “Login with AppleID”, or “Login with Facebook”. Traditionally, these had been vertically ordered email-first, then SSO options.
The team wondered if the rising popularity of SSO options (primarily for reasons of security) would imply that these options should be listed first. They created a simple test to flip the options to see if the SSO-first approach would result in more signups and created CaringBridge sites.
The desktop version of this test showed a decrease in signups—however, the mobile showed promise. So they decided to isolate the mobile test to keep it running longer and cut their losses on desktop signups.
experiments.keyLearnings: Ultimately, this experiment failed to produce a winning treatment with any difference. While there was a small directional lift, it was not close to statistical validity. The team moved on to higher priority tests to drive more new users.

experiments.title: How visual reinforcement of a premium affects donor conversion for a postal chase donation page
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that visual reinforcement of a premium for postal chase visitors will achieve an increase in donations.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/16/2021 - 05/22/2021
The Navigators were sending Easter cards as a premium offer to their direct mail file during the Easter season. They created a donation page for this campaign to receive traffic from the direct mail campaign and also as a landing point for Facebook ads which were shown to that audience to drive additional giving.
The donation page was very short and to the point. On reviewing it, the team wondered if adding a picture of the Easter cards (to reinforce what was sent in the mail) would increase conversion. They put an image of the card on the page, and added an additional paragraph of incentive-focused copy to remind them of the free premium they received.
They split the traffic to see which treatment converted more donors.
experiments.keyLearnings:
While this experiment didn't validate due to low overall traffic, it reveals more tactics that can be used to directionally increase conversion on these pages.
While the same audience is targeted with both campaigns, we must remember that there is a time-delay between the direct mail piece arriving and seeing the digital ad, and must overcompensate to remind them of the piece they received in the mail (which could be one of many). When this reinforcement is done properly,

experiments.title: How a form on the right side of the page affects email acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: A right side form could harm conversion rates
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/30/2021 - 06/04/2021
Focus’s web team wanted to roll out a new landing page format with the form on the right side of the page instead of the bottom. We wanted to assure that this did no harm to email acquisition. So we tested this on a new offer they were launching called Preparing Your Kids for the Teen Years.
experiments.keyLearnings: The majority of the traffic and conversions were on a mobile device. This test showed no results because on a mobile device it is almost exactly the same user experience. So we are going to retest this format on a high traffic page to only desktop users.

experiments.title: How making gift type the first decision point affects donor acquisition on premium offers
experiments.researchQuestion: Clarifying gift type at the beginning of the form will not harm donor acquisition.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/12/2021 - 05/09/2021
FamilyLife found great success using tabbed donation forms on their main donation page. But before rolling this experience out to all of their donation pages, we wanted to ensure that this user experience did not not harm donor acquisition on acquisition offers. So we tested this on one of their most high performing premium offers, A Quiet Place. This was a 50/50 AB split test using an Optimize redirect. So half of the traffic saw the original format and the other half of traffic saw the tabbed form.
experiments.keyLearnings: As we were hoping, the tabbed form did not harm new donor conversion in any significant way. Neither revenue nor donations saw a valid decrease in this test. The next step is to roll this page out to their microsite.

experiments.title: How a new webinar landing page design affects registrations
experiments.researchQuestion: Will adding a time/date bar on the landing page affect webinar registrations?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/07/2020 - 01/20/2020
One of the most common replies we get to webinar notification emails is people trying to clarify what time the webinar is actually at. Despite our best efforts to mention the time and time zone in every piece of communication we send, there still seemed to always be some level of confusion or clarity needed.
To accommodate for this, we added a time/date bar and widened the column of the template in the treatment version.
experiments.keyLearnings: We saw no change in registration rate as a result of this experiment. In this case, we see that as a success. We were able to make the time/date of the webinar much clearer and more apparent without diluting or distracting from the primary value proposition.

experiments.title: Impact of changing the design of a banner ad and directing it to a monthly donation page

rows.name: Dear Reader to pledge to monthly gift

experiments.researchQuestion:
We wanted to learn two things:
Would changing the design of the ad so it was conversational and matched the look/feel of the site decrease visual friction for visitors
Would directing people to a monthly donation page increase donor revenue on the ads.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 07/17/2019
For several years, we have been running banner ads on a popular pro-life website to try to drive traffic to become donors. Our most successful ads have been ads for our pledges, the most recent being one against infanticide. We wondered if making our ad match the look and feel of the site, rather than have its own design, would decrease friction for visitors. Additionally, we wanted to see how well conversational language would work in the ad. Our final desire was to see how well directing people to a monthly donation page vs a one-time donation would impact the revenue generated from the ads.
experiments.keyLearnings:
We ran the test for a month. Both ads had great engagement, however the ad on the sidebar got far more clicks than the dear reader variation. We also, as expected, got far more donations on the sidebar ad as it led to a one-time donation page. We brought in 19 donations and raised $633 on that ad. However, through the dear reader variation ad, we brought in 4 new monthly donors and raised $195.70 in their first months gifts. This will result in $2380 over the first year of their giving, 369.7% higher revenue than the control ad. 

Moreover, while the sidebar ad had far more clicks than our variation, it had a lower conversion rate to donors, even with it using a one-time donation page. .27% of clicks on the ad became one-time donors while .31% of clicks on the dear reader ad became monthly donors. While we didn’t have enough samples to validate this portion of the test, it is encouraging to see that we got higher donor conversion on the dear reader variation.

Our next test will be to change the sidebar ad so it drives traffic from a pledge to a monthly donation page. This will let us see which ad brings in more monthly donors.

experiments.title: How a radical redesign and donation bracketing affects revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: How will a radical redesign and donation bracketing affects revenue?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 05/29/2019
Jerusalem Prayer Team had a successful micro-donation acquisition campaign to raise money to feed Holocaust survivors. However, they wanted to see if they could increase conversion or revenue through additional landing page testing. They created a treatment that centered the value proposition at the top, included a picture of a Holocaust survivor, put the copy on a white background for more legible reading, and then reoriented the gift array. Previously, the gift array offered $3 and $90, with a pre-filled $30 ask in the open field. The treatment page offered the $3 gift, along with a $45 gift and a $90 gift. The hypothesis was that the landing page elements would increase conversion and the bracketing might increase average gift. They launched an A/B test to determine a winner.
experiments.keyLearnings: The treatment produced a 67% increase in revenue that had a valid level of confidence, though with the small overall sample size of donors, this was not fully valid. This showed that the page elements did not, in fact, increase conversion, but that the bracketing did increase the average gift enough to validate the separation between the control and the treatment.

experiments.title: How a radical redesign of a main donation page affected conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will adding third-party validation and more context about the organization in the copy increase conversion rates on the donation page?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/12/2019 - 05/07/2019
The Texas Public Policy Foundation (TPPF) is testing their donation pages to increase conversion rates. They wondered if talking more about Texas and how TPPF can help Texans and the rest of America be more free would make a bigger impact on donations. They decided to take a radical redesign approach to their main donation page. They added a header with a call-to-action, more copy that explained what TPPF does and who they help, removed links that took people to a different page, and added an image and quote of a famous Texan for third-party validation.
experiments.keyLearnings: The results from this experiment were surprising. The radical redesign caused a directional 38.4% decrease in revenue, but with no statistical validity. The original donation page had a better conversion rate, even with the less-inclusive copy and the links to another site. However, because the confidence level is not at or above 95%, this experiment is not statistically valid. TPPF should continue to run experiments on their main donation page, but overall conversions combined will make it difficult to validate a result.

experiments.title: How the landing page design can affect conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Which design will allow for the greatest number of gifts and revenue?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/24/2015 - 09/25/2015
A recent experiment was done to bring older landing pages up to the latest levels of optimization. However, what this experiment proved is that a design that works for one page may have the adverse reaction on another. Based upon these results, we decided to revalidate the main Heritage donation page to ensure that we were using a design that was achieving the best possible results for us.
experiments.keyLearnings: After 90 days, this test had not yet reached statistical significance so we decided to turn it off in order to move to the next experiment. However, based upon a level of confidence above 85%, we can classify this as "approaching validity." This result reinforces that the current simple design of the main donation page is effective with the current amount of copy.

experiments.title: How the donation button design impacts conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Which donation button will drive the most traffic to the donation page?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/19/2016 - 08/12/2016
To optimize the donation funnel for the Susan B. Anthony List organization, we did an analysis to discover the biggest opportunities. What we found was that there were not enough organic visitors getting to the donation page to do any testing in that area. We decided to move our efforts forward in the funnel to try to drive more people to the page.
We had the hypothesis that if we made the donation page easier to find and put it into “first person” language, we could drive more visitors to the donation page. We decided to create an experiment to test the idea.
experiments.keyLearnings: The treatment had a nearly identical click through rate and donor conversion rate when compared to the control. The big take away from this experiment is that the button was not hard to find in the first place and that we did not add any significant value with the change in copy. Without those two factors influencing traffic, the treatment did nothing that would impact visitors making it to the donation page.

experiments.title: How a radical redesign impacted donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will the donation form radical redesign increase donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/13/2016 - 05/23/2016
Jewish Voice Ministries was testing out a radical redesign of their standard donation page. They wanted to validate the impact of multiple factors on the conversion practice. For this experiment, they increased the amount of value proposition copy, moved to a two-step form, and switched to a two column layout to move the donation form closer to the top.
experiments.keyLearnings: After over a month of running, this experiment did not reach a high enough level of statistical validity. When analyzing the treatment, it is difficult to determine what led to this result given the variety of factors tested.

experiments.title: How more visual and design elements on a landing page affect downloads
experiments.researchQuestion: Will adding more design and visual elements lead to more downloads?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/10/2019
NextAfter produces a number of reports, studies, and eBooks each year and has a ‘template’ for the download landing page which is very simple and copy heavy. With The Canadian Online Fundraising Scorecard there were some strong visuals and infographics within the report so we wondered if we shared more of the visual components on the landing page and gave the landing page a bit more of an aesthetic that matched the report itself if people would be more willing to download the report.
experiments.keyLearnings: There was a very slight increase in downloads on the more designed version so it is worth looking at. The treatment had a signup option right away as opposed to the control where you have to scroll/read before you provide your information which means you may choose to download, or not, before you experience the offer or value so we could keep more of the conversational flow similar while adding more visual elements to see if that makes a difference. The visitor also has a pretty high motivation when they hit the page so perhaps the quick download added value. Again, worth looking at in future experiments.

experiments.title: How a change in layout plus addition of video affects sign ups
experiments.researchQuestion: Will changing the layout and adding video to a page affect sign ups?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/21/2018 - 04/20/2018
Hillsdale College wanted to increase conversions on their “Constitutional Conversations” online course sign up page. They wanted to know if removing endorsements and other offers from the enrollment page would impact conversions. They tested the control, which included endorsements and other offers, against a treatment with only the sign up form, a video, and short copy.
experiments.keyLearnings: We did not see a statistically significant difference between the control and treatment versions in this experiment. What’s more, since there were multiple factors being tested, it is hard to know if one of the aspects (video vs. no video and endorsement vs. no endorsement) would have moved the needle by itself. In the future, we will want to isolate those individual variables as separate tests to understand their specific impact.

experiments.title: How a radical redesign of a donation page affects conversion rate
experiments.organization: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
categoryNames: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
experiments.researchQuestion: Does a redesigned donation page that removes header content increase conversion rate?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 07/03/2018
The Missionary Oblates were running a high-urgency fiscal year end campaign and wanted to test whether the format of the donation page was causing friction for people coming to the page and giving them decision anxiety. They proposed a radical redesign that removed all other links from the header and top of the page and streamlined the form and body copy. The copy was the same.
experiments.keyLearnings: The experiment was not statistically valid, but we did see a directional lift of 6% with the redesigned page. This concept would be good to test with additional campaigns along with other elements of the page to produce a valid lift.

experiments.title: How email design impacted opens with a prospecting email list
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a simpler email increase the likelihood of the email being opened?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/23/2018 - 05/25/2018
ADF was using their existing email welcome series to “warm up” the purchased prospecting list from Axciom. The open rate of this list was significantly low compared to the standard house file emails so we decided to test some deliverability tactics to improve this metric. We had found the sender did not play a significant roll in increasing the opens so we decided to test the style of the email. The hypothesis was that a simpler email would not be placed in the “Promotions” tab of systems like Gmail and Yahoo which would increase the likelihood of being opened.
experiments.keyLearnings: We did not see a statistically significant different in open rate between both the designed and plain text emails. This is actually beneficial for ADF as their standard emails to their subscriber base have the more “designed” style which means this email file will fit well within their audience.

experiments.title: How a radical redesign of an in-site ask affected clickthrough rate
experiments.researchQuestion: How will a radical redesign of an end-of-article call-to-action affect clickthrough rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/01/2018 - 05/15/2018
Leadership Institute had seen a large jump in donation traffic through the implementation of an end-of-article call-to-action on their CampusReform website. They wanted to test to see if improved copy and layout could increase clickthrough rate from the site and hopefully increase overall donation revenue. They created a treatment that added increased persuasive copy, a third-party quote, and statistics to bolster the value proposition. They also made a softer ask in the button, asking the reader to “learn more” rather than “support Campus Reform now”
experiments.keyLearnings: Ultimately, there was no valid difference in clickthrough rate. After receiving several hundred impressions per CTA, the treatment showed a slight decrease in clickthroughs, but nowhere near validity. The team went back to the drawing board to try to find something more radical that would positively increase clicks.

experiments.title: How changing the layout of an acquisition page impacts conversion.
experiments.researchQuestion: Would laying the page out differently help increase conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/28/2018 - 04/11/2018
After a person signs a pro-life pledge, they are taken to a confirmation page for the pledge they just signed that is also an acquisition page for the Pro-life 101 online course acquisition page. Care Net hypothesized that simplifying the copy on that page into a stepped approach might help increase conversion of the online course. They developed a treatment that laid out the same copy from the control differently in an effort to make the page easier to read.
experiments.keyLearnings: After running the experiment for over two weeks, there was no significant difference in conversion and had a low level of confidence. What this tells us is that a simplified, stepped approach has no real impact on this page and that a more radical redesign is needed to effect conversion.

experiments.title: How a radical redesign impacts call volume
experiments.researchQuestion: Would a radical redesign increase calls?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/12/2017 - 01/25/2018
One of the top webpages for the Pregnancy Decision Line is the “Types of Abortion” page. We hypothesized that a radical redesign of this page – removing the header image, adding value proposition to the headline and body copy and connecting personally with the end-reader through the tone of the copy – would motivate more people to call, which is the primary objective of the page. They split the traffic coming to this page and tested the two against each other
experiments.keyLearnings: After six weeks of testing this page, we weren’t able to validate the experiment. There was a slight decrease in calls from the treatment, but because of the test’s low level of confidence we aren’t able to pull any learnings from the experiment or know with certainty that the radical would decrease call volume. We will propose a different angle in testing to better understand the motivation of the end-user.

experiments.title: How a radical redesign impacts name conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Would a radical redesign increase conversion?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/22/2018
When evaluating the performance of the Pro-Life 101 online course, Care Net flagged the acquisition page as the biggest opportunity for optimization in the funnel based off of it’s conversion rate. They had been running ads that featured an image of a family. This same image was also featured on their acquisition page. After testing different images with their Facebook ads, we found that an image of a fetus increased click-through on the ads and ultimately people signing up for the course. They hypothesized that creating a radical redesign of the acquisition page featuring the same image of the fetus from the Facebook ad, changing and strengthening the value proposition and using testimonials of people who had already taken the course might help increase conversion. They A/B split the traffic coming to the page and tested this radical redesign.
experiments.keyLearnings: After the running the experiment for over a month and only reaching a 20%, there wasn’t a significant difference between the control and treatment. This suggests that a radical redesign of the page doesn’t impact conversion. That said, because the test had low power as well, we would need a much larger sample size the validate that claim. We propose testing more specific elements on the page to both increase conversion and understand the audience better.

experiments.title: How donation page design affects donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Will the new landing page design affect conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/10/2016 - 06/24/2016
Hillsdale College had recently redesigned their main website. They wanted to apply the new design, which was simpler and used new fonts, to the donation page. But they understood the importance of testing this first, and set up a test between the control design and the new design. The content on the pages stayed the same, which meant that this experiment was really to validate that there was no difference between the two.
experiments.keyLearnings: There was no statistically valid movement either way. In this case, it was a good thing — it meant that the new design didn’t affect donor conversion either way. Though we always like to see the big green arrow with the win, this is a critically important step that many organizations miss when they choose to launch a new design without validating the impact.

experiments.title: How simplifying a donation page and removing designations affects conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: How will removing designation options and reducing visual friction affect conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/07/2017 - 12/21/2017
Though Compassion International’s fundraising focus centered around child sponsorships, they do have a general fund that is collected on their donation page. This page offered donors the opportunity to contribute to a wealth of funds—from HIV to education to clean water.
Their team wondered how all of these designation options affected conversion. Did they present too much decision friction? Did they cause donors to think too much about their gift size?
They wanted to test a single donation ask with one place to enter a gift. Their hypothesis was that this would increase conversion by reducing decision friction. At the same time, they wanted to test the impact of reducing visual friction by moving the copy from being overlaid on the image to a white background, reducing the size of the image.
experiments.keyLearnings:
There was no statistical difference between the control and the treatment. This tells us two important facts—removing the list of gift designations didn’t increase giving by reducing decision friction, but it also didn’t decrease giving by offering fewer options to give. Thus, the amount of giving options was neither the problem, nor the solution.
This indicates that the primary factor affecting conversion on this page might not be friction. Instead, this indicates that the value proposition is the element which should be tested and strengthened. This page’s conversion rate of nearly 50% shows that users are quite motivated when they show up. If we can successfully increase the force of the value proposition, then we have a chance to increase that conversion rate.

experiments.title: How a radical donation page redesign can affect donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a mission-focused page improve donor conversion against a premium-focused page?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/17/2016 - 03/28/2016
Illinois policy Institute was promoting their free e-book about Mike Madigan to several Facebook audiences, and then asking for a donation on the following page. They had a premium-focused page that was not producing donors, and wanted to test a more mission-focused page. They create a treatment that removed the premium (in case that was “getting in the way of the donation”) and focused on the value and the efforts that they are undertaking to remove Mike Madigan from office.
They set up an A/B test to determine a winner.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The mission focused page failed to produce a donor as well, which fosters a few possible conclusions:
The offer might not create enough perceived value to solicit a donation.
 	The audience might not have enough time to digest the offer before making the decision to make a gift.
 	The offer might be targeted at a less philanthropic audience.

experiments.title: How a radical redesign for a year-end donation page impacts conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Would a radical redesign impacts conversion on the year-end donation page?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/21/2017 - 12/27/2017
During the Focus on the Family year-end campaign, we hypothesized that we might be able increase conversion by testing their current year-end donation page with a radical redesign. The radical redesign included removing the video on the page, adding a countdown clock on the page, increasing value proposition and added a giving thermometer.
experiments.keyLearnings:
We weren’t able to validate this experiment but initial data shows us that there was no real significant difference between the control and the treatment. With such a low level of confidence we can hypothesize a couple of take aways and next steps:
The more we learn about the Focus on the Family audience, the more we are realizing that this audience has a very different motivation and we must take a different testing approach.
Normally, we can dramatically impact conversion with radical redesigns. But with the Focus on the Family audience, we want to learn more specifically about what impacts donor motivation and conversion. To do this, we need to isolate specific variables in our testing instead of doing radical redesigns.

experiments.title: How a radical redesign impacts donor conversion during year-end.
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a radical redesign motivate more people to convert?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/19/2018 - 01/27/2018
For FamilyLife’s year-end campaign, they had a special matching gift they offered to people in an effort to motivate more people to give during this critical fundraising campaign. The donation page was focused on this matching gift and encouraged people to give before December 31 to take advantage of this incentive. We hypothesized that while people will be motivated by this incentive, they might be more motivated if we increased urgency and reminded them of what their gift will be used for. We developed a radical redesign of the donation page that led with a FamilyLife value proposition, increased urgency with a countdown clock and also included the match incentive.
experiments.keyLearnings: The experiment didn’t validate, but we did see a directional lift in conversion of 10%. An incentive like a match is great, but we are able to motivate more people to convert if we can also remind them of what their gift is going towards and increase urgency visually. People don’t give to give, they give to have an impact and we must communicate what that impact is.

experiments.title: How simplifying the email design affects clicks
experiments.researchQuestion: A simplified email will have a more personal feel to it which will prompt a greater number of click thoughs.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/20/2014 - 12/22/2014
The standard format for DTS emails is a designed layout with images and a color scheme that matches the website. In an effort to increase the number of visitors to the calendar year end campaign, we created an experiment to test the standard design against a simplified email.
experiments.keyLearnings: The design of the email did not have any significant impact on the supporter’s likelihood of clicking on the email. It also did not have any significant impact on the donors’ propensity to give. Moving forward we will utilize the existing email templates to save both cost and time.

experiments.title: How donor page redesign affects visitor motivation
experiments.researchQuestion: Which donation page will result in the greatest conversion rate and revenue?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/20/2016 - 06/13/2016
After the Safe Bathroom campaign initially launched, we noticed that the donation page had a relatively low conversion rate of 1% to 2%. After examining the donation page, we decided to run a radical redesign that would reduce the length of the page while reinforcing the reason they initially signed the pledge that led them to this page.  This radical redesign would also bring in imagery from the original page to try to reinforce that original value proposition.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This experiment is a good example of why it is necessary to give experiments enough time before making any quick decisions. At the initial launch of the experiment, the Control seemed to have a significantly higher conversion rate but, as time went on, this difference leveled out. By the time we ended this experiment, there was less than a 70% level of confidence in either treatment meaning neither showed significant signs of improvement over the other.
This test largely focused on tactical aspects of the page such as form length and imagery. In the end, those changes were not enough to sway the visitors to give a gift.

experiments.title: How a new landing page design impacted donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: How will the new redesigned page and donation form impact donations?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/17/2016 - 11/10/2016
The Heritage.org website is in the process of a redesign. As part of this transition, it was suggested that the existing donation page design be migrated to the new format. This would include the transition to a new look and feel for the donation form itself. Before making this change, we wanted to measure what kind of impact it would have on the performance of the donation pages.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running for the length of a money bomb campaign, we determined that the new design had no statistically significant impact on the performance of the campaign. While this is reassuring, it has led to two more questions:
How does the donation form design specifically impact conversion? We want to isolate this factor to ensure the more modern design is not having a negative impact on the older donor base.
Once the new site launches, will this new design actually increase conversion by maintaining visual congruence.
Both of these questions will be tested in upcoming campaigns.

experiments.title: How the landing page design affects conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Which design will allow for the greatest number of gifts and revenue?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/24/2015 - 09/25/2015
A recent experiment was done to bring older landing pages up to the latest levels of optimization. However, what this experiment proved is that a design that works for one page may have the adverse reaction on another.
Based upon these results, we decided to revalidate the main Heritage donation page to ensure that we were using a design that was achieving the best possible results for us.
experiments.keyLearnings: After 90 days, this test had not yet reached statistical significance so we decided to turn it off in order to move to the next experiment. However, based upon a level of confidence above 85%, we can classify this as “approaching validity.” This result reinforces that the current simple design of the main donation page is effective with the current amount of copy.

experiments.title: How simplifying the email design affects clicks
experiments.researchQuestion: A simplified email will have a more personal feel to it which will prompt a greater number of click throughs.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/20/2014 - 12/22/2014
The design of the email did not have any significant impact on the supporter’s likelihood of clicking on the email. It also did not have any significant impact on the donors’ propensity to give. Moving forward we will utilize the existing email templates to save both cost and time.
experiments.keyLearnings: The standard format for DTS emails is a designed layout with images and a color scheme that matches the website. In an effort to increase the number of visitors to the calendar year end campaign, we created an experiment to test the standard design against a simplified email.
